text_clean: pashupatinath temple is one of the must-visit places in kathmandu. it’s one of the most important hindu temples in the region, and the whole area has a very intense and authentic atmosphere. every evening there’s a ceremony (aarti) by the river, which is definitely worth seeing. it draws both locals and visitors, and it gives you a real sense of the spiritual life of the city. if you walk up the surrounding hills, you’ll also find more temples and viewpoints, which are much quieter and offer a different perspective of the complex and the river below.

we visited pashupatinath temple one evening to attend the aarati ceremony. upon entry, and man told us he would show us where the ceremony was and then began taking us on a full tour. i should have just walked away, but my kindness got the best of me and i allowed him to show us a few things before i insisted that i wanted to grab a good seat for the ceremony. at that point, he pressed us into paying him an absurd amount for his "tour". we did not pay the price he wanted, but instead gave him 200 rupees. again, as a person who has traveled all over asia, i should have known better, but beware. if you don't want a tour, be firm with these "guides". the aarati ceremony was amazing though, really powerful watching the ceremony with cremations occurring in the background. i always feel a little strange attending religious ceremonies for religions that i do not belong to, but we felt very welcomed by others around us. highly recommend while you are in kathmandu.
amazing divine energy and holy temple.

i really liked the place as the way it. people gathering to worship. market selling flowers and all. children running in the street. great hindu style temples and structure surrounding everywhere bit dirty bit unmanaged but there is a beauty in it.

pashupatinath temple is one of the most sacred hindu temples dedicated to lord shiva. located on the banks of the bagmati river, it is a major pilgrimage site for devotees from around the world. the temple complex features ancient shrines, beautiful pagoda architecture, and a deeply spiritual atmosphere. it is also a unesco world heritage site.

this temple is not a jyotirlinga, yet it is one of the most sacred and important religious place for hindus. the temple is deeply connected with lord shiva and has many fascinating stories and legends associated with it, which make the visit even more meaningful. the temple complex is very huge and beautifully spread out, with many small temples dedicated to different deities are inside the premises. built in traditional nepalese pagoda style architecture, features a square, two-tiered roof covered in gold plating, intricate carved wooden structure and four silver plated main doors. as a walk around, you can feel the spiritual atmosphere everywhere. the shiva linga here is ‘chaturmukh’, which mean it has four faces representing different aspects of lord shiva. all four side doors for darshan opens at 9:30 am, before that, devotees can have darshan from the front side. the entire darshan process is simple and well organised. there is no unnecessary pressure from pandits asking for money for rituals, thus we were able to complete our darshan comfortably and peacefully. the temple is located on the banks of the holy bagmati river. in the evening, a beautiful and peaceful aarti is performed by the river, which is truly divine to witness. on the opposite side of the river is the samshan ghat, reminding us of the cycle of life and death, which is an important belief in hinduism. mobile phones are allowed inside the temple complex, but photography is strictly not permitted. when we visited, we saw many “naga sadhus” probably because we went a day after shivratri. their presence added to the spiritual aura of the place. prasad can be purchased from the stalls outside the temple complex. you will also find rudraksha mala being sold here. overall this temple has a very strong and positive vibration that can truly be felt from within. it is a place that gives peace to the mind and soul. may lord shiva bless everyone who visits this holy temple.

the pashupatinath temple presents itself as a sprawling, impressive ensemble with many stairs and terraces. only from a heightened perspective does the complex develop its full effect and convey the spiritual and architectural grandeur of this important sanctuary.

my trip to pashupati, nepal, last year was an unforgettable experience filled with spirituality, culture, and breathtaking landscapes. visiting the pashupatinath temple, one of the holiest hindu temples dedicated to lord shiva, was the highlight of the journey. the temple’s serene yet powerful atmosphere, with the chants of priests and the sight of devotees offering prayers, created a deeply moving experience. walking along the bagmati river, i witnessed the sacred rituals and ceremonies, which gave me a glimpse into nepal’s rich traditions. the intricate architecture of the temple and the surrounding ashrams made the visit even more special. beyond pashupatinath, the trip also allowed me to explore the vibrant streets of kathmandu, savor delicious nepali cuisine, and interact with the warm and welcoming locals. the blend of spirituality and adventure made it a well-rounded journey. overall, my pashupati nepal tour was a deeply enriching experience. whether you’re seeking spiritual solace or cultural exploration, this destination offers something truly unique. i would highly recommend it to anyone planning a trip to nepal!

i felt it was really important to visit the burning ghats because public cremation is such an important ritual in hindu life, but i also felt a little sheepish about potential voyeurism. it was definitely a solemn visit, but i was surprised to discover that next to us (on the opposite bank from the families and ceremonies) many nepali people were gathered to watch the proceedings, and that they do not know the deceased either. we were told that people come to educate themselves so that they will be familiar with the process when they need to perform it for their loved ones. as an example, the young woman in front of me had her 4-year-old on her lap watching as an ambulance delivered a shrouded corpse to the waiting relatives. in general, once the deceased arrive, the families pray at one of the riverside shrines and then dip the deceased's feet into the river. from there they move down river to the part of the bank where the cremations are performed. we saw a religious man with an earbud and smartphone chanting into a mic. we wondered if he was performing prayers for a family member who could not attend in person.

there are some experiences in life that as a western traveller, you can’t really prepare for, and this is one of them. one of the holiest hindu pilgrimage sites in asia, there are practices undertaken here that have been happening for hundreds maybe thousands of years. the complex takes in ten different sites including the peaceful gorakhnath mandir where you are surrounded by many shivalaya’s – shiva shrines close to a wooded area. there are several busy hindu temples. as a non-hindu, you can only view these from the outside, but it is still possible to admire the architecture. there are colourful dreadlocked holy men known as sadhus, people offering meditation with healing bowls and fortune tellers all derived from the mysticism of history. the last and most striking images are of cremation. when we visited we saw the family of a deceased person bring down their wrapped body to the rivers edge where they carefully applied purification rights to the head and feet. from here it is taken around 100 meters to a funeral pyre at the edge of the river where a wood structure was already prepared. it is set alight. when the process is finished the ashes are raked into the river. there were at least six of these ceremonies taking place when we visited. it is possible to view the process from across the river at a respectful distance. entry to the site is 1000 rupees per person.

pashupatinath temple is an absolute must-visit for anyone coming to kathmandu. as one of the holiest hindu temples in nepal and a unesco world heritage site, it offers a profound spiritual experience unlike any other. the temple is dedicated to lord shiva and is set on the banks of the sacred bagmati river, adding to its serene atmosphere. the intricate architecture, golden roof, and impressive pagoda-style design make it a breathtaking sight. the temple complex is vast, with many smaller shrines and peaceful courtyards to explore. one of the most unique experiences at pashupatinath is witnessing the cremation rituals on the riverbank, which are an important part of hindu tradition. the sight is both humbling and deeply meaningful, offering insight into the culture and spiritual practices of the locals. the air around pashupatinath is filled with the fragrance of incense and the sound of chanting prayers, creating an environment of peace and devotion. visitors can also explore the nearby forest and ashrams for a tranquil escape. whether you're a spiritual seeker or a traveler interested in learning more about hindu traditions, pashupatinath temple offers a sacred and unforgettable experience.

pasupatinath temple is more than just a religious site; it's a spiritual oasis that pulsates with a unique, serene energy. nestled on the banks of the sacred bagmati river, this unesco world heritage site is a living testament to nepal's rich cultural heritage. as you step into the temple complex, you're enveloped in an aura of tranquility. the intricate carvings, the rhythmic chanting of mantras, and the gentle flow of the river create a harmonious symphony that soothes the soul. the temple's architecture, a blend of hindu and buddhist influences, is a masterpiece that reflects the country's diverse religious tapestry. beyond its architectural grandeur, pasupatinath is a place of profound spiritual significance. pilgrims from far and wide come here to seek blessings, offer prayers, and connect with the divine. the atmosphere is charged with devotion and reverence, making it an ideal spot for introspection and inner peace. whether you're a spiritual seeker or simply looking for a moment of respite, pasupatinath temple offers an unparalleled experience. let the positive vibes of this sacred place wash over you and leave you feeling refreshed and rejuvenated.

pashupatinath means "lord of the animal". it is said that when the pandavas went to kashi atoned for killing the ancestors in the kurukshetra war (to get rid of the sins of brahman hatya and gotra hatya). but lord shiva went to north india. the pandavas started worshiping the lord shiva. lord shiva became satisfied by the austerities of the pandavas and appeared to the pandavas in the form of a bull. seeing the pandavas, lord shiva took the form of a bull. bhim could recognize the bull as lord shiva and tried to catch him. the bull started running away and disappeared. while his hand stayed in tungyanath (uttarkhand), hump stayed in kedarnath (uttarkhand), his face stayed in rudranath (uttarkhand), his navel and stomach stayed madhyamaheshwar (uttarkhand), his hair (jata) stayed in kalpeshwar nath temple (uttarkhand). altogether is called pancha kedar. in his head part emerged in pashupatinath, nepal. pashupatinath is also regarded as jyotirlinga because it is believed to be half part of kedarnath jyotirlinga.

a beautiful experience and a must visit place to worship if you are in kathmandu or in nepal. you will see and explore the richness of nepalese l, hindu culture and of course the journey of life and death-in aryaghat.
god bless (help) us every time if we remember or pray with our heart. visit nepal one time in your life .never miss #pasupatinath temple#

cremation by burning. it's not so surprising for me because when i was in india, on the banks of ganges, i saw cremation. but to know the process it's great here. my guide jayram explained to me that when the body is burnt all its positive and negative energies are burnt with fire. nothing can escape fire, so to completely destroy the body firing cremation is important. it's great to know the hindu culture.

temple where cremations of the deceased and ganga aarti ceremony are performed in the evening. both are visually very exciting, unfortunately they are very strict in not allowing non-hindus to pass on the side of the river where they hold funerals. once finished and when there are fewer people, however, it is quieter. unlike varanasi in india there weren't many people taking photos and videos and the funerals were more heartfelt.
nice place to experience new culture and traditions. looking at the life how it turns to end. gives different ways of lookibg life

this was my second time visiting a hindu crematorium after varanasi. i felt it was more open than varanasi. in varanasi, some photography was strictly prohibited, but here many people were taking pictures of the cremation, and it was quite open. how you feel about it is up to you, but i definitely think it's a place you should visit at least once if you come to kathmandu.
my experience visiting at pashupati nath temple was very peaceful and religious, we got chance to see the evening aarti. overal our visit to pashupati nath was great.

i was sadly surprised to see that after paying 1000 rupees i was not allowed to visit the temple where are performed the puja (rituals). it's a pity. in the churches and buddhist temples you're not asked to what religion you belong

it was a divine experience. you will love it as a spritual place and not as tourist. don't go if are going only as tourist because you tend to compare it as a tourist place only. this is a spritual place with divine energy which only a true heart can feel

pashupatinath temple is considered as principle pilgrimage site of hinduism. the temple is on unesco world heritage sites list for decades. the extensive hindu temple has a sprawling collection of images, inscriptions, ashrams, temples and images. the temple has 275 holy abodes of shiva as well. temple architecture is quite intriguing as it is built in nepalese pagoda style of architecture. the temple is located on the bank of holy river, bagmati where cremation of dead body takes place.

one of the holiest hindu temples not only in nepal, located approx. 5 km from the center of the capital. it is dedicated to the god shiva. situated on the bank of the bagmati river, right next to the gates used for traditional cremations. the current temple building was built at the end of the 17th century. it has the form of a nepalese pagoda with a gold-plated roof. only hindus are allowed to enter the temple. we start our visit here by buying a ticket at the tourist office. the ticket costs 1,000 rupees. on its back there is information about the regulations and rules for visiting this place. for tourists, the only option is to cross the bagmati river to its eastern side by bridge. from the other bank you can watch the main temple, funeral ceremonies and cremations. you can also easily walk between the numerous chapels and take photos with the sadhu "saints" posing [for a fee]. the complex has been on the unesco world heritage list since 1979.

this temple is dedicated to pashupati, a form of shiva and is near the bagmati river in kathmandu. it is a world heritage site it was built in the 1600s to replace the previous one, destroyed by termites

we stayed at hotel kailash kuti inn which is just opp the mandir. for hindus, who go here for religious yatra, it is best option. fully veg hotel. we have darshans at mandir 3 times. 1. first day when we reach there in the afternoon, we went to mandir around 5pm. evening aarti is at 6pm. all the four inner gates of mandir are open for darshans. 2. on the first day at our request hotel reception called pandit ji at hotel and we fixed for next morning. also we purchased rudraksh malas from outside the hotel bazaar, which is the main rudraksh mkt. abhishekam at mandir is from 9-11am. we went with panjit ji at 8.30. there he do pooja for around 1 hour in the mandir premises at some fixed place away from garbgreh. after that he took us to garbgreh and we had darshans from just 3-4 feet away from the lingam. only mandir main pujaris are there near the lungam. they put our rudraksh malas on the lingham and give us back. they also give us two rudraaksh malas from there own. we paid our panjit ji as fixed by us. they charge inr3100 for pandit or inr 5100 for two pandits etc. pooja done by them was really very good. they themself got parchi from mandir for abhisekam. 3. on the last day of our trip to nepal, as we had purchased more rudraksh malas, we went again to mandir for darshans. pujaris there touch the malas there and return us.

a very sobering place to visit. from our vantage point on the opposite side of the river we were able to watch families bring their dead relatives to be cremated on the side of the river and then their ashes were swept into the river. their deep respect for the dead was evident in the way the whole process was undertaken. swimming in the river were a number of small boys who had no concern for what was floating in the river.
apart from its temple structures and surroundings of the day, the evening aarati is something you must not miss! if you miss the aarati then you miss the essence of the pashupti vibes!

this is a holy temple of lord shiva. the temple structure is quite unique compared to other hindu temples around the world. only hindus are allowed into the temple. others can be around the premises and go around it. no photos are allowed inside the temple premises. it's believed that one should visit this temple and get a blessing before going hiking in nepal. we were told to do so and we did. our quest to reach abc was very smooth. at the back of the temple is the holy river of baghmati where prayers and rituals for the demise are carried out. you will need to pay nr1000 for each foreigner to enter. it's a very rare scene and if you can't take it, better not see it.

if you are visiting kathmandu for the first time, you must visit pashupatinath temple. wonderful group of temples with a lot of mythology around it. from temple architecture point of view as well, there is a lot to explore. you can hire a guide as well to understand more.

very spiritual visit. beautiful temples on one side of the river, multiple burials taking place on the other side. if you are not a hindu you are not allowed inside the temple but the view from the other side of the river is still very impressive
was in main sanctrum and it acmt be explained in words. a sacred feeling. om namah shivaya. oldest shiva temple with lot of history and diverse.
beware only hindus are allowed inside. yet the view of the funeral ceremony and cremation is a unique experience.

here you can watch burial ceremonies all day long. with plenty of respect, you can watch the ceremony, including washing and cremation, from the bridges or directly from the other bank. for europeans, it will take some getting used to, but it's a clear case of getting to know other cultures. tip: monkeys live throughout the temple. so be careful with the things you carry with you.

the entrance fee cost you 1,000 / person and you will see the dead bodies being cremated in public. nothing special from this temple especially for non hindu as they will not allow you to enter the shiva temple which i found it very odd.

for the westerner, this is such a complex mix of values that we may find very confronting. such as… - the public display of family grief with onlookers and tourists looking on. - kids frolicking in the river right next to a ritual body preparation. - the expectation that tourists will take pics of a cremation (see rule 6) - trinket selling vendors pleading for a sale just a few observations. maybe we get it wrong? 🤷♂️

we arrived in pashupatinath with a guide; we entered the complex from the tourist side - entrance fee is 1000 rupees. on the road to the main scene there are residential buildings and various retail outlets, monkeys and livestock roam everywhere. the temple is considered to be the world's most important holy shiva temple and is visited by locals every day. tourists are not allowed to enter the temple. cremation takes place every day throughout the day, so you can visit this place at any time. the process can be observed from the opposite side (where we came from), visibility is good. since people burn here all day long, it is better to take a mask with you. and so, the place perfectly conveys the culture of nepal.

a special way to understand hinduism is by looking at how they handle cremation and view life. they believe that humans are closely connected to nature. this shows a fascinating aspect of their culture and how they see the link between people and the natural world.
pashupatinath temple is the oldest .hindu temple in kathmandu. it is known for certain when pashupatinath temple was built
a devine experience, very unhurried and blissful. we went early in the morning and had a good darshan.

during volunteering in nepal the volunteer organisation planned some tours of many of the main tourist attractions and heritage sites in kathmandu. during the visit, the guide was very well informed and lived at the site in a cave directly beneath the temple. when arriving at the temple, there were massive crowds and we were told that the prime ministers’ wife had passed away and her funeral service (cremation) was happening at the temple with many police, army officers and officials present including the prime minister. we toured the temple courtyard and sat in front of the cremation area for the prime ministers wife. our guide talked us through the temple and the ceremonies. we then had the opportunity to meet the prime minster and then went to our guides cave. we didn’t enter the temple as it wasn’t open and it only allows hindus inside. it costs 1,000 np to enter. i’d suggest organising a guide and politely saying no to those selling things in the area. we visited during a very important event, so it was very crowded, but would assume this would be different during normal times. it’s a very sacred place and linked to shiva and the hindu gods. definitely worth a visit but i would recommend being respectful as it is a location where people celebrate their dead and cremate their family.
beautiful place but there is a restriction inside main entrance. that was kind of sad to us. yet the place to wonderful
tourist are not allowed to enter inside the real temple because only hindus are allow. so we will have to watch next to the river.
a serene look into life, death, and religion. i have a deep respect for nepalese and their traditions. pashupatinath is just special.

the most famous temple in kathmandu. currently this is the largest temple premises in the world. photography is strictly prohibited inside the main temple but allowed elsewhere. there are a lot of temples and shiva (pashupatinath) temple is the main one. amazing vibes.
this is for those who wanna see shiva and his bhakths, photography is strcitly prohibited and you can feel the inner peace

this is a very popular temple of lord shiva in kathmandu, nepal. devotees from different countries visit this temple to get the blessings of lord shiva. temple is very big and there are many small temples around the main temple.

i am teaching in kathmandu for several weeks, and was excited to visit this temple today. we were happy to pay the 15.00 usd to support this historic sight. perhaps it is my fault for not doing enough research-- but to discover we were not allowed (as non-hindus) into the temple courtyard was extremely disappointing. (it was our understanding that we could enter the courtyard, just not the temple.) there is not a lot more to see that we could not see at many other historic temples in india and nepal without the expense. the cremation grounds are interesting (keeping a respectful distance)- but admission is not necessary to see those. just want to make it very clear- you will not be able to see much for the price that you will pay.
the temple is as good as its fame. very spacious and peaceful. the priests are quite helpful. overall a nice experience

pashupatinath temple, one of the busiest place with mass gathering, is a hindu temple dedicated to lord pashupati. it is located in kathmandu, nepal near the bagmati river. it is nice place to visit when you are in kathmandu. its shines nepal culture and harmony among people.

a must go place whilst in kathmandu. please explore the outside areas near the river where the dead are cremated. i walked the scene from dr.strange movie, made me feel like i was part of the movie. this was one of the film location. the temple compound is very clean and peaceful with excellent temple inside.
a sacred hindu temple dedicated to lord pashupati and currently largest hindu temple in the world, near bagmati river.this is build on 246hectares with main temple along with 518 mini temples

it was nice to walk around the exterior of this temple however after paying 2000 for two people i was not allowed in as white tourist are banned from entering, keep this in mind before buying the ticket.
pashupatinath is a must visit place for hindus. the spiritual energy and cultural significance is great. it will be a transformative and unforgettable experience.
hindu culture and uniqueness is very transparent from this place. recommend this place to visit without limits your time ! cheers

we were really disappointed by this temple. whilst it was strangely fascinating to see bodies being cremated it wasn’t worth the entry fee (rs 1000) which we thought we very expensive considering prices in nepal. it was a 30 minute taxi journey from thamel to the temple. as soon as we got out the taxi, we were hounded by men saying hello, trying to sell things and asking for photos which continued along the walk to the temple and in the walls itself. it was the only place in nepal we felt unsafe (collectively we have travelled to the majority of asian countries) due to being three female travellers in our 20s. the temple grounds were littered with rubbish and sadly had multiple people begging. there was also a constant hounding from workers offering a tour which we didn’t want. when ascending the steps there are multiple temples and shrines but felt unable to explore them due to the monkeys and men. perhaps without feeling unsafe it would have been more enjoyable but compared to other sites in kathmandu, it was disappointing with an expensive entry ticket.

death really comes close here. i am surprised how open they are and taking photos isnt strictly forbidden (as in varanasi) as long as you are respectful. i think its good therapy for us westeners to see this and remind ourself of death - something we try to hide from and forget of. anyhow a very good expericnce even though somewhat stomachwrenching.

the visit to this temple was both moving and thought provoking.a guide took me around and explained that the body after death was cremated on the same day according to the religious practice. the body is washed and wrapped in a white cloth and all the family are involved in the cremation ceremony which is not a sad event but a celebration of the life. when the body is prepared for the cremation a taper is placed into the mouth and a male member of the family light up the taper! an experience i shall never forget.

pashupatinath temple is located in kathmandu about 1 km north east of kathmandu airport and 4 km east of touristic city called thamel. which is dedicated to lord shiva. this is only 5 faces( east,west,north,south and zenith) lord shiva's temple in all over the world. it is situated on the bank of holy river bagmati. this temple was registered in world heritage site in 1979. it is the hindus's greatest sacred temple where only hindus are allowed but all the other religious people also visit outside of it .as i have visited several times ,i would like to visit again and again. its found not only sacred place but also attractive art and architectural pagoda style temples, natural river, forest. love to visit again and again. i recommend every visitor may visit to get its, experience.

it is one of the best place for hindu people and impressive architecture structure of the temple on pagoda style. it is also a place which make you more emotional while saw cremation on the bank of bagmati river.

visited in december 2022. we went to this place as part of a full day sightseeing tour. we went around 5 pm and the air was too cold. since it is a hindu temple, we need to remove our footwear and the cold flooring didn't help our cause. moreover the officials were cleaning the temple with cold water. we had a good darshan (view) of the deity. we stood in line multiple times for darshan as the lines were small. we were told there will a premium line if there is crowd. as expected, the premium line has a fee. the view of deity is much better after the lights are switched on. like in any hindu temple, the main deity is surrounded by multiple other deities that you can walk around and pray. like swayambunath temple, there are a lot of monkeys around. as long as we don't open food packets or water bottles in front of them, they will not disturb us.

very holy place .it is a place to see the trust in mahdve-god.to know the history.millioners devotees come there to fullfill their wishes.one must see its a rare opportunity to see this beautiful temple.one can make shopping of moti and rudraksh from goverment approved stores.and to move here and there nearby temple.
one of the famous temple in kathamandu. you should visit this place. to know more keep on eyes to our youtube channel "journey adviser".

interesting cultural experience. we saw 3 stages of cremation concurrently. 1 body was still being cremated and the 1st son of the deceased was getting his head shaved; the second cremation station was just done and the caretaker was cleaning up the site by sweeping the ashes into the river; the 3rd cremation station was just getting started. overall, an interesting temple to visit to learn about hinduism & nepal’s belief in after-death. be careful of the touts and “guides” who naturally tag along beside you and suddenly became your official guides. do stop them if you are not keen or negotiate a price before the “guides” start their grand tour. otherwise, it will be a usd50 affair for a short tour of 1hr or so. having said that, the “guide” was quite informative.

a wish that was long pending had a pandit come with us and offer darshan and puja he acted like a guide and said which one was important. immense energy was fely from the sanctum sanctorum. an experience to remember throughout my life
a large religious site with pashupatinath temple adjacent to the river. cremations take place here. lots of street peddlars and beggars. entry nrs 1000 pp.
you can see from the opposite sides, the process when the departed arrives since all the family & relatives give their last prayer before cremation.

this is an interesting place to visit while in nepal. i learned a lot about hindu culture and especially how they honor the last day of their beloved who passed away. they have to perform certain rituals before they’re cremated. loved ones are there and assisting to finalize the cremation. people can watch from the other side of the river. the temple is a beautiful site, i think it’s perfect for the ones who are gone. i say bring a mask if you can’t stand the scent of the smoke. prayers to all who passed.
best place nearby international airport hindu temple old temple and you can see cremation of hindu culture best for history culture and religion.

pashupatinath temple is among the 12 jyotirlinga. beautiful temple and must visit if you are in kathmandu. as the temple opens at around 4am best time is to visit at that time as it is completely empty. it closes at 7pm that is after the aarti. after 6am it starts getting crowded.

we went there twice on our trip. the first time due to a heavy rush on monday we could not have proper darshan of mahadev but compensated that with a beautiful darshan on friday morning. please be aware that there are huge crowds on monday and saturday so prepare accordingly. also the temple remains closed in the afternoon from 12-5 and is open in the evening only from 5-6.30. also be careful of the monkeys. but do visit the other small temples near the main shrine.

wonderful holy temple in kathmandu - just a mile away from the airport and gets crowded many times during peak morning and evening hours. have been visiting the temple year after year and the glory of the deity and the premises is intact. cleanliness needs some improvement.

sadly did not get to have a look. was dropped off by a lovely taxi, then proceeded to be followed by 4 men. as a foreign, solo 50yo female tourist i had to take evasive action to keep myself and my belongings safe. it is locally known of bag theft, pick pocketing (found out afterwards). lucky some hindu ladies seen how distressed i was and stayed with me until a taxi came. from discussions with locals it is common but goes unreported. in my experience i would not recommend females to visit here alone.

a truly interesting experience. live cremations happening right on the river and a huge complex of temples to see. we picked up a tour guide (somewhat unexpectedly - he just started talking to us!) but he was actually super informative and very interesting (ended up costing ~$30 which felt a bit steep though). definitely worth a visit!
must visit temple for hindus. lord siva with five mukhas. amazing temple which transcends time. felt lucky to be visiting.
it is one of the best spiritual place to visit in kathmandu. it is really different experience to be there and see all death ceremony of hindu people.
the best place to find inner peace for yourself and get some rest from the daily stressful life.and the best time to visit this place is between september to december.

one of the 12 jyotir lingas worshipped by devotees from all over the world. most sacred and spiritually elevating place of worship. many legends and miracles are associated with this temple. one has to to leave shoes outside and not to use mobile phone or camera inside the premises. pl avoid lots of touts roaming around and be careful with strangers. a must visit for all those who believe in divinity.
amazed by the stories told by mr. deepak from alphine club of himalaya, the full trip was so amazing and i decided to explore more about the hidden stories in each temples.
best experience with eternal peace, homage to lord pashupatinath temple. shandya aarati was great to see with lots of spiritual feelings.
very famous indu temple, where cremations are being done, lots of people coming here for indu religious holidays, many indians from india come to visit this holy place.

located on the way to boudhanath stupa, both of which can be seen on the same day. there are many smaller temples surrounding pashupatinath, which are also worth visiting. you will see bodies being cremated by the river, just like they do in varanasi, india. some areas can only be accessed by hindus and are off-limits to non-hindus.
very nice and great place in kathmandu.we have a great time with a family and great shiva puja .must visit once
this temple is a vast, sprawling area with multiple religious ceremonies conducted in the open all the time. we saw nonstop cremations, consultations, and blessings, all with wandering sacred cows
the temple cannot be entered by non-hindus, but there are many other aspects of this site worth seeing, especially the cremations. little pricy at r1,000 per person.
it was so peaceful to enter the shrine. so many devotees attending the temple on a daily basis. nice opportunity to see the main deity up so close.

the temple is located on the banks of the bagmati river on the eastern side of kathmandu. this location felt very sacred and crucial to the local people. do plan your visit to keep at least a couple of hours as the temple will usually have long que. you can't enter the actual temple if you're not hindu, however, it is still worth having a wander around the complex and you can see some of the beauty inside.

unfortunately due to my travel schedule i was not able to see it early or around the sunset, evening as it is recommend. the entrance fee is again steep, 1000 npr, at least for the high price you get a large ticket (a4 format). it is hard to sneak in without paying. heavily guarded by police. despite the high fee non hindu people can not entry the main temple. pashupatinath is an important hindu site and temple. all day through you can see the ceremony and cremation, mainly from the platform or terraces across the bagmati river to the east. towards to gorakhnath temple and lingam shrines you can come across monkeys inner temple courtyard: 4:00 am - 7:00 pm the site is close to kathmandu runway and ktm airport. you can take a local bus if you have enough time and know which one to take, taxi about 500-600 npr.from thamel i took motor bike taxi arrange by my hostel for 140 npr, to get back just look for motor bike drivers with a hold for the mobile, most likely this is the motorbike taxi.

i was not sure if i would enjoy visiting a site dedicated to cremations, but it turned out to be fascinating. this unesco sight on the banks of the bagmati river is a hindu temple dedicated to lord shiva. this is where people come to cremate their dead. funeral pyres for the line the banks, and it was really a cultural experience. you will even see people burning shrouds only, which means the person who passed away is not in kathmandu and they do not have the body there. be sure to climb higher and higher, where you can take in the entire complex from above. it is truly visual overload--you won't know where to look first. because the property is so large, it will not feel crowded. a must-see while you are in kathmandu.
the energy of the lingam is amazing . you can feel the energy once you are inside the temple the power of the lingam draws you . 🙏
contrary to information, it was very clean. darshan mesmerizes one. easily accessible. rudraksha shop nearby

this was an interesting place to visit. the funeral ghats were very interesting, but it felt a little wierd watching someones funeral from the other side of the river. as a westerner i was not allowed into the temple as only hindu's are allowed inside. this was a bit disappointing given it costs usd$10 to enter the complex

as a devote of lord shiva, i always go there on monday alone or with my family. such a nice temple which symbolises hinduism. it gives a different vibes about religions . i feel blessed and happy to have such a beautiful place in nepal.
it is a big place to visit. it takes 2/ hours to visit the whole place. if you want to know more about the hindu religion you can take some knowledge from here. this place includes a soshan ghat .

one on the most holy of hindu sites in nepal! from across the banks of the river i had the honour of watching as the bodies of those recently passed were prepared for cremation, placed on the pyre and set ablaze. there are about 8-10 pyres you you can see various stages of the process. once the body is completely reduced to ash it is placed in the river which flows into the most holy ganges in india. this was an experience i won't soon forget.

pashupatinath temple is one of the most significant hindu temples in nepal and is considered to be among one of the holiest hindu shrines outside india. the temple complex contains many other temples dedicated to other deities like shiva, vishnu, shakti and ganesha. it is one of the oldest temples in kathmandu and was built during the licchavi period. it is dedicated to pashupatinath, an aspect of shiva. must visit place in kathmandu.

as i am a hindu, it was such an amazing place to visit pashupatinath. everyone should worry about the monkeys in this place. one suggestion is, do not take photos inside the temple as it is prohibited. it is a great religious place to visit.
i got experience guide with worm hospitality and unforgettable memories from pariwar holidays team. so i rejoined with this company.
one of the unique way to look over the hinduism. looking at the crimination and life. believe we all human are so close to the nature. very interesting representation of culture.
wonderful visit of our kathmandu stay. the biggest hindu temple belong to lord shiva. we stay 4 hours with my friend. in there. highly recommend for all .
quite the experience. meeting that person waving tree branch was cool. also really enjoyed the walk up the hill. good cardio and the view from up there is nice too.

pashupati is regarded as the biggest hindu temple in nepal. and the temple has so much to offer. the massive area of the temple is famous for sightseeing and for puja. it is also known as the temple of shiva, and shiva is the most worshipped and respected god in hindu culture. pashupati is also the destination for rites and cremation. i have had the privilege to visit the temple during aarati time. aarati usually occurs between 5-8 pm and around 5 to 10 brahma performs a ritual, which includes, singing songs (bhajan), dancing, and calling for shiva. i literally got goosebumps while hearing the bhajan. it was a good experience, but please note that during this time the area is usually crowded as shown in the picture.
a great shiva temple, probably, the most popular one in nepal. a lots of yogis, hermits and priests can be seen around the huge compound. a stunning sights to experience

a must visit place for hindus. please note that non-hindus are not allowed in the temple and photography inside the temple is also prohibited. also, beware of monkeys on the site. all in all, this place is quaint and clean.

this is a beautiful temple and we are blessed to be able to witness and so freely stroll it. recommend that people do not take advantage of this privilege, however. it's rare to have such a place of worship open to tourists. please read about the appropriate behaviour before you go in. dress respectfully and be sure to remove your shoes when you step on the scared ground.
a peaceful place to hang out. a must-visit once in a lifetime! very famous place among the locals and devotees all around the world.
it's an amazing place and must see place when you are in kathmandu. the cremation of hindu people is unusual for me. i enjoyed visiting this place.

a really amazing place if you want to get in touch with the hindu culture. we went there on a saturday and the whole szenario there was a little bit unrealistic for us. on the one hand people come here to burn the bodies on the other hand people make tiktok videos. a great atmosphere which is really not typical for us europeans!
the pashupatinath temple of lord shiva has the feeling like in a different world of god's own place.
everybody needs to visit this place once in a while to experience what truly life is. whenever i’m here, i get to talk to myself a lot and a new me is always born.

a complex of temples dedicated mostly to indian gods such as shiva, there are also several sadus who try to have their photo taken with tourists to collect some money, in the complex there is also a burning ghat, where the indians cremate the bodies with a ritual on the banks of the river
a great religious place to visit in kathmandu. it's the hindu temple of lord mahadev. must visit place in the town. peaceful
i felt inner shakti and peace when i was there. the temple and surrouning are lively and you may easily stay there for while.

even as we travelled a lot, we had the so called book experience for giving money for entering the inner part. only after long talks and friendliness of a good english speaking jong man, we were lured into giving money to the babas, who are more dressed as and alternative kind of begging suits, than actually people looking for enlightenment. of course we could not enter with the so called special list guests we would be on. never the less we had a beautiful view on the site and the cremations and find a spot we could take pictures and meditate at the same time, just in peace.
aarya ghat is the destination of people! you feel your own here. pashupati nath is the place to call lord shiva.
story begins and end but here i can see the life ends of life such a sorrowful life but its true it goes and goes. very holy place for hindu in nepal. very nice to know about the history of it.
visited this hindu temple, a unesco world heritage site in june 2019. the architecture was quite interesting.

thank god that the nepal government has opened the temple from 6am to 1 pm. this temple makes nepal as a tourist attraction for all the tourists worldwide. great and magnificent temple. you feel blessed and blissful once you reach the idol of lord shiva and huge statue of nandi. very well maintained. excellent. a must visit place for every hindu.
wow ! it is world heritage temple in nepal. we have visite and spent few hours. we have seen many tourist came here for speritul purpose.

one of the most mesmerizing rituals feelings while visiting pashupatinath temple . we saw the priests perform the aarati by taking oil lamps and moving them in circular motion and dedicating their act to the divine.

the powerful feeling of the temple is outstanding. it is a huge temple. p sometimes very crowded with hindus on pilgrimage. hindus only have entry into the main shiva temple. across the river are the cremation ghats. very moving to see the preparations for the cremations, lots of photo opportunities. so much to see here
om nama shivayya shiva temple is one of the oldest temple i went many times, very lucky to have darshan
pashupatinath, greatest shiva temple must visit during evenig retual. truly heaven for all hindu.

the pashupatinath temple is considered to be the most sacred hindu temple in nepal. the temple is located on the banks of the bagmati river on the eastern side of kathmandu. this is where many of the deceased followers of hinduism are cremated, but not in a closed furnace of a crematorium. here in nepal cremations are done open air, and at this temple the bodies are covered in cloth and placed on a stone slab right next to the river bank. pieces of wood and straw are placed around the body and then it is lit on fire. after about 3 to 4 hours, the cremation is complete and then the ashes and remains are swept into the river. this process is part of the hindu religion that they will be reincarnated.
a lovely serene temple at kathmandu. an absolute must visit when you are in the region. a quick 1 hour

this temple dedicated to lord shiva is perhaps the biggest hindu temple in nepal. within the city limits of the city of kathmandu, the temple is said to attract devotees in large numbers throughout the year from all parts of the world, particularly from india. some say the this temple dates back to 400 bc. it is unesco approved heritage place. situated on the banks of the bagamathi river, it was built in the 5th century . the main deity is to be seen through four entrances , narrow . lord shiva's face is seen on four sides of the lingam besides on the top. it is only one of its kind. the temple could be still better kept. car parking is vast but dirty. there is no fee for entrance to the temple.

we visited this hindu temple one morning around 9am. the complex itself is quite different than the buddhist temples we visited in kathmandu. it was obvious from the moment the taxi dropped us off that this would be a unique experience. you will need to work your way past all of the street vendors selling both souvenirs and other items for offerings. follow the path (and hoards of people) until you reach the ticket booth. as western tourists, we were definitely in the minority here but we were there to learn about the temple, not to worship or mourn a loved one. someone will stop you and have you purchase an admission ticket. the cost was 1,000 rupees, which we felt was worth the experience. if you do not want a tour guide, you will need to stop the "friendly" local who begins talking to you and showing you the way to follow the path. if you choose to utilize the services of the guide, establish the price up front. this was pretty much how it seems to work at all of the major tourist sites in kathmandu. i will say that we found the guide services to be worth the rupees spent. it was a bit expensive compared to some of the other site guides, but still a decent price. the guide we had was very thorough and explained quite a bit about the hindu religion, the funeral process and the temple itself. if you decide to go on your own, you will probably wander around without truly understanding what you seeing. we did have the opportunity to witness a funeral. the guide explained the process to us and we found it fascinating as it is completely different from what we are used to. there are also sadhus at the complex. of course, in true nepalese fashion, you can take pictures as long as you give them a donation. this temple is definitely worth a visit, but make sure you go earlier in the day. they close from noon-5pm each day. if you want to see the funeral process, make sure to be there by 10am.
another wonderful temple simply loved its architecture and woodwork very nice it’s a surely must visit place

this location felt very sacred and crucial to the local people. it is not to be visited without careful consideration, as it is the location at which many people cremate their loved ones. it was a very moving experience for me to witness families saying goodbye, leaving tributes and placing their family member on an old style timber pyre, then cremating them before my very eyes. this experience is not for everyone. but for me, it was very worthwhile.

the many reviews cover this attraction well. i have been here four times. the largest hindu temple complex in the world. worth seeing, but know what you are in for as a westerner. be aware and expect the following as part of a unique cultural experience: 1. filthy and smelly. litter everywhere. 2. open cremations. real corpses being burned in open view. 3. beggars and solicitors will target you relentlessly. 4. you will walk through monkey and cow manure. 5. watch for monkeys and other pickpockets! 6. the "holy men" are really beggars dressed to earn rupees from tourists. 7. open sewage in the river, sometimes with children swimming in it! 8. sensory overload, and i mean all five! 9. the hawkers will try to guess your religion and sell you an appropriate trinket. 10. you are viewed as a profit opportunity. you will be charged more for everything. do not be offended. the $11 admission is well worth it. why would i recommend going? because you will not see another hindu temple complex like this one anywhere else in the world. the contrast of filth and beauty is stunning and typifies our human experience.

said by our guide to be the biggest hindu temple complex in the world it is a collection of quite impressive structures strung along the sides of the bagmati river. you cannot actually enter the main actual temple unless you are a hindu but the most dramatic sight is the cremation ghats along the river where the hindu cremation rituals are practised day and night. although normal for hindus the sight of the burning structures smoking along the banks of the river, indeed many people seemed to be treating the site as an afternoon outing, is sobering and thought provoking. not to forget the gaudily coloured holy men who may relieve you of money to take pictures.

pashupatinath is one of the four most important hindu religious sites in asia for devotees of lord shiva. this is considered a very holy place. i walked in to watch 4 different spots along the river engulfed in fire and releasing smoke. and also that familiar smell of me accidently burning my hair with the hair dryer when i was younger. then i was told that it's cremation going on. i was told that our body is just a vessel and needs to return to the elements of the universe; earth, fire, water, wind and sky. and that was a spot all of it came together. the nepalese and indians believe if one is cremated here, they will not have to suffer with rebirth. i haven't seen this many dead in one place let alone watch them burn. but there was some kind of peace about this place. this place is definitely not for the faint-hearted.

the temple is one of the best temples that i have seen in my life. the biggest nandi ( lord shiva's ride - the bull ) stands at an awe inspiring 30ft by 12 ft ( approx ) . i stood there and observed nandi for a long long time. the shiva linga has 5 faces.4 on each direction and one on top ( the nirankari roop) . look out for monkeys and don't leave any personal belongings unattended otherwise it will disappear within a fraction of seconds. photography is strictly strictly prohibited inside the temple. don't even try it. better to go either during the morning aarti or evening aarti time . do observe the silver work done on the walls of the main temple, the bahirav figures and the paanch pandav on the upper side .all gaurding the temple . there is a bit of pushing and hand holding done by the nepal police but take that in good spirit and avoid any conflicts in such a peaceful place.

pashupatinath is my favourite place in kathmandu. it's a sacred cremation grounds where hindus bring the deceased to be bathed in the river and put on the funerary pyre. i can respect that some people may feel shocked since they believe funerals should be done in private, but i think it's also poignant that you can see families with their children and babies sitting on the river banks the whole day, watching mourners bring corpses to publicly and ritualistically burn them. when death is made public, the taboo surrounding it is reduced, and it becomes an easier more natural conversation to talk about it as an important part of life. be prepared for local guides to come up to you and start telling you about the place and the rites, and then ask for money. you may politely decline and just say you're here to watch respectfully. you may also see cows walking up the stairs to the toilet area to have a drink of water, as well as dogs and monkeys. i would not recommend using the public toilet here.

pashupatinath temple is one of the most famous places in nepal. it is a must visit. the temple complex is very large and a lot of people visit everyday. the deity of the place is lord pashupatinath. it is also one of the oldest temples in the world.

the pashupati nath temple is a sacred hindu temple complex at kathmandu which is inscribed on the unesco world heritage sights. this hindu temple precinct is a sprawling collection of temples, images, etc only hindus are permitted to enter inside the main complex others can have a view from outside only. inside the complex the richly ornamented pagoda houses the sacred linga (holy symbol of lord shiva) are there. the temple is open since morning 4 am but closes just after the sunset.

rather a ramshackled group of temples, but most interesting and a unique experience for foreigners like us. we witnessed the actual cremations of deceased hindus, which was a rather peculiar experience, certainly for us.
nothing like this in the western world. as a tourist it is great to visit a place like this, however very strange to be a part of such intimate life-events of the local people.

we had hired a cab and pashupati nath was one of the attractions for the day. we visited just about it was closing in the afternoon and hence a piece of advice: the temple is closed between 2-5 p.m. we did make it in nick of time. the main temple offers views from all four sides and we managed most. photography is strictly prohibited. nearby are 522 "shiv lings" and you criss-cross them a riveting experience. architecture is splendid and you sense a history around this huge complex. wish bagmati river, which flows alongside was cleaner and not clogged. it's also a place for cremation for it's believed you wash off all your sins if your body is put on pyre here.

interested in seeing this ancient ramshackle hindu temple, i took a taxi here from the domestic airport (for rs400) and paid another rs1000 to enter. i declined a guide. it was at the time of a traditional cremation ceremony, so it was quite crowded by the bagmati river bank. the river is very polluted. there were bonfires burning as part of the ceremony. i didn’t have a map or guide book so i just wandered around. it was pretty hot so i only stayed an hour and a half, i didn’t see everything. also only hindus can enter the temples. as a westerner and tourist, a few people wanted my photo, which was ironic, since i was there to witness them. the locals seemed lovely! one girl even gave me directions to boudha stupa. there are some brightly yellowed dressed shadu (holy men) who will pose for money. the row of temples had a lot of shiva phallic idols and animal statues. it would be interesting to read and learn more about the symbolism. my advice, read up before you visit.

incredible temple! i was travelling alone, arrived at 2pm and comfortabley spent 4 hours inside wondering around, taking in the atmosphere, visitings the smaller temples within the complex. i attended the main aarti and the aarti at some of other temples within and time flew by. i believe the aarti time inside the complex happens before the aarti across the river so you can visit both. note at the back of the temple you have an ariel view of the rituals on the river if thats your thing (personally i avoided that). however please be aware of the closing time, not of the temple but the shoe racks. after an afternoon of prayer like most i sat outside watching the children play and monkeys run havoc. i returned to the shoe rack at 6:40pm to find that it had closed and my hiking boots were locked inside. to cut a long story short, despite asking guards and pleeding with the security office no one was willing to open the shoe rack and return my shoes. and guess what, i had a flight the next morning. despite asking people the opening time i received 3 different answers of 5:30am, 6am and 6:30am which didnt fill me ith confidence. the next morning i turned up at 5:45am, the shoe racks were open and i managed to get back my hiking boots. note the temple is virtually empty at 6am and you can pray in piece rather than the scrum in the evenings. all in all this should be top any agenda.

respectful on how to end one life in a open and holy place/ river. bachhareshwari temple: do check it out too, as lot of traditional wood craving with eroticism, based on hindu "bible". walk up to the hill and check out couple of hindu temples too. be aware of all the monkeys in group as they are aggressive. enjoy enjoy
really one of the holiest place in nepal,which was highly satisfying and made me emotionally charged. inspite of presence of large number of people, had a clear darshan of lord shiva.jai bholenath.

must see place in katmandu. an amazing place. a lifetime experience. i know i'll never forget pasupatinath and always remember especially when i think about the life. cremation is a different experience you'll never forget.
the place to visit for something new you can see over there about the hindu religion and in the evening time the praying to the god with music and big crowd was wow .

quite a well organized hindu temple than most i have been to in india. it's the only jyotirlingha outside of india. foreigners and non hindus (only buddhists mad sikhs are allowed) cannot enter the temple to offer prayers. it is said if you take a round of this temple it's almost equivalent to visiting char dham as per hindu mythology. must visit

this is mystic. amazing place. not as much crowded as other hindu places of worship in india. the architecture left me spellbound. little temple domes next to the main dome adds to the charm. the temple is closed from noon to 1700 hrs. exact timing can be checked.
this is a must for anybody who visits kathmandu. the people here are honouring the life of the recently departed, observe from a distance and be respectful, please!

visited at 10.00am and we were dropped off at the car park. chilled walk up the street in between market stalls. 1000 entrance fee easily paid, which we thought was worth every penny. tour guide offered his services, but a simple ‘no thank you’ and off we went. we watched in amazement and everybody was so friendly. in total we spent two and a half hours, which we will not forget. easy to get a taxi for the return journey, just head back to the car park. it was a privilege to watch the ceremony so closely and to learn so much about their faith. definitely worth a visit, my only criticism would be the ignorant tourists with big cameras intruding. people are saying goodbye to loved ones- yes we are interested and want to share our story, but a respectful distance should go without saying!
this is must see temple in kathmandu. government still didn’t finish to rebuild after earthquake many parts of temple.

well if you go to kathmandu, you really need to go to this temple. because is one of the most importants. very nice. and makes you think about life. really you can not go out of kathmandu without seeing this.

oh, it's a very energetic place. so interesting, and so impressive and tragical for me, european man. there are many people chanting mantras, making rituals in the smoke of cremation fires, monkeys looking for something tasty, flowers and leaves, old and new buildings. but the most impressive thing is a house for old and poor people. if you are brave to see all of this, it may change your life.
paahupatinath temple is must important place to visit for all human being, where you can enjoy with many hindu cultural activities !
due to earthquake damage in 2016 a lot of restoration was being undertaken. we have been twice before and would encourage seasoned travellers to visit.
it is one of the jyotirlinga and on the must-visit list of every hindu. it is 900 years old temple very well maintained and clean. the evening aarti is quite nice.

not sure i'd put this down as a tourist site, seems very disrespectful and i found it intrusive to be watching as people cremated their loved ones - albeit from the other side of the river. fascinating to know about it but the guide could have explained from afar without us having to be there. oh and it has more monkeys running around that the so called monkey temple so beware!

this was my second visit to the place. the mandir is divine and is beautifully maintained by the government. it was a beautiful experience of puja. the best thing was animals were also allowed in the temple premises and were given respect.
not a lot to see, but what there is, is well worth it. you get a calming effect just walking around it.
5 faces of shiva and they are open at different times, we went in around 2pm and was able to see all the entrances of shivas face. it is a must visit and had an amazing experience

the complex and the crematorium is a very central attraction and restrictions are in place not to use mobiles inside as they are strict. plenty of shiv lings inside and you may be able to see a funeral at the same time on the banks of bagmati river
it's a place where the nepalese cremate their departed. i saw a body being prepared but did not take a photo. i feel like it was a very intimate rite for families only. this is a must visit place.

pashupati nath is a hindu temple. the architecture and history is interesting and i would recommend a visit. please be aware that it is a site actively used for preparing and cremating those recently deceased and honoring the dead. while tourist are only allowed across the river, this might be upsetting for some.

first, the template and surrounding area requires a bit of a walk - perhaps 1/4 mile. once back to the area, watching the hindu traditional cremations along the river is riveting. you don't need to stay too long to see one start or end. get a guide for this site so you can really get a feel for the process including those that come to die touching the river.

i didn't realize this was the site of the local outdoor crematory. i didn't stay long and had mixed feelings about even being there. two bodies were prepared and cremated along the river.it was difficult to adjust to the smoke and smell. tha temple and site aren’t conducive to tourists, especially when only hindus were allowed into the main temple. it’s a natural; cycle of life so it may not bother others who go.
if you want to experience what life is like at the end of life then this is a very special place. go in and leave your western thoughts outside the gate and experience life as lived by others.
life is embraced on one side of the river, death on the other. bring your sense of wonder and leave a piece of your heart.

pasupathinath temple is one of the holiest place for hindus and lord shiva is main deity. it is situated near bagmati river in the heart of kathmandu city. temple is opening early in the morning and devotees can view from all four sides only during particular timings. bairava and other deities are also there in the complex. we had nice dharsan on a full moon day.

in contrast to my expectations, the temple is small but has 4 gates and if you want to pray god shankar from all 4 gates, you need to go between 8 am and 11 30am. rest of the time gates open for few minutes. only one gate remains open for the darshan time. 6 am to 12 noon and 4 pm to 8 pm. lots of small temples around. river bagmati (in a pretty bad dirty shape) passing behind. however temple is clean and not very crowded. queue of 50persons when we visited but may be due to hindu new year day. there is a place with multiple shivlings which requires parikramna. shoes counter is outside temple premises and is free so do not leave your shoes with any shopkeeper who may attract you with such offers. payment rs 100 for saarc countries more for others. photography not allowed

could spend the day watching cremations, activity, sadhu’s, monkeys, etc. fascinating and atmospheric. didn’t bother with guide, just took my time to explore. interesting walking there and back from thamel area. highly recommended.
different culture, could not be the best to see but feel its culture what we taught and give . life ending place to explore different ways of celebration
different culture, could not be the best to see but feel its culture what we taught and give . life ending place to explore different ways of celebration

this temple holds open cremation ceremonies for the hindus. it is a compelling sight. a must see. we did have our guide, rabina, with us to add insight and context during our visit to this world heritage site.
it seems strange to be able to see people grieving as their loved ones are cremated, but the celebration of life makes this a must see place. the cremations are carried out with care and love.

after an exhausting and " adventurous " bus trip from gyriong to kathmandu and only a few hours of sleep, we were lucky to be able to do a lot of sightseeing under the wonderful guidance of gyan bahadur muhkiya ( we simply called him gyan). gyan was hard working, prompt, personable and very efficient. he not only guided us to visit all important places of interest, he helped us with other errands like exchanging money, selecting a good restaurant, shopping for some particular souvenirs, items of clothing, he would gladly helped. some times i think he got overwhelmed as he tried to accommodate all 16 of us. he has good knowledge about history, religion and the kathmandu area. with such a tight schedule, he managed to take us to many wonderful " not to miss" places of interest. yes, we visited swayambunath, pashupatinath temple. they are such beautiful temples with interesting religious and culture differences. gyan explained to us the funeral tradition in the river, the cremation and we watched the sacred ceremony with flowers and tears ….and were simply amazed. of course, we visited kathmandu city, pagan among other places and the ladies got their shopping done too. given how crowded those places were, such a large group we were and the limited amount of time we had, i think gyan did a wonderful job as a nice, patient and caring tour guide. i certainly recommend gyan for those of you who want to travel to nepal. in addition to english, gyan speaks japanese and french .

i sat with many others on the far bank of the holy river, mesmerised watching the rituals as the hindus laid there loved ones to rest. as the bodies are washed, the funeral pyre is prepared. the temple and grounds cover a large area. there are souvenir stalls and plenty of car parking.

a must if you're visiting nepal. the hindu funeral traditions here are important to observe (from a respectful distance) for the western traveler. it differs so much from most western cultural practices. a great way to get a look into this wonderful, beautiful land.

we visited pashupatinath temple in kathmandu. such a beautiful temple .we worshiped god shiva with milk and rudrabhishekam. nice pooja. we feel for just a minute i tie with shivji ji . feeling better and feel that i was fly in the sky. beautiful moment of my life. we got prasadam and we see all the shivlings . we drop coin in the backside river . we also see one new thing infront me a dead body was funeral. it was vertical horirble for us .then outside the temple we bought pearls strings for relatives and come back at the hotel
excellent temple in khatmandu, it is a must visit place , generally there are long queues to offer puja.

we were not allowed inside the confines of the temple as it is restricted to hindus only, which, given its religious significance is reasonable enough our guide then took us to the river where corpses are washed prior to being processed to the funeral pyre where the body is placed and cremated in full public view. close family members also have heads shaved. this is a fascinating insight into their culture but we did feel somewhat intrusive at a time of grief and mourning for the families, but our guide assured us that it is seen as respect not intrusion once the body is burned, which takes around 3 - 4 hours the stone beneath the pyre is unceremoniously scrubbed down and the remaining bones washed into the river before a new pyre is set for the next corpse a lesson in life and death
i loved our visit; i should have gone ahead and hired a guide. the complex is beautiful and we were a little hesitant where we could and couldn’t go and if photos were ok.

we went to one of the most important pilgrimage site for hindus, pashupatinath. the temple complex is huge and resides on the bank of holy bagmati river. the main temple complex is dedicated to lord shiva which only a hindu can visit. nevertheless, we were allowed to soak in the spiritual vibe from the other bank of the river. also, we got the opportunity to witness the open cremation which is itched in my memory. indeed a beautiful experience.
a guide joined us for over an hour tour of explantions of hindu and the ceremonies. we saw 2 dead cremation ceremonies and with a guide explaination it can make sense.

i dont know never seen that before but it was quite interesting in the nepalese hindu culture they burn the people after death. it was very hard to watch people crying during these season cuz they loose the love onces overall the area was very huge not that much clean but the inside the temple you can find out so many god and goddess statue and all must visit once
must visit hindu temple in kathmandu. u can see burial & prayers and it has hindu religious significance attached to it.
a fascinating insight into the religion and burial service our guide gave us an extremely good explanation as to the rituals.

travelling here as a group, and you're quite protected from being hassled by hawkers. once you understand and respect the religious importance of this place and the process, it's 'not quite' as confronting. we were amazed how many locals just came here to sit and watch the cremations, i think this also helps accept what you witness. even though i went here, i wouldn't have thought i'd missed out if i hadn't seen it.

at this place people are cremated. entrance fee for foreigners rs 1000. here you can watch how loved ones cremate there family members. this all happens on the other side of the small river. still having a good view. lots of people trying to be your guide or sell stuff. 50 people are cremated here every day. interesting to see.

you need time to understand the complex religious significance of this place. get a good guide in your own language who can give an unbiased explanation of the hindu and buddhist aspects in play here! don't forget to watch the locals and domestic tourists in this place.
amazing place but be careful as it’s very dirty, guides are persistent and the sadhus are ruthless. go at night time as i wish i did.
since i visited this temple in bhaktapur i didnt go to the temple in kathmandu its a replica here and it is under repair.
there is a reason as to why pashupatinath temple is one of the most admired and visited places in nepal. once you be there, it's not difficult to guess. though could have been managed bit better.

i am an geek for architectures. this one is unique as it’s a hindu temple with buddhist architecture. entrance is restricted for hindu only. the main temple might be closed in late afternoon. we waited till 5pm to have darshan. do roam around and check all surrounding smaller temples. also no photography inside the main complex.
one of my favorite places in kathmandu. the architecture is just amazing, and the history very interesting. there are some excellent restaurants in this area.

this opened my eyes to the one thing that we must all do, die! we saw a family lay a father to rest in the hindu way by burning him. the whole thing was very emotional to the family involved and also to me, now that i am a pensioner. we watched it all and it pulls you up with a start.

the hindu burial place, where the corpses are washed, blessed and burnt. although nothindus are not allowed to temple itself, it is a place one has to see and to be at remembering that all that was born has to die.

a must visit for first timer and repeat visitors. watching a cremation is a profound existential experience. the atmosphere in the temple grounds is a constant reminder that you are not in kansas anymore

i visited the area as part of a group tour with a nepali guide. we were advised to keep an eye on our belongings and stay with the group. being foreigners, we weren’t allowed to access the temple, but we walked along the river. there are ‘holy men’ dressed in orange who are keen to be photographed for a fee and a few market stalls. our hindu guide was excited to have us watch a hindu cremation. it was quite confronting to watch the deceased person’s body being washed by family members and moved to the cremation alter. it was at this point that my daughter and i walked away from the group as we didn’t wish to see the body alight. sitting a short distance from the group, we were approached by a man who offered to purchase my teenage daughter from me. thankfully our group arrived at this point and our guide shooed him away. quite confronting.

this is most important reason for many indians to visit nepal. this temple on the bank of river bagmati is world famous and is frequented by many hindus for religious purpose. it has four gates and the main gate of temple opens at 4am but all four gates of main temple open at 9 am only for few hours. apart from pashupati main deity there are many small statues of other hindu gods especially bhairav ji which is huge and unique in the world. people do religious ceremonies on the banks of river as cremations are on the premises of the temple.

this world heritage site is totally amazing. preserving the old temple and it's structures are really wonderful to see most especially in this modern age. if you are planning to visit nepal, don't miss out pashupatinath to visit.

pashupatinath is such an interesting place but also rather confronting with multiple cremation taking place all day on the bagmati river. the main temple is only accessible to hindus but you will be able to walk around the rest of the precinct. beware of the guides, they attach themselves to you and are of limited value but still cost 1000npr. great site and a definite must visit while in kathmandu.
we went to this place & found the most beautiful friendly people you could find just had the best time make sure you put this place on your to do list

we visited the day after the festival of deej when ladies in their finest costumes queue for hours to pray for spiritual enlightenment. this is a big date in their calendar and is a wonderful spectacle to see the ladies in their finery. the temple complex has some areas off limits to those not of the hindu/buddhist faith but the architecture is tremendous and there are a number of private gatherings having services provided which can be observed and enjoyed by visitors which gives a good insight into these religions. next to the temple complex is the river where cremations can be observed.
it’s a really good place to pray for hindu religious people can enter in temple, others are not allowed to enter from main gate. but other religious also visit around this area. highly recommended!

it is one of the most imprortant hindu site but unfortunately the main temple is open only local to hindus but there many other interesting building to see. there is also the place for the burial ceremoneys which is something unique and you get to known oother religions beleives with regards to death. but if it will be your first time it is kind of shock for some and thus you have to be strong and be prepared,
it is old shiva temple. the energy here was charged by yogi shiva. shiva is inventor of hatha and tantra yoga. i feel more relax to meditate around temple.

this temple sits beautifully beside a river. and the temple is a cremation site, ashes are thrown into the river. non hindus cannot enter the inside of the temples but can still go inside the temple compound. entrance fee is 1000 rupees.

great places to visit, according to my guide, one of the most important hindu temple in the world i.e. pashupatinath. lot of peoples and interesting thing is you aren't allowed main temple if you aren't hindu.

lots of tourists from india, this is a place where every hindu wants to come, barefoot and with painted forhead. if you are not hindu you can only see the ouside (1000) ruppies. walk to the side of the river where the dead people are burned. careful for the monkeys, they steel food.
it was the first time i visited pashupatinath temple and i want to visit the temple again and again, beautiful and huge complex, wonderful vibrations and divine blessings of lord shiva.

the hindu temple is highly patronized by most people who visit kathmandu. most areas of the premises except for the main shiva temple is open to visiters. only hindus are allowed in here. it is a worthy visit to make. the hindu cremetorium is in the premises by the side of the river. tourists must take precaution to avoid guides who will approach them and start describing the place as if the service is within the ticket money & then demand for a higher fee. this service is not worth more than rs. 250/=.it is best to ask them at the very inception & agree.
