one of the most important temples of bhagwan shiva situated at the banks of river bagmati in kathmandu.this temple is one of the seven unesco heritage sites in nepal. a must visit temple for all hindus. adjacent to the bhairav temple, unique 525 lingams are installed in a swastika shaped maze. for good health and longevity, one has to do parikrama (cumambulate) walk through a maze like path touching all the lingams and then exit from a different end.though the temple opens at 4 am and closes after evening aarti at 7.30 pm. during the day, temple is closed between 12 noon to 5 pm. depending on the time available at your disposal, best would be to be there well before 7 pm to join the arti and have darshan of other temples in the compound. om namah shivaya

my boyfriend and i visited pashupatinath temple with open and intrigued minds and we learnt so much in the process. we paid for a guide and are pleased we did so as we would have cluelessly walked into the funerals otherwise. be aware that the guides pounce on you and then charge 1000 rupees at the end - we were unaware of this cost and didn’t have enough to cover it! the area is impressive and very busy, with lots of temples to admire. the riverside is where the rituals take place and you can view from above. having the guide meant we learnt about the rituals and understood what was happening. very shocking for us westerners but an eye-opening experience.

we visited nepal primarily to take darshan of lord pashupatinath with our family. we could take very nice darshan from all sides of temple. you would find many shrines inside the temple but two or most important. one is of lord pashupatinath and another temple of lord nagraj. no kind of mobiles or cameras allowed inside shrine !
monday's are very crowded. if you are a first time visitor, please avoid. other days you can see the temple peacefully; spend time in each diety. worth a visit of 2 hrs.

a very popular spot similar to the ghats of vanarasi, but a smaller scale. i love this spot very much, sitting along the riverside, watching families saying goodbye to their beloved ones, a bit sad but very peaceful.

colorful and interesting glance into another culture. a fascinating place to wander a bit. complete with yogis, monkeys and vagrant dogs. having a guide made it much more intelligible to a benighted westerner.

visited the shrine in sept. 2019. it was sunday and so not crowded . went from thamel by taxi with fare rs. 600/- on reaching walked on foot to the sanctum sanctorum. only a handful persons were there in q. had darshan from the gate where the priest took flowers from my had and offered to lord. highly satisfying. then had parikrama and found other temples situated in the premises. there is a unmat bhairav temple in one side which one must visit after lord pashupatinath darshan. also saw rituals on the bank of river baghmati on which bank this famous temple is situated. namah shivay.

the temple complex is huge and the whole place is beautiful, the nearby shops selling religious items add to the beauty and sanctity of the temple. here rudraksh is famous and one can see almost everyone wearing a rudraksh mala. the temple is pristine in its glory, the main temple has 4 deities of lord shiva carved into one single stone so one can view the beauty of the lord from all 4 angles during the pradakshina. it is a known fact that the king of nepal himself used to do the pooja of the lord. a must see on every visitor's itinerary visiting nepal.

so, here is why you should visit the temple and what you could expect. 1. it is one of the four most important religious sites in asia for shiva devotees. 2. the largest temple complex in nepal. 3. this is unesco designated cultural heritage site. 4. the streets around the temple can show the street selling culture of the country. they sell mostly worship items and other items related to hindu traditions. 5. there are also enough places to eat for cheap if you feel hungry. 6. as a foreigner you can experience the hindu cremation culture. 7. monkeys are also present in large numbers and they are the kings there. 8. people swimming in the river for holy bathing as well as looking for valuable 9. you can also meet sadhus (sages) who have an amazing appearance. you can also meet fortune tellers. 10. at evening, if it does not rain, then you can also experience the “ aarati” which happens just beside the bagmati river and infront of the shiva temple. 11. the temple is very old so you can also experience the ancient art and architecture of the country.
energy of the place is amazing. arrangements for darshan was proper and cleanliness is maintained. evening arti was another attraction.

after reading reviews in ta we decided against entering the compound to avoid the 1000 rps fee which didn’t make sense, having just came from patan durbar square which also had 1000rps fee. instead we walked around outside the temple and observed the local hindus making their way in. the streets around the temple are a little charming, plenty character and selling worship items. we are obviously foreigners but never harassed and felt safe. there are signs everywhere that the zone outside the temple is a beggar free zone, in fact quite clean vs what we were expecting considering the reviews. we didn’t see any ceremony during the time we are there around midday.
nice temple! according to our guide it is one of the oldest hindu temple in nepal. it is crematory place too.

one of the power center of this earth. temple compound is well maintained and clean with all basic amenities. step garden in well maintained, with a specific place for pigeons to feed. we took a 1100.00nc receipt from nepalsbi within compound to worship shiva from inside.
in a small location you get a lot. the main temple - that non-hindus can't enter. the crematoria - where the dead are burned. many small temples. get a local guide - there's a lot to see.

very good temple.got darshan of evening arti.it was excellent.divine feeling in the middle of chanting prayers by a group of people.done pradakshin inside the sanctum 2 times during arti . very big compound.the cremation ghat near the temple is very dirty.no water in the river only a very little muddy water.

it is one of the beautiful temple i have visited, as soon as you enter the temple you see the huge nandi and the doorstep.the main shivalinga can be viewed from all the four sides, but once you are inside the sanctrum, you can see only 3 faces of shivlinga, and for the fourth one you need to enter from the front. main sanctrum has only natural lights which is very nice to preserve. around the temple, you will have small temple sof bhairavnath and about 501 small shivalingas. i found the temple interesting which had mix of hindu and chinese architecture (the adobe) avoid going on saturday as it is rush day, and there is no system of queue (though is queue but it is largely mismanaged) on others days you could have peaceful darshan.

for the pashupati and the crematorium we took a guide who was just at the entrance, where you pay the fee. absolutely to recommend is ananta! he told us a lot about the crematorium, the animal sacrifices and other hindu traditons. as european for me it was fascinating but a bit shocking to see how the bodies were burned in public. but it's interesting to see how other cultures handle with the death. the temple cannot enter as a non-hindu. so we could only see it from the outside.

as part of the kailash yatra it is imperative to seek blessings from the holy pashupatinath temple, so we did get the blessings by way of evening aarti at the temple. as is common with every place which is thronged with too many people cleanliness is something which one has to be prepared to bear with. monkeys are also present in large numbers and one has to be careful with the belongings and eatables like prasad etc. this temple has historical significance and among the most important jyotirling of hindus. situated on banks of bagmati river in kathmandu nepal this is unesco designated cultural heritage site.

thus temple complex is considered as the last one to be seen after the chardham darshan. with the huge crowd getting turned up here, it resembles a typical indian temple. the temple closes by noon ,hence plan the visit before 12. a huge nandi situated at entrance. the sanctum has four doors opening to four directions,revealing the four faces of the linga. these all four doors will be open during abhisheka time. in addition there temples of vasuki, lord krishna, ganapathi and bhairava no photography is allowed inside due to heavy rush, and poor queue arrangement , be alert on pick pocketers.

i visited this place and a guide explained me about the meaning of it (be careful though because some of them ask quite some money). for me the most impressive part was to see how hindu’s deal with death. it is very different than in western cultures because they burn the bodies near the river and throw the ash in it. it is a very normal thing here for the family to see the burning body. children are swimming in the river as well (or looking for valuables?). anyway: worth a visit and i would also recommend to ‘hire’ a guide to explain you about the place.
a lot things to explore here.best heritage place in the world having big area.temples,jungle,river,monkeys,many kind of birds is the beauty of this place.

i watched a funeral, from the preparation of the body and final adorations and mourning by relatives to it being transported to a woodpile and cremated, with a heavy heart, the aaarati ceremony afterwards lifted the spirits and washed away the sadness of the relatives and myself. you must go through this experience to understand the true meaning of shiva.

it is famous hindu temple of lord shiva. there is history behind this temple. the evening aarti is main attraction. it is also declared as world unesco site. it near the airport. there are entry across river for foreigners.

i visited this site as part of a city tour of kathmandu. whilst you pay to enter the site you can explore all of it unless you are a non hindu in which case you cannot enter the main temple. this is a very important temple of nepalese hindus. highlights include the religious cremations which take place regularly on the steps at the side of the river. a very interesting place certainly - recommended.
one of the most impressive temple i´ve visite before and it doesn´t matter if you are buddist or not you have to visite this temple
if you are interessted in dead prozesszions of hindu people and you would like to feel about the hinduism culture you have to go and visit this temple and get a feeling around the end of our life

went to this place with my indian family and was not allowed to enter because i was a foreigner. they said it was because i was not hindu, but how would they know? an american-indian could have easily gotten in ( and be a non-hindu) just for looking the part! and even if you were hindu, if you are foreigner, you are still not allowed in! and worst yet, foreigners have to pay 1000 just to see behind the temple! if you don’t want to be mistreated, dont go! it’s not worth it to make the start of your day bad! these photos will be the only view you get from behind bars. hope this helps you make a decision and if you still decide to go, good luck!
very large temple areal well worth visiting and observing a religious burial ceremony on the site of the river. unfortunately it is not allowed to enter any temple for any members of other religions.

we visited the temple on a friday afternoon and there was a ceremony ongoing. its good place to learn more about the hindu culture with a guide to explain what is happening however you might feel uncomfortable witnessing the whole process of the ceremony. as for us, we had left the area right before the cremation of the remains as its really awkward witnessing someone mourn. we were there for almost 2 hours but the ceremony was not yet finished so we decided to leave. bring a mask with you when visiting the place and a npr 1000/- entrance fee for non-saarc national.

the 1000 npr entrance fee is a bit insulting for westerners who can't even visit or see the temple they are paying to maintain. there isn't even a proper viewing platform to see the main temple instead you can kind of sort of peek a tiny portion of it from above. yes the ghats and minor temples are interesting but you will see better just wandering around old katmandu or patan.

everything was amazing at this temple except people inside who are not even staff would take ur cell phone if you take videos or photos and would delete everything before even you can check it, this was disgusting and not acceptable, another thing was that one old man was just showing us the place around and was forcing us to pay as he was the guide which we didn't liked, so stay away from such people and beaware of whats happening around you as this is a huge place whr you might lose ur valuables.

i had a guide and was grateful, without him i wouldn’t have gone in! we went on the last monday of the nepal month so it was packed with women ending fasting. so much color and beauty! it’s a real holy place. this is where end of life ceremonies and cremation take place along the river (so you may feel a bit uncomfortable) and people are there mourning. the main temple is only for practicing hindus and others are not let in. you will also be requested to remove shoes (unless it’s a madhouse like i saw!) there are also little shops on the perimeter. the “temple” is actually many buildings, temples, river, park and more.

hindu temple, one of the four most important religious sites in asia for shiva devotees. the largest temple complex in nepal, it stretches on both sides of bagmati river which is considered holy by hindus. the golden temple is closed when we went there. interestingly, fortunate enough to observed a cremation ongoing on the site. two bodies being cremated while one still waiting.

after hearing from most of my friends about the aarti at the pashupati nath, i finally visited there. i felt different inner peace within me. the flowing river, wind breeze and beauty of the movement of fire lamp by the pundits, there was different strong feeling. during the aarti, you could hear 50-100 people clapping and chanting the prayer. i would recomend to visit pashupati nath at evening aroung 6:00-7:00.
after returning from my ebc heli tour, i took a tour to pashupatinath, a unesco world heritage site in kathmandu. visiting the hindu temple was a new experience for me. i enjoyed my visit there.
when we went there there were 2 bodies being cremated and 3 waiting to be cremated. made for a very somber visit. all in all we are glad we went.
this is oldest shiva temple i visited. loved the art and sace, river and nice temple. being yoga student i felt good energy.

this ancient shiva temple is well known as an important pilgrimage centre for hindus. it is even mentioned in the shiva purana. one is swept away in waves of spiritual bliss as soon as you enter the temple precincts. it is a massive complex with the main shiva temple and other smaller temples being housed in the inner courtyard. photography is not allowed inside the inner courtyard. non-hindus are also not allowed inside the inner courtyard. but there is a place from where the temple can be viewed.
world heritage site. very spiritual inside the temple with many shiva lingams, no photography allowed inside. very historical and old
it was a nice visit with friends.a lot things to do here.even u can go for small hike here.historical area.a lot things to learn here.overall it was beneficial visit.

tha pashipati is one of nepal's largest religions sites and its is also very familiar on the world this temple is very old structure there are many sculptures worth seeing here i think those who will be visit here you will be fascinated by this idols so you must visite this and one thing in nepal that's reason i recommend that you get a lot to see here theology of nepal's

this temple is filthy, dirty and smells of urine. let's not forget these things. however, this is the first time i've seen open hindu cremation, the full ritual, from washing the corpse to setting the pyre alight is in full view. it feels awkward and shocking to watch a stiff dead body being washed and then literally breathe in the smoke from the flames consuming it - but a unique insight into death within another culture. definitely for these reasons, this temple is not suitable for young children.

lots of noise and people. beautiful temple. there are funeral pyre at the back of the temple. its really humbling as there are people of all ages being cremated. make one think that our time in earth is very limited so its so important to be happy and do what you want to do much as you can. good place to just watch and relfect. i always go here every time i go to nepal. its one of the holiest temple for hindu but it does not matter what religion you are you can still see the temple from the back. there are also holy men. however the proper sadhu are away from the temple in the woods within pasupatinath.

pashupati nath is one of the major attractions in kathmandu. had amazing architecture and you should atleast spare around an hour to visit it. there is a ghaat just besides the temple where the nepali cremation happens. photography inside the temple is prohibited
it is a very interesting cultural area. we took a guided tour and learned a lot of information. i recommend going with a guide.

very impressive temple to visit. i recommend to book a guide there, since they can share a lot of information about everything there is to see. highlights for me where meeting the sadhoes, learn about hindu funerals (you see them burning their deaths, very impressive and beautiful ritual), and see a tantra temple. also possible to visit the elderly home beside the temple.
visited this place today and really liked. i never had chance to paryicipate in this aarti puja at pashupatinath and glad i did it today. don’t miss out this while you are in kathmandu.

we took a guide at place so we could understand more of what's going on, maybe not really place for children if you don't want to introduce them to death in early age,for us it was fine. our toddler daughter was a dream to see "colorful yogis" that's the reason we went there, but they are "money,money,money" all the time was a really big disappointment, we would give money anyway which we did but went u approach them and wanna take a picture they covering themselves with umbrella or have special guy who doing it until u give them money so then they start to posing for you. if u taking too many pictures ( i took 5) they guys covering camera. and then they end up asking for more money,we paid to all this "holy man's" much more then we paid for entrance,as we didn't want to disappoint our kiddo. too expensive to be "blessed". palm readers on the other side was nice men's,there only one guy who speaks english but we choose who spoke only nepali as he looked more wise and calm,our guide kindly translate for us. we witness one funeral and burning bodies,better take a mask for cover nose and mouth if u not a fan of burn bodies smell, especially for kids recommended.

a holy place for hindus. beautiful temples and architecture. the ritual cremation at the river makes the visit very impressive as it is almost a violation of the privacy of the family of the deceased, but it gives also a view in the culture of hinduism. it was a precious experience.

it was my super memorable trip visiting pashupati temple in kathmandu on the monday morning with so many devotees and worshiper visiting early morning with flower garlands and with purified mind and heart worshiping the lord shiva , pashupatinath temple. i will visit soon again. it is worthy to visit.
it is said that if a hindu has the resources then one must visit shri pashupatinath temple at least once in lifetime.very thought provoking.
if there is one reason that i need to visit kathmandu then it has to be this highly revered temple.just avoid mondays and special days when the crowd can be overwhelming.
one of the most known temples in south asia is the pashupatinath temple. people throng throughput the year to get darshan of lord shiva
what a fascinating experience! a bit gruesome but worth spending even longer then what your guide would normally give you
must visit if you are in kathmandu temple closes by 12 noon to 4 pm, no entry ticket can reach there by taxi and you need to walk for 5-6 minutes and you will be temple.

had visited this hindu temple in kathmandu a long back ago. this temple is located on the banks of the bagmati river and is a popular tourist attraction. this hindu temple is dedicated to lord shiva and is the oldest temple complex in nepal. it is a unesco world heritage site and is a hindu holiest shrine. it is visited by pilgrims from nepal & india as well as foreign tourists. this temple complex has many smaller shrines, an ashram, small lingas, a cremation site near the river etc. there are some sages living in this temple. mahashivratri is celebrated in this temple with great pomp & enthusiasm. before entering the temple, footwear should be removed. photography is allowed. outside the temple, there are shops selling rudraksha malas or necklaces. there is no entry fee for visiting the temple. the road leading to the temple is crowded, filthy and dirty. this hindu temple is an ideal place of worship for local devotees as well as people from other faith. it is a must visit and should not be missed while in kathmandu.

the pashupatinath temple is not a beautiful place, but a must see if you are interested in hindu culture and religion. watching people praying and cremating their dead is something that makes you think about some aspects of life.

this famous religious centre is a real must see to get an impression of all rituals about tradtional cremation of the hindu. with some respect we followed the rituals from the other side of the river. is is for western people a little shocking by moments but it's part of the culture so very worth a visit and really a must see
pashupatinath has a peaceful vibe to it, which despite being flooded with devotees give you a serene solitude to feed your soul with energy coming from panchmukhi shivling.

religious area used by the hindi faithful for cremation rituals on the river. the entire area is serenity, colorful and peaceful. you can spend a few hours observing. watch out for the aggressive monkeys.
a very holy place, and very interesting to watch the dead being burnt on the pires.! kids diving into the thilfy river for gold or anything else of value.!
i don’t recommend this place to cuplé with kids. burning dead people all the time 365 days. coming by taxi from thamel for only 5000 rupiah

honoured to have been able to experience some of the holy and sacred hindu rituals that would normally be performed behind closed doors for families only. pashupatinath is te holy burial ground where all hindus live to be creamated and ashes scattered in the holy ganges. when there, make time to learn about the culture from one of the (government certified) guides for a small fee. this helps the locals and their families, plus you dont have to read the brochures! it was interesting to see the back to back cremation ceremonies through the day and it is indeed true how the hindu's believe that death is only one stage in their lives and the body is only a medium to host a soul.

every time i come here i see some kind development around the temple. temple area is under renovation since a decade and progress can be seen. very clean and well managed! non-hindus are not allowed inside the temple area, however they can have a nice view from river side where crimination is done. a beautiful view from this point is guaranteed. take a guide and ready to hear stories and tone around. am sure you will love this beautiful historic place. hindus will definitely love every moment! if you have time, try to visit around 7pm when aarti takes place at river side. peaceful and nice experience. do not miss!
place of devotion, nice two dharshan. local priest arrange pooja and dharshan. good view of the surroundings

this is an unesco world heritage site, and i can understand that after the earthquake it all takes a lot of money to restore and repair everything. but the surroundings of the pashupatinath temple are a bit disgraceful to say the least. as one of the main tourist attractions (with entrance fee), i would expect it to be is a better state. the temple itself is beautiful, and the process of cremation and burial add to it's authenticity. lot's of monkeys and beggars complete the package.

pashupatinath hindu temple not far from the airport which is hidden thankfully by a forrest, one of hindu's most holy sites.built over a large temple complex which some you can enter and some area's are for hindu's only.there are a couple of walkways to the entrance the main having a long avenue of food and souvenir shops to by pass,its 1000 rup to enter the temple grounds but like i said you cant go everywhere,but what you can see is quite historic/cultural/and slightly horrific to some.there is two sides to the temple grounds ,each side of the river of souls/bagmati river,which you can cross via the bridge.there are hindu temples and a mother theresa sanctuary,very close to the ceramonial burning platforms on the rivers edge,yes !, hindu people use this as there final place before ritual burnings that are done in full view of anybody that is there ,usually its family members and they must stay untill the body is fully consumed and emptied into the river.to some it can be a horrific sight but its done everyday and night ,and on the opposite side of the bridge ( same side )is where the well off/politicians and royalty are burned/consumed they cannot mix with ordinary folk. you may see the coffins been washed in the river to reuse,and people looking through the ashes that are emptied into the river for any gold that was in the bodies.across the river you get the better sights of the temple area as its built higher and gives some scenic views,you will come across lots of smaller temples,and sadhus holy men who follow shiva,there is a really good avenue/optical illusion of lingas and yoni's which seem to go on for ever , very cleverly built.on some of the temple walls there is a lot of sexual carvings of men/women which you find in many hindu temples.also some nice wall murals/paintings and lots of monkeys looking for food from tourists.this temple complex will open your eyes up one way or another,there is a lot of cuture/history and tradition here , well worth a visit ,but not for everybody, oh and watch out for the bulls, they are quite large here.
the first word that came from my mouth was "wow" when i saw the amazing art and sculptures of the holy pashupatinath temple. the temple is amusing and has stunning looks.

i was so underwhelmed by this temple site. it is such a disappointment to find it in such a dilapidated state - compared to the other religious temple sites in the city. in some the buildings what seemed like homeless / drug addicts were laid out making for a very unnerving experience. on the far side and up the steps are more dilapidated temples - a very dirty and depressing place.

pashupati nath is an incredible holy site to visit. for families, be aware that there are bodies being cremated on the river. having a guide can be very beneficial, just be sure to discuss price up front to avoid confusion with your guide later on.

this temple is one of the sacred temples of hindus. even though the temple is neat and clean the adjacent river is very dirty and polluted. need to be cleaned. hindus are cremated in the bank of the river and certain that the ash from the cremation is thrown in to the river too which makes it more dangerous.
shri pasupathinath temple, lord shiva is the main deity and one of must visirt temple in kathmandhu, nepal - this is our second nepal visit and many thanks to lord shiva

excellent feelings came from the time to reach kathmandu from birgunj by road.there by, the awesome nature have shown its own pleasure.all the sight scenes have stolen this heart.moreover seeing mt.everest at the chandragiri hills cable car journey was the most highlighted part of the nepal tour.
i visit in kathmandu and one most important temple call pashupatinath temple where uncountable people are visit even people from india as well.
spectacular hindu ensemble and worship place. very busy as always. beware of touts who bandy about with claims to vip access and/or guided tour experience

we did enjoy this part of our tour however as foreign tourist and not a hindu, you cannot enter the main temple complex. we did feel a little frustrated however the experience of seeing the cremation was a very interesting experience. be aware that after you past the temple entrance where remember you cannot enter the place is also filthy and there are dog feaces near and around the entrance as any many homeless children just playing on the steps in the dirty water. a unesco site and places of worship should be treated with the respect and dignity and like many places were visited during our month long trip this is not the case. the pagoda-style pashupatinath temple was constructed in 1696, but has been a site of hindu and buddhist worship for far longer. the nearby riverside steps are nepal's most holy cremation site. despite being clogged with garbage and black with pollution, the bagmati river is actually an extremely sacred river. pashupatinath is the nepalese equivalent of varanasi on the sacred river ganges. the cremation ghats along the bagmati are the city's most important location for open-air cremations and we were there to see a cremation which was very interesting and weird at the same time. we then walked along the river behind the temple to view the preparation ceremony this was even more strange. but still very intertesting

the pashupatinath is an hindu temple located in kathmandu, that has a very large in area which you can access most of the area (there is an area with access restricted only to religious that tourists do not access), among traditional religious constructions of different sizes among trees and dozens of monkeys circulating around (be aware not to have food in your hands because monkeys the monkeys will want to take it out of your hands anyway and with this they may inadvertently hurt you. there in pashupatinathyou will find a good and interesting experience of hindu tradition and culture. on june 2019 i paid 1000,00 nepalese rupies for the entrance (about nine us dollars). i thought the place in general, especially as it is an important hindu temple, should be more careful in terms of cleanliness. there were a lot of garbage and neglected parts of the structure in general that should be better taken care of because of their historical, cultural and religious value.
the place where i saw the dead people burn after dead, that was quite sad moments. having famous temple pashupatinath for hindu peoples.

very holy place for hindu. visited and saw such a temple for the first time because i hadn't been to kasi / varanasi too. enjoyed visiting and very curious to see cremation just by the side of temple.
didn't spend too much time here. may be on my next visit i'll look at this temple more closely. very important for the n epalis of course!
i'm not too much into temple visits. so the other experiences in nepal were far more captivating. of course, this temple is perhaps the most important one in nepal.
the waiting queue can be quite long. what is more frustrating is people cutting queues. towards the end there was a lot of pushing … disorganized.

a must see for all hindus , do beware to enter from the front gate and the entrance from the other gate is from the crematorium end . unless you are okay to seeing live cremation. i would not recommend that if you have minors accompanying you. a very holy place and you can feel the energy when inside.

beautiful temple area across from where hindus perform cremation of loved ones on funeral pyre. very interesting to see. you are across the river so close enough to watch ritual, but not too close.non hindus are not allowed inside the temple but interesting non the less. a monkey stole a hand sanitizer off of my purse thinking it was a snack.
just be aware that non-hindus are not allowed into the temple. the surrounding area is great to walk through.
located near the airport this place is considered very holy. hindus come in large numbers to offer their prayers to lord shiva. note : reach the temple befoe 5:30 pm as it closes at before sun set.

foreign tourist are made to pay more than locals and indian nationals. and that's not the worst part, there are no clear sign at the ticket counter or no information to tell tourists that if you are not a hindu, you cannot enter the main temple complex. i paid the ticket price only to be told by the guard at the gate that i can't enter and i wasn't even refunded the ticket! i'm left bewildered and frustrated, with nothing to see at the entrance. i'm not alone as a few foreign tourist is in the same predicament as me. i felt ripped off. this place is also filthy and there are dog feaces near and around the entrance. i think a unesco site and places of worship should be treated with the respect and dignity that it deserves, unfortunately the pashupatinath committee doesn't seems to care about the upkeep of this place. until things change for the better, avoid this place, save your ticket money and go to swayambhunath temple instead.

pashupatinath temple the architecture speaks volumes of the history.beautiful & strong pillars, powerful feel of the place. crowded especially at arti times, not as clean as cone would desire s pls of worship to be like

one of the seven world heritage sites of kathmandu valley. it is a complex of 498 temples but the main temple is dedicated to shiva,the hindu god who destroys evil and dates back to 5th century. it is situated on the banks of the bagmati river,holiest river of nepal. regarded as the most sacred area for cremation. also popular site to encounter monkeys and sadhus or babas,the holy men. regarded as one of the holiest hindu temples in the world. another must visit site.
the must visit place of kathmandu. the sculputre and archtecture is mind boggling. the only bizarre thging is the long que to enter the temple and no photographs are allowed.

being a shiv bhakt, i was really in awe of the one of the oldest shiv temple in the world , i could feel the charged vibrations from the whole atmosphere. i even meditated and felt bliss. must visit for every hindu pilgrim.
a must visit place. very clean and well maintained. divine and peaceful. if you are planning a visit to ktm, this should be on top of your list.

pashupatinath temple was a great experience. unfortunately it's full of people trying to sell you things or push their services onto you. other than that, the cultural experiences are something you should definitely experience. it's not the same as varanasi in india, but still something else entirely. and the monkeys were quite aggressive, getting in peoples faces. so just don't be an idiot when you're there. and be respectful of the mourners.

pashupathinath shivaling is very unique because it has shivas face on all the four sides, facing north,east,west and south. the temple complex is huge and is always crowded. on having darshan, you get peace and tranquility. baghmati river being one of the holiest, is very filthy due to lack of maintainance. otherwise pashupathinath temple is a must visit place when you visit nepal

teuly divine feeling.after completing chardham yatra of uttarakhand it is must to visit pasupatinath to complete it. because kedarnath and pasupatinath combined is considered one sham.went there with family and had amazing darshan.

though, this is my eighth visit to the lord pashupatinath temple, i always feel recharged and elated whenever i take a darshan. this is one of the twelve jyotirilingas, with great significance for spiritual flowering and esoteric wisdom. photography is not allowed inside the premises of the temple, and generally there will be huge rush. every visitor should be very careful about touts who try to dupe pilgrims and extract money for some reason or the other. it's always better not to fall prey to these middlemen and protect oneself. as this is large premises it's necessary to prioritise what could be seen depending on availability of time. if there is any known local person to accompany, that will be the best option.
a famous hindu temple in kathmandu representing hindu god shiva. a huge shivlinga in this temple is worshipped by hindu devptees from all the world.

i started the visit from riverside where bodies were brought in, stripped of clothes, covered in white or orange sheet, feet was washed with holy water for bhagmathi river, then burned on the river bank. i was there for 15 mins and saw more than 10 bodies. it reminded me the philosophical saying that we come to this earth naked and leave this world naked without taking a single material that we accumulate by fighting the world. i saw a tall body is being burnt and the feet was sticking out which was folded back using a long stick. the river is holy but extremely dirty. the smell of burning flesh and visuals of half burn body parts left me disturbed for sometime. it is definitely not for the faint of heart. i was told only hindus are allowed in to the temple. the temple visit was left me with unusually calm feeling.

pashupatinath is hindu biggest temple in nepal even lot of indian are went there to worship and pray. everyday in the morning there are religion song singing and lot of people are involve to pray and they expect always healthy as well as lot of property from god.

this open-air cremation site was a bit of a culture-shock for us, but the tradition associated with the ceremony was touching, and the riverside setting and adjacent temples make for a memorable visit, especially if you're high up (beware of the monkeys) admiring the sunset. a bit pricey to get in ($10) compared with other attractions in the city, and sadly non-hindus aren't allowed to see the magnificent-looking temple complex. easy to diy in a taxi from thamel or from the giant stupa on the eastern side of town.

god shiva world famous and unesco world heritage sight situated in capital of nepal. temple premises widely open and on main highway. lots of parking space available front of temple gate. inside you can deposit your shoes at counter and also have washrooms. no photography allowed inside temple. this is unique shivaling which i ever seen in my life as it have five faces of god lord shiva. very spiritual power you feel there.

a must must visit place if you believe in hindu ideology. neat, clean and well organised temple located in the heart of city. you need to walk around 500mtr from parking to entrance. you would definitely feel the positive vibes. note- try reaching the temple before 6 for crowd free visit.

pashupatinath temple in kathmandu nepal is a huge monument which is an excellent visit by all nationals . hindus are only allowed to enter the sanctum sanatorium. clicking of pics is strictly not allowed. river bhagmati flows adjacent and all the final rites are done at the ghats . it’s a wonderful temple with positive vibes and rich history. it’s a fifteen min journey from the airport and do avoid monday, tuesday and saturday as it’s crowded. any given day during aarti time is crowded and will take anywhere between an hour or more. lot of improvement work is currently taking place around the monument . holy place which has an history and it’s an abode to the lord shiva . aum namah shivaya. 🙏
visit to pashupatinath temple is open to all but the main area is restricted to entry for hindus only!

this had been removed from our itinerary as the company we travelled with had feedback from other travellers saying they didn’t enjoy it. i don’t think it’s about enjoyment rather than an interesting insight into the rituals of a different culture. as we were not hindu, we couldn’t go into the temples but you can walk around & just be there. after paying our entrance fee a local guide started to explain about the place, we agreed a price with him after some bartering & he showed us around, explaining the rituals, beliefs & roles of the sadhu’s.

i didn't like this temple because i felt like the people in the temple, the alleged "holy men" included, are using the temple to make a quick buck out of tourists. this ruined the experience for me, even though the temple and the rituals being performed were interesting. after i bought a ticket, a guide followed me in. i assumed it was because some parts of the temple were off limits to tourists so he needed to flag that out. he ended up giving us a tour of the temple, which was helpful. however at the end of the tour, he was very pushy about the "tip". he asked for usd20 per person! we gave him usd 3 each because it was a short tour (about 1 hr), and also because we didn't have much money on us. in the end i had to show him my wallet to let him know that i had no money at all on me and i was giving him everything i had. as for the "holy men", i was told they gave up family and material possessions to live as monks in the temple. however after we took photos with them, they asked for money. bearing in mind i had only usd 4 in my wallet, i gave him usd 1 and he was unhappy, asking for "more money". at that point, i walked away but i no longer felt safe in the temple.

there are certain limitations for non-hindus such as entering the temple, however, there is still enough to see and visit. initially, from a western perspective, the events at the bagmati river seem solemn and personal. i found it a little surprising that there were hundreds of local 'spectators'. a different view on life and death.

being of irish lineage, funerals were one of the few times my family got together and it was generally a memorable and fun experience. when i share these experiences with others outside my family, they always look at me funny and its obvious they do something different. as a result, i've always had a curiosity about how other cultures deal with a loved one's passing. well, a hindu cremation is much more ritualistic, serene and reverent. it was worthwhile to have a guide explain the process both before, during and after the event. i found it moving. there were several pyres at various stages and you could walk along the river and observe. there were monkeys around trying to get food where they could find it. there were several priests offering their services for a fee. i would recommend going if you have an interest and be respectful.
beautiful old temple but too many people out there. maybe because we came on the weekend and so many people came for prayer

no visit to kathmandu can be complete without visiting the old pashupatinath temple. its amazing with a very unique shiv-ling! an old shiva temple, full of positive energy which my whole family felt while there. it was very crowded when we went so be prepared for long queues if you want to do any pujas. if you only want to pray and come back, it was very quick.
not a pleasant place at all. i did enjoy the public display of cremation and our guide kept making a joke of everything.

very old lord shiva temple with many small temples inside. special poojas performed by pundits all around, as in most of the hindu temples. only queue system, short qs, good dharsham. additional attaractions:daily bhajans behind the temple in the mornings & aarthi on the banks of bagmathi river in the evenings
another beautiful place of hindu worship in kathmandu steeped in history and important to the hindu people in nepal. lovely architecture and interesting place.

being a spiritual seeker i have dream to visit this place it was magical. very ancient and lot of beautiful statue and golden roof of temple was magical. i feel great energy of shiva due to high energy. would love to return back in coming future.

we are so jaded today that even the sight of human bodies consumed on pyres is for most tourists no more than a good photo op but this is one of nepal's unmissable points. come early morning- but anytime is good- and with plenty of time to walk the maze of shivalingas, temples and burning sites beside the polluted bagmati river as you are surrounded by leaping monkeys, posing sadhus, hindu priests and worshippers. the main temple is out of bounds for non hindus but its a small fraction of the area. keep your shoes on! its allowed and you dont want an infection in such a place. you can stand right beside the grieving families and watch the bodies but be respectful about taking photos. despite the millions pouring into the place, toilets are very scarce. use the one near the shoe deposit area. entrance is 1000 npr. guides are waiting there too.

no visitor to kanthmandu would like to miss the opportunity to visit the pashupatinath site, which is so richly endowed with places to see and stuff to buy. there are temples and idols so esoteric and monumental for the lenses of their cameras. the temple is about 5km from the city. to get there, one can hire a taxi or ride on a minibus or tempo. the pashupatinath temple symbolizes nepal's national glory. it is the centre of faith and relgious piety for the hindus and buddhists of the world, as well as a unique treasure that forms part of the world heritage.

we visited here in april 2019. in our opinion, it was not worth it. being foreigners and non-hindu, we were very limited to where we could go. i understand the religious significance of the site and appreciate it. just didn’t feel welcomed there. it’s a lot of money to watch a cremation, which we personally felt was an intrusion on a private moment, so we didn’t. left with a feeling of our money was wanted, but not us. holy men are located throughout the areas where foreigners are allowed and for a price will bestow a blessing. the monkeys were very aggressive. the buddhist stupas were by far better places to visit.
the temple is busy in the morning and best time to visit. the local people are helpfull and buy pooja items after bargain.
this is a temple where funerals are held. it is a sacred area and very serene. it was interesting watching across the river how local nepalese look after there departed.
not something special and you pay 1000 roupies to enter. don't try it! also the place there isn't something special to see.

my score is obviously based on the touristic value of this attraction. i understand the religious value. dead bodies left unattended aside the river. the stream is so slow that contamination is clearly visible after discarding so much biologica. i would not recommend

this is one of those places that we feel is a must. one's first impression might be of unease, but it is quite fascinating to experience the rituals surrounding death in the hindu culture. by watching from afar one is able to witness with respect the various aspects of the cremation process. the temple is off limits to non-hindus, so that is one drawback to the visit.

while non-hindus cannot enter the main temple, there is still plenty to see around the comlex. the most interesting and confronting aspect is the open air cremation of loved ones on platforms on on the bank of the holy bagmati river at the back of the main temple. there are also many small buildings on the opposite river bank containing lingam stones and dedicated the shiva. watch out for the many aggressive monkeys which wander around the complex.

this is a huge temple and the holiest of places for hindus. one cannot enter the main temple but there is still a lot to see. the cremations are visible but out of respect one should not venture too close or take photos. definitely not to be missed!
beautiful shrine center of the heart of kathmandu and heart of hindu peoples. you can the life end here.
we nearly got washed awa, caught in a rain storm. this was a very interesting site and worth getting wet for. don’t go past it

the temple has huge complex. its better if you get accommodation near so you can visit it easily. the temple open at 4:00 am and its best time to visit it. also at closing time of temple, @7:00 pm, all doors of the temple are open and you can have darshan from close by.
the place could be better managed by a private company. good luck. making changes on garbage disposal should be promptly placed

we went to the temple quite early in the morning around 6.30am, that time the temple was quite empty and the queue for the darshan was less. we did our darshan quite easily in less time. the temple is supposed to be crowded on mondays and saturdays, so avoid those days. camera is not allowed inside the main temple but you can carry it till the main entrance of the main temple, but not inside. the architecture inside the main temple is beautiful and worth watching. there are monkeys inside so be careful if u have any food in hand.

this unesco world heritage temple is very holy, and non-hindus are not allowed into the temple itself, but the grounds are impressive, and for me it was the first time i had seen purification and cremation (done on platforms along the bagmati river). very lovely and peaceful.
very old and significant hindu temple. site of open air riverside cremations. confronting from a western perspective, but part of life

pashupatinatha is the most sacred hindu temple in nepal which is on the bank of bagmati river and visited by both hindus from all over the world. hindus can go inside the temple. non hindus can see the temple from eastern side of the river bank. a serene place.

three of us went here to visit what us supposed to be one of kathmandu’s main temple attractions and we were put off by the place and the scheming people who seem to operate in this area including some that appeared to be in cahoots with the ticket sellers. this appeared to be a trend at several places in kathmandu. tourists are requested to pay 1000 rupees here and you are harassed by a few men who stand around doing very little. one member of our group was actually a hindu and was also told he cannot go to the temple, which seems to be against what i have read and the rules of the temple. to actually get to the temple from the car park area (where cabs drop you off), you must walk along a road filled with beggars and then multiple shops / stalls selling religious artefacts, or other memorabilia. then once you get to the area immediately around the temple, you are told to remove your shoes - and a lot of locals do - but actually it appears that you do not need to and many people are wearing their shoes around. i believe, as is normal tradition, you only remove your shoes if actually entering the temple so try not to leave any expensive shoes lying around. there are also many monkeys around but they do not appear to be aggressive. i think the entire organisation of this place for tourism is poor, especially if charging 1000 rupees. it requires better signage and removal of scamming locals who try to rip off or mislead visitors. we were driven away by the misinformation and the lack of an explanation as to what 1000 rupees was for and then being offered a “guide” who could not explain much to begin with. quite sad as this is meant to be a religious place and i am sure there is a lot of interesting things to see and experience here, given the right opportunity and time.

highlight of kathmandu trip - must visit. beside baghmati river , well maintained. leather items / mobiles not allowed. cremation ground on the back side - gives a perspective to life and death, mahakaal .
i went there on sunset time and felt very peaceful there, special sacred place, no noise, no dust, no rush. during the night lightening adds more charm to this place.

i have been to this temple several times. the temple is only accessible to hindus, hindus do not pay 1000 npr but are not allowed in bit they can see the temple complex. there is a lot to see, the burnings, the sadhus, the old temples at the end of the complex, that unfortunately have been damaged by the earthquake. and don't forget the aarti. because there was so much to experience, i went there a few times.

the temple and surrounding area were heavily crowded due to some celebrations going on. in addition, the close by holy river is used to dispose of the ashes of the deceased, who are burnt in big wooden fires on the banks of the river. deceased's feet are first washed, a ritual we witnessed. what a different world this is.
one of the most holiest place for hindus in nepal. if you are not hindu or a buddhist, you can't go in, but it's still worth a visit because you feel how important the place is.

pashupatinath is perhaps the most revered hindu temples in kathmandu. it is a beautiful temple with good vibrations. if one has the time one should attend the evening arti, it is simply divine. and one should also visit the caves of tilopa and naropa in the same complex.

after paying fee 1000 rp and some more for local guide we had unique experience from local culture. we saw ceremony for dead people. toke picture with holy man and fed some monkey. this place is must see

we were thrilled to enter this temple and be enveloped in a different scene from the rest of the city altogether. a must see temple for all who visit nepal. we were awestruck by the beauty of the architecture and the holiness of the entire place. very wonderful experience.

the lack of signage and direction is what makes this place so frustrating. we wandered around not really knowing where to go until we were barked at by staff and redirected to a poorly advertised ticket booth. after paying 1000 nepalese rupee each we were asked if we wanted a guide and we declined. we appreciated the offer but had already paid for a few guides at previous attractions and just wanted to go at our own pace this time. we had sort of assumed that if we made our way up to the river we would be able to see the cremations taking place but we seemed to be fenced off from the river so we followed along the fence line trying to find out how we could see the river. other people seemed to be standing on the other side of the fence but we could never work out how they got there which was so frustrating. and it wasn’t just a matter of staring through the bars because there was two lots of fencing with a gap in the middle so we couldn’t get close enough. none of the other tourists seemed to have a guide and even though the temple grounds cover a vast area we seemed to quickly exhaust the areas that were accessible. there were these huge concrete steps that seemed to go on forever but we were pretty reluctant to go to the top of those. we didn’t really know where they led and we were struggling a bit with the air quality so getting up those steps really did feel like going up mt everest. we realised though that everywhere else was a bit of a dead end and having paid for the attraction we wanted to get some value out of the experience. once we got to the top we followed along a path that seemed to go on forever. people were walking in the opposite direction so we were concerned that at the end of this trek we may have to turn around and the stairs had started to descend so it wouldn’t all be downhill on the return. i felt as though we were walking parallel with the river but the river was completely obscured from view, we could only see snatches of it through gaps in the trees. perhaps there were no cremations happening that day or perhaps i didn’t really understand the point of the tourist attraction. we speculated that they intentionally make it so unclear so people resort to paying for a guide but we were too stubborn. anyway, after walking for what seemed like forever we exited the temple on the opposite side which did lead us out to the river but there was nothing particular to see there. i don’t mean to sound so whingey but it did cost us 2000 rupee altogether. overall it was a bit disappointing.
can visit this temple but as hindu .but hard to find even a toilet. the infrastructure is too old and need to repair most of the things

this is the main hindu temple in kathmandu. unfortunately it is only open to hindu. non-believers can circle outside and peek over the walls to see some of the icons and activities. some activities also spill outside.

major cremation site for kathmandu hindus. all through the day and night bodies are cremated on platforms on the river bank. although slightly macabre it is a fascinating spectacle. further along from the ghatts are structures containing shiva lingams and here you will find saddhus ( holy men). there is also one of hindus most sacred temples containing a huge statue of nandi (shivas bull) although sadly only hindus can enter. but you can stand at the gate and catch a view of his back end. an interesting place to spend a couple of hours .
visited the temple and had dharshan of lord pashupatinath ji .the temple is beautiful and all the small temples around main temple are very beautiful. god bless you all.

pashupati nath temple is supposedly one of the holiest places for hindus. temple is dedicated to lord shiva and only hindus are permitted to enter. right next to it is the temple of guheshwori on the banks of the holy bagmati river. there you can witness cremations on raised platforms going on nonstop. a very unique experience for an outsider to say the least.
impressive area, with some nice tempels and views. ofcourse with the ghat’s as highlights. strange, educational experience for a non-hindu i guess. go see them. be respectful!

the biggest tourist trap or conjob we ever met. 1000 rupees each for entrance, the ticket guy followed us in (a group of 8) despite the fact we did not request for any guides. throughout the "tour" we were provide illogical and out of the world information which was rubbish to us as we have some knowledge about hinduism. the guide asked us to go near some priest constantly smoking marijuana. with a few blessing words and a red dot on the forehead, the demanded for 20usd for a 2 min ritual. at the end of the 30 mins tour, the guide demanded for another 1000rupee from each of us. we didn't agree as it was not stated before hand. out of courtesy, we tried to tip him 800 rupee, in which he did not accept and started to berate and curse us. fortunately we left safely.
an ancient temple with many stories associated with it. a must see for anyone visiting kathmandu. recommend visiting around 6 00 pm in the evening to catch the aarti.
beauty place.not visit at weekend cuz its a lot of people and almost impossible to go inside. when u like hiking near is budha tempel on hill
a hindu temple that should not be missed if you visit nepal , very holy and good place to visit. i feel very good and awesome after visiting there , very well maintained temple.
a famous and sacred hindu temple complex that is located on the banks of the bagmati river. worth to visit heritage site !
a interesting place to see. beware of the scamming tour guides and monks. tickets are 1000 to get in.
this is one of the very few temples which you should not miss when you visit nepal. very holy and well maintained.

one of the important hindu religious temples on the banks of the bagmati river. the campus is very clean and the crowd is nicely managed by the temple authorities. this temple complex was in the list of the unesco world heritage sites in 1979. a lot of devotees offer prayer every day.

this is a very holy site for hindus and also buddhists. the site is vast so allow a few hours to look around. there is also a deer park and the photo ops with some of the local sadhus make for some interesting photos of your trip here, worth a visit if in kathmandu , one of the must see ,s in kathmandu.

as listed one of the heritage sites under unesco, pashupatinath is one of the major temples of hindu religion. along with the religion they got other attractions also the hindu monks, old age homes . and tere is aarati every evening from 6 pm to 8 pm and that is very magnificent

its good old architecture .sacred hindu temple complex that is located on the banks of the bagmati river,pashupatinath is more than just a religious destination. it is a combination of religion, art, and culture. it offers peace and devotion. pashupatinath temple is one of the 8 unesco cultural heritage sites of the kathmandu valley.
highly recommend this place for great new experience. i felt like its totally new for the western world

great place to observe the local shrine and temple with rich culture history. i was amazed, when i saw burning the dead body in pashupati nath temple and also quite sad seeing the relatives crying also. overall, great place to visit this place.
it is an interesting complex of temples. a good place to sit and watch live go by at the ghats. also a good place to learn about the rituals and traditions around death for the local community.

being naive and out of touch with world and local news, i was quick to learn how the 2017 earthquake caused such damage to this city and it's splendors. this site being one, cordoned off in places being repair, scaffolding everywhere indicated of repairs.one had to use their imagination what glory was before and will be restored one day. fun to find spots where buildings were intact to catch pictures of the ancient glory and history. a must see.

being naive and out of touch with world and local news, i was quick to learn how the 2017 earthquake caused such damage to this city and it's splendors. this site being one, cordoned off in places being repair, scaffolding everywhere indicated of repairs.one had to use their imagination what glory was before and will be restored one day.

felt like a stepping into a storybook temple, it was truly an amazing experience. the temple is strictly for hindus. i would suggest a morning darshan. be careful of the several touts peddling vip darshan as it is controlled well therefore don’t fall for it.
there's lots of area to move about or just sit and enjoy the place.one can enjoy good photography here. there are good food and shopping joints within as well as outside it's premises. a must visit!

it's a huge temple complex in the center of the town. the main garbha gudi has a shiva lingam, with an engraving of the face of lord shiva, on 4 sides. you can go around and see the god from all 4 sides. every evening, near the temple, on the banks of the bhagamathi river, a evening aarthi, is held.

very confronting place to visit dead bodies cremating in open and dirty . i would never recommend anyone to go there just be aware of the holy men there don’t take photos of them or u might have to pay them . don’t take flowers from them they will ask for money u don’t have to pay them but still they will ask .
there are so many temples in nepal and this is another one that is worth a visit while in kathmandu.

during our visit, we saw bodies in various stages of cremation: fire building, fire starting, huge flames, hot embers and no remains and finally, washing the embers into the river. quite messy and a bit confronting.

a functioning temple of high esteem for hindus. hence extremely crowded. also, if you are not an asian hindu you will not be allowed to enter the main temple precinct. that said the temple has 4 parts - the main temple precinct which houses the actual deity and the nandi (holy bull), the secondary precinct which is outside the main precinct and has subsidiary temples and all sorts of sadhus waiting to make a money from foreigners who want to click a photo, the burning ghat or crematorium and lastly the other side of the river on top of the hill. lets start with the last first. the "river" is actually almost a drain (sorry for this but this is what the government has transformed the bagmati river to in this area). it is a very narrow strip of water that hardly flows and is full of rubbish. on the opposite side of this stream is a small hill lined with small temples. you should climb all the way up as there are beautiful views from there. also halfway down the stairs you can also sit down and see the process of burning of dead bodies (for whoever is mentally aligned to that). back on the temple side of the river / stream, you have the burning ghats - these are just ghats and normally people who come there to burn their departed family are present. beyond this is the secondary precinct which has a lot of smaller temples - you can visit these even as a non-hindu. some of the temple architecture is really fascinating and beautiful. this is a place of living tradition and culture and hence quite interesting and at the same time a place that deserves respect to be shown. finally you have the main temple precinct that houses the main shrine and the huge giant nandi (bull) statue and also multitude of smaller temples. there is always a line / queue to see the main temple but it is quite organized and there is no hustling by the temple functionaries and no pressure for paying money and things like that. you can if you want, view the shivling (the main idol) and walk away without paying a single penny. the organization inside the main precinct while not top notch is pretty decent given the crowd that it handles. no photography or video or mobile phones are allowed in this place. i went in to the main temple with my dslr closed - one person asked me how you have such a big camera here - i told him it is switched off and closed and he was ok. everywhere else it is not a problem. you have to enter without shoes of course. we had a guided tour, where the guide made us open our shoes and keep all our leather belongings in a shop and then gave us rubber slippers to use till we came back. this is the normal procedure. you can keep your wallets and stuff also but depends on your risk appetite. there are huge crowds all the while and if visiting with small children then it is a must to keep good control over them as it is very easy for a child to get lost in the crowd. we went on a saturday around 10:30 to 11 in the morning. it was quite crowded but not in a way that you cannot move at all. overall this is a must visit to see religious fervour and some tradition and culture. we did not wait around for the evening arati on the banks as the sight of the river was quite depressing.
holy place for hindus, also called 5th dham of load shiva.peacefull, positive vibes.good management.

a very visible and open view of cremation in hinduism. grab a guide at the gate. i paid 1000 rupees for one hour. he explained the entire process and i would not have got much out of it without a guide.
real authentic nepali atmosphere. families performing the cremation of their deaths. you can walk around the entry and skip payment.

absolutely worth a visit . and make sure that you get a local guide as we did to help you understand whats going on around you. it really opened our eyes to the hindu history and culture and helped us understand more their way of life.

had an amazing darshan during the special evening prayer at pashupatinath.this temple is located on banks of baghmati river. one of the key holy sites for lord shiva. a must visit for all hindus travelling to kathmandu. the river banks behind also serve as a place for last rites for the dead.bodies are burnt on the banks as per typical hindu tradition. if you cannot stomach this avoid going to the rover bank side.

one of the biggest and most popular hindus temple. the pashupatinath temple is a famous and sacred hindu temple in asia. pashupatinath temple has several temples and structures in park area. it's a must visit for hindus. daily cremation ceremonies along the river.
it’s a great experience about concept of death.lots of people come there before sunset. various songs as a special ceremony for corpse. it takes about 3 hours.

we thought we'd be seeing some beautiful temples but the experience was more confronting than anticipated with at least 3 cremations occurring whilst we visited. the access to much of the temple complex is restricted for those who aren't hindu and we didn't want to intrude upon families' grief by staring at the bodies of their loved ones. it seemed a bit macabre. the river where the cremations occur is very dirty and full of pigeons and monkeys. this was also the place we experienced the most begging and hawking in kathmandu. whilst an interesting cultural experience, it wasn't for me and i wouldn't go there again.
really pretty in the night with all the lights. for hindus it is a very sacred place and so it can be crowded on certain days of the year. so please check before visiting.

pashupatinath temple has several temples and structures in park area. it’s a must visit for hindus. daily cremation ceremonies along the river. bridge separates casts cerimonies. you can see monkeys roaming though the park, holy men in their service, artists dressed as gods such as hanuman, etc. a little bit of everything.

at first it seems intrusive, but it is a great way to get some insight into the hindu cremation ritual. watching is very personal and may be uncomfortable for some, but it is enlightening. the temple is off-limits to non-hindus, so you will have to be satisfied with a walk around the grounds. the sadhus that hang around posing for pictures for cash are intriguing.

was 10 bucks to visit for foreigners and was quite a morbid place. you basically stand on a bridge and u can see bodies burning on both sides of the river for different status classes and family members crying. they have bodies burning 24/7. probably wouldn’t have gone again if i was to do it all over again. mostly locals when i was there and mostly i guess was to observe the funeral process. pretty sad place.
view of real life cremation traditions seen on site and explanations provided by our guide, mr puru, made this visit a great experience.

please don’t misunderstand my low mark, it’s only related to the very expensive entry ticket of 1000 rupees and there is hardly anything you can see there. the main temple is not allowed for non hindus and ground around temple within the complex will not provide much. on the other hand, when you cross the bagmati river, you will have a magic view on the temple which cost nothing. so my suggestion is to certainly visit but do not pay for it just enjoy the stroll, atmosphere and views from the other side of the river.
although non-hindus cannot enter the temple proper, there is plenty else to see, from macaques deftly avoiding the razor wire to open-air cremations.
we really liked this place during the sivaratri fastivals with many people and funny sadu we enjoyed there alot. we will visit there again

we visited this important temple on our first stay in kathmandu - we thought about returning on our second stay but decided against when we saw the huge crowds for maha shivaratri! we were of course not able to enter the temple, but gained a good view from the opposite bank of the river and outside the gates. certainly well worth a visit, although you do have to close your eyes to some things you see.
this is one of the holiest places for hindus and is spiritually and culturally rich. the earthquake damage is being repaired slowly. i love the peaceful atmosphere.

humbling and sombre place, it can get really overwhelming with the sights and sounds but it is an experience that will make you think about life and how we are not so different after all, wherever we come from. did not take any photographs as we felt it was disrespectful, the picture is forever imprinted in our minds.

pashupati nath temple is an unesco world heritage site in nepal. a lot of construction is being carried out around the temple area for the tourists. sasuki car taxis are very popular in nepal being imported from india. it has a small market to buy souvenirs.
a must visit for all hindus all over the world. non hindus are not allowed. however, replica pashupati nath temple are also in india for those who are unable to visit nepal due to age and cost.

it was great experuence vising pashupati nath, biggest hindu temple, sits on the bank of river.there are number if shrines around temple where devotees offer worshiping. it seems the temples architechrual form was unique and started early medival time. you will get pashupati by a taxi from thamel which you exoect 400ruoiys
i love coming to pashupatinath. as a foreigner, i am not allowed in the temple, but strolling the grounds and taking in the spiritual atmosphere is always very refreshing and enlivening.
a visit there will remind you of a forgotten reality : death ! the cremation of these bodies and the ashes bluntly swept in the flowing canal remind you of what this life if worth !
pasupatinath is an unique place to explore a new knowledge about hindu culture and rituals. i visited pasupatinath many times with my friend and it's always feel great to been there.

no need any explanation for people to visit pashupati nath temple & its surrounding. but i suggest you to visit its few surrounding area which are as best as pashupati nath. gauri ghat, kirateshwor temple, guheshwori temple. are among the best.

its one of the most popular hindu shrine among hindus of nepal and india. it belongs to lord shiva (pashupati nath). although there's always good crowd in the temple and visited by vips too, the management has not been able to keep the place clean and well managed. people must be careful when carrying foods inside because of monkeys. shoes, phoyography, talking in phone, etc are not allowed inside the temple.

very bustling with many worshipers. a unique visit, especially with many cremations going on (where the body, ashes and wood are then dumped into the holy river), something you won't see in most countries. do note that foreigners/non-hindus are not allowed to enter the main temple, but there's plenty to see outside the main hall of worship.

amazing opportunity to see hindu rituals in many forms. not for all, as you may see people who are being cremated, and that can be disturbing to some. i found it an adventure into something the west doesn't have.

one of the most famous hindu temple in the world. the history dates back to fifth century ad. this is a shiva temple. the entry inside the main temple is open to hindus only , others can have a visit around the temple area. if you are to visit the place, do check the opening hours in the newspaper as the place is open in limited hours only. camera or photography is not allowed inside the main temple complex. special pujas can be offered on payment of specified fee. a must visit place for hindus devotees.

the pagoda-style hindu pashupatinath temple along the banks of the bagmati river dates to 1696. it was named a unesco world heritage site in 1979, and was not damaged in the 2015 earthquake. the temple (really a complex of temples) is a sacred place of worship and serves as the seat of nepal’s national deity, lord pashupatinath, the “lord of the animals”. you won’t be able to enter the temple compound unless you are hindu. but from the main gate you can catch a view of the backside of the statue of nandi, shiva’s bull. you can also view the back of the temple from the east side of the bagmati river. here a series of ghats (steps) lead into the water, allowing ashes from the open-air cremations to be washed away by the river. as a westerner, i appreciated the opportunity to visit a place where centuries-old hindu rituals are practiced. there is a unique spirit and energy here for the hindu traditions of life, death and reincarnation.

we arrived and paid the $10 (i don’t know how people can complain about the fee in one of the world’s poorest countries) we took a guide (highly recommended) thats cost us another $10. his knowledge was amazing and he took us via shortcuts to all the best photo spots and points of interest. we watched the pagati arati that starts around 1840 from the opposite bank, all the the while families are cremating their dead on open pyres making for a truly fascinating look at life and death in contrast. no matter the caste or the wealth in life, all of these hindus end up on a pyre on the same river bank. don’t be put off by the monkeys. they stay away from you and don’t be pushed into paying too much for photographs with the holy men/gurus, pay what you can afford but be wary that these donations are what they live off. overall a fascinating and worthwhile experience.
nicer and cleaner. more organized and great spiritual ferling. evrytime we come to kathmandu we make sure to visit this place.

an eye-opener on the religious & sacred complex where you can witness the cremation in the open. only hindus are allowed to enter the temple.but it is already a majestic view just seeing the whole place from outside.
a must see hindu temple, where lord shiva have 4 faces. whereas other parts of the world that you can see lord shiva in the form of linga. only here in pashupati nath temple lord shiva have 4 faces.

we were told about the activities going on at this site. the locals use it for a public cremation ceremony. nothing can prepare you for the sights, smell a and activity. totally foreign to americans but opens your eyes to the rest of the world, especially nepal

the lord shiva temple visit is must if you are visiting kathmandu the ultimate creator /controller of universe.the architecture of the temple is excellent .the special thing which i seen is that if the person after death is cremated there then he will will directly go to heaven. we have attended the evening aarti/puja which is worth watching.

one of the best places i have visited in nepal. this place has some kind of spiritual powers that you will feel the moment you arrive there. try to be there in the morning because there are many things to explore and to know about. don't miss the river aarti aka evening river prayer. you can't take pictures inside the temple however you can go on the other and take a picture from above and its the most stunning view you will ever have of any temple. take a local bus or a tuk-tuk instead of taxi as its more reasonable and easy mode of transport.
we visited this temple along with the others on our temple tour. there many areas to walk around and while some areas are being reconstructed after the earthquake it was very interesting.

although only hindus can enter the main temple, there is a lot to see and learn here. from the naughty monkeys to the cows and other animals, it's an enlightening place. the cremation of people is sobering. it makes you realise that the end may come at any time. the ground is vast and full of different things. i had a guide and he was knowledgeable and nice. he helped me to understand all the various activities.
pashupati nath is a must see place in kathmandu. it is the center of hinduism and is always full of various lights, sound and smells. great place
this is where the funeral ceremonies take place. it is an experience you should not miss! very somber, beautiful and educational in regard to learning the customs of other cultures
had the opportunity to observe the magnificent evening aarati at the pashupatinath temple. it was a great experience for me. i had never seen something like that in my life.
