hindu temple, number of people coming from indiad, local people doing some of their ceremonies, not allow to enter inside the temple if you are not hindu

one thing i can reviewed is the shops/market outside the temple. most of them selling items for prayers. love the colourful powder where they usually use it for kolan. they were vibrant colours. the people are friendly and more than often, the younger males offer to give a tour inside the temple without being forceful (which is nice compared to other attractions). my friends went in to have a look but only a hindu can enter the actual temple. one of my non-hindu friend just went around the place and witnessed live cremation next to the river. psa - definitely not for the faint-hearted. for those who wants to enter the temple ground, do prepare 1000npr and enjoy the activities happening within the area.
its a main attraction of kathmandu and one of the oldest temple of lord shiva must visit when your are in nepal
pashupatinath is for pilgrims forever reverent. recovering from 2015 earthquake pilgrims do not hesitate to flock to pashupatinath in any weather condition.

this was a good intro visit for me to get a bit more familiar with hindu beliefs and practices. the cremate on site and this might be an issue for those with aversion to that. however, it is quite a facility and worth including on your visit to kathmandu if time permits.
always a divine place for hindi worshippers, a must visit place everytime we come to kathmandu, kept well organized these days

this world heritage site on the banks of the bagmati river is one of the most sacred hindu shrines in the world. devotees meditate and pray on the steps, and many families are there to cremate loved ones on pyres along the river. the individual pyres are decorated with ropes of crysanthenums, piled with tiers of wood, and surrounded by mourners. we were stunned at first when the pyre was set alight, but we're very humbled to witness this ancient honorific rite. nameste.
it is one of the most holy places for hindu. we like the place. also, we could attend the aarti. they also offer abhishek. the priest are very good while performing abhishek. good place.

this one of main attractions of kathmandu. our visit to temple was very smooth and have darshan nicely. temple premises is not so clean as anticipated. lot of monkeys are there, so, visiters must beware from monkeys. holy place must be visited to seek blessings of lord shiva.

avoid friday and saturday as crowded , considering these are weekends. we went on sunday , a working day and had fast darshan / prayers here. lovely altars and do keep small changes for the various priest sitting around.
om nama shivaya holy place in nepal. whenever i go here im blessed .though its polluted i always feel its purity
ancient old, holy temple of shri pashupatinath is a must visit in kathmandu. there are 4 entrance to the main temple viz east, west, north and south. pujaris there hail from south india.
note for visitors to the temple: there are entry restrictions for foreign visitors so please check before visiting the temple.
for travellers wishing to visit the temple note that there are restrictions for entry for foreign visitors. please check before visiting the temple.
this time me n my mom came to katmandu for our vacation n its was completed dharsan of pashupatinath i heard it was a dreams come true my trip was fruitful.
if you are a devotee.you must visit this place. a wonderful place with massive religious and cultural significance

i was so happy to visit pashupati nath temple because it was one of my dream, but i must say that there are scammers inside the temple as well. one local person cheated me inside the temple by saying that he will arrange pooja's , he did that but charges me a lot. so its my advice that be cautions inside the temple as well

we just visited the temple and not the surrounding structures, as they had some rituals going on and the place was crowded by locals. so maybe the timing was wrong. the temple as i said too crowded, the path was not clean and very difficult to do puja or darshan. only hindu will be allowed inside the temple, luckly we were helped by someone and able to do darshan atleast. maybe will visit again later, to see the temple complex when its not so crowded no photography allowed inside the temple

as this was where we witnessed our first open pyre creamation.only hindus are allowed into the temple.typical temple setting where one would need lots of change as you are expected to contribute at every turn. the funerals are carried out on the river bank. those not entering the temple view the funerals from the outside.not for the faint hearted.

many open air cremations at different stages were taking place while we were there. bodies were being prepared for the afterlife by the river-side. sadhus, tourists, pilgrims, mourners and picnickers milling around. if you are from a culture unused to dealing with death so openly, or sensitive to smoke, queasy about grey-faced corpses being fed with rice water or if you have have young children. be prepared for an eye opening experience. well worth the visit albeit potentially a little disturbing.
historical place with wonderful experience. aarati was fantastic feel like peace even in a busy place.

a wonderful experience for all senses. watching the rituals take place each evening has been very memorable. a small entry fee will allow you to wander the complex and then sit and watch the evening service. highly recommended.

you can easily spent here a half day to just witness everything that goes around. i would recommend to take a guide who can tell you a bit more details about the cremations. later on you can just walk around yourself. the only thing i dont like is that the guides sometimes ask for more money/tip after guiding. to me it is just rude. the tip we gave was not bad at all.
really a site to visit in kathmandu. most visited place in nepal. you just enjoy the culture heritage.
every body have to visit and darshan in pasupatinath in nepal. it will be great experience new visitors

airport is not so far from this temple. it is also unesco world heritage. you can see alot of hindu people standing in queue for pooja mostly in festivals. this place is also famous for monkeys. keep your food in safe place

it is a holy place , but the main temple is not open for non hindu people. it is under world h heritage site. there is a holy river near by the temple.interesting thing is that people are burnt here after their death. if you are once here you should not forget to visit here.
pashupatinath is a temple of lord shiva, hindu religion. a really nice place to visit, its better to go in the early morning and evening for the nice views. covering with nature. highly recommended!

har har mahadev. foreigners can give this a miss. i believe foreigners have to pay an amount to enter the temple and can go only till a particular section. under the imposition of such condition, better not to visit. very dusty around. lot of construction going on. wouldn't have gone to the temple if i wasn't devout. from parking area to the temple, one needs to walk atleast 700 meters. it was difficult for my elderly parents but somehow they made it. wasn't harassed by pandas/ priests like how it happens in indian temples. neither is the temple area as dirty compared to temples back home in india. went around 2pm, so i guess wasn't too crowded. cannot even see the shivalinga properly as they didn't turn on the lights inside the sanctum sanctorum. however there was a sense of achievement after visiting the temple. guess us hindus feel that way upon completion of a pilgrimage. actually did not have the visit in my itinerary as was not intending to stay in kathmandu. but missed my flight to pokhara. so decided to go to the temple in the evening. but when i reached, the temple was closed. god wasn't interested to have me i guess. on my way back, i again tried to have darshan and was successful.
this is the place where you can find most amazing spiritual experiences. friendly people with rich culture

the temple is unique .with 4 sides thrown open for devotees to have a good look at the lord .the priests are kind but the main thing which draws me is the burning ghat behind it.and the small pieces of wood each old man and woman gets to work at outside the temple area so that there is never any dearth of wood for cremations . it is a unique culture and it works efficiently

on the bank of dirty bagmati river. in the centre of the city, old, crowded, non-managed,guarded by armed personnel, filthywith mile long cue of believers and stray monkeys and shiva in dark, damp, sacred interior of the temple of world famous nepalese architecture with intricate woodwork lattices. however the enchanted devotees will not allow you to stand still and appreciate the main temple's architecture by jostling for view of the god.

a powerful temple with vibrant energy. it is one of the prevedic temples with shiva in the form of pashupathi (one who had conquered the animal nature within oneself/ lord of all animals). whenever you visit this temple don't forget to visit the guheshwori temple with in the temple complex. behind the temple along the banks of bhagmati river i saw many deadbodies being burned which reminded me of kashi.there are hundreds of small shiva temples within the complex. the place is at the same time beautiful, peaceful and powerful. must visit.

it was very nice and beautiful place to visit,specially in evening time . because in the evening time the view is very nice coz of lights. specially for hindu people it was very nice temple to pray. there is big statute of lord shiva.
beautiful inside pashupati temple, really peace , everybody should visit this place at least one tume if they r in nepal
best place for make you feel relief.arti at might was awesonme.beautiful dance in bhajan.death body was bured there.epic place.highly recommended.
like many other temples of kathmandu ,this temple too is dirty and in an unclean environment. can be avoided.
before visiting kathmandu we read on the internet about it and were keen to visit this temple but the place turned out to be quite opposite. lot of filth, lot of crowd.very depressing.

absolutely divine. the place is well maintained and clean. we heard that there is entrance fee for indians but there was no fee and we had free darshan. must visit if you have visited kedarnath to complete your yatra.
thankful for the visit. historical site with a lot of value for hindus. an experience that is unforgettable.

frankly speaking chinese people not like visit the temple much, but i am curious on the cultural of the local people. i visited there with my chinese friend who visited the temple first time even she had stayed in the city many years. good place to know more on local people
historic temple of lord shiva also known as pashupati nath here. it's a hindu temple. great place of worship.

a large temple complex - almost a small varanasi. this is a famous shiva temple which is a world heritage site. you can't enter unless you are hindu but it is still worth exploring. the complex is large and the sight of cremations on the ghat are a revelation- please be respectful with your cameras

this is a must visit place for all. its a pilgrimage. you are fortunate to be at this place to see jyotirling of swyambhu shankar. a nice, well managed place, where a lot of people visit, hindus specially. don't treat it a tourist attraction, but if you want to see and feel divinity, hindu shraddha and devotion, you must visit. it is advisable that if you are visiting in morning, visit around 11 a.m., when you will be able to have special darhan / view of swyambhu god at around 12 a.m. for proper darshan and pooja, it is a good idea to engage a pandit / panda, who will properly show you the temple and perform pooja. i was charged rs. 11,00/- (nepali rupees) ( equivalent to about 700 indian rupees), but it is worth. if you are in kathmandu, its a must visit place. take care not to buy anything, specially rudraksha, as you will end up buying fake products. most of the rudraksha, which will be offered to you ( ek mukhi) are a farce. avoid buying. if you can't resist your temptation, at least bargain heavily. there is no standard in pricing, which are mostly multifold.
its a special divine experience when you visit pashupati nath temple at kathmandu. no recommendations or explanations needed. just go for it and experience the divine.
the temple to see in kathmandu. very historical and powerful temple of lord shiva. need to see to believe it

a world heritage site. the approach, temple were clean. well laid out. we had a good darshan and also had an opportunity to have a parikarama when the aarti was going on. the rear side needs improvement as the pond on the rear side was a bit dirty. a must visit place for hindus.

it’s probably the most interesting temple in kathmandu, because, in addition to the atmosphere and the temple itself, you can learn a lot about the culture as well. i would recommend taking a guide, there are many available at the entrance. the cremation ritual is a strong experience to witness but it shows a lot about the hindu faith. please do have respect and avoid taking pictures.

a place where lord shiva's origin was found as per puranas. a place all hindus must visit atleast once in their life time and if possible funeral need to be done at the back yard of this temple. one must attain salvation definitely by getting his / her funeral rights here.
pashupatinath temple is a sacred temple for hindus and holds historically important place. as you enter the premise you can feel the energy which is divine. must visit place when you are in nepal.
interesting funeral ghats, with cremations taking place. good areas to walk to see cenotaphs. watch out for the monkeys. non-hindus not allowed in the temple.

well planned, well organised temple for shiva devotees. no photography is allowed inside. the vibes permeating the compound is breath taking. stop awhile after pooja, sit at a corner, meditate and take it all in. truly joyful.

the one of the most famous and oldest shiva temples in the world. located quite close to kathmandu airport, an early departure while boarding your flight is most convenient way to visit. mobile/ cameras are not allowed inside and shoes to be left at counters before entering. evening aarti is at 5.30 pm. pujaris are quite helpful and not pastering type. there are shops outside temple to buy rudraksha mala and other mementos. we did not encounter much rush in last 2 visits and 1-2 hours was sufficient

a temple that must be visited by shiva loving devotees / hindus. 15-20 kms away from kathmandu town - not much queue - thousands of year old temple - art / nature lovers enjoy this temple ambience too, which very huge. religious minded people love this place.
unesco world heritage hindu temple with access to hindus only. for people only to understand its history as opposed to visiting to see. must visit place.

this is a temple where only hindus are allowed and photoography is strictly not allowed inside the temple, this place is full of visitors and a very beautiful temple inside and is well maintained and clean also, behind the temple there is ghat also for cremation. this temple is worth to visit loved to visit this temple and amazed also.
world heritage site. crowded place. not maintained very well. lot of dust around. some road construction activities were going on. but worth a visit.
full of visitors. very atmospheric. ancient, venerated and very sacred. queue to see the main idol. go on a non weekend/ holiday. burning ghats (for cremations) behind along the river - quite unique

imagine a river, low water and quiet one (bagmati, not basmati). old buildings, palm readers, monkeys. smoke, long hair ashes covered sadhus and spirit. dictionary said spirit is a being separate from the body, and that many religions believe continue to exist after the body dies or even the form of a dead person, similar to a ghost, or the feeling that a dead person is present although you cannot see them. pashupatinath is the hindu place where bodies are burned in order to set free the soul of deaths. very mystical location where you should watch and elaborate life's meaning. you should take a look as a living ghost, respecting people mourning. important hindu temple adjacent, not open for not hindus!

photography inside not allowed inside. only hindus allowed inside. it’s really good temple . if you believe in god you will feel very good inside. otherwise also it worth visiting the beautiful temple. you can cherish the architecture of temple. beautifully made temple . temple is clean and well maintained.

pashupatinath is not a good place to visit. world heritage site but extremely filthy and in bad shape. cow urine all over and smelly. there are pickpocketers around and criminals around the place where flowers are sold! beware! the place is best avoided!
one of the jyothirlinga, very divine and spiritual. very old temple. felt blessed by visiting this temple. the babas who pray inside the temple look very powerful and blessed. please do visit.

the main temple is not open to non-hindus, but behind it are the ghats where cremations take place. it's a real-life thing, but it's very intriguing to see how these cremations are done (obviously not the sadness of the relatives of the deceased). the fee for the ticket is quite high, but i think it's worth to see this.

we had our tour guide saroj from corporate adventure treks wall us through this area and explain it all to us. made me reflect on hope and the need for certainty after death. so interesting to see what was happening here for the local hindu people.

unesco world heritage hindu temple. this is a must do on all international traveler's kathmandu bucket list. only hindus are allowed inside the inner sanctum sanctorum. the outer area of temple is big & beautiful ancient architecture.
for the amount of visitors it has the temple is really clean and a must do for visitors. people who visit temples in india would find this to be a different experience.

one of those places you can not gets into unless you are a hindu; i understand and respect this, but why ask people to pay for the privilege of standing outside? the area is also filthy dirty, rubbish lying around and the sellers everywhere; someone has to sort this out, and quickly.
a lovely area which is still recovering from the devastating earthquake. this area is worth a visit, but will be better when the building work is completed

a must to visit this extensive temple although much of the area is restricted to hindus only. we went in the evening and suspect in the day suspect it will look tired and shabbie. we were actually there when hindu funeral ceremony in place where they burn the bodies by the river. yes it's a must visit but 1000r per person is excessive!
definitely, a must visit during day time. check the bhajan timings and do go accordingly. photos are a strict no, so you can skip carrying heavy camera gear if you are planning !

this in one of the 8 unesco heritage sites in nepal. this temple is dedicated lord pasupathi or 'lord of the animals', who is an incarnation of lord shiva. lord shiva is considered the national deity in nepal. this is the largest temple complex. i particularly enjoyed the bagmati aarti in the late evening. it is truly mesmerizing. it is performed by 3 priests simultaneously and some devotees dance the 'thandava', a cosmic dance by lord shiva. strange to see the aarati being performed on one side of the river bank whilst a dead body awaits cremation right opposite it.

well known this shiva temple has one of the uniqueness of the lord having a face that too in all four directions. crowded and can easily get convinced for special pooja at a cost . adjoining the temple is a crematorium which reflects realities of life.

an open hindu cremation place where people come to ritually say a final goodbye to their loved ones. it’s a must see for when you’re in nepal. especially around 7pm they have a ceremony on the other side of the bridge where they host a concert of singing and dancing people who are celebrating life. it’s the full circle of life.
yet another mandatory stop on the tour of the city. not necessarily a pleasant subject, but very interesting.

the main temple is situated very close to kathmandu international/domestic airport near the river bagmati. it is open from early morning to 2pm and then from 4pm to 6pm in december. mondays, friday and saturdays a bit crowdy in morning and evening. maximum time for darshan is within 30-45 minutes. foreigners are not allowed to go near the sanctum sanctorium.

it was interesting to see all that goes on there and to learn a bit more about the religion, but it's a very commercial place and none too clean! we did a walk through and observed a bit, but i was ready to leave as, honestly, it was a bit gross.
a good experience here. main temple area is busy. priest at temple is friendly and prepared to answer questions.

i visited the hindu shiva shrine, also recognized by most hindus as the head of most shiva temples, on two occasions: to seek blessings of the lord, and to observe crematory function of a relative. on both occasions my mind was fortunately stable and there was nothing but curiosity. pashupatinath temple is a beautiful pagoda style architecture, and in the inside the ''linga" at its top has four faces, of which one face has "ardha nareswar" depicting shiva and parbati conjoined--and emitting a message that both are one. since it has a notice stating that the temple is for hindus only, for others they can go around its back following the "bagmati" river and can witness cremation at the "ghats" as well as the temple architecture from outside. byapta

it is a beautiful temple dedicated to lord shiva. worth it if you can manage to go inside and see the grandeur and beauty of it. i feel humbled to have visited inside.quite a breathtaking experience. beautiful architecture and first hand experience of cremation too. sadhus are seen occupying certain spots as it was close to the shiva festival- mahashivratri.

in reviewing this temple it was highly recomended which must have been a local. tourists i highly recomend you avoid this place. 1000 rps round trip, 1000 rps per person to get in. told tour guides we didn't want one but, one said come here and we got thinking it was part of the high cost to get in. it was not he demanded 4000 rps. then 1000 rps for two necklaces to get someone to go away. $80 us dollars for a terrible trip. there is nothing here for tourists it is only a cremation site for locals. from the smoke and cost we went straight back to our hotel room. planned on make it a day seeing other sites in kathmandu but this was sickening and disapointing. i didn't even come away with one picture. once again, i highly recomend you avoid this place if you are a tourist.

as someone who grew up in a culture where we are pretty much shielded from the reality of death, this temple was quite the humbling experience. i didn’t understand why they don’t allow foreigners or even foreign hindus in the temple. that was the only negative against the place. walking around the grounds was great and then we stumbled upon an area where funerals/cremations were taking place. me and my mate had a good 5 minute debate about what we were looking at. when they went to re-kindle one of the fires and we saw the human skull, the debate was settled. there was something very weird but also very spiritual/enlightening about this area of the temple. i wasn’t prepared for what was going on, and it left me a bit shell shocked, mostly because they allowed pretty much anyone to view these ceremonies, take pictures, and tour around. i definitely recommend going, but do be mindful that people are mourning their loved ones.

this unesco awarded hindu temple complex includes the temple as well as cremation ghats. for the ghats there is literally a gallery with vendors selling drinks and foods for those watching families grieve for family their member being cremated. non hindus are not allowed into the temple - so not really sure what the hefty entry price is for. most temples do not charge a fee and permit entry to the temple.

this is a beautiful temple, and it was wonderful to be in the presence of so many devotees. unfortunately, this temple does not allow entry to westerners - not even westerners who are practicing hindus. i've been to hundreds of temples throughout india and never experienced this kind of discrimination. i asked nepali friends about this, and all weren't sure why this was the case, other than it was the tradition.

we took a taxi from boudanath stupa to visit pashupatinath temple for about 250npr. we were immediately approached by a man who was very polite and assisted us in paying for the entrance fee for 10usd per pax. initially we thought it would be another scam (it could have been) , but turns out that he was offering his guide services around at around 20usd for about an hour and a half around the site. at first we declined his services, but as we walked to the entrance of the temple, we realised that he offered information that was interesting and would make the expensive visit make more sense. we learnt that foreigners were not allowed into the main temple. only "true blue" hindus could enter and offer their prayers. but we we go around the area and take pictures at a respectful distance. the non-temple area was quite a large area to cover. we also witnessed several cremation ceremonies going on in the pashupatinath temple, along the river bank. we were enlightened by the guide on the procedures. the procession was a sight to behold. there were also religious men who lived in caves, whom offered tourists or devotees photogtraohy moments at a fee. there were also "religious" men who were offering palm reading services along side the river bank. apparently, a short segment of the dr strange movie was also filmed there. so that was interesting. all in all, we did not regret paying for the guide and was intrigue by this attraction.
one of the biggest events in kathmandu. it is absolut necessary to visit this place. a big area with the special crematorium

fascinating, beautiful, large hindu temple complex and cremating grounds located just in the outskirts of kathmandu, near the airport. considered the "local varanasi", here you can directly watch the funeral cremation ceremonies. don't miss it!
spent an hour or two on our half day tour. some great photo opportunities. there were a few passing away rituals happening. only down side was getting hassled to buy things.

quite a special experience, watching the cremations first hand. it is something you cannot see in many parts of this world, but it gets quite personal. we did it, and we do not regret it, but probably would not do it again. :)

what an experience, this is like nothing i've seen before. our hotel tried to talk us out of going when we did due to it being busy with a festival. we were so glad we went any way, the area was decorated and there were people everywhere. such an interesting experience. a couple of tips - as a foreigner there will be a guide at the entry desk and they will most likely just start talking with you and giving you information. i'd recommend you agree on a price prior to continuing (we got to the end and weren't sure if the price was fair). along the way you will see the sadhu's (painted holy men), if you take a picture with them - they expect to be paid. hopefully the money goes to the hospice like we were told. you'll also come across people selling jewellery and trinkets, if you have no intention of buying anything don't give and ounce of hope - no means maybe - maybe means yes. soak it up and enjoy the experience, note: it's a good idea to have a guide so they can inform you of where you are allowed and when it's appropriate to take photos to avoid offending.
the pashupatinath temple. pashupatinath is another name for lord shiva. he is believed to be protector and preserver all living creatures in the form of pashupathnath. int, 1979 ad, this temple

the place has a hindu temple that can not be accessed if you are is not hindustani, the guides take the foreigners to a grandstand in front of the crematory places. does it make sense for tourists to see the details of the preparation of the bodies and their cremation? this should be subjectonly to relatives and friends of the deceased and tourists should not be allowed to enter.
the must visit place for every hindu people. peace comes with the blessings of lord shiva. well built and maintained.

i had the privilege of witnessing my first body being cremated in traditional hindu fashion. the entry fee is steep for tourists at 1000nrp (nepalese get in for free) but once in, there are guides wanting to show you around. we had this one bloke tell us the basics of cremation and the ceremonies around the passing of a family member. he later asked us if we would like to pay for a more in depth understanding of the temple and it's significance another 1000), but we declined. he was a certified guide but we just enjoy looking around. witnessing the cremations was very heavy but if there's one thing i implore visitors to do, is to be discreet if you want to take photos. there were a few tourists who were getting amongst the ceremony trying to get a shot of those who have just lost a loved one, like it was a movie set or something. it was really disappointing that they had no respect for the people they were taking pictures of. i would hate having some tourists snapping away at a funeral i attended. so please be respectful and don't be trying to get the best close up of a lady's tears.

we went there to view this beautiful temple. the color were superb and the people were so very kind to us. we were not allowed to view the main temple as we are not of the hindu faith, however, we did witness a cremation. i am still processing that experience with so many unanswered questions. i am in complete awe of their religious activities. we walked the area that we were allowed to and it was peaceful. i recommend this highly.

hindu shrine on center of kathmandu valley. we learned about hindu culture, religions and sacraments on the bank of river bagmati. we were blessed to see live cremation and the purpose of it with buring.

the pashupatinath temple is suppose to be a famous and sacred hindu temple. what i experienced however is a an expensive (1000 rupees entrance fee for foreigners) filthy dump site. words alone cannot describe how filthy and disgusting this place is, so perhaps some photos will. if you are non hindu you are not allowed into the main temple. instead for your 1000 rupees you can just waddle around outside in the filth. as we left i caught a look at the sigh which says that the entrance fee goes towards keeping the temple clean. what an absolute joke kathmandu.
very good opportunity to check out a different culture for the treatment of recently dead bodies. crematories are working all day long. hinduist temple not allowed to visit for non-hinduist people.
just opposite to the hotel maharaja, walking distance. one can perform pooja just outside the temple. the can have a darshan. no one is allowed to touch the lingayat.

it is a huge temple and a must see when you visit nepal. they said that there are always bodies being burnt on the river bank. but we did not see that. although there was the smell of burnt bodies in the air. it is a beautiful and huge temple. some may find it a little difficult to cover the huge area bare footed. but it is definitely worth a visit.
last time i had visit there its nearest to airport, when you leave from kathmandu you can plan it. i had visit only 1 time

we were planning to go here. however when i saw the entrance fee of 1000 npr and you would not get to go inside the temple, i refused to pay that much. it would be ok if we could use our credit cards. i'm not sure if i wanted to see bodies being cremated either.
it was a really intense and interesting visit as there are cremations happening from dusk till dawn. definitely a good experience and seeing another way of dealing with death.

pashupatinath draws thousands of devotees every day. the architecture is splendid but the earthquake has caused widespread damage to the structures surrounding the main temple. there narrow and muddy river bagmati flows next to the temple complex.

i found this place fascinating. i would have liked to have seen more but restrictions on access to the temple apply. a little disappointing but i understand this because this is a very sacred place for hindus. i respect that. this leaves the main attraction therefore as the cremation area on the bank of the river. totally alien to western culture but clearly an important ceremony for hindus. i think it is well worth a visit to have an insight into their beliefs’. i really enjoyed it.

there is something in the air in pashpatinath. an aura of the ancient, timeless ceremonies focusing on the cremation of the dead occur on the ghats. we sit and watch. so does everyone else. it brings life and death instantly to our eyes, a powerful reminder of that which awaits us all, that which we often try not to focus on, that which we try to avoid. it's all there, the wooden funeral pyres, distraught relatives watching on all in a setting of chaotic beauty. often missed a walk to the top of the hill is a must where hundreds of tiny temples inhabit tree lined paths scattered with bells and monkeys. the cafe up there does very good momos. a trip to kathmandu really is not complete without an afternoon in pashpatinath. take your time, sit and watch let's the atmosphere take you forward or back in time under the tropical sun. beautiful

the complex is huge, entrance to the temple is only limited to hindus. no photos are allowed inside as they are many people praying at the various temples. we got a guide to explain the place. my opinion, it’s not necessary and a waste of money. it was very interesting to see cremation taking place by the river.

very old temple. abhishek part is performed outside the temple. there are four doors . but all the doors are not open thro. out the day. the lingayat has four sided face & at a time only three are visible & the fourth one is visible from its back side . the fifth face is on the top . need an hour .

this place is not as touristy as varanasi and it is very touching to watch the ceremonies of farewell, preparing the bodies, that afterwards will be burnt here. you are quite close, but on the other side of the river, so you don't disturb the griefing families in their goodbyes.

we went here on a very hot day in may. the guide we had was extremely informative and gave his own personal story about the process at pashupati nath. it's a little confronting to be in the presence of a cremation as it happens, but enriches your cultural appreciation of the nepalese people and their belief systems. well worth a visit.

while paying the 100 rupee entrance fee, i did not know what to expect. the first thing we encountered after all of the vendors at the entrance, and several sadhus, were the crematoria, where cremations were taking place out in public. this fascinating ritual is a very important one for the practitioners of hinduism, as the bagmati river eventually flows into the ganges. the shrines we encountered were numerous. there were many shiva linga shrines that lined up beautifully. we went high up to the viewing ridge of the eastern side of the bagmati. the smoke was thick in places and the monkeys were numerous but the beauty of the temple was evident. although we could not go to the western bank of the river, we were still able to view some of the temple complex. afterwards, we went around and approached the temple from the western side, but were still unable to walk in. amazingly, the earthquake of 2015 didn't seem to affect this temple very much.

while paying the 100 rupee entrance fee, i did not know what to expect. the first thing we encountered after all of the vendors at the entrance, and several sadhus, were the crematoria, where cremations were taking place out in public. this fascinating ritual is a very important one for the practitioners of hinduism, as the bagmati river eventually flows into the ganges. the shrines we encountered were numerous. there were many shiva linga shrines that lined up beautifully. we went high up to the viewing ridge of the eastern side of the bagmati. the smoke was thick in places and the monkeys were numerous but the beauty of the temple was evident. although we could not go to the western bank of the river, we were still able to view some of the temple complex. afterwards, we went around and approached the temple from the western side, but were still unable to walk in. amazingly, the earthquake of 2015 didn't seem to affect this temple very much.

usually crowded. but the darshan does not take a long time. visit in the evening during aarti time. there are no electric lights inside the sanctorum and only oil lights are lit. its a fantastic sight to watch. also see the majestic bhairav temple.

we were told that this hindu shrine is nepal’s oldest and holiest. the temple is revered by the nepalese people as this country has the highest percentage of hindus in the world. pashupatinath temple is a unesco world heritage site and honors lord shiva who is one of the three most important deities in hinduism. as non-hindus, we were not allowed to enter the temple but could see it from the outside and also witness the cremation ceremonies on the riverbank. this is something that most westerners have never seen before and takes some getting used to. as part of the culture here, it is to be respected and reflected upon. worth a visit.

we saw the temple from the other side of the river. only hindus are allowed to approach the 14th century temple. there has been a temple here for over 3000 years. we spent most of our time observing the cremation rites for people at the crematorium site along the river. when a local person dies, they are cremated within one day. the body is washed with the water from the bagmati river then covered with ceremonial wrappings. the eldest son or male of the family walks around the body on the pyre with the torch 3 times and then lights the fire in the mouth of the deceased. workers put more wood on and around the body and wet straw on top to increase the heat. the body burns for about 4 hours until it is dust, then the dust is brushed into the river. during the burning the eldest son’s head is saved except for a top knot.

pashupati nath, a unesco world heritage site, is one of the most holiest hindu temple in kathmandu. hindu god, lord shiva is worshipped there. foreigners are not allowed to enter inside the temple premises. they can get a glance from opposite the bagmati river. there's also a cremation place by the bank of the river. it's incredible there! the art and artefacts dates back to 15th century.

pashupati nath is one of the most holiest hindu temple in kathmandu. hindu god, lord shiva is worshipped there. foreigners are not allowed to enter inside the temple premises. they can get a glance from opposite the bagmati river. there's also a cremation place by the bank of the river. it's incredible there!
it is interesting place where dead bodies are cremated according to hindu beliefs. expect to get strong weird smells around it.

this is one of the huge temples visited so far. very nice experience of taking darshan of lord shiva. the shrine can be seen from 4 directions and offers excellent views. the entire premise has many temples associated with various gods like ganesha, hanuman, krishna, bhairav, shiva etc. huge nandi is seen on the entrance side after getting inside the premise. the other side faces a river side view. nearby area also houses thousands of small shiva ling swaroop. construction work was happening in the temple area. heavy crowd was experienced as it was a weekly off (saturday). ancient temple associated with pandavkalin story is conveyed here. the campus also has rudraksha tree and many shops to buy them. must visit and one gets a divine feeling after taking darshanam. om naham shivay
religiois place for visit. be aware of monkey. want to listen a aarati then go evening at 6.30pm.crowded on special ocassion.

be prepared for the entry fee (which i think was about 1000 rup each) but does not give entry to the main temple (hindu only). we did not take a guide and maybe in retrospect we would have learnt more but i am not one to listen to long stories and prefer to look around on my own. you can observe cremations on the river but a little weird watching the private ceremony's or others

a very active temple, packed with worshippers making a pilgrimage and those saying a final farewell. curious looking holy men surrounded by camera toting tourists. monkeys wandering around. all fascinating to watch.

incredibly interesting. we booked our kathmandu sight-seeing tour through himalayan sanctuary adventure ltd., and the guide they provided was wonderful. he walked us through the temple and explained everything. a wonderful stop. glad we did it!

when we reached the bridge on bagmati at 1830 on 4th nov . on the bank of bagmati opposite to pashupatinath temple one pious flame was dancing on the tune of jay jagdish hare whereas on the bank nearer to the temple another flame on the pyre was telling the universe was ‘ mithyaa ‘ . the first flame was associated with the aarati of pashupatinath . the aarati was offered to bagmati , pashupatinath and his consort parvati . three priests lined up on the bank of bagmati from where they could see pashupatinath temple . aarati , ultimate ritual of worship where four wicks soaked in ghee (purified butter) were lighted and the light offered to the almighty . the aarati started with worship to the panchayan deities – shiva , vishnu , durga , surya , and ganesh – . then three large brass deepmalas consisting of 54 deeps were raised in honour of lord pashupatinath . first , the priests lowered the deepmalas four times indicating they were worshipping the feet of the lord then rotated them seven times on the top suggesting they were worshipping the head of the lord . all these motions were in perfect synchronization . the atmosphere was full with the burning of incense , chanting of vedic mantras , lighting of oil lamps , playing of classical instruments , ringing of bells and sounds of singing hymns in praise of lord , after a while , nearly everyone got up and chanted shiva panchakshari strotram ( om na ma si va ya representing the 5 elements of nature ) , the end of the aarati was marked by hailing for the wellness of every living soul in the universe and thanking the lord for the kindness bestowed by him . the aarati started regularly on every evening from 2006 . during winter , aarati starts at 1800 and continues upto an hour . while the aarati was going on , on the opposite bank dead corpse was brought by his relatives . whereas in india women never go to the crematorium to witness the cremation , here i saw women coming to see the departed soul ( dead body ) for the last time . again another surprising custom i noticed was the grass bales were washed in bagmati and then used to light the pyre . this was the second flame on the another bank of bagmati .

this is one of the most intriguing places i've ever been to anywhere in the world. this is one of the holiest hindu sites in all of nepal. it is definitely not a place for the faint of heart, as people cremate their loved ones for everyone to see.

i have traveled all over the world, seen countless of unique and amazing sites, but thos had to be right up there. this is one of the most holiest site for hindus in nepal. this is where they take their loved ones to be cremated, and pay their final respects. we sat there for about one hour and couldn't believe what we were looking at. just breathtaking and a must see.if you can stomach it.

stopped there for a few hours and just couldn't believe what we were witnessing. this is one of the most holy hindu temples in all of nepal, and this is where people bring their loved ones to be cremated. a once in a lifetime experience.

1000 rpn entrance fee for foreigners. if you can prove you’re hindu or indian it’s free (ie. passport or citizen card or some sort of identification). non-hindu people are not allowed in the main temple. there isn’t much to do in the area. witnessing the cremations would be the biggest highlight, but i personally thought it was weird that such a private matter (mourning the death of a loved one and preparing their cremation ceremony) was publicized at a unesco world heritage site, where tourists are snapping photos and exploring around. anyway, it’s definitely once in a lifetime experience but i didn’t think it was worth 1000 rpn. it can be emotional seeing the bodies and cremation ceremonies. it’s sad to see the river, where the ashes are poured in, is completely polluted with plastic bottles and garbage which are mainly due to the local children who play in and around the river.

third most important shiva tempel in the world. burial site for kathmandu residents. unesco heritage site. expensive for tourists but a must. near airport and buddha bodnath stupa. nice combination to do. early morning is best time to go for burials and onward visit to bodnath with good restaurants and views.
best religious place to visit and it consists most ancients temples which you definitely love to see.

i went to this temple twice, you should try to come around sunset, then you can see the temples and the beautiful buildings, and you can stay to see the evening ceremony, blessing the river. it is beautiful to be able to experience this much culture, and something as private as a cremation, in this beautiful culture.
the most holy place for hindus from nepal and india. similar to varanasi. the burning of the dead bodies happens in public.
we went to kathmandu during dashera festival in the month of october. to see the temple and pashupatinath, we were amazed and it was truly hindu pride

it’s one of the holiest and most important hindu temple in nepal. very much a spiritual and non-touristic place. good to see that they have preserved it as a peaceful place of worship for hindu community instead of selling out as a tourist attraction. for tourists best time to visit is during the arati around 6-7pm.

the entrance fee to this temple was very exorbitant at rs1000. however, i found it worth it because i got to witness a hindi cremation ceremony first-hand. i went down to get a closer look and saw how they dressed the deceased and how his family and the priest went about the ceremony. it was long - around 1 hour. you can't take photos or videos of the cremation ceremony up close though. you can see the holy river too and where they cremate the bodies in fire and throw the ashes into the river. there are also some monkeys here. as a tourist, you can't go inside the main temple of shiva so that's disappointing for how much you paid.
a spectacular place to visit on the banks of the bagmati river where there is a sacred hindu temple and cremations take place on funeral pyres on the river bank.

came here with a guide. was keen on the open cremation and while i was there there were 3 taking place. these happen all day and all night. very local and unique experience. do be quiet and respectful though. compound is peacefully and quiet for a nice walk about. sadly foreigners cannot enter the hindu temple. definitely worth a visit.

this is one of the most popular temples of lord shiva around the world. once in the temple, all you can feel are positive vibes. positivity all around. definitely a must visit if you’re in kathmandu. note: non-hindus are not allowed inside the temple. nonetheless, they can purchase a ticket for npr 1000 and enter the complex to see the temple from outside.

the most important hindu temple in nepal is worth a visit for everyone. be prepared to see and smell bodies being cremated, that's quite unusal for westerners and might be shocking for some people. even though it's a little dirty most of the time, it's a wonderful and fascinating place. to get out the most of it, i recommend to take one of the officials guides at the entrance. this time we were a group of 7, each paying 300 rupies for a tour of 2,5 hours (we gave substantially more because we were very impressed). the guide provided a wealth of information around hindusim, buddhism and nepali traditions.
good religious place for hindus but the approach road is in shambles.inside the temple there was not much rush on the day of my visit

this is a must see place. it has an amazing atmosphere. it's a place where you can spend any hours. don't miss evening aarti, it's just awesome. the rituals, the chanting, small temples.all make this space special. i visited pashupatinath 2 times in few days and i'm gonna visit it again before leaving.

pashupatinath is an incredibly important site for nepali, being a religious site of observance for over 1000 years. here a series of different aged temples line the river where ghats smoulder with funeral pyres. as a functioning cremation site there are funerals occurring continually at the site; so expect to see cremains, pyres, and sacred purification rituals occurring along the river banks. foreigners pay npr 1000 each that goes toward maintenance and restoration as some temples were damaged in the 2015 earthquakes. the day we visited the palls of smoke were recognisable on the walk to the entrance and the ashen smell will follow you throughout the visit. fascinating to observe the hindu and buddhist faith as experienced through their mourning. challenging, and fascinating site.
it is holy place for all the beings intelligence. actually i can not say it is the temple only for hindu but for all beings.

i expected a lot. and i saw a not very clean temple complex. foreigners are not allowed into the main temple - fair enough - that's also why you don't have to take off your shoes (someone could've told us before we did. they actually took our shoes and gave us the number, but didn't tell it was unnecessary!). then you take a stroll to the other side of the river, you can see people burning bodies of the deceased. it all looked dirty though - the river, the bank, the stairs. everything. then there are monkeys - be careful, they're aggressive and i saw one attacking a woman to get her shopping bag. there are also cows. so there's cow poop. and people walking there barefoot. it was pretty gross. it's a pity because it's an interesting place, and if it was better taken care of (and for 10$ a ticket it really should!), it would be an amazing attraction.

for a westener following at a close distance the rites of burning the dead is a somewhat disburbing but also exhilarating experience and at pashupatinath you get that opportunity amidst holy men posing for photographs and monkeys in droves playing in and around the river. visit to the hindu temple is not allowed for non-hindus and the area other than the actual burning sites on the river is a waste of time. the 1,000 rupees entrance fee is not justified compare to other sites in kathmandu.

a trip to see the temple and its surroundings. non hindus are not allowed inside the actual temple so our visit only involved outside views. surrounded by colourful stalls the actual temple and cremation site sit right on the holy river. we saw several cremations taking place in what is a very public show of grief. further up the hill are numerous shrines to shiva with trident symbols and also phalic representations. holy cows and monkeys abound the holy men caused concern in one of our party especially as they rubbed human ash on themselves very interesting visit

this temple, on the banks of the rather dirty bagmati river, overlooks the cremation area for hindus . one can see the funeral pyres along the steps leading down to the river. while non- hindus are not allowed into the temple, its surrounding and images of the interior are impressive. the entrance fee for tourists is 1000 nepali rupees. you can buy fruit at the entrance to feed to the numerous monkeys who live in the trees surrounding the site.

this is a large hindu temple location which is spread over a large area. its on the outskirts of kathmandu. be prepared to see cremations taking place which for western cultures may be a little disturbing but this is the culture and how these events happen.interesting place. cremations tend to occur in the morning and early evening so you can either time a visit to be there or avoid as desired

we were destined to meet the lord pashupatinath that evening.the temple closes down at 7 pm, and we reached there at 7.10 pm. there was a motley crowd and people were being asked to leave the temple premises. do not know why, the guard asked us to hurry and visit the lord, before he went into rest. words fail me in describing the satisfaction that we got after "meeting" the pashupatinath. the temple is very clean and was well lighted. we also got to witness the aarti across the river, from the ramparts of the temple. what else could we ask for ?

we did a 4 stop tour in kathmandu and for me this was the best one. although lots of earthquake damage still exists it’s one of the places where it still feels very connected to the hindu religion and not be over run with tourist traps and shops. there were a few cremation ceremonies whilst we were there which was very interesting to watch. get a guide you can explain to you what’s what as there is very little in the way of information or directions inside.
hindu only temple of lord shiva. not a jyotirlinga but of same importance. try sitting on the platform of the temple at the backside near the bank od river. you will feel just peace.
shiva is calling listen the silence within u.it was a motorcycle trip to nepal and during mooson . one of the divine and sacred land of shiva in land of buddha.

this is a really interesting place to visit to get some understanding into how the local people manage the last rites of their dead and you may need to be prepared as you will witness the bodies being cremated by the river. aside, there are multiple temples, interesting buildings and monkeys to see. definitely a place worth visiting.

lord shiva’s abode in himalayas. this temple is a historical place with many generations of nepali kings and its people associated with it. it has a great vibration and you instantly feel calm and peace in the temple vicinity. the temple tends to be crowded so be ready for some line. there are local guides though , who can help you take you inside with ease . next to the temple , on the bank of a river one can see hindu crematorium where last rites are performed as per hindu religious system. be aware of touts and pick pocketers while visiting. .

if you are not a hindu then you are not allowed to see the inner part of the temple but can just look from outside. the external part is impressive and the adjacent cremation ghats on the banks of bagmati river can be visited by non-hindus. it is a personal choice whether you go to see people being cremated or not. it is a macabre place though. however, there are many smaller temples and these are dedicated to lord shiva in the same complex.

as a non hindu you cannot enter the actual temple but can wander the outer grounds. if you go up around the left side you can kind of look down into the courtyard. behind the temple down a road by the side you can see the cremations. pretty sobering stuff. beware the money grubbing babas. they are opportunists, not holy men! a guide also tried to jump me us at the gate with his foreign friendly charm. i shook him off as i prefer to go at my own pace.

while we weren't allowed into the temple itself because we are not hindu, we were able to walk the grounds. we were witness to many priestly offerings to worshipers, as well as a couple religious cremations that touched us deeply. it is definitely a profound experience!
the temple complex is huge and in the middle of the city. there’s always a rush for the evening aarti. it’s one of the most revered shiv temple in hindi religion.

mr.keshav dahal was very accommodating and organized a whistle stop for our short stay. our guide driver top notch. we would love to return and further explore the wonders of kathmandu and i’m sure we will be contacting dahal to organize any guided trips.
preparation for cremation takes place on one side of the bridge and cremation on the other. son of the deceased spends 13 days, isolated in the temple grounds as a grieving ritual.
its a little creepy if you can't understand the rituals of cremation. funeral pyres and bodies being prepared for cremation are quite visible, but it is a holy place for saying goodbye to loved ones.
as a usa citizen and resident, i've never seen anything like it. it is shocking! the public burial ceremonies were incredible.

he is like i said already in the title one of the most important hindu-temple in nepal. he is lying a little outside of kathmandu, near the boudhanath stupa. like a lot of hindu-tempels you are as not hindu not allowed to go inside. but on the other side of the temple you still have a good view. he is also famous for the cremation. we were there on a monday at about lunch time and they were many people there for the cremation. to have an inside look of the hindu-world this temple is a good start.
clean temple, heritage site, you can see the evening aarti, it is a sight to behold. beware of monkeys.
there are live funerals occurring here, you may breath in the smoke of cremations that can be observed here at the headwater of the ganges.
with some locations restricted to hindus, the complex can be walked through, and funeral rights can be observed from the opposite bank of the river.
this place is highly revered by the natives and has so many stupas and monuments to visit. i really enjoyed the architecture.

visited as i wanted to meet the holy people; this is what i was told. first they did not want to let my friend in (nepali) as he was not a tour guide and thought he was going to provide me with info. more to the point that they want to rub their tour guide off you. after much discussion they let him in. the tour guide did not feel honest to me, as he was too much concerned about money. to get a picture with the "holy men" you need to pay. as i sat next to them, giving me blessings. they whisper 2,000 for all okay. then the next 1,000 more okay. this made the experience less authentic. you can sit and watch cremation of bodies, as they have on average 25 per day. this was morbid but interesting to hear about what happened and how the ceremony will take place. i recommend to explore by yourself and don't trust the tour guide.
it is group of temples with main temple of shiva. it closes at 6.30pm and after lunch 1pm to 4pm. you can not enter the mail temple but see it from the door only.
the many religious influences and constant odour of death permeates one's sensibilities making this a unique experience.

this is basically a public cremation site. bodies are brought out, prepared for burning and set on prepared platforms for cremation. smoke fills the air. we did not find it peaceful. we did find it interesting and informative with a native guide i hired beforehand. the river is polluted, there are lots of vendors constantly asking for business, and the monks want money if they even think they might have inadvertently gotten into a random photo of yours. of course, this is how it is at many temples and tourist sites in kathmandu.

the place is well visited, and it is a quite interesting way they send off their dead relatives. pay 500-1000 rupees to get a guide to explain what is going on and the reason they do it this way. it’s quite interesting. it is not considered rude to film og take pictures. women do not have to cover themselves to visit.

whilst this was an amazing insight into local life and clearly a large part of their culture, as a westerner i felt uncomfortable with the obvious death. there were bodies everywhere at different stages of preparation and the locals weren't phased, i just felt uncomfortable.
was so happy after may 20 years to again visit pashupati nath temple. just an very ancient spot for hindu devotees that has its own spiritual charm.
this is the place where i realized the priorities in life. compared it with the meaningless things we run after. also visited the ghat where cremation was taking place. this place touched my heart.
this is the place where i realized the priorities in life. compared it with the meaningless things we run after. also visited the ghat where cremation was taking place. this place touched my heart.

fascinating site. the temple is perched next to a hill that is a nice walk to get a glimpse of the entire area - including the cremations. it is certainly an experience walking up the hill watching a cremation while lovers canoodle and kids play guitar, laugh and enjoy themselves. it definitely took me out of my comfort zone was but was fascinating, interesting and interesting insight into religious life in nepal. do not miss it!

with the help of guide purchased ticket for panch teerth abhishekam of lord pasupathinath. in spite of crowd abhishekam was done religiously and had good darshan from all sides. huge temple complex with many deities to visit. nice architecture and pious atmosphere.

a revered place of pilgrimage. the notice outside clearly says entry for hindus only, however no one seems to be checking, nevertheless due respect to the sentiments of the hindus, no foreigner seen entering the temple complex. like all great religious places here too, the mandir priests try and push you through the 'darshan' as fast as possible. have a contented darshan ! offer your prayer! and feel the solace of being at pashupatinath mandir !. a must visit place for all hindus visiting kathmandu!
this has been declared as un world heritage site. it is a great holy temple of hindu people must see in kathmandu

non hindus cannot go into the temple complex, but you will be able to see the decorative entrance doors. guides will approach you and not leave you unless you address the issue at the outset. if you do not want a guide, you will need to be resolute, patient and firm, but ultimately your profuse apologies will persuade them that you will not waiver and they will go off. of course we may have missed out, but that was our choice. after seeing the entrance doors you may wonder what you have paid the entrance fee for. well, head back as if going out and turn down the path on your right. at the end is the entrance to the riverside. cross over the bridge and go to the left or right to (respectfully) view the burning ghats on the other side. you can spend quite a while watching as the rituals are quite slow, but it will also give you pause for thought about your own impermanence (which is always a good thing)!

this place is just amazing, and an absolute must-see if you’re in kathmandu. it’s quite expensive to get in (1000npr) and you can’t visit the main temple, but there’s still plenty to see. you do need to be careful with a few things, though. after paying the fee, a guide will start giving you directions and following you around, giving you a nice guided tour of the place, which lasts for about 45 minutes. it isn’t until the end that he says something along the lines “if you’re happy then i’m happy”, which is where he expects you to pay him. i asked him how much he expected, and he said “20$”. obviously that’s way too much, and clearly not what you should pay. he will also try to get you to pay 30$ to take photos of “authentic” holy men. again, ridiculous request. there are also a lot of women trying to sell you some tacky jewellery in a very insistent way. overall, i did enjoy the place, but i wish i had told the guide not to follow me around, as it really made the experience much worse. everybody is nice at a price. i think that’s important to remember when visiting kathmandu.

a must visit temple in kathmandu. the temple is listed on unesco world heritage site due to its value and importance. many hindu devote comes here to worship lord shiva. the temple has many priests and holy man (baba).

it is a great holy temple of hindu people which is listed on the unesco world heritage site. huge temple full of devotee. in special occasion and festival thousands of devotee comes to worship the god and do the ceremony. the temple has a holy river bagmati flowing from the side where funeral ceremony is done as well. it is very close to the international airport.
all hindus who come to nepal goes to lord shiva temple. a holy place for hindu religion the usual time of the arti is 6.00pm daily

the main temple here is just for hindus. as other reviewers have pointed out you can see dead bodies being cremated which i found very difficult to see. i also felt sad to see other tourists taking photos, imagine if someone turned up to your loved ones funeral and took photos, dark tourism, and so if you read this maybe think that's it's not juat a tourist attraction.

a must visit for devout hindus at least once in their lifetime.for tourists from abroad it is advisable for them to walk to the back of the temple and from a vantage point can view the rituals being performed at the banks of the bagmati river.the funeral pyres are to the left from this view point.at night the arti takes place from here and it’s a must.the usual time of the arti is 6.00pm daily and lasts about an hour.

this un world heritage site is literally a must-see for many hindus, in order to gain spiritual fulfillment (along with varanasi). if you cross the bridge behind the temple and climb the stairs, you will find a respectful viewing area to watch cremation ceremonies of the high-ranking deceased (there are other slabs further up the river for the common folks). but if you continue up the stairs, you will find rows of stupas that are quite pretty (you'll also frequently find sadhus here who will pose for pictures for money), and then a monkey road that leads down to a back gate exit. the grounds are quite large so you may wish to get a guide (such as pink mountain) before going there.
all hindus who come to nepal must visit here to get blessings from lord siva! just go inside and get peace!

lord shiva"s big themple it is called pashupati nath .many many small temples are there .kailas parbat ,bagmati river,also near by there. when people died his or her dead body is burning fire is in there .

hindu temple can not be enterd by non hindu’s. interesting complex with lost of dogs and apes. it becomes disturbing once you realise that you are an uninvited guest to someone’s cremation. watching the ceremony is intriguing and cultural interesting, but also quite unpleasantly intimate and emotional. a religious gathering of hindu’s with spectators on the side is a strange uneasy combination
the experience is "different" - you get to see the cremation of people who passed away, but this experience is not for everyone, especially when several cremations take place in parallel.

this hindu shrine dedicated to lord shiva is visited by thousands of devotees during the month of shivaratri festivals.otherwise too all hindus complete their everyday ritual by visiting this holy shrine.

this is one of the most popular shiva temple across globe. photography not allowed inside temple premises (i wrongly clicked one) . there is a giant brushabha brass statue inside the temple premises. built by the royal this is well maintained and architecture is best. many devotees and tourist visited the temple.

this is one of the four main hindu worship places . only hindus are allowed inside. if you want a quick entry you can buy rs1100 worship coupon and separate line to enter. then is valid till 11 am. no photography allowed.

this is a unesco world heritage site but is sill very active. when we visited and i presume everyday there were several cremations going on. it is a little shocking to western eyes. it was a glimpse into another culture that is memorable.

arrived on wednesday 04/07/18 with my driver & tour guide & admission fee nr1,000 this was certainly an experience in respecting the hindu way in preforming human cremations & the peshupatinath temple area has been following traditions for hundreds of years. certainly an unusual attraction for tourists keeping in mind we must at all times respect other cultures & what they believe in after death.
a large temple that has ghats, similar to varanasi in india. the temple and the ghats are interesting to visit. allow a few hours to see and take in the atmosphere of the place.
this place is so magical. it was the highlight of our visit and definitely not to be missed if you are in kathmandu, especially at dusk. really an amazing palce

pashupatinath, famous and popular iconic hindu temple in kathmandu. a must visit for every traveller, though foreigners and other sect beleivers are not allowed to enter, they can watch some of rituals been activated from outside. as most of other religious places this also has too much of positive vibes cleaning your aura as soon as u enter the premises. must visit place in kathmandu.
coming from ny i was pleasantly surprised to witness so many intimate rituals being performed here. it's a must see when in kathmandu. i recommend getting a guide, unless you are hindu.

pashupati nath is a must see in my opinion, seeing the cremation ceremony at first was a bit of a cultural shock i guess you would say but once you look at the ceremony as a whole it real is a beautiful thing.

the place is nicely managed and it is easy to do darshan but there is a big problem in the information advertisement. neither the nearby stalls nor the guards knew what time would the temple would open. we had to wait long hours for few seconds of darshan.
kathmandu has a lot to offer and after while you may not to wish to see more temples etc however this one is worth it.
a world heritage site, famous for grand temple dedicated to lord shiva. a sacred place for hindus who come here for worshipping from all places around the world. a place must to visit for tourists.

may be a world heritage site but i will not dare to go back again. my father inlaw took a photo by mistake ( he was not able to read the hindi or english signs ). once they approched us we deleted the photo. however some local guides were extremely rude with us ( probably becasue we did not take any guide ) and brought three different security guards to check his phone. even after we showed them every single folder inside his basic phone they were not satisified. their attitude was also intimidating and threatening. would not dare to go back again.

at first sight, you do not really realize what is going to come. as soon as you enter the premises of the temple, you will simply lose yourself in an experience which cannot be written in words. the place is teeming with devotees wishing to have a glimpse of lord pashupatinath, the presiding deity. you are first greeted by a huge stone bull called nandi, who is the lord's vehicle. one is quickly reminded of thanjavur temple in southern india. the restlessness of waiting in the rather long queue quickly disappears at the very sight of lord. as you circumambulate the temple, an unusual and even shocking sight greets you. when you peep over the compound wall, you will find at least 5 to 7 bodies being burnt and more waiting in queue. surprisingly, there is not an iota of bad smell associated with corpses and the whole area is filled with the smell of pepper. for non-hindus, this is an unusual sight and i would advise them not to photograph the corpses or the grieving families. afterall, everyone goes through this and one must respect their privacy. be careful of teeming pandas, a typical sight in many temples, who will keep promising you of many things for a sum. remember, you will only be taken for a big ride as there are simply no short cuts. nobody is allowed to touch the main deity, so do not fall for this scam. do not miss the evening aarti which begins at 7 pm. i would advise you to be there at least by 6.30 pm. overall, an experience you must not miss, particularly if your soul needs solace.
me and my guest went their to see the biggest hindu temple of nepal . we could feel those fresh air and good vibes. i recommend everyone to vist there .
it is one of a must-visit temples in kathmandu. if you’re lucky, you’ll get to see the cremation ceremony of dead people during your visit.

pashupatinath lies on the banks of bagmati river and this hindu temple precinct consists of temples, ashrams, inscriptions and images. boudhanath area, which is just 5 mins drive away from pashupatinath, is rich in tibetan culture within kathmandu and is home to more than 50 tibetan monasteries (gompas).

this is a special place. it’s not the most interesting or beautiful but this is where they clement the bodies of the dead. it has a special effect on you when you these ceremonies. the place itself is nice but you cannot visit the temple. it’s a point still not to miss

pashupantinath temple is located in north-east of kathmandu the capital of nepal. it is one of unesco world heritage sites in kathmandu. it is most popular for "shivaratri". shivaratri is one of major festivals for hindu devotees. shivaratri is held every year in winter season and on that occasion, over 2 million of nepalese and foreigner devotees visit the temple. in addition, it is funeral spot for hindu people and they believe to do that in this temple. it reflects traditional arts, custom, and culture. there are ancient inscriptions carved on the rock built for temple and useful buildings.

lovely temple and a must visit for hindus. very much a spiritual and non-touristic place, good to see that they have preserved it as a peaceful place of worship for the local community instead of selling it out as a tourist attraction.
it’s one of the holiest and most important hindu temple in nepal. it can be visited anytime but the best time to visit is during ‘aarathi’ at 6.30 pm.

i feel really blessed visiting pashupatinath temple. one day watching tv, i made up my mind to visit shivji temple and all of a sudden pashupatinath temple comes on the tv. it was like a sign for me.om namah shivay.
i sugest all hindus to visit this sacred temple which is situated at the bank of bagmati river. creamiation ceremony is another attraction to see
pashupatinath temple, sacred place for shaiva hindus is located atvthe bank of bsgmati river. you can observe the creamatory system and process of hindus.
it's a must visit in kathmandu. great spiritual place indeed. it was my first place to visit and i thoroughly enjoyed it.
if one is visiting kathmandu, nepal, one cannot help but visit pashupati nath. the feeling of serenity one gets when visiting here in remarkable.

the place is interesting due to its significance to those of the hindu faith along with its age, but there is not a ton to see. it is definitely worth seeing, but it does not take that long to see even with a guide. some might feel uncomfortable here with bodies being cremated in the open, so keep that in mind.

on my recent visit to nepal, my first visit, going to pashupatinath temple was a given. one of the 12 jyotirlingas or 12 abodes of bhagwan shiva is a mandatory visit for hindus. an ancient temple and a most prominent among shiva temple was an abode peace and piety. the temple on banks of bagmati river is a sharp contrast to similar temples we see in india. place is clean, quiet, serene and well managed. the entrance through a massive gate forwards you to not take any photographs inside the temple. something that i missed noticing, i dared take a snap of the massive statue of nandi, the ride of bhagwan shiva. and a temple guard promptly asked me to delete the photograph. barring that, the temple queues are well managed, orderly and quiet. it was a very humbling e perience. temple closes at 7 pm in evening with a aarti on river bank. i unfortunately missed that due to some confusion. but this is as grand as the one you see at haridwar or varanasi

hindu temples mostly dedicated to shiva on the banks of the bagmati river where cremations are held daily, connected to a park, many steps, extremely interesting and spiritual even if you are not religious. world heritage site, not to be missed.

it was a different and moving experience to watch hindu cremation rituals held at the bagmati river. the main temple is strictly for hindus only. one can ascend staircase on the opposite temple complex and have the aerial view of the pashupatinah temple complex. there are holy men and monkeys everywhere, watch out for your personal stuff. as this is a worship place, wear appropriately.

pashupatinath is on the banks of the river bagmati and is a prime site for cremations. it is worth trying to ensure that you visit when cremations are taking place as it gives a fascination insight into an aspect of hinduism not normally witnessed. there was evidence of earthquake damage and the 1000 rupee charge to get into the temple has probably been levied at that level to help with rebuild costs.

it is expensive for foreigners to enter. but worth it to experience hindu cremation. i know it sounds ghoulish but it was an interesting cultural experience. it was hard to watch a family saying goodbye to a loved one, then the body being burned. if you are squeamish, don't go. many guides will approach you offering their services. we chose not to get one. we walked around the area, saw the monkeys, holy men. all in all, it was a must see.
unusual experienced i ever had, the spiritual vibe is so strong but i was really enjoy it. we have to respect the rituals. the ritual start around 5pm. enterance fee 1000npr. wearing 'close' clothes.

don't be deterred by this place being a funeral pyre site. and above all respect the mourners. i saw tourists gawking at the bodies and taking photos within a couple of feet away. this is unacceptable. you can take photos from the bridge over the river with a zoom lens. be aware that most of the people who want to pose for photos demand exorbitant fees. check out what is appropriate

i wasn’t sure what to expect from pashupatinath but was no disappointed. the temple itself looked nice inside from what i could see looking in but unable to go in unless you are hindu. however we were able to watch the hindu cremations on the river which was fascinating and moving to see. it is a privilege to be invited to see this part of their culture. beautiful surrounding shiva shrines up the steps surround by trees. some were quite dilapidated and over grown with vegetation giving it a mysterious but peaceful atmosphere. definitely worth the visit, however, expect some hassle from local guides. 1000 rupees
