this temple is one of the holiest for hindus. legend has it that when lord shiva used to take the shape of a deer and graze around the bhagwati river, the locals caught wind of it and tried to catch him. they caught his tail and the remaining body flew to another realm. it is on those remains that this temple is built. you'll experience a divine sense of calm once you enter the temple complex. even those who are not extremely devout will feel a strange magnetism towards the 4 sided idol placed in the centre of the main temple. before that, a giant nandi bull made of brass welcomes you as the protector of the temple. the complex has many small other temples including one of lord bhadra, lord vasuki and lord hanuman. the local temple administration does not permit non hindu's to enter the main complex and they're allowed only till the outer gates and on the other side of the river. even then, it's a must visit if you're visiting kathmandu.
lovely place to visit. u feel great with peach of mind . feel you are in safe hand and would like to come again and again.
most famous temple around. not too far from the main location. market outside is a little mess and not so clean. visit in and around the temple to find great view.

visited this place with a group of friends, and we were accompanied by a tour guide. our visit was in the afternoon i believe it was a thursday. there was a lot of activity going on at the entrance. there were crowds of people, mostly locals who appeared to have come for worship. most had red markings on their foreheads. there was a procession walking towards the main temple, a young girl was being carried under an ornate umbrella, she was dressed in decorative red and was probably a kumari devi. there were also men who were dressed up in drag with wigs and wearing saris and make up, dancing, travelling with the procession. the large nandi bull shivalingham statue was visible to us from inside the main temple, who were viewing it from outside. only hindus are allowed into the main temple therefore, we bypassed it and went to the side of the temple where we could watch ceremonial cremations taking place at the banks of the baghmati river below. there was a dead body wrapped in cloth, laying on a stretcher. the guide explained that the feet of the corpse will be first inserted into the water. this is to allow the corpses's spirit to leave the body through its feet. he explained that in hinduism there is a belief the spirit has a desire to stay with the mortal body, so it is gradually made to leave the body and into the river. saw some mourners beside the body. a crying female who was apparently the widow of the deceased, was being consoled by a group of women. the time had not yet come for the body to be cremated therefore we walked away. but from our viewpoint from above we saw a pyre closeby which looked like it had just finished another cremation, judging by the smouldering embers. it was an interesting experience. very serene, and not morbid. there is no admission fee as it is a temple. photographs are not allowed to my knowledge.
would love to see more, but unfortunately, non-hindus are not allowed into the inner temple area. in some areas, we were shoo-ed out of the temple compound.
excellent darshan. the temple has a very high attraction and has a soothing effect when you are inside. very old temple and very neatly maintained.
highly religious place. lord shiva's temple. very beautiful temple. we are blessed to get opportunity to visit the temple and make prayers.

spiritual energy is very palpable within the temple grounds. our guide was very knowledgeable on hindu religion and what makes this temple a must visit by a hindi devotee. the traditional cremation along the river was very somber. while photos can be taken, one has to be respectful of the rituals and the actual ceremony being held.

maybe it seems a little scary to be there at first, but it’s completely wrong. it’s one of the most different experience that i have had. it’s a great and unbelievable ceremony of death! the ceremony starts from near sunset till about 2 hours later. people sing and dance and monks pray for the souls all during the ceremony.
the trip here was very special. so glad to have made it here. while the temple itself is stunning, this is one of the most sacred temples.
this is an absolutely stunning and peaceful place. a must visit for everyone. the energy the temple has is special.
pashupatinsth is well worth the effort. we had hired a guide who was very experienced and speak english very good. this temple where hindus come to released for lacm or beetter words. nice temple.

.you don't see everyday. yea, i kinda knew what was going on here before i came, but wow, it really hits you seeing it. in a way more interesting than funeral/cremation ritual itself is that there are so many "spectators" (more local than foreign) here watching it. in half a way a kind of carnival atmosphere. i saw two bodies cremated, one from the start of the ritual dipping in the river. it is hard to describe everything i saw here - just see it yourself. by the way, it's 1000 rupees admission, and you don't even get to go in the temple - hindus only.

cleaner than lasdt year. great temple but lack of information on poojas opening hours. admin staff are not interested in selling /canvssing for poojas office opening hours do not align with temple opening hours.

when i visited at around 3:00 pm serprisingly not many people because main shrine was closed. finally we foundout it opens at 5;30pm. there was a price list for pooja at admin office but the staff were not interested in selling them. finally my nepali friend got some information on the pooja details. went to book but was told no atm or electronic transfer facility except for donation. we went back with money at 5:30 pm when the temple opened. there were no staff at the office. they said the office closed at 4:pm so it appears no pooja in the evenings other than aarathi at the river. i could not understand the office hours. counters open when temple is closed and counter closed when temple open. it appears they discourage performing poojas. temple is cleaner this time than last year
you get great divine vibration. please do not miss the evening aarti. ensure you have sufficient time to go around the temple as the temple complex is very big.

go to temple after 09:30am on a working day to avoid crowd. (monday is holiday) you will have crowd on holidays/festivals. don't get caught up by guides or priests. must see bagmati river aarti in evening. very pleasant atmosphere with live singing. go little early to have right spot. it is free.

we visited here knowing that only hindu's are permitted to visit inside the temple, however our guide took us for a tour around the outside where able to watch several very public cremations taking place by the river side. i have never seen this before and felt a little uncomfortable / intrusive watching nepali families saying goodbye to their loved ones in such a very public manner. we also saw several holi men which are willing to pose with us for photos (obviously for a tip to do so). all in all a very different experience for us, glad we did it

not far from kathmandu you may find this little varanasi complex with their gats, temples, crematory rituals, families.a place full of life.and death. we felt a lot of respect and spent some time just watching the ceremonies and activities. you may be surprised but we took no photos.our family felt it was the wrong place to do so. an absolutely must in kathmandu, along with the stupa and the morning flight to the himalayas!

i had visited pashupati nath temple in last october 2017 and love the teple overall kathmandu also. hotel's near temple were nice and good for stay, therir are budget hotels and expensive one also. mostly nepalies are freindly to tourists. temple is old but maintened nicelly. shiv is four head, i wish they allow people to pour water on him as like other places. darshan was nice. nandi is very very big, and main temple is surrounded with many small temple. this is very very near to hindu style of construction of a temple where main temple is there and surrounding temples were there.

i was racially attacked by a registered tour guide at the ticket booth for saying that the 1000r entry fee for tourists was a bit crazy. i was verbally assault by this man and mocked, he stated “oh look at the white foreigner can’t pay 10 dollars in” and he then stated “ your kind came here and murdered our ancestors. things escalated so quickly with this man other tour guides and eventually the tourist police had to become involved. i was in fear of my safety. this man attacked me in front of many people and publicly humiliated me. he also after i called him out as a racist accused me of being racist. this man an employee of the guide association of pashupatinath, is meeting tourists from around the world in a places of worship, filled with that kind of hate and racism. i have never in my life been treated with such a total lack of respect or dignity. it was plain to see from this man’s behavior that tourists are not welcome here. i would advise you to avoid it at all cost. it’s not worth the hassle or the entry fee. what a waste of a day, unsafe for tourists avoid avoid avoid

my visit to pashupatinath in the evening was an experience i will not soon forget. the sight, sounds and the scent in the air brought together a time i have little to compare to. we watched and listened from the hillside in the forest with a birds eye view as the daylight fell and darkness, music, prayers and sweet smoke filled the evening sky.

on the banks of the river that runs into the ganges, so considered holy. cremation ghats in daily use during daylight hours. only hindus allowed inside the temple but plenty to see and walk around outside.

we were fortunate to be here with an excellent guide and on a festival day (although there seem to be a lot of festivals). tbh it would have been daunting without the guide as there were so many people queuing we would have been a bit lost about where we could and couldn't visit. a must-see in kathmandu.

one of the most sacred temples and a jyotirlings of hindus.the temple complex is situated on the banks of bagmati river where the cremations take place.there are many small temples in the vicinity as well besides the main temple complex.i suggest that taking a guide is a must.the monkey menace here is terrible so you have to brave that.

the pashupatinath complex is situated on the banks of bagmati river.in addition to the main temple complex there are numerous small temples in the vicinity.people of hindu religion cremate the bodies of their loved ones on the banks of bagmati river.there is also arti in the evening.

pashupatinath temple is one of the 8 unesco cultural heritage sites of the kathmandu valley dedicated to lord shiva, pashupatinath is one of the four most important religious sites in asia for devotees of shiva. built in the 5th century and later renovated by malla kings, the site itself is said to have existed from the beginning of the millennium when a shiva lingam was discovered here. the largest temple complex in nepal, it stretches on both sides of the bagmati river which is considered holy by hindus. the main pagoda style temple has a gilded roof, four sides covered in silver, and wood carvings of the finest quality. temples dedicated to several other hindu and buddhist deities surround the temple of pashupatinath. just outside the temple complex you will find 100s of little shops selling pooja offerings and selling traditional nepali gurkha hats and one can also buy locally harvested rudarkash beens too

i had amazing journey to this place around middle of march 2018. even though pashupati nath its very old building, but manage very well. some part under construction but still open. also we had chance enjoy great nepal food in the top restaurant close pashupati nath.

pashupati nath is good to visit as very different than in our culture as personally was happy to see all but some of my group were not so pleased to see the activities of surrounding the area. in did it worth to visit it. please go with the tour guide you will learn more about the place and culture.

this is not a place to visit without a devout hindi guide who can explain the rites of preparation of the body for cremation so that you can understand the process and what is happening in front of you. this is a place to put away your camera and respectfully watch and listen - remember the people around you are mourning the loss of a loved one. this is not a place for children. this experience may change how you think about death.
it looks really amazing with the mass of people, many brahmin and the many holli people. pashupati really looks likes destination during the festival as well as
some how we arrived from the river site and skipped the entry ticket office. we were early and it wasn't that crowded.

the holiest temple of the hindus in nepal. situated beside the bagmati river right inside kathmandu, it is a very crowded place. it is a unesco world heritage site. cremation goes on round the clock. some places are out of bounds for non-hindus. it is a powerful place for devote people. worth a visit.
not too sure what to say but when we were there, there was a funeral when the dad body gets burned and afloat in the river
very impressive place to see, but unfortunately not all the places are allowed to visit and the entrance is pretty expensive. they wanted 1000 npr for entrance!

pashupati is a spiritual place where people come to die with their feet in the water. they are moved from the dying rooms to the water’s edge in their final moments. we watched as the recently deceased were bathed by their families and wrapped in cloth and flower garlands. and sprinkled with a red powder. after a short ceremony they were moved to the burning ghats. here the funeral pyre is lit and the body burned. it sounds quite macabre and a bit voyeuristic but it was actually very touching to watch families take care of their loved ones and prepare them for their final journey. it is a place of quiet contemplation but will the usual asian hustle and bustle (such as someone selling candy floss!) it costs 1000 npr to enter the site and it’s worth going if you haven’t been to a site like this before.

has high spiritual value, very neat, minimal security as compared to pilgrimages of india. evening arti at the back of the temple on the river bank was also good, it was like smaller version of ganga arti in varanasi and haridwar.
this place is very powerful, specially during the morning tome lot of local people exercise here and playing the games

pashupatinath temple complex is nepal’s holiest hindu shrine, it’s situated along the banks of river bagmati. before the temple complex one can witness cremations as it’s where the devot send their dead to the next world. the complex is really huge & if one is a tourist the one must hv a guide to assist on what’s happening as you enter the compounds thru the golden gate. in a complex of statues & deities one has to pass by & with a guide one understands what each is related to in hindu religion. quite a bit of walking inside is covered by bare foot hence be prepared. some loose rupee notes or coins are needed if one wants to offer to the deities.

this is a lovely hindu temple. world heritage listed and worth an hour or so. monkeys jump around the temple area, and you can perhaps witness a public cremation across the river. this is one of the iconic sites to see in ktm. recommended.

must not miss pashupatinath temple in kathmandu. the temple is very powerful and palpable. there was a lot of rush and i would only visit one of the faces of the five-faced linga. impressive infrastructure to the attached crematorium. shows the importance of a hindu king ruling the place for a long time. rituals for after death given a lot of importance. pashipatinath is also a center for occult. its important to be careful as well.

this place pashupatinath temple is a must visit place in nepal. the temple is very near to the airport and it’s easily very much approachable. the entry inside the temple is strictly for the hindus only, and others can’t enter the temple. we went to the temple in the evening hours around 7 pm and were very lucky we could have a good view of the lord shiva from 4 sides as during the evening hours all the 4 doors are open. the place is very beautiful and one will completely get mesmerised by the temples beauty.
it is a huge temple complex with lot of small and different temples. however, there is no reliable public transport to the temple from thamel. its recommended to book a cab.

i visited the place to full fill my one of the wish. visit was purely for religious purpose. part of temple was damaged in earthquake. temple arcitect is worth to see. evening arti one must see. around temple there are good handicraft shop & one can get good precious stone at very low price.

not very crowded when we went. the shiva darshan from four sides is unique. the vasuki shrine and bhairava shrine are special. lot of lingams with bees attacted by the nectar and flowers offered make this place interestng.
a local guide took us there, just for the experience of it. it's been a long time ago when i had to fight my way out of the crematorium.
a must visit in kathmandu. beautiful darshana done. we went in the evening so the evening aarati was enthralling. photography not allowed inside the temple.

impressive fervour around the temples and cremation grounds. photos should be avoided or non intrusive. funny sculptures on some temples, look up. funny sadhus some naked with dreadlocks and mobile phones (to nirvana?)
pashupatinath is one of the attraction point in kathmandu with old religious history and beautiful art. only hindu people are permitted for entry in the main temple.

the history, the traditions and the death in a place so strong where you can meet destroyed people (for looses of relatives) or smiling family or friends group to an religious afternoon. see a crematory is an experience very very strong!
one of the largest hindu temples i have visited. we have witnessed the funeral rights with pyres, families and prayers.

we went in the afternoon and as soon as we got out of the taxi it started rain very bad but when it stopped we walked to the street and when you start smelling bbq, you know you are there :p fire, monkeys, prayers, cows. people crying and praying. i loved it! my favourite place in kathmandu! super touching, beautiful! pay attention to the clothes you wear because this is one of the most sacred and holy places in nepal.
the most impressive were the faces of the people visiting the place. each one has peace on the eyes and respect for the place gods and people, life and death in full harmony

i was prepared for visiting the place, but the reality was much beyond by expectations. priest preparing their blessings, people paying their respect to their beloved dead without crying, but feeling that death is soul's salvation. the special people working for the cremation of two bodies,. the hole place is so special. you could feel the harmony between life and death

the hindu traveler will be and should be here for the last part of god shiva can be viewed here. enjoy the arati in the evening and you will find the most holy place in nepal and it will provide you the best ethic of being religious

unless you’re a hindu, it’s a bit weird. whenever you think about it, you’re paying to see some bodies burn. little bit weird not going to lie. also, you’ll be able to see them burn for a bridge outside the temple (if you really need to see some hot corpses).

if you are interested in knowing and witnessing hindu cremations, then this is the place to go. i had a blocked nose - fortunately - but the people i was with said it did not smell at all. of course, except the wood smoke. there is an interesting experience but witnessing the cremations includes being part of the suffering and outcry of the relatives which is a troubling experience and feeling. i felt i was invading their privacy and did not feel comfortable. on top, you cannot enter the temple unless you are hindu. therefore, make sure you do not pay the 1000 rps that i did end up paying.
this place was so busy and when we entering this temple get asked to pay 10 12usd for entrance to the temple i really don't like this otherwise the place was soo good and big temple hindus
a grand temple dedicated to the almighty shiva. a must visit place in kathmandu. the upkeep of the temple needs attention.

as much as kathmandu welcomes with serenity and a sense of spirituality, one walks away from a visit to pashupatinath temple with an awareness of the power that resides in the temple, a deep sense of calmness and peace. a rare temple that is crowded and damp yet, touches one a core spiritual plane. silence and quiet within, amidst the noise that is outside.

heard about this wonderful ancient temple but when visited found this is more than the legend. the auspicious temple grounds and the cremation ground just outside the compound where you can see bodies being cremated on fire make this some how eerie yet spiritual. travelers except locals & indians are nominally charged but others pay a bit more.

we hired a tour guide ammar guni for his guidance for world heritage sites of kathmandu pashupatinath temple. visiting the viewpoint and its surrounding areas. it is in a great location with alot of local people.
if you are in kathmandu,you cannot miss this place.i was here at the time of evening aarti and it was mesmerising.

the thing is you can have the best part for hindu for the religious matter. the 12 jyoti linga in india and the head is in nepal and that is pashupatinath. it is in the bank of bagmati river and it is one of the unesco heritage sites located in kathmandu.

never seen a shrine in the state of awfull condition. monkey poop,trash and pheavily polluted stream. adding to all that smell of burning human flesh from funalral pyres. some wash their faces,relatives wash the feet of deseased and some searching for treasure in the water at the same time. not for the faint hearted.advised to take precaution if you haven't experienced any thing as such before. definitely need a mask, avoid any food or drinks and very good shower after visiting.

one of the must see temples in kathmandu with a rich history and culture. built around 400bc, this area doesn’t look as damaged as other parts of kathmandu after the 2015 earthquake. some people in my group found the cremations on the bagmati riverbank confronting, so be prepared.
shiva is eternal and being in the abode of the ultimate being is an experience. very clean temple which is spread across. very natural surrounding, well maintained by locals.

this is a good place to visit to view the temples and the process of hindu cremation. we were here for almost 3 hours wandering around the site, and experiencing a cremation ceremony. for the cremation a bit of respect is required for the family and friends, the best place is across the river, that way you are a respectful distance from the mourners, we checked with a few people and photos are ok as long as you are respectful. take into account is that you will probably see the deceased during the cleansing of the body. what was unusual was the young guys going around selling candy floss. definitely worth a visit, again a few local guides trying to get your business but this time we had a guide book which had a great map and explanations so we could go at our pace.

this is by far the most amazing temple in kathmandu. the temple is very sacred and holds utmost importance for hindus. it is very clean and no photography is allowed. there are many big and small temples of many gods and goddesses but you have main shiv temple in the centre and other temples around it. the evening aarti at 7:00 pm on bagmati river is special and it is one of its kind in kathmandu. near the river you will also find active crematorium and people getting cremated. so make sure that you pay respect and don't take photos. you should plan for at least 2-3 hours before starting your trip.
a very old temple which is revered by the hindu community. a must visit place during visit to khatmandu. very crowded place but worth a visit.

pashupathinath is both a temple for prayer and a cremation site. it's a place where you can observe life thriving through the religious pujas, singing & dancing. at the same time death, loss and death rituals can be observed. it is a distinctly different perspective from the norm where rituals of life and death are segregated. definitely worth visiting. the place is relatively easy to get to via public bus (which is what i took). depending where you get off you'll have to navigate there. it is advisable to get there for the first puja of the evening which is said to be really auspicious. i somehow got pushed to the front so i got to see this puja. be prepared to remove your shoes at the entrance and dress modestly.

i personally love this place , its like you step back in time. dont miss this area if your in kathmandu for a few days. im in nepal every year running a small tour and this always seems to be the highlight for my people.
very old temple with lots of culture and histry.famous hindu temple .its surrounding is very nice ,sit infront of temple nearby the river bank and relax .

when you pay for your entry ticket, there are guides ready to escort you around the area. having someone explain the history and the temples, ceremonies enriches the experience. just ask how much before you commit

you can spend whole day in pashupatinath complex and not get bored! it's quite large area but easily accessible by foot (except of the central temple that can be entered only by nepalese hindus), so walk around or sit down somewhere and just watch the life going on. the best spots are the banks of bagmati river. you will be barely able to absorb all the things that are going on there, things that are unthinkable for western minds but are perfectly normal for people from this part of asia: hours long public cremations, kids fishing for valuables with magnet on the rope right underneath the cremations spots, people doing their daily hygiene in the dirty water of the sacred river, holy men (sadhus) with painted faces and dreadlocks. it might be culture shock for most of the people but is one of the most interesting places i have seen and it's definitely worth the visit.

on the first day and the last day of our kailash manas sarovar tour, we visited the pashupati nath temple to seek blessings. the temple premises are clean and devoid of beggers and alm seeking people which we see in most of our temples. the pooja conducted by 3-4 pandits was very religious an involve rudram, namkam etc. people were asked to stand in the queue to have darshan of the lord from any of the four dwaras (doors). there were no short cuts or line breaking by the pilgrims. during aarti time in the evening, all the devotees are allowed to visit the lord through every door in a line and mantra jal and flowers were given by the temple poojari at the end of aarti. our experience was very divine in the temple premises and we felt like spending a lot of time there visiting several of the temples inside the premises.

this is a legendary temple in kathmandu and is visited by hindu devotees from all over the world. we went right in the middle of the day and was quite hot then. it is advisable to vist either early in the morning or in the evening

it famous and sacred hindu temple which is located on the banks of bagmati river. a very peaceful place which is visited by locals and tourists in large numbers daily. the temple complex consists of small temple and shrines all around and monkeys jumping all over and around them. visit during early morning or evening to be a part of the aarti.

really interesting place. highly recommend going for aarti at 6:30pm. there's a lot of monkeys here, but they seemed chill. seeing the bodies prepared for cremation was very moving and important. non hindus can't go in the main temple but there is much to see besides that as well. i paid $400 nr for a cab from thamel.

the holy temple of lord shiva pashupatinath is very religious for hindu religion. this is one of the 4 must visist place before any hindu die, the huge pashupatinath statue, basuki temple, shiva linga , 108 shiva linga and many more religious temple will take about 2 hours to any hindu visitors.

i visited the temple with my wife, mother brother and his wife. the temple is one of the famous religious locations for the hindus within the valley. the most beautiful piece of architecture is the huge 'ox' statue inside the temple. then the river banks and the surrounding was something everyone in my family loved.
you might feel a bit bizarre looking at someones funeral, but the tenderness and emotion of it all is an eye opener and everyone should see this, take it in and analyze it. fascinating

approximately 2 km from tribhuvan international airport lies pashupatinath temple, an ancient lord shiva temple .the location is known as gaushala. when you enter into the area through the entrance from gaushala stop you will find local vendors and a nepal sbi atm van for the convenience of the visitors from outside.moving towards the main temple you will find flower and prasad sellers you can also buy a sankh(a sea shell that is blown during puja in hindu culture) ,and a rudraksh garland. moving past these stalls there are vendors exchanging coins for cash.you can exchange some cash for coins. i will tell you later why people buy some coins here. moving towards the temple you will find concrete paved stairs leading towards the main temple. on the right is the way towards the aryaghat (cremation area ) on the banks of bagmati river.since only hindus are allowed in the main temple non hindus don't have much to see towards the main temple yah ofcourse you can take pictures from far. if you are a hindu you can move towards the temple. there a free counter you can safely keep your shoes and chappals in a bag and you get a token that is to be shown when you return to get your belongings back.till the gate mobile and cameras are allowed.even non hindus can come till this spot. you have submit your camera here.mobile are allowed to keep in your pocket .but usage is strictly prohibited. inside the temple you fill find huge nandi in the main area. lord pashupatinath temple is built in the nepalese pagoda style of architecture.beautifully carved wooden rafters,golden pinnacle and doors covered with silver sheets. inside there is a huge black shiva linga .i was able to get view of it from all four sides. you will experience some very powerful vibration out there. there are other temple to visit around the main temple. from the back area you can see the view of aryaghat below there are open display of cremation ceremony of the dead on the banks of bagmati river. from this place you will see a top of a temple on aryaghat with coins on it. it is believed that if you throw or release a coin from above and if it rests on the pinnacle of the temple below. you will get your wish fulfilled.there are many lengends and beliefs out here .other temple of basukinath and scluptures of budhanilkantha and hanuman can be seen here. you will get a good crowd if you visit the temple on monday .in the month of shrawan ( end of july to mid of auga ust).on the day of shivaratri about 1 million people visit this place and the numbers are increasing every year.

this place is peaceful and. the temples are designed beautifully here. non-hindus are not allowed to enter the main temple but you can take a beautiful picture of the surroundings. the night view is stunning.

i was stayed at kathmandu peace home near thamel my hotel manager manage all my trip and he booked a car for me at evening. i am very excited to see those things what i saw over here. hindius after death what they do, the monkeys and baba's makes me very interesting. at the temple people doing "aarati " at evening that was so cool!

this temple has many other smalle temples and places to sit and meditate. there also flows a small river next to the temple incase one wishes to carry out certain ceremonies. monkeys and other birds are welcomed and do not cause any inconvenience or disturbance. it is not crowded most of the days and one can take ones time to pay offering and pray to the gods.
its very good cultural and religious place but entry for only hindus. good to be here but the cremation sites on the bank of river make me feel some uneasy.
pashupati is the biggest hindus temple in nepal. here are many shiva status and beautiful animal and temple is very beautyful

non hindus are not allowed in the main temple, but you can still see and photograph a lot of incredible places from the surrounding areas. entrance is expensive by nepalese standards (rs1000 / $10) but less than you'd pay in many other countries. expect a guide to attach himself to you and then ask for a tip at the end.
sacred sight and temple. worth a visit, very important place of worship for hindus. great views from the back side of the temple. it is old and historical sacred place. i recommend it.

i took my sons here to experience a funeral in another culture. the whole business seemed pretty business like for the first two funerals. then a family came to bury their mother/husband, and with the sincere grieving, and the care that they took with their departed mother/wife's body, the way they lavished her with the red powder (forgot what it's called), washed her face and lips over and over, and let her feet sit for a good long while in the river, you got the sense of how people here part with their loved ones. it was a good experience for my sons, especially in seeing the difference in the families. people mourn differently, but we felt that the last woman was more deeply loved.

we were lucky to shift our plans to kathmandu last moment from pokhara. if you believe and it's possible then do visit this place for lord shiva. we didnt know about temple timings and reached only 2 mins before the closing time in afternoon. lucky i would say.

second holy site for the hindus after varanasi, it is a must visit for prayers but also for cremation of departed ones. if you feel uncomfortable with witnessing a cremation, please refrain to visit. otherwise it is very interesting to witness the entire ceremony. stroll along the ghat on the opposite side of the cremation pyres. beautiful shiva shrines all aligned to form a kind of tunnel. the main temple is not accessible to anyone but priests but hindus can visit the courtyard which has a huge golden bull.

pasphupati nath is peaceful area im visit with my bestfriends but all security guard from entrance always asking entrance fee but again im very smart im speak again in nepali bhasa if im not speak nepali god nrs 1000 ruppes for entrance but fisrt asking me nam i tell mero nam yuddha then asking me gharma i tell jhapa then asking me nepali citizenship then one of my friends he fight security in not asking my citizenship card very crazy of nepali police for money

there are lots of monkeys in the temple premises, so be careful with your 'prasad'. there's cremation grounds right behind pashupatinath, apparently it's a touristy thing for foreigners to do, however i was put off by it. temple aarti is something to wait for. i brought my parents for it, and there were quite pleased.

temple is hardly 5 minutes away from the airport. there are various temples inside the campus. we got a chance to see the pashupati nath main temple. it is a beautiful shivlinga. temple closes at 2pm and opens in evening. beware of monkeys.
every time when i enter pashupatinath temple, my heart and mind becomes very peaceful and clam liked blessed by god shiva live.
its such an amazing temple. the two aartis at the temple - one inside the sanctum and the other one on the banks of the river bagmati ganga is not to be missed. lord shiva blesses every visitor here.

to maintain religious purity, non hindus are forbidden entry into this sacred precinct! at the entrance this breaks down to a racial barrier, as indian christians can just walk in, because, “they look like hindus!” while i respect the concept of purity and sanctity, i know no pure men of any religion, and i know that a civil society can only progress as an inclusive one! on the other hand religious prejudice is even a right in america!
this is a very big shiva temple with ancient carvings. part of the temple is ruined due to the deadly earthquake.
this is one of the most revered hindu shiva temple across the world. bit damaged due to the earthquake. neat and clean.
i appreciated the culture here although there are restrictions for non residents who have to pay, and then are seen as the moving atm.
this is one of the unesco world heritage site. temple dedicated to lord pashupatinath, this temple is considered as a masterpiece of hindu architecture. must visit.
the stupa is located a bit away from the city center, can view the katmandu city from the tip. must visit once.

for all travelers visiting kathmandu, this is a must visit place. it has a different jyotirlinga than what you usually see in temples. the only thing that could be better managed over there was queue for normal visitors & those doing abhishek. since it was not very well guided places, there was a single queue leading to chaos.
one of the most divine places i have visited in nepal. dedicated to lord siva, the temple in itself is a grand structure. people from far and wide come to visit this marvelous temple
i visited the temple of lord pashupatinath, during my visit of nepal. its one of the best temples dedicated to lord siva in south-asia.

very large car park, the temple has world heritage status. the temple and environs are fairly clean, however, very poor signage and absence of guides made it difficult for us as tourists to appreciate the grandeur of the site

never saw such open cremations in my travels ,so it was very interesting.there were few cremations going on and 3 corpses being prepared for cremation.not smelly as mentioned by some.it was a costly entrance ticket ,though.usd 15. especially not being allowed to visit the temple as we were not hindus.

a sad but surprise place watching how hindu's are cremating there. but the scenery are great, sadus are well decorated and friendly mood to take photographs. lucky that we had an excellent tourist guide ammar raj guni who explain us alot about hinduism. in summary its worthy to visit there at least if you want to be in nepal.
anicca. if you know the term which using by buddhist. seeing the cremation process leave us speechless.
the arti in the evenings is a delight to watch. the crowd is engaged in various bhajans in praise of lord

we went at 6am to be there before the crowds bit it eas alreafy pumping with groups singing and chanting to traditional music. very spontaneous and captivating to watch. take ypur shoes off at the booth but beware of fresh manure from animals that obviously roam the precinct.and monkeys that hover above the worshipers and drop a few "offerings" of their own on unsuspecting and clearly oblivious locals.

this is supposedly iconic place in kathmandu. i had great expectation for this religious place. was highly disappointed by the state and cleanliness of the premises. the site of cremation on the bank of river was very disturbing. the river is in completely horrible state. i hope they do something to take care of this iconic temple!
a hindu temple very close to the airport. located on the bank of river. the temple is clean and maintained.

i was mesmerised with the management of the temple. of course for me and my family it was in the list of must to visit in life and after visiting this shrine, i and my family felt very happy and satisfied. darshan of lord shiva made everything awesome. we were blessed with very good darshan-labh.
this the historical pace in kathmandu and ancient one, we been their to visit, we can not enter the main place but it is on to overview once from the outside.
a must visit pashupati nath temple for the hindus. the continuous dhun made the atmosphere sound so good
visit here was very nice and enlightening. the linga here was very rare sight and must do thing here.
it’s an experience.must go early in the morning to beat the crowds. the ceremonies start at 9 am and the rush crowd is then.
a historical place in kathmandu. it is worth visiting and spending some quiet times there to view how local treat their loved ones in present and in their passing state.

excellent temple complex to worship lord shiva, many temples within the complex, need to take of shoes and leather stuffs. get some flowers and offering from shop, usually long queue to worship. if you r in rush handover offering to a volunteer o. front door, they will help. can have good view of shiva linga inside and pay respect. enjoy some bhajana chanting on the side of river, pay respect to many gods while circling around the main temple. renovation work is ongoing.

a site that should be a spiritual and serene experience. unfortunately many foreign tourists are put off visiting by the aggressive guides at the entrance, who also push for tips after hired. if a flat rate was established and guides truly accredited many more people could enjoy this unique, historical and spiritual site and learn about hindu culture. i would love to see the experience of visiting this site improved to allow people to come away with pleasant memories focused on the significance of the site.

it is very unique place i believe you won’t find anywhere else. this place is where dead are being burned to ashes as part of their ritual/traditions. it is very interesting place, but for those who can handle it. not for children.
same as varanasi, temple is beautiful , same ganga aarti, everything is like our varanasi. repair work is going on in temple. it is hot there.

some cool temples and a lot of religious people around it praying, doing yoga and stuff. we got there before 7 am, which was cool because of few people and a lot of monkeys. we saw a funeral, which made a special impression.

the temple offers its prayers to lord shiva. the main temple requires you to walk barefoot but you can carry your bags with all your stuff inside. photography strictly prohibited inside the main temple area. however, you're free to click pictures of the surrounding area. sit, relax and think about your "cycle of life" on the banks of the bagmati river which flows across pashupatinath.
crowded but that is what is suppose to be. lord shiva resides there so one should meditate there without fail
very well kept temple premises with long history and beautiful surrounding too. visitors and staff need to make it more cleaner
its nice place, but visitors do no care for cleanliness of place, so also staff not geared for cleanliness, but otherwise such a memorable religious monument

it is one of famous jyotirlingas of shivas which is very crowded place sorrounded all over main temple of shiva and other small temple of lord ganesha , lord bhairav, lord hanuman lord krishna and so on
it was a great time to be in the temple. although crowded we received darshan and conducted poojas as we desired. neat and clean the premises are swept continuously,
we had such nice opportunity to have evening aarti & morning pooja. we can chant only one word "adbhut". lord shiva blessed us with great darshan & opportunity of rituals.
beautiful sculptures, all hindu gods & godesses. a must see. be careful of touts/guides.the pujari puts the cash you give into the hundi. a very good habit
a most see in your temple visit to nepal specially during one of the major holidays as a westerner you will only be allowed in certain areas of the temple
pashupati nath is very very ancient hindu's temple . it is nearly 1 .5 km far from airport. there is monkey.

my primary interest in it stemmed from the fact that it was one of the locations for shooting the marvel movie, dr strange, starring benedict cumberbatch. of course, it’s also of interest that this temple complex is on the list of unesco world heritage sites. only hindus are allowed into the main pashupatinath temple. for people of other religions, all the other parts of the building are open except the main temple. if you visit this complex, remember that it’s not just a single temple you’re visiting. it’s a sprawling collection of temples, ashrams, images and inscriptions encompassing 264 hectares of land, including 518 temples and monuments. so there’s a lot of walking involved. there’s also a crematorium on the banks of the river that you can visit, if you like that sort of thing. i preferred to stay with my group and watch the monkeys that scurried about the complex.

my primary interest in it stemmed from the fact that it was one of the locations for shooting the marvel movie, dr strange, starring benedict cumberbatch. of course, it’s also of interest that this temple complex is on the list of unesco world heritage sites. only hindus are allowed into the main pashupatinath temple. for people of other religions, all the other parts of the building are open except the main temple. if you visit this complex, remember that it’s not just a single temple you’re visiting. it’s a sprawling collection of temples, ashrams, images and inscriptions encompassing 264 hectares of land, including 518 temples and monuments. so there’s a lot of walking involved. there’s also a crematorium on the banks of the river that you can visit if you like that sort of thing. i preferred to stay with my group and watch the monkeys that scurried about the complex.
only hindus allowed, worth visiting, very spiritual and significant for all hindus to visit especially shiv devotees.

interesting place as you enter the complex,you walk past the river and the funeral ghats before you go to the main temple where hundreds of shiva devotees waiting in line to make their offerings and prayers and performing rituals with families.

i came here to see the temple and to view the ceremonial cremation ghats. as a non hindu, i was not allowed into the temple which was a bit disappointing but the ghats were worth the visit alone. well worth the trip and the entrance fee

this is the historic and one of the most important hinduist temple. i didn't know at first that it has crematorial sites nearby. not feeling well at first, but it's a good experience overall. beware of what the guide asks you to pay.

a trip to nepal is incomplete without visiting pashupati nath temple. the areas is very well manged there is a parking space, dedicated space to keep shoes, wash hands and legs. the only thing the visitors must be careful off the the pandit's sitting in the other small temples inside the big one. they con you by giving you a rudraksh ki mala, generally they will give you 5 mukhi mala which outside will cost you around 50 nepali rupees and in return they will ask you to give them dakshina and not less than 200 nepali rupees. out of curiosity i spoke to a local shopkeeper sitting just outside the temple who told me that these pandit's have made it their business, they con people for money and they are not even ashamed of doing this sitting inside such a holy place. i got conned, so people just a warning please be careful.
i want always more and more blessings from our lord shiva. everything i visit nepal, i visit pashupatinath. when i reach there, i feel so calm and feel out of stress.
wow simply wow . such a beauty we have . pashupatinath temple is not just a small temple but a huge which includes so many small temples. this temple allows only hindu people
the temple is old and well maintained. far too many monkeys. the temple is under renovation given the damage caused by the earth quake in nepal. a typical hindu temple.

pashupatinath temple is a must visit temple in kathmandu. it has a very important significance in hindu religion and it is a part of "char dham yatra" in which kedarnath darshan (india) and pashupatinath temple kathmandu are considered as head and body of lord shiva.

this haritage religious place i heard about since long but hadn't visit yet this time kathmandu trip with my husband we planned to visit this temple pasupatinath and i found such nanu things i saw here feel totally different
only hindus can enter the temple proper; however, non hindus can enter the larger complex and also observe the cremations riverside.

a must visit ,if you are in kathmandu . lord pashupatinath ,as the god of all animals is a sacred temple for hindus . normally huge crowds ,so at least plan for 2 hours . one can offer poojas by paying 1100 npr minimum and then you get to see the sanctum sanctorum from inside and pooja ,milk worship is done in front of you . for this please go before 10 am in the morning
as every one knows that this temple is world famous. visit to kathmandu is incomplete for every tourist without having darshan of pashupati nath temple
one of the best management and good helpful people in side temple good security and to mennu another more temple around good blessing

spiritual place. too many people make this place quite congested. approach road is quite bad. the place is quite nice and people are very helpful. we managed to go easily with my mom (80 years) on a wheel chair. people really helped.
very helpful people. despite the rain we enjoyed our time there. did some shopping about the rudraksha, and souvenirs

if you are from europe, you will likely have a big fee to enter the complex and you cant see the main temple. the main temple is rather poorly organized. shoes can be checked for free. the snack and sweet shops after your trip are a must. the food is great and much cheaper than in thamel.

pashupatinath temple is a fascinating abode of lord shiva, and is located in the lap of the himalayas. the river bagmati flows right behind the temple, where aarti is performed in the evenings. one is fascinated by the statue of the huge bull (nandi) facing the lord's shrine. lord pashupatinath has four faces facing the four directions, and one must circumambulate to have darshan of all the four faces. lots of little shrines around the main shrine dot the temple complex. the padt (pashupatinath area development trust) is managing and developing the temple complex and the surrounding areas. some structures that were damaged during the earthquake are also being restored. an adi shankaracharya mandir is also under renovation.

we found this place to be the most fascinating in kathmandu. it probably helps greatly to have a guide to explain the place and what is going on as well as the significance of everything. a guide found us near the ticket booth and he turned out to be great.

the place is very divine and always full of lord shiva's bhakts. you can experience divine smell in air after coming here. the place is usually crowdy and you have to have some spare time for darahans. you can also enjoy the aarti done in evening. local market is also there where you can buy lot of things for you and your loved ones.

pushpatinath is a beautiful temple with clean surroundings. if you plan to go with family be prepared to walk a little till the temple. there are often long queues but also ways of cutting them! it is very close to the airport and hence a trip can be planned while coming or going to the airport.

this is a hindu temple and once in their lifetime, hindus are required to pilgramage here. located on the bagmati river (which eventually flows into the ganges in india), you will see cremations occurring on edge of the river. monkeys galore and the occasional cow can also be found wandering through the complex. only hindus are permitted inside the golden temple, but you get good views from up the hill. be warned of the guides. even if you say no, they will follow you and expect a tip at the end. definite location to spend a couple of hours to see into a different culture with so much activity and colour particularly on saturdays.

the pashupatinath temple is a famous and sacred hindu temple complex that is located on the banks of the bagmati river, approximately 5 kilometres north-east of kathmandu in the eastern part of kathmandu valley, the capital of nepal.

only hindus are allowed inside; however, non-hindus can visit the areas surrounding the temple and even observe the open cremations. this is an important pilgrimage site for hindus and is designated a unesco world cultural site as well. a cultural must do when in kathmandu in my opinion.

this enormous compound is worth the 1000 (~$10 us) because it's a gorgeous and large-scale site. so many nooks and crannies, fancy wood work, temples, and relaxed calm tourists and locals enjoying the scene. also, we didn't expect monkeys there but there were!
my guide from swotah travel took me to pashupati at evening for aarati, it was surreal. i really loved the spiritual ambiance there.

pashupatinath temple had been place in my heart begin hindu and grown in and around this place. i would not rate this as my review or rating. if you are religious person of any religion and had tolerance to accept other view, do visit and see this world heritage site.

definitely worth going! we visited the temple on may 2018, went there about 3pm and stayed to around sunset. it is a beautiful temple, covering quite a big area, so you definitely need a guide. there are plenty available at the entrance, where you can also purchase your ticket. i would recommend to negotiate the price with the guide beforehand!

its totally a different and thrilling to see burning of a dead body. i didn't see it properly but my husband took many shots of its. the hindu culture is amazing than our christainity. anyways, the temple and the other monuments were very nice. tour guide sujan dhakal described a lot about its history and the cultural beliefs. amazing.
before entering into temple premises, enquire about the different categories of pujas performed. huge temple premises, open air. non hindus cannot enter into the temple.

peace positive energies blessings discipline divine you will find it all. large temple complex, but the people there are helpfull. before entering the temple just read and get details about the different types of pujas offered. there are different categories of puja, starting from 1100 nc. a must visit in lifetime.

one of the most sacred hindu temples, pashupatinath temple should be on every traveller's list. definitely hire a guide (you can find them around the ticket booth) in order to understand the history of the temple. the one hour tour was very insightful. you'll witness body cremations along the river bank, be prepared for that. we went in the evening, and watched the aarti, which was really interesting!

it is a huge temple of lord shiva and very beautifully and artistically made indeed. you are not allowed to take pictures or videos inside the temple premises so i don't have much pictures of temple from inside. the only thing i don't like about this temple was that deliver where the aarti is done was very dirty. 1 very strange thing that we noticed in this temple was that the funeral or the rituals before the funeral with the dead body are done in public right at the place where the aarti is held. this was something very different i've never seen that anywhere before. so if you are attending the maha aarti at pashupatinath temple you will definitely get to see the rituals be done with the dead body before the funeral then and there.

for anyone not fully aware do your research before deciding to go, this is an open air crematorium and there will be deceased persons visible, we saw faces of the deceased and bodies being burnt on cremation pyres. while it is a great insight into hindu burial and apparently an honour for the families for people to observe a cremation it may not be for everyone and could be confronting. the ashes of the deceased need to flow into the bagmati river as this flows into the ganges in india and is of great religious importance. it was a humbling sobering but also a touching experience.

pashupathi nath is in the city centre of kathmandu. it is historically a significant temple in nepal. read up on this temple before your visit as this will add value to your trip. i enjoyed my visit here. decent dress code applies at this temple as all others do.you will also have to remove shoes whilst visiting the temple.
it is a large temple complex and as expected it is always crowded. avoid late mornings and evenings. the evening aarti is quite nice.

a heritage shiva temple with good landscaping and arrangements made for easy pooja in the temple. within 1 km of the airport. there is a market near parking with restaurants and selling chinese cheap quality items.

visit is a must but temple usually crowded. free shoe safe available. the temple needs maitainence, proper security and cleaning facilities. the road drive to the temple is horrid with no inch of the road plane with no pit holes.

the place is near the airport so a tour or taxi is needed (remember to negotiate the price first or ask for the counter). this place is very sacred so you need to be reapectful. despite you can not come in to the main temple watchitng the surroundings and the ceremony to cremate the bodies is impressive. the whole place smell of death. it is worth a visit.

here you can see some of the age with the traditional temples, it was slightly dirty but that is the real deal. you will see some holy men around here also. but don't be fooled some are just after money. however be polite if you get a photo it is respectful to give a little.

a world heritage site. temple of lord shiva , one of the holiest temples for hindus on the banks of river bagmati in kathmandu valley. actually it is a complex of many temples. the main temple of lord shiva is called pashupatinath temple, with 4 gates. a very ancient temple said to be having a history of more than 2000years. a huge beautiful brass basava(bull) is in front of the main door of the temple must see place when you are in nepal.
the largest hindu temple worshipping the hindu god shiva. cremations take place on a daily basis at this temple. it is a nice huge temple, but i advice people to keep their noses covered.

its really a holy and historical pilgrims of nepal,its surrounded by holy river bagmati and very beautiful forest along with herds of wild deer.its very peaceful for those people and saints who are looking for mental peace to enjoy.thousands of domestic and foreign people visit this temple everyday.hindus and boudhist are really fascinated by this holy shiva mandir. recently the prime minister of india mr. modi visited the temple and worship according to hindu rituals.
unesco heritage pilgrimage for lord shiva followers and all hindus.hindu cremation ground and temple area.

deeply touching moments, here where dead meets life and we were able to be part of unbelievable moments. if you only have a few hours or a day, surely try to visit above anything else. this is life and words cannot describe your experience.

we had an amazing day and temple on saturday which is a local holiday. due t to was quite some local people. the deity is in the form shivling & is unique in the world and. best overall worth visiting once in a life time. no mobile pictures are allowed.

pashupatinath temple, the largest hindu temple worshipping the hindu god shiva who is known as pashupati that means the master of the whole universe, is a unesco world cultural heritage in nepal. located at the bank of the bagmati river, 5km northwest of kathmandu city, the holy temple has a reputation of “the temple of living beings” for it attracts thousands of pilgrims from all over the world come to pay homage and get a deep exploration of hinduism culture every day. with astonishing architectural beauty, pashupatinath temple complex is the embodiment of faith, religion, culture and tradition. a must-see activity here is watching the cremation ceremony by bagmati riverside; erected in a.d. 5th century, pashupatinath temple is a sacred land for hindus and nepalese buddhists as well as a concentration camp for nepali sadhus. this religious site comprises about 260 hectares with a main pashupatinath temple and a large amount of small mandir around. the main three-storied temple is a nepalese pagoda style of architecture, which is only opened to hindus for entry. there are many legends surrounding the temple. one of it says that , lord siva and parvati once his there in form of antelope but the devtas found them . in the process a horn was broken and established there as siva lingam . once a cow starter showering milk on the lingam and people came to know about the sanctity of the place . the temple in the current form is built in pagoda style with the big nandi in front and .sacro sanctum, or the main idol is a stone mukhalinga with a silver yoni base bound with the silver serpent. the lingam is one metre high and has faced in four directions. these faces represent various aspects of shiva; sadyojata (also known as barun), vamdeva (also known as ardha nareshwor), tatpurusha, aghor & ishana (imaginative). facing west, north, east, south and zenith respectively representing five primary elements namely earth, water, air, light and ether. each face has tiny protruding hands holding rudraksha mala on right hand and a kamandalu on the otherhand. you have to plan visit to this temple .sometimes it gets crowded. photography inside temple is prohibited.

visited the temple on saturday which is a local holiday. hence there was quite some crowd. the deity is in the form shivling & is unique in the world. overall worth visiting once in a life time. no mobile pictures are allowed.

it's a must visit place for followers of shivji or bholebaba. no photography is allowed from inside the temple. it has 5 faces of shivji and we can see the face of pashupatinath at the completion of darshan of all other 4 faces.

here you can see sadus. where the dead are burned. when you go, you will encounter monkeys wandering around. we recommend that you visit this place with a private vehicle. ajay, swotah travel set us up with a special car and guide. you will absolutely come to a ceremony.
lots of history. must visit despite the chaos. beautiful setting near the airport with the river bagmati flowing through the temple complex

one of the ancient temples of siva ,in kathmandu , pashupatinath defines nepal in terms of hospitality , spirituality and devotion . it is believed that the temple was constructed in 400 bc ( source wikepedia) ,but no definitive proof of that . it was renovated last in 15th century by the then king . there are many legends surrounding the temple. one of it says that , lord siva and parvati once his there in form of antelope but the devtas found them . in the process a horn was broken and established there as siva lingam . once a cow starter showering milk on the lingam and people came to know about the sanctity of the place . the temple in the current form is built in pagoda style with the big nandi in front and .sacro sanctum, or the main idol is a stone mukhalinga with a silver yoni base bound with the silver serpent. the lingam is one metre high and has faced in four directions. these faces represent various aspects of shiva; sadyojata (also known as barun), vamdeva (also known as ardha nareshwor), tatpurusha, aghor & ishana (imaginative). facing west, north, east, south and zenith respectively representing five primary elements namely earth, water, air, light and ether. each face has tiny protruding hands holding rudraksha mala on right hand and a kamandalu on the other. unlike other shiva lingams in india and nepal this pashupati shiva lingam is always dressed in its golden vastra except during abhishakam, so pouring milk and ganga jal is only possible during abhishakam through the main priests. ( copied from wikipedia)
this is one of best historical place in kathmandu valley. here you can see many ancient place and statues.it is very important to see a ritual involved with a cremation.

this a must place to visit, most hindus come to nepal to visit temple. again we visited this time on monday, generally monday's are busy but being a holy day it was very busy. here there are different type of darsan, if you want close darsan there is a long queue to join it could take hours on busy day. there is an another queue which shorter still able to see from climbing a ramp this is in between the nandi ( the bull) and the temple. there are 4 doors for darsan only one door opens in the morning but in afternoon pilgrims can see from all 4 doors. other way of avoiding the long queue is doing puja with local priest, they have their own ways.

this is a big part of nepale's hindu culture. hart of hindu pilgrimage in nepal. monks and negantas gather around the temple and cremating deth bodies at the edge of river and it was a different experience we never had before.
the site itself is impressive. but what makes it unique is the cremations taking place on a daily basis and experiencing the different local approach to death from other cultures.

this temple needs no introduction and is on every traveller's list. i visited pashupati nath temple today and tomorrow prime minister of india is scheduled to visit here. the temple is conveniently located and can be reached by road. i really liked the way footwear is collected and stored in individual bags. as soon as i entered the temple, i was greeted by larger than life nandi - lord shiv's vahan. on moving further there is this old nepali architecture building inside which is panchmukhi shivling. it was a pleasure to visit this place.

its a very nice and very old temple. you feel peace in this temple a lot of peoples comes regularyly in this temple for worship. if you have intrested to do the special pooja dont go direclty to the poojaries they demd more you do it oon proper way just go to the nepal sbi bankcounter near to the gate of temple fill the form and deposite the amount for special pooja and do your pooja this helps temple members to develop more in the temple.
very ancient temple considered as second varanasi. huge complex with many small temples. evening harathi is a must see. there is no demand for money from any priest or guide. well maintained.
despite the crowds and the pushing and pulling, most fulfilling darshan at the temple and the vibrancy of the temple is to be felt than written.

i stumbled across this by walking off the beaten track and coming into the back route following some locals. funerals are held here on funeral pyres so you need to be aware of this. both the temple, the shrines and the people are full of colour and it has many places to sit and contemplate or just people watch.

we have visited temple on 25 march 2018 on our trip to kathmandu. the area and surrounding is very big. bagmati river is flowing adjacent to temple. darshan to bholey baba was very nice. hindu must visit this holy tempel.
we went to see the ceremonies that take place here. it was shocking and interesting, a completely different way to stay farewell to our dearest. totally reccommend it un kathmandu

this may not be for everyone, but it is a fact of life and religion for the hindus in nepal. the bagmati river is deemed as holy and so cremations take place along its banks. i took photographs here, some may not wish too. the services are very interesting and on the side of the river their are ample opportunities to take other pictures of local holy men (they want paid too!). definitely recommend
this temple is reserved for hindu faith followers. it seems very popular with the faithful. it does lead to the cremation grounds, which can be reached without trespassing on the sacred ground.

the tempel area is very big and nice to visit. it is similar like varanasi. the sadhus the river the burning ghat the holy tempel i would recommend it to everyone to visit when you are in kathmandu. it s a holy place for hindus
must see and visit. take a guided tour so you get to know about the temple ps: only hindus are allowed in this temple, not even foreigners can enter the temple

this is a hindu temple and the entry fee is npr 1,600 per person; if you are a non-hindu you can not access the temple complex. the most elaborating aspect of this complex is the hindu cremation process; at any given time you will find few dean bodies been set on fire and a few being prepared for cremation by families; the ash is washed away to the river which ties closely with the hindu beliefs - a very sacred religious venue. you have hermits but to take a photo comes at a cost - everything about this place is highly commercialised and found this to be not an uncomfortable experience (personal perspective).

a very nice temple, you have visit the temple from 930 a<m to 1130 am. during that time all the four doors are open and you can view five faces of pasupatinath. you need to walk around to see the whole temple. felt very holy also scary because in that temple backside all death rituals are happening. you can see the people waiting with their relative's dead body to perform last rites.

this is the usp of nepal in addition to (of course) the everest, lots of people visit this place both locals as well as hindus from india. the gate of the temple opens up only at specific time. they perform aarti just outside the temple everyday in the evening around 7pm which is worth watching.

if you want to enter the place, you have to pay 1000rupees. before that, you get to see a local bazaar with paints and other traditional hindu things and some buildings which towards the sunset can give you the chills (great for photography). you'll see there traditional funerals - cremations. it was my understanding that, to the left, they burnt the poorer ones, whereas to the right - the richer ones. the smell was a bit awful and eerie. if you cross the bridge, you'll see a road leading you to a huge and beautiful park where you can find monkeys (be mindful - they can rob you ;) ), cows among temples and deer kept behind a fence.
should this really be on a unesco list if people from all backgrounds (i.e. non-hindu) are not allowed to respectfully appreciate it?
hindu temple. really enjoyed guided tour to learn more about the place and hindu religion. also you can see open cremation here.

pashupatinath temple is a beautiful temple dedicated to lord shiva. one of the most sacred hindu temples of nepal is located on the banks of sacred river bagmati, around 5 kilometer north east of kathmandu valley. this temple comlex is listed on unesco world heritage sites since 1979. only hindus are allowed into the main temple. pashupatinath is one of the 14places in the city where cremation take place and it is supposed to be the best one of all as the sacred river bagmati here eventually flows into the holy ganges.

felt one with the universe here.the place is simply magical and had such a wonderful time here. only hindus are allowed foreigners should at least go and see the place till they are allowed it's totally serene place. nepal is truly magical place.
we have been here for evening darshan and able to get patience darshan without a hushpush. able to veiw the pashupati nathji from all four sides.
we were lucky to get the aarti at evening time and had patience view of all four side of the pashupati nath temple

wow. this place is quite something. many funerals happening along the river bed. incredible to watch from the other side of the river respectfully, can be quite difficult to watch people grieve say goodbye to their loved ones but equally facinating to see the traditions of the faith. worth a visit but just watch out for the heat and the smells of the burning corpses.
pashupati nath is a very famed temple of nepal. our pm narendra modi has visited this temple when on official visit to nepal.

one of the major religious place for hindus, this is a major attraction in kathmandu, nepal. the place is neat and the crowd management is also good. best time to visit would be in the evenings when we can see the arthi as well. this place is very close to the thribhuvan international airport. non hindus are not allowed in this temple. photo and videos are strictly not allowed in the temple, even usage of mobile phones are also not allowed.
holiness persists thorough every nook and corner of the temple. the crowd was not at all unruly even though we visited the temple on monday.

pashupatinath is a sacred hindu place in kathmandu on the banks of the bagmati river, close to tribhuvan airport. it is also the site of several funeral ghats; an area of religious significance which may be a little confronting to western visitors. out of respect, non-hindu visitors should stay on the eastern bank of the river. pashupatinath represents an important part of the nepalese culture, and is well worth a visit.
pashupati nath is one of the highly religious places in the world. the view from the temple hills is just breathtaking.
the pashupati nath temple is simply amazing. the minute you enter inside you feel very peaceful. best sight is a few monkeys sometimes.

visited this temple for about an hour during a day tour it was quite overwhelming to watch the cremations that were being carried out on the side of the river amazing experience- a must see when visiting nepal for overseas visitors as it is something so culturally different from what we are accustomed to
all good, shops for souvenirs are many and reasonably priced. wished the sanctum sanctorum was better lighted inside.

it is very famous and sacred hindu temple of lord shiva. there are many other small temple surrounding the main temple. the experience is hard to explain of visiting the temple. it is also the crematorium site. one of the must place to visit.

organised visit. you have to be a hindu to enter the temple itself. lots of history. there is a cremation place which one can go and see the cremations (if any) taking place. its a personal choice one that i avoided. just be careful of anyone promising you "vip" type access and "vip" prayers. there isn't such. you all just queue up.
we went on a religious day which is to be avoided. very busy and one does get pushed along with the crowd which was a good experience.
this place is full of symbolism, here you can see the cremation ceremonies and all the rithuals related with it.is really interesting. the architecture is beautiful.

expensive for foreigners (1,000 rupees per ticket) but interesting to view the cremation ceremonies going on - if you can cope with the grieving and the smell/smoke. plenty of places to get good views if you cross the river and climb up a bit. no need to take a guide (plenty of 'guides' offering their services at the ticket counter).

1,000 rupees per foreigner for entry ticket. we ignored the many requests/offers for people to be our guide after we'd bought our tickets - and turns out we didn't need one. it's not difficult to just walk around and self-guide! it's sobering and sad to see/watch the funerals and cremations (best viewed by crossing the river by bridge and climbing up a bit to the viewing areas. also worth climbing to top of steps and looking around the stupas up there. after finishing there, we crossed back over and walked around the other temple areas. there's one temple area where foreigners with a paid ticket can avoid the long queue (and no need to take shoes off) to go in past the security guard (by the 'exit').which we did - although you can't go inside. nor can you take photos beyond a certain point. worth spending 2-3 hours here in total.

very old shiva temple, reasonably clean as temples go. no great push for donations by any priest. you can feel your faiths spirituality in the temple. you can also elect to do special puja if you wish which is performed outside the main temple but within the precinct. no pushing etc and lovely experience. do elect a less busy time and day, the hotel where you are staying will be able to guide you. once you enter the temple you can hire a temple trust guide at a reasonable price who can take you around and thus make a more pleasant experience for you.

this is one of the must see sites for nay visit to kathmandu. my favorite part is to just wander among the older temples at the top of the hill above the river. it is very interesting to see the ritual involved with the cremations. this is a place not to be hurried through.

when you are on the entrance gate suddenly some man ask you buying tickets when they sad tourist face. if you are indian and nepalese pilgrims tourist then you can go main temple,pay god and not need buy ticket but non hindu's must buy ticket. and also not permission go to inside the main temple. near by a river, fire burning. actually, this is criminal thing after hindu's people death. death people families, friend and relatives are crying, they lost man! you might be feel also sad! there is sadu baba. baba looks funny. everything, perfect. great experienced! i like, recommendation this nice place.

only by contemplating death can we appreciate life (a personal view). being able to watch some of the actions leading up to the cremations on site certainly made me think about life and death. it could feel voyeuristic to watch the burning of bodies but the temple vibe was too uplifting for that.
very weird and scary place where you see people cremated. i am not into temples but i guess it was weird/interesting to see the cremated process.

so this place is okay, i'll be honest and tell you that i have seen much better temples in my time. tourists have to pay to get in can't remember how much but indians and locals go free. so firstly there appears to be more than one entrance, our taxi driver took us to not the wrong entrance but one which entailed a fair bit of walking. (less than the other entrance i'm guessing) let's just say we took the scenic route. anyway on our walk we saw lots of cremations, we had to tell our 5 year old they were burning logs. my main advice is that as a tourist if a local offers to take you around, just refuse. he will ask for $30 after he's taken you around. it's a con, he won't show you anything you can't see yourself. the only thing that he will do is he will take you down a back way and get a 'priest' to pray with you. the 'priest' is also getting his share of the €30. if you are a tourist all you need to do is put your shoes in the bag, get a ticket for them, and go for a wander around the temple. there is the facility to wash your feet should you wish. enjoy!
really interesting hindu temple. there is always a funeral going on so you can see this side of life as well.

i only managed to get there after dark but was lucky enough to catch the aarathi ritual, which was lively and enriching. there were also multiple public cremations going on at the same time. even though it was night time, the place was still full of activity. i had a local guide so it was helpful to have him describe some of the ongoings for me. wish i had more time to visit in the day - next time!

pashupatinath is arguably the best place to visit in nepal. the temple is very unique in design and so is the idol worshiped inside. you can have the view of the idol from all the 4 sides of the main temple. mobile is allowed but photography is not.
