i thought it would be interesting to watch the cremation ceremony, but i didn't realize it would be uncomfortable (at least for me) seeing corpse plus the smell of the smoke too. i saw some people wearing mask so i suggest you bring one. there is nothing much to see on the temple itself because it is for hindu only. but overall it was a new experience for me. entrance fee for foreigners is 1000. and taxi from here to thamel costs 300. not sure though it if is a good deal, but i think yes it is, considering the traffic in kathmandu.
this place has made me rethink my ideas on death itself. i must admit it's quite a shock to see the cremations from up close but it's beautiful in it's own way, very educational as a westerner.

while the pashupati natu in itself it is beautiful. much history. over 1000 people cremated there a day. the ceremony began a 7 pm . it was beautiful to me as a send off of the soul leaving the body and let the familys, while sad, help celebrate that their soul has moved on. may bother some but i loved it.

there is a river where they put the dead after burning them. and places for women and men to pray, separately (it's a temple). and those funny and cheerful holy men! and the monkeys are not missing, they are always feeding themselves in the place. nice, picturesque, i only was not interested in cremations.
kailash travel advised us to go here and they were right, walking up the lane to the temple we found the nepali buying ceremonial things for the cremation ceremonial. we were really impressed.

i was really looking forward to witnessing this hindu ritual and although the temple is always open, i later learned that no cremation occurs after dusk. there were very few people there, the holy men had all gone home and other than the structure itself, there wasn't much to see. i should have done my research to maximize my experience and show the respect this site deserves.

the temple was beautiful. the arts of dwelling is awesome and the burning carcass make you think of life. the temple is one of the must visit place in kathmandu. perfect to visit at evening during aarati

this is a great stop if you have some time to kill before you fly out of kathmandu as it is right next to the airport. go in the late afternoon/early evening if you can. this was one of the highlights of my trip. you can really feel the spirituality and history in this place,
i was in awe of the spiritual vibes and history of this sacred place. i spent several hours here and was really glad i made this visit a part of my trip
if you walk here from boudhanath, you could enter from near the river without any entrance fees. managed to witness the cremation process which was quite an eye opener.

this is, apparently, a very important location for hindu people and that was clear today. nepal new year was probably not the best day to visit! very interesting with many holy men on site. walked through to the deer park and down to the river on the far side. so many monkeys!
we didn’t go inside the temple as we aren’t hindu but did go out to see where 24 hour cremations take place on the bank of a small river. an absolutely fascinating place. loved it here.

pasupathinath temple was a relief to the mind and soul. although access road was in bad shape, it was pleasant clean and spiritual inside. a long queu did not hamper the feelings. it was indeed a cleansing occasion

it is a must if you just want to learn a little more about your spirituality and the values that it can deeply take when you enter in to other ways of tkinking. we don't have to agree or disagree, just take it as another approach to open our mind on human beings !

we had half a day free and someone recommended that we visit this temple to better understand the people of nepal. when we walked in, i remember thinking that i have no idea what i am looking at as there are no helpful and informative signs in english. you have to pay 1000 nepali rupee entrance fee so understanding what is going on would be nice! a helpful young man introduced himself and asked if we wanted a tour. price for a guide seems to be 1000 for about an hour but we bargained for 900 as we could more easily split the sum by the 3 of us. this guide was called sumit kharel highly recommend him! it’s a fascinating place but you do need a guide to explain everything. much of it was damaged in the 2015 earthquake but the biggest and holiest temple was unscathed. only hindu’s can enter though. probably the most fascinating part was seeing how they burn the bodies next to the holy( but grubby ) river called bagmati river. it feeds into the ganges. the kids try to fish out coins with a magnet but honestly. you would not want to be near that water! there is an old people’s home in the compound and a hospice next to the cremation area. our tour lasted about 90 minutes and we paid up the requested 1000 rupees. it was worth every one of them!

visited on a festival day. was wonderful to see so many happy families in their colourful saris celebrating their faith. further exploration of the site took us to the cremation area. as an australian it was confronting to witness a cremation in progress and i felt for the grieving families but it was an interesting experience. a definite must do.

visited this temple as part of a full day sightseeing tour with himalayan social journey. very interesting commentary from our guide and whilst most westerners might find it a little bit morbid to visit such a public cremation site, it was still good to see and learn about the nepalese traditions and beliefs. quite emotional but worthwhile seeing.

i love this place and found myself there on many occasions. i didn’t give it an excellent review because you can’t really get close to the main temples unless you are hindu and there can be some real pressure from people who try to trick you into guiding you, then guilting you. be respectful and kind and it will take you far, but avoid the hard sell and tricksters. the ghats are a must see and require walking around a bit, this is sacred space. be sure to walk up the hill behind the ghats opposite the river from the temple.

this is a beautiful part of kathmandu that is full of nepalese culture. there are cows, covered in flowers of worship, monkeys, sadhus (definitely worth getting a photo with these religious men - you can’t miss them - they’ll be very colourful but you’ll have to negotiate payment to pose with them). some of the buildings surrounding the temple are suffering structural issues due to the earthquake a few years ago. it’s quite sad to see the devastating effect it has had. the funeral pyres are also visible, lining the river. throughout the morning, you will see bodies being prepared in the river, wrapped in orange garments and burnt upon the fires. once completely burnt, the ashes are pushed into the river, which is difficult to see when, just further up stream, there are children playing and women washing their laundry. if you’re in kathmandu, this place is a must-visit.

it is not usual to have a funeral under open special in asian country. you could learn the culture and traditions of hindu"s funeral - that they burn dead bodies beside the river that they believe could wash their sin and bring them to afterlife. stay quiet and respect people under the ceremony; and you could not enter the temple unless you are a hindu.
great temple by the looks and weirdly moving and intimate to watch the cremation ceremonies. it has the potential to improve a tabooed western attitude towards death and mourning.

the site itself is very interesting as you get to see the holy men around some of the temples. however once you got close to the holy river, it was hard to ignore the strong smell from the live cremation that is performed there. the experience itself was interesting but it was quiet overwhelming to see the burning of the bodies.

i recently visited to pashupatinath temple in kathmandu. temple is very beautiful, also the incarnation is taken place behind the temple. when i was in kathmandu the temperature was very cold which was around 14 degree celcius in the morning. i really feel very pleasant atmosphere. also i visited narayanhiti durbar but unfortunately it is closed on tuesday & wednesday so i missed. i will again visit to this place.
on a lazy saturday , mr and my friends decided to visit pashupati nath temple. though crowded on saturdays it was nice to see the worshipers thronging the temple. must visit the temple at least once.

i went to nepal with my maa. i really like the pashupatinath temple (gaushala). also cold atmosphere which was 14 degree celsius in morning. also i visited the narayanhity palace, unfortunately it was closed on tuesday & wednesday so due to less time i missed. only negative thing is pollution you the wear the mask due to dust. but overall experience was very nice i want to go again.
one of the most holy temples of the hindu faith. it has great architecture in and around the temple. do visit when not too crowded to walk around comfortably around the place.

amazing temple and architecture. you are only allowed inside if you are a hindu. there was a man offering advice about blessing times, and claimed to be s holy man. asking for money outside the temple and followed us inside, pestering us for money in exchange for ‘blessings’ .it’s a shame people choose to try to make money this way at a holy site . lots of monkeys around inside and outside the temple. if you are a tourist people will convince you to buy lots of things to give as blessings. be careful
so nice temple ,it is hindu biggest god mahadev shiva´s temple. really beautiful ,many many small temples are there around pashupati .it is very ancient temple.

i was apprehensive to visit this religious site due to the fact they burn bodies but i found it the most calming, peaceful and spiritual place. it's truly somewhere i would recommend to everyone visiting kathmandu and wanting to escape the madness of it all!

not being hindu, this was very strange yet fascinating. this location is a long a holy river. this site is also a place of pilgrimage as well as cremation. to understand and appreciate what you are saying, a guide is most worthwhile. as a tourist, we were able to walk along one side of the river and watch several cremations taking place. the wrapped bodies, after being anointed by water from the river, are placed on wooden logs and straw is placed around the body. we observed as various rituals were taking place.
this is a beautiful temple & definitely worth a visit. although a sombe please to visit, it is very interesting too.

i had reservations about coming here as i'd never seen a dead body before. it was an extremly moving sight to see the ceremony of hindu people saying goodbye to their recently deceased loved ones. something i will never forget.

admission 1,000r. as well as being nepal’s holiest pilgrimage site and the site of one of the world’s most important siva temples, this site is also a working crematorium, which might not be to everyone’s taste. although there is a lot else to see it is hard to ignore the funeral pyres alongside the river and the resulting smoke.
pashupati nath temple shows the image of god shiva, the hindus people have a lot respect and pray to god shiva .

unesco heritage site and well deserved. it is a complex of hindu temples mostly unaffected by tourists. sadhu and fakirs happily do their things though you can spot the ones who has their eyes on tourist $. as you walk further upstream, you can see rituals by family members preparing to cremate the body of their loved ones. we saw several of these simultaneously at different stages of their rituals. respectfully we all stood across the small river to observe this. not for the faint-hearted.

this was a very interesting and informative place to visit to understand the history and the details of the services that take place their. it gives you great insight into the spirituality of the locals and was very unique. the buildings and architecture are amazing, great for people watching and i loved seeing the monkeys roaming freely about. worth the visit!

this important site is a must visit to get a real feel of religious life in nepal. this is where bodies are cremated so is rather somber but fascinating. lots of amazing small and large temples on the grounds which are very extensive.

this complex cost 1000 rupees ($10) which is excellent for a world heritage site. the money goes to good use for repairs from the earthquake a few years ago. there are local guides around the ticket booth. our guide was awesome, charging 500 rupees ($5) per person for an hour tour. his explaination of the culture and customs was invaluable. the main temple only hindus can enter but you can get a glimpse inside through the gate. however the rest of the complex is open for us. we went down by the river to see the open creamations. there are usually about 35 creamations a day. crossing over the river we went to see the holy men and where they live. they are glad to give you a blessing and have a photo taken with them, but expect to tip. on site is also mother teresa’s home for the poor.

a must see. something you won’t see many places in the world. close to the airport. funerals normally take place in the morning but continue to afternoon and are everyday. 1,000 rupees for tourists to enter
this place is a huge temple and cremation ground. many fierce barking dogs are there. some saints and local people see you that you are not expected. only hindus can enter to the temple,

this was one of the more memorable places we visited during our week in nepal. at the heart of this complex of temples and protected buildings is the riverside open crematorium around which distraught relatives gather to pay their respects to loved ones. while it at first feels as though you are intruding on a private moment, many nepalese unconnected with the grieving families are also present and seem to spend part of their day relaxing here. it's a place that is in some ways harrowing but also enlightening. relatives purify their dead loved one on the banks of the river, before carrying the body to one of the cremation areas where they are placed onto a wood pile which is then lit. the air is occasionally dense with smoke and there is nothing really hidden from view here. there are real tears, genuine sad dramas unfolding here in what is a traditional farewell for nepalese deceased. it's expensive, by local standards, to get in (about $10), but the experience is worthwhile for a fascinating insight into nepalese life and death. it is, of course, not everyone's idea of a vacation visit, so choose wisely. guides tend to loiter around the ticket area and latch onto your accent before offering to escort you in. it's hard to shake them, but their commentary is useful (well, our guy, anyway), and they simply ask you to pay what you feel for their service, when i followed this advice i was then asked for extra notes in my own currency (or my foreign note collection), so they obviously have an amount in mind. either way, this temple will stay with me forever as a heart-tugging place that lifts the lid on a, perhaps, little known aspect of nepalese religious culture.

this site is an important funeral site for the hindus of kathmandu. a river runs through the site, where families cremate their dead and then throw the ashes in the water. at any time, there are several funerals ongoing along the water, so this is an opportunity to see this practice. there are several mini-temples worth seeing, but the main temple is not accessible to non-hindus.

as a devout hindu, this place holds a certain attraction for me and anyone like me. this is an ancient shiva temple located at the foothills of mystical and of mythological significance himalayas. a must visit place for religious people as well as history buffs alike.

go there early morning (around 8) and walk around, see family bringing their dead bodies before cremation, preparing them and finally burning them. you cannot visit the temple by itself but already a lot to contemplate from the outside.

there appears to be two entrances to the temple, although i was taken through the entrance where the dead bodies were cremated. avoid this entrance. the temple itself is huge and it was divine to watch the four faces of shivalinga.
this is hindus cremation main center and most of the hindus people visit every day for praying here.
not only highest hindu temple, crematorium is different. monkey & the holy river remind past and future life

i asked around, and searched the internet, looking for snake charmers in kathmandu and the overwhelming response was, if they can be found, they would be in pashupatinath. i had forgotten this is also the place where families take their deceased loved ones, cremate their bodies, and leave the remains in the river. there were four cremations going on when i got there in the mid afternoon with two more being prepared. there are several sets of stairs that take you to other places in the park, and there are monkeys here, though, not as many as there are at monkey (swayambhunath) temple and this is the first place i found actual hindu holy men. i spoke with a "guide" on my way out who confirmed for me there are snake charmers here, they just weren't there the day of my visit.

we came here and i was a little hesitant as i am hindu myself and this hits close to home. however, we walked in and the complex was interesting, with lots of unusual buildings. we then got to the gnats where the ceremonial burning pyres are and the smell is overwhelming but the whole ritual is emotional and i was just watching families, the rituals and just overcome with emotion at it all. hard to take but worth a visit.

it is, indeed, quite an experience to see a body being cremated in front of your eyes (before the remains are thrown into the river below). other than this, and the presence of a few sadus (always good for photo opportunities), the main temple is only accessible to hindus and the rest of the area, although interesting, is probably not the best sight in kathmandu.
a must pilgrimage for every hindu is shree pashupatinath temple. had to face long queues having visited on a monday. great peace of mind.

pashupatinath temple is considered to be one of the most important temple for hindus. it is a major attraction during the mahashivratri festival. people from all parts of nepal & india visit this temple to offer prayers to lord shiva.
world heritage temple, located in huge area, not maintained well but now some renovation work has started.

in my opinion this place could be skipped the driver dropped me and my friend off at the entrance and we paid 1,000 rupees for the entrance fee (which our hotel’s owner already warned us we don’t need to pay for it, expensive and can’t see anything much) of course there is something to see like architechture and how they burn death body near the river, but that’s all we could see foreigners aren’t allowed to pass the hindu place where things happen there (that should be a highlight there) so this place isn’t worth 1,000 rupees in my opinion and we also think there must be the gate you can just easily walk in without paying for the entrance fee cause we got lost there and found out therr is no reason to pay for it

experience this religious hub for a mix of religious, cultural and spiritual experiences. very emotional to watch the cremation of hindus take place on raised platforms along the river. amazing that pashupatinath temple is one of the 8 unesco cultural heritage sites of the kathmandu valley. a must see.
this is one the famous hindu holy place. this place give you peace of mind. this temple is only 1km from airport.

if you are interested in the rituals surrounding death in the hindu religion, this is the place for you to visit. you will see dead bodies, women wailing, strong smells and emotional sights along the bagmati river. i would not recommend children visit this area.

interesting experience is the only way to describe this visit. as other post explain, it’s not something you see every day. burning bodies during a burial ceremony while people linger around and watch. as a non-hindu we were on the opposite side of the river in the tourist area. our guide was very informative and we were very happy to have all of his knowledge however they are very non-official guides and expect a nice tip when your done. they also make sure they take you past the sadhus so you can sit with them and take a pic as your being “blesses” and they also expect and ask for a tip. so expect to pay quite a bit more then the entrance fee. overall it’s quite an experience that you definitely always feel like an outsider when your there as a westerner but everyone is used to it and everyone is very nice too. interesting experience indeed.

if you are not a hindu believer and coming for pray i don't recommend for visiting. place is really not well maintained and you will not see anything at all paying 1000 rupees as a foreigner. the simply will not let you inside main temple even if you paid, u can see cremation thought, but if you are soooooo interested. this process i personally cannot watch under any circumstances, i don't find exciting watch dead people. with all respect - my hubby is hindu. there are a lot of nicer temples and stupas for visiting in katmandu! just skip it. save time and money, it also situated far from center.

it is a unique experience where you can get to see cremation of the dead by their grieving families beside the river that hindus revere. i thought about how i would feel if i was one of them and it felt intrusive. to me. having said that, it is a crowded place and the locals seem to be used to it, so it may well be me imposing my views. i would note that non hindus are not allowed into the temple and so i cannot comment on what can be seen there.

this is the complex very unusuall way of cremation , large hindu temple with a atmosphere of old time. it seem that you are in the old time, many centuries ago, here the time is stopped. the hindu people do this cremation few hours after death, during the all day, never mind of the time. it is needed several hours to burned a human body and throw the dust in the holly river bagmati there are a lot of people with orange and colorful clothes, in ritual behave and with a holly manners.
the peaceful place u will find in kathmandu valley.environment is clean .not much crowded (except shivarati and othere occcasion) and entrance is free,

this place is sacred temple for hindus. the temple considered the biggest and most important here. besides prayers, this place also served as a place to manage demised bodies in a most respectful way followed by burning ceremony
one of the oldest temple in the world, it is located at the heart of the city and very near to airport. it is a must see famous temple

very near to airport, it is lord shiva temple. lots of shiva lingas are found in the temple. aarthi dharshan in evening is super. one of the oldest shiva temple in the world. powerful and beautiful lord shiva temple.

devotees of lord shiva , throng this temple to offer prayers .it is a blessing to be able to do this , once in a lifetime . but like all other hindu temples , the crowds are huge and frenzied .although we did get the chance to glimpse the deity , it was not possible to offer a prayer for more than a few seconds .the architecture is unique , with many small temples for different gods of the hindus . but , like all other hindu temples , this place is more a tourist attraction than a place of worship . it is a jewel of nepal

quite scary seeing cremation of dead bodies near the river. the place is very smoky and my hair stick with ashes. i feel sad at that place. waited till after 6pm for the fire show and people are jolly about celebration of god.
it is located on the bank of bagmati river.it is one of the biggest hindus temple. it is ancient temple .

i often visit this place with friends i like to spent time here seating top hill have many chares to have relax but the thing wich i didn't liked there's rule for non hindu can't enter what's strange shouldn't i think.

to visit this crematorium area may seem macabre and i resisted visiting till my 3rd trip to nepal. this is the circle of life and we as westerners need to embrace like the nepali people. they show their grief publicly and that's a good thing in my book. as a contrast on the way out our guide saw a hindu wedding. he asked if our group could visit and we were welcomed like long lost friends. i feel so blessed to have been able to witness these scenes.

i love this comples of ancient temples dedicated to shiva as "lord of animals" - so much jungle & so many monkeys - tourists aren't allowed in most of the temples (discrimination) & he over zealous gaurds will try to force you to pay 1000 pupees ($10) to just walk onto the premises. you can always go through the jungle paths to avoid the fees, but it's still a bit of a search to find the best places to see there- i recommend going off the trails into the sleshmantak jungles & also sitting at the sri goraknath temple at the very top of the hill. the babas (hindu renunciates) there wear huge earrings through the center of their ears which supposedly opens a psychic channel.
pashupati temple lies in the central as well as capital of nepal. it was wonderful hindu temple and i conclude my trip was worth visiting with my wife.
that is the place of burning death people, everything is smelling, thick air , different people. bagmati dirty holy river. must tell you that it is really place it should bee seen
it is fascinating to watch this temple with it's own cremation ghat. beware of pandas. haggle a lot at the shops outside, almost to 1/3rd of the price.
it is situated from main kathmandu around 3kms. you can go easily by any auto here. famous of shiva temple and very large nearby bagmati river.

this is the site where many citizens of kathmandu bring their dead relatives to be cremated. it is always smoky - bring a mask. it also has many monkeys, dogs and cows, which seems irreverent, but wasn't in fact. very moving sight.

right in the heart of the city, you'll see it as you enter the city from the airport. the actual complex consists of many temples and occupies a huge area. this attracts hordes of hindus and the temple is usually packed on any day. the approach road is narrow and dirty. note - only hindus are allowed inside. also, photography is strictly prohibited of the deities, main temples etc

this is a great religious (hindu) place. entry to inside the temple is restricted only to hindus. there was a lady outside trying to sell a beaded chain (rudraksha mala) to me so that i can pass off as a hindu.:). depending up on the time of visit, the place can get very crowded. a visit is highly recommended.

the holy shrine of lord shiva on the banks of river bagmati. great pilgrimage place for the hindus. the temple compound is filled with other smaller temples.one can arrange to have special puja done by paying to the mandir committee.
been to this temple because of hindu religion, very crowded and not to clean. not very organised, ok for once
its great pleasure to go there, evening times aarati make peace on mind, its a main temple for the hundus, located bank of bagmati river

pashupati nath is situated hardly 2.5 kms from the airport and is easily accessible. avoid mondays as there is insanely long queue even at 6 in morning to visit the pilgrimage. this is a holy place for hindus (both indian and nepalese) and dedicated to lord shiva and is one of the 16 jyotilings.

even if non hindus are not allowed in the temple, the temple area is beautiful. it is a very pity, that the earth quake destroyed so many relicts. still not built up. unesco gave the money, but the rebuilding goes very slow.
great temple. a fusion of buddhist and hindu architecture, a nice old temple. well preserved and clean.
the area is crowded and messy. if you look like a foreigner, you are not permitted to enter the temple.

pashpatinath is one of the most mind changing places i have visited last year. my ideas about burial or burning death people have completly changed. it’s amazing how these mostly hindu people deal with death and how we as western people can be part of their rituals, without being disrespectfull.

another great blessing to be able to come to pasupathinath temple for the 4th time in my life, only god knows who i can thank for this and really privileged to have my wife together this time, managed to get to the the main shrine although brief but was very thankful for the arrangements

pashupati nath was great. before you enter, you have to pay around 10 dollar. the price is kind of high. but the atmospfere at pashupati also get me high. i loved it, esspecially i did no tour with a guide, but with a streetchild. i was blessed to meet this awesome young guy. he told me everything about the place.
this a hindu temple. every hindu person should visit this amazing place. don't forget to take pictures of sadhu also.
we had spiritual walks in the temple and had an opportunity to see four doors opened. it was a mesmerizing experiences although it was crowded by flux of devotees.

pashupatinath is not anymore what it was before.that is a fact. but they take too long for rebuilding and rebuilded houses look not so nice. nevertheless it is worth visiting, because you still feel the spirit of shiva and his devotees.
beautiful active hindu temple where cremations are done by the river (so polluted though). it's an incredible experience to see that.

1,000 rupee government enforced entrance fee ( helps with restoration ) i had an official guide / which i don’t normally bother with but there is so much history, culture and information to take in i thought it well worth it and my guide was excellent for 2000 rupees. i was there for more than 2.5 hrs - quite strange to stand and observe the end of life for the hindu religion. i would definitely recommend a visit here as it is the most holiest temple for hindus in nepal.
a live working temple, with a rich heritage. the main temple is for hindus only, but you are free to wander around the main temple.
a place where you get a glimpse of the past and architecture. it's a shame that the government does not maintain these place.

there is a long history of this place about the lord siva and the hindu myth, which is definitely worth the read and exploring. our trip was organised with tour guide and has lots of knowledge. while there is plenty to see in this area and smoothly the tour guide handle it. its really for locals and those of nepalese/indian heritage and big temple in history. there are certain areas that are off limits to foreigners it means only hindu beside other not allows inside the main temple gate .

they charge you 1000 to enter into the gate then don’t let you inside the temple. no whites allowed. they should tell u before they charge you . oh wait there’s a crematorium if you want to see dead bodies

there is a long history of this place, which is definitely worth the read. while there is plenty to see in this area, its really for locals and those of nepalese/indian heritage. there are certain areas that are off limits to foreigners.

it costs 1000rupees to get in and you a double side printed pamphlet telling you all about the significance of the temple and some amazing stories about shiva etc. we spent ten mins reading all this and walked along the banks of the river where cremations are being carried out and reach the temple just to be told that non hindus are not allowed. we felt that this was very calculated in getting foreigners to pay huge amounts and giving all this information about a temple that you can't even visit. we were very disappointed as were other tourists in the place. it would have been nice to know this fact before purchasing a ticket.

a really intense place. it’s sacred and every hindu should visit it. the staff is friendly and helps you with darshan. no photography is allowed inside the temple. the cremation alongside river bagmati is heart touching.

the most important hindu temple in the city. non hindus cannot go inside the actual temple but that's only a small part of the complex and grounds. ceremonies outside, cremation pyres at the river are an eye opening experience.

a place of death, ultimately. it was my first introduction to hinduism in the country and i must say it didn't draw me in. there are people who come here for their final days to die, people being burnt. that said it is very interesting and there was some beautiful architecture, and walks into a little hill settlement round the corner
we visited in the morning around 8.30am. we went from hotel royal singi takshi took 250 n.rupees. god shivas you can worship.
i really recommended this sivan temple. we can feel the positive energy in this temple. it was very pack on maha sivarathri. other days will be normal.

i was born in nepal. kathmandu is a beautiful valley with a lot of places that is listed in world heritage. but when we talk about pashupatinath temple, it is one holy place that one should visit. its loaded with tons of monkeys for sure! but the temple is oldest and most beautiful! people across the world come to visit the temple! the time of shivaratri is tough time to get in and see the shivalinga! the entire process of worshiping the god is amazing! the sadhus sitting on the bagmati riverside are always peaceful and they have been there for pretty long time!

as a westerner it was slightly weird to witness the burning of bodies alongside the river as a guy brushed the embers into the waters and we photographed the whole process. interesting in so far as cultures go.
wonderful temple. a must visit for all hindus. non hindus are apparently not allowed in the temple. will definitely visit again.
a beautiful temple surrounded by tons of monkeys piles of garbage . total mood killer. but worth a visit if you have the time while in katmandu.

phashupatinath temple is famous and very sacred hindu temple of lord shiva. the area of pashupatinath temple has many other temples ,it is hard to explain the experience of visiting pashupatinath temple ,so peacefull,one should visit this temple at least once or again and again.

centuries old temple, beliefs are there to claim the existence of a temple at the place from the third century bce. it survived the earthquake of 2015 without any damage. the entry is exclusive to hindus. though others can visit the area.the temple is at the bank of river bagmati. there is one main temple surrounded by many other small temples. this is a temple of lord shiva. check the local newspaper to find out the timings when all the four doors are open. there is arrangement for special pujas also on chargeable basis. we find monkeys all around the temple area so we must keep the prasad hidden. mobiles are not allowed to use within the main premise. we can't take pictures also inside the temple.

it is one of the historical and religious of nepal ,also famous for hindu pilgrims from all over the word ,i really enjoyed the art and architecture of this temple. i felt honored to see the ritual of life and listen to the lovely music. like most sacred places you just naturally speak in soft tones and move with an awareness.

it is hard to explain the experience of visiting pashupatinath. the cremation ceremony is heavy to witness, but also beautiful. make sure to get a guide that explains everything that is going on, but make sure you settle on a price before!

this old temple had a rich history ,related with bhole ,lord shiva or shivay.this is one among jothirling ,visit to kathmandu is not complete without visit to this place .in the evening around 6pm , gathering will be comparatively less and one can get blessings easily

must visit place in kathmandu for hindu. big temple with peaceful atmosphere. temple staff does a good job of keeping the place clean. one can see life and death side by side. one should see the cremation area behind the temple.
pashupatinath temple is one of the biggest hindu temple it's world heritage site,recommends to visit

this must be one of the to do list in kathmandu. very beautiful especially in the morning. mandir opens at 4 am and closes at 7 pm. please do remember the timings as we had to return back since we reached at 7.03 pm .
pashupatinath is a holy place of hindu and it is the best place to be from every aspect whether it be for the religious or architectural or spiritual purpose.

the crematory ghats at this world heritage site are sombre and make one reflect on the hindu way of accepting death compared to the western way. it is a moving experience in a field of temples and a sacred river.
the experience there is astonishing. you'll be able to see the hindu rites along the river. this is very emotional and you'll never forget it for sure.

the temple itself must be beautiful, but paying 1000 nrs to walk around the patio and see monkeys (not even well looked after monkeys, there was a dead baby monkey!) is a total rob. i'd understand maybe half of that to support the maintenance but paying that amount of money and then not even be allowed to see the temple (which looked incredible peering from the door) because we are not hindu is absolutely ridiculous! speaking with hindu friends i've understood the religious never forbid entrance to temples to people of other religions so i find this ban real nonsense. i'd understand not entering with cameras, touching or certain behaviours but i recommend people not to enter the "temple" area and proceed directly to the crematory.

the place is very good for hindu believers as well as others . some of the places are restricted for believers of other religions. the place is perfect to visit and changes the spirit of everyone by the view of buring corpse by the bank of bagmati river.

pashupatinath temple is one of the biggest and historic temple in kathmandu. this world heritage site makes visitors wow . situated near tribhuban international airport , pashupatinath is the must visit place if u are in kathmandu or outside.

it was a totally different experience for us. lots of visitors, though most of them were locals, a good number of tourists was also seen. there is no ticket required for saarc citizens, which was a plus.

my daughter and some locals talked me into this one. i'm so thankful they did. we went on a holiday at sunset, perfect timing. i felt honored to see the ritual of life and listen to the lovely music. like most sacred places you just naturally speak in soft tones and move with an awareness.
never miss this temple if you are visiting katmandu. it is a beautiful shiva temple. shoe lockers are available
located near the katmandu airport. many devotees and tourists visit this temple. easily accebile.one of the best temples in nepal
we go long way road to there, and cant go inside the temple, but i do respect people who hindusm. but at least they give as map, things we can visit so we dont need to pay the tour guide
felt like a bit of a voyeur watching the funeral ceremonies but at the same time found it fascinating. the whole temple complex is well worth a visit.
i was been pashupati nath by taxi .spend there a few hours.very ancient and beautiful hindus god mahadev temple.

pashupatinath temple is one of the historical temple in kathmandu valley. it is one the world heritage site. it is a must place to visit if you are in kathmandu or in other cities. it is easy to locate as is situated near tribhuvan international airport.

this temple complex is the holiest place for hindus from nepal. it was fascinating to see the temples and buildings on the other side of the river and the people, especially the sadhus. i felt privileged to be able to watch the burial ceremonies and try to understand one of the important elements of hindu beliefs. our local guide was a great help for understanding what we were seeing.

pashupatinath temple is one of the biggest and historic temple in kathmandu. this world heritage site makes visitors wow . situated near tribhuban international airport , pashupatinath is the must visit place if u are in kathmandu or outside.

one of the most fascinating places to visit if you get the opportunity to visit kathmandu. significant religious and cultural experience that one should go through regardless of your background. you do have to navigate your way through the pesky stall owners that try to sell you items. if you're not a believer then you may get restricted access that may limit the overall experience. in my humble opinion you need a drive and a driver to get the most out of this experience. scary but awesome. smelly and blizzard but worth it!

this head of the12 jyotirlinga shrines of lord shiva is the major attraction of kathmandu. the original construction of the temple is unknown. the current structure was built in 15th century by lichhabi kingdom. the temple complex is located 5 km from city centre closed to the airport on the river bagmati. around the main 2 storied temple complex you will find rama temple, guhyeswari temple and vaishnabi temple. the temple wooden door is beautiful and a must see. once you enter the gate the first thing you see is the large golden statue of nandi, the famous bull of lord shiva. the tempke area is about 264 hectares with total 518 temples inside. you need to keep your shoes in one of the shops from you can buy offerings at npr 150 onwards. they will provide you rubber sleepers which you need to keep near the main gate and enter bare foot. we visited pn a wednesday and fortunately there was very less crowd inside. we could go close to the deity and quietly give our offerings peacefully. we did a complete parikrama of the temple. the main burning ghat of kathmandu lies behind the temple complex and hindus feels it holy to have the last rites at this place. you can see the pyers burning from the backside of the temple complex. a scene not recommended if you have timid heart. last but not the least. do take note of one important fact. do never try to take photographs once you cross the main gate and don't even take out your mobile also. it can get permanently confiscated. there is no entry fee in the complex.
when i visited this ancient temple of lord shiva in kathmandu i was totally surprised with the architecture and the huge nandy bronze statue.

a moving and deeply symbolic place where hindu’s clean and cremate their dead. it’s hard to explain the cultural gap between the western sanitisation, medicalisation and hiding of death and it’s rituals: and this public, symbolic, emotional practical disposal of the dead. surrounding this open, routine management of the end of life, the street kids smile and ask for chocolate whilst the cremated remains of citizens are swept into the holy river so that another pyre can be built. it’s not shocking, but it is thought provoking.

only hindu inside temple is strictly enforced. but many other things to see , even monkeys. several cremations at the river in progress on my visit. interesting to observe cultural and religious ritual.respectful and mournful. cremation similar to that performed in india. may be challenging to observe.

located on the bank of holy bagmati river in kathmandu, nepal, the lord pashupatinath temple is a holy and sacred place every hindu people worldwide. built in malla era, thousands year ago, it is believed that the roof of the temple is made of pure gold and walls of pure silver. in hindu mythology, it is said that the lord shiv used to play and wander around this holy land including sleshmantak forest with his beloved wife parvati incarnating different forest animals. people cremate their deceased beloved ones on the bank of bagmati river and on the palm of lord pashupatinath. it is believed that once cremate there, the deceased soul rest in peace and heaven. nepal government has build electric crematorium nearby now to save environment and forest but people's strong belief on final rites of their beloved ones in traditional fire pyre is so strong that any one can see lines of burning pyres daily. once you see, the burning pyre, you will be nostalgic and rethink about yourself. many people abandon their bad and unsocial activities and changed themselves after visiting this holy place. this holy place is a home of many sadhus as well. they are always praying for peace and prosperity around the globe. shivaratri (birthday of lord shiva), balachaturdashi and teej (a day when mother parvati remain on fast and beg lord shiva as her husband) are the main days around a year when there is a huge crowd gather to offer lord shiva their sacred gifts, worship and pray. the main temple has four doors depicting four sides on earth and four mouth of the lord shiva. it is believed that the pray, worship and arati shoud go daily without any interrupt. so malla king started appointing the main priest from south india. at that time, it was believed that all the people in nation are the children of the king and once the kind dies, the children are automatically on sorrow and they can not worship and pray. only hindu people are allowed to enter into the holy temple premises but one can study and enjoy the atmosphere just sitting next hill to the river which is a part of sleshmantak forest. hundreds of small and large temples including ram mandir and guhyashwari temples are around the holy site. the site is just a kilometer away from tribhuvan international airport and many 3-4 star hotels are available there for domestic and international guest.
visiting the temple as not a devotee is gives us different perspective. lot of people and crowd. enjoyed the construction of the buildings.
its a very big temple. has four doors. a different feeling when you visit this holy place however cleanliness is an issue.

do remember the following when going- only hindu nepali/indian citizens are able to enter the main temple and there are guards to stop you if you try. luckily, i was able to say i was indian and they let me through. there are a lot of dead bodies being cremated in the back. you have to check in your shoes in a bag at the beginning- they give you a locker key and you come back for it. overall an experience to remember
the hindu temple is quite beautiful and actually my favorite of all the temples we visited. the site is quite foreign and definitely worthy of a bucket list.

must place to go! learn about hindu religious, cultural and traditions. place of silent, life of death, we spent a long time there to see the start to finished. can’t express through words and you must go see! this place is close to airport and it’s cost around 500-600 rupees depend on traffic hours ( one way).

this place is one of the must-see-palces in kathmandu. but: if you are not nepali you will be left with a lot of questions after your visit. so better prepare with a local (guide) – and take care of the "holy men" around, they are perfect business men. . . - but to understand nepali life, you should go here even questions will be raised. . .

pashupatinath temple. dedicated to lord shiva, pashupatinath is one of the four most important religious sites in asia for devotees of shiva. built in the 5th century and later renovated by malla kings, the site itself is said to have existed from the beginning of the millennium when a shiva lingam was discovered here. hindu death body crematory here. you can see funeral process about here. really worth to visit it.

many nepali people's final wish is to be cremated at this location. lots of history at this site, and i got to witness a preparation cerimony at this place, both sad and siding knowing life is short and sacred.
a best place to visit for understanding hindu religion culture and art. a great piece of art beautiful temples but little polluted. and also evening arati is good to hear.
the temple is unique in quite a few ways. rotating the stone behind the temple is believed to eradicate past sins.
i am proud to visit there .it's very popular and very famous place in nepal.many people visit there to see nepalese cultural

phashupatinath temple is famous and very sacred hindu temple of lord shiva. the area of pashupatinath temple has many other temples and monuments. it is really great to see only one tree of rudras inside the temple complex. there is active crematorium on the bank of river because of that neatness and cleanliness is not there. there are many fake guides so one should beware of such people.as this is the famous sacred place and because of god grace it was not much harmed in the earthquake. one should visit this temple at least once or again and again.

one of the holiest hindu temples in the world, this pagoda-style temple is only hindus are allowed to enter the main temple, but nonetheless worth the visit to this 17th century temple which was not harmed in the earthquake.

the temple area (non-hindus are not allowed into the major temple itself) is very impressive. the cremations, happening at the riverside 24/7 are a bit frightening and highly unusual to western eyes - the smell may even cause nausea on first approach, but you get used to it. but it is also a very direct way to learn more about hinduism and its culture, so i do highly recommend a visit there. a word of warning though: we were "kidnapped" at a fake ticket office by a fake guide, a few meters away from the actual ticket booth where professional guides are for hire. beware of handwritten "tickets" signs and anyone that tries to approach you by him/herself, offering to take you to the entrance. you won't loose them for another half hour when they ask you for their tip. just walk away until you reach the yellow sign "ticket office and guides". at least, the guy who "caught" us did a decent job explaining the sights, but we only found out later he was fake and felt stupid for not having been more attentive. also, most of the so called "holy men" (sadhus) are very annoying in trying to get a top in exchange for a photo with them. you can see so many of these guys around town, especially in durbar square - don't bother.

i have been to kathmandu couple of times but this is first time i visited pashupathinath temple. this is lord shiva temple with active crematorium on the bank of river. i didn't pay even simple penny to visit the temple. it was unique experience looking over the cremation place from the wall of pashupathinath temple. there are lots of other temples in the campus like lord hanuman temple, vishnu temple and local gods and goddess.

nice hindu complex. this is where they cremate bodies and throw ashes in the river. as a tourist you cannot go to the other side where cremation happens, you can only see it from the other side of the river which is fine by me. why would you want to intrude on a family's grieving, intimate and private moment? as a matter a fact when a body was brought to the cremation site we left. i really did not want to see that. we did see some chinese intrude and tour that cremation area which we thought it was unacceptable. no empathy or common sense these people! we walked all the complex and were asked by some yogis or sadhus to take pictures with them. not sure if i am saying their proper name (they are the holy men that wear the orange garment and paint their faces). while we were sitting with them, one of them whispered in my ear "$20 dollars for each person ok?" in my head i was thinking "you are out of your mind old man!" we did tip but only $200 rupess. are they kidding me? they are suppose to be selfless and spiritual people but here they are ask asking for american money! so be mindful that if you take pictures with them, you must give them a tip.but do not fall for their asking price.
the whole temple campus is huge and also features amenities for pilgrims like safe shoe keeping, drinking water etc. the darshan was swift and well managed.

everyone wants to go to see families in mourning and death rituals. i wouldn’t recommend this as a place to photograph or, far-worse, video people in distress, but maybe some of the older public artifacts are ok for that. use discretion and have respect for local people - they aren’t there for a fun holiday.

the bad: 1,000 rupees to enter ($10 usd) was the most expensive tourist attraction i visited. also be aware access to the temple is restricted and not open to tourists. be aware: i was scammed out of 1,000 rupees ($10 usd) for a 20 minute tour. he asked for 2,000 rupees but i should have given him only 500 rupees. the shifty tour guide also i suggested i take a picture of a hindu priest and donate 1,500 or 2,000 rupees ($15 to $20 usd). call me cheap but i really hate getting the "gringo" treatment. the good: the temple grounds include a crematorium that is accessible by tourists. be aware actual dead bodies are burning on piles of wood and covered. definitely worth viewing if you are not deterred by burning bodies. to be fair most of the people i have encountered in kathmandu have been honest and super friendly.

many beautiful buildings around . old and ill maintained. the best thing is the experience you feel with the presence of cremation place on the bank bagmati river in temple complex. the sadhus smoked pipes all add to a different things all together

hinduism is the largest religion in nepal. pashupatinath is a very important holy place for hindus and as a non-hindu tourist, i am glad that i have been here, as in order to get a better appreciation of nepal, it was good to go to a place that is so significant to so many people in nepal. as a tourist, if you don't like crowds, avoid festival days. also, be prepared for a high level of pollution in the area.

pashupatinath temple has been very fampus old shiva temple. i visited the temple after over 20 years. i was however disappointed to find that the temple management does not maintain any cleanliness in the temple complex.

best time to visit temple is 5-7 pm in evening as one can get darshans of all 4 sides of shiving from inside and followed by arti at bhagmati which itself is quite a sight. avoid any specific days such as shivaratri, mondays or any other shiva festival days to escape from heavy rush. no prasad to be offered at temple hence dont buy anything.
it’s one of main temple of lord shiva. it’s on bank of river bagmati. very good temple. nice atmosphere. it’s main place to visit in kathmandu.
one of the most holy places for hindus, kathmandu has a whole lot of holy places for tourists who wish to combine fun with pilgrimage

the temple is at start and at the end is place where dead bodies are burned of hindus only and in the temple only hindus are allowed and leather is not allowed inside no photography is also allowed in the temple

this is a must visit for all hindus. it will take about 30-45 minutes for completing all the darshan. its advisable to take a local guide who will charge inr 500 for taking through the temple. this will help in understanding the significance and the rituals attached with the temple.

every evening after sunset there is a special puja held - arati. hindu priest are singing and dancing with fire lamps. even these days the dead are rarely burned at the riverside, but in a new electric oven, still it is a place to be reminded that we all are going to die one day. behind the river you will find a big ground with trees and temples, a place to meditate and find calm within that busy city. the main hindu temple cannot be visited inside by visitors, only hindus can enter. respect the place and dress accordingly!

this unesco heritage site is a must for visitors of kathmandu who like to see hindu culture especially on how they perform their funeral rites. i learned about hinduism through books and videos but seeing it with my own eyes gave me depth in understanding their unique culture. visitors need to pay 1000 rupees to enter.
i had wonderful darsan at pashupatinath. me and my family have dream to reach shiv,'s place somehow i fullfill my desire to get a there and pray for belowing with him. jya pashupatinath.

i visited two years ago, and the spiritual and philosophical experience of watching the cremation ceremony still stays with me. it's not for everyone, but i was deeply moved by the proceedings, and the many people, both locals and foreign visitors, seated on the opposite bank contemplating the ongoing ceremonies. the temple compound itself was beautiful, though a big part of it was only open to hindus for praying. i think that's fair enough, respecting that this was foremost a place of worship. however, the entrance fee for foreign visitors should be correspondingly lowered in my opinion. the stagnant river into which the ashes were swept into was putrid, and people seemed indifferent to it. i wonder if it was for religious reasons that it wasn't cleaned and managed better, or simply negligence.

the lord of nepal . pashupatinath the temple is awesome. there are many other temples around the main temple. after the darshan or seeing the shiva lingam one should sit near the temple take deep breaths and enjoy the inner peace. if you sit in peace for sometime here shiva will give u bliss too. enjoy and tc a must visit om namah shivaya .

now this is one place i'm not quite sure how to recommend or review. there is a hefty fees for tourist entry and it all seemed very money making opportunity with even the "sadhus" bargaining for pics with them. there are some really lovely temples here but the area is such a tourist one it just took the joy out.

it is the world heritage and you can see why when you get there. the place is a complex of buildings and the center is the temple. over there, you can find place for holy men to stay and they are there too, they greet you and give you holy water and pray. along the river you can find people praying and do rituals for the departing family member.

i wish i could say that pashupatinath was a highlight of the trip – it is a really important hindu temple and it was on our must-do list – but it wasn't my favorite part of the trip. on the upside, it was huge and there were plenty of places to explore. on the downside, it felt a little invasive and inappropriate to be exploring around people's personal funerals and cremations, and seeing people photographing these things. if you're looking for culture, you'll definitely get a different side of it here so i would still suggest going. but i would also just suggest keeping your distance from families who are saying goodbye to their loved ones out of respect! also, there are lots of men dressed as hindu holy men who charge you for a photo and to be "blessed." don't fall for this.it's just a way to get money from you!
pashupati nath is very very historically hindu temple .god shiva maha dev temple . we see all thing. there .

this is an important religious site which gives visitors from other cultures an insight into hindu ceremonies. watching grieving families washing the bodies of deceased family members and then having a public cremation is not something western visitors will be used to but it is an important ritual for the hindu religion. this was a very respectful and interesting visit.

upon arriving at pashupatinath temple, my fiancé and i were told the entrance fee was 1,000 rupees. while this is a bit steep in my opinion, we decided to pay the price (doubting that we would ever be back in kathmandu to see it). after buying our tickets and entering the temple, one of the tour guides began to explain about the historical and religious significance of the temple grounds, architecture, and open air cremations. although we hadn't asked for a guided tour, we naively assumed that it was included in the price of the ticket. the tour guide was quite thorough and took us around the temple for about 30 minutes, offering to take our photo at certain spots along the way. you can take photos with the face-painted holy men around the temple (for a price) but we decided not to exploit the religious aspects of the temple by taking advantage of the photo ops. at the end of our tour, our tour guide explicitly asked us for a tip equal to the price of one admission ticket. feeling somewhat duped, we abided his request. take aways: if you don't want to pay extra, you should explicitly state that you don't want a guided tour. the tour does offer you many bits of information that you won't get otherwise so keep that in mind. the site is rich with hindu culture and history and well worth the ticket price in my opinion. be respectful. real people come to this site 24 hours a day to cremate their loved ones. don't be intrusive and respect their privacy.

1000 rps to go in the complex but we were told we couldn't go inside the temple. we were told we could watch the cremation smoke !?! if breathing the dead is your gig step right up otherwise avoid this place.

upon my arrival at kathmandu, i passed by the pashupatinath on my way from the airport to the city centre. the taxi driver commented that the smoke in the air was from the cremation ceremony. having read about the no holds barred open display of the cremation of bodies, i was not sure whether i was “up to it” to visit the temple. after spending 11 days in kathmandu/ pokhara/ ghorepani-poonhill, i finally decided to include visiting the pashupatinath along with the boudhanath and the swayambhunath on the day before i fly back to kuala lumpur. i hired a driver for the day because i did not want to go through the hassle of bargaining with multiple taxi drivers to get from one place to another. nevertheless, a one-way taxi ride from thamel to pashupatinath should cost about npr400 or less (if you are good in haggling). at the time of my visit, the entrance fees to this unesco world heritage site was npr1,000. remember to keep your ticket because you are required to produce your ticket at the other entrance to the ritual cremation area. at the ticket booth, you would encounter a number of guides touting their services. while you are not obliged to engage their services, having a guide to show you around the complex could be helpful. even though i was the only asian visitor that morning, most of the hindu worshippers greeted me with kind smiles. the main grounds of the pashupatinath temple are off-limits to non-hindus. there’s quite a bit to see around the vicinity outside the off-limits grounds. after exploring that part of the complex, the next on the agenda would probably be witnessing a public cremation. hailing from a multi-racial country, i have a deep respect for the hindu religion and culture. my respect increased many folds after watching the process of the bodies being prepared for cremation and the cremation process itself. this is very different from the wakes and the funerals which i have attended. what i assumed was unnerving turned out to be a visible act of love by the family members to bid a final farewell to send off their loved ones. smoke and open display of the heads and feet of the bodies aside, i am humbled to be able to witness this meaningful ritual.

if you havent visited this temple, then people say your trip is not even worth it. it is crowded but worth it. avoid going on holidays. nepal has a saturday holiday so avoid that day. avoid people offering to take you inside beating the queue for a fee.

this is a must go to site for any visitor travelling to kathmandu and wanting to see hindu culture. this is a very sacred site for hindus and it is particularly interesting as you can see cremations along the riverside. definitely worth a visit and it will cost you rs1000 to enter the site.

the temple is very crowded so go with enough time at hand. beware of touts and people offering to take smaller routes to get you glimpse of the god. best is to follow what normal people are doing. well managed by the temple staff otherwise.

here it's definitely worth to take a "guide". you easily find inside some people who tell you for a tip something about the nepali culture and the temple. be careful with the monkeys.don't hold food or soft drinks in your hands.they will take it. otherwise the whole temple is just a really holly place and it's surrounded by a special atmosphere. be respectful to the familys who burn their loved once there. a must to do in kathmandu.it's the second most important hindu temple in the world after the nepalese.

this was an extraordinary experience. you get to experience the culture and beliefs. it was fascinating. be prepared to pay for pictures taken of the monks. very interesting life and death beliefs. overall very informative.
we spend nearly 2 and half hour by watching ganga arati at bagmati river bank. evening view of paushupatinath temple and spiritual environment creates positive vibes. makes our evening beautiful.

spent several hours here, very interesting. a local guide would have been helpful because there is a lot to see over a large area. if upsetting to you, avoid the cremation pyres. non-hindus were not permitted to enter the actual temple.

this temple is a unique hindu temple as compared to the indian temples, it has pagoda architecture and beautiful silver carvings all over the temple. photography is not permitted in the temple. note that you may be told that the temple closes by 12 pm however the best time to visit is around 12 30pm as at 1pm the inner door opens and one can see all faces of lord shiva. neatly maintained. lot of restoration work is on. very close to this temple is a shakti peeth guhyeshwari many do not know about it and miss it, please try to visit both.

one of the 12th jyotir linga temple of shiva. must see place for hindu. try to darshan before noon ( temple gate closed during noon). in and around lots of place to see with different mythological background.
the 5th century build temple is the center attraction point of hindus traveling to nepal. the pashupatinath s regarded as the head of four dham (pilgrimage sites, 3 in india)

sri pashupatinath temple in kathmandu dates back to 400 ad and a very holy place for hindus. the architecture is mesmerising to say the least and the shivalinga is great to see. a huge nandi sits right in front of the sanctum sanctorum. one can offer specific poojas . time from 0930 to 1100 hours . for that you need to pay in npr ( inr 2000 and 500 not accepted as of date ) and get the receipt ,go to east side gate and the pujaris inside will do the pooja .
awesome monuments must be once in your life. amazing people and lifestyle ans sadhus there. sadhus have nothing but happy and satisfied.
my day visit to pasupatinath turned out to be a best day out in kathmandu. i would recommend everyone to visit this place once if you are in kathmandu.

we walked through a small village where the stalls were selling arty and craft items, monkeys were roaming freely, we reached the bagmati river and walked along this for afew minutes not really knowing where we were being taken and what we were going to witness. the smell of burning in the air which is not uncommon in nepal. had a photograph with holy men who were sat on concrete steps (at a charge of 200nr) i gazed across the river to see burning bodies, a month before we came here i watch a documentary on bbc1 sue perkins in varanasi (india) who visited the ghats along the river, this was very much the same. it was intriguing to see the cultural difference in attitudes and rituals compared to how we deal with this in britain. i was shocked and intrigued at the same time, a feeling of intrusion crept over me like i shouldn't be there watching families wash, prepare and mourn over their loved ones, but at the same time there was something really special about this experience.

most sacred temple of hindus, situated on the bank of river bhagmati. it is nearby tribhuvan international airport, kathmandu. there are four entrance to temple. you can buy rudraksh garland, flowers and other things for worship. price is 100 inr. special worship can also be done or only darshan can be done. only four priest can touch the lingum in temple. they belong to bhat community from karnataka, india. their helpers are bhandari. there will be rush on monday. photography is strictly prohibited inside the premises. outside photography is allowed. there are various temples and sculpture inside the campus including lord hanuman and unnmut bharav. on the bank of bhagmati river, dead body of hindu people from kathmandu is burnt. inside the temple you can feel soothing environment and chant your prayers. most important, muslims and cristian are not allowed in main temple. foreigners are being charged for entry and are not allowed inside main temple if they are not hindu.

stayed for about one hour walking around and watching events unfold. at one stage i did feel intrusive as families were seen mourning their loved ones but at the same time intrigued to see what was happening. most people were respectful of this keeping their distance when taking photos etc.

kathmandu is known by pashupatinath hence a must visit to pray. however over the period of time it seems to have been less in maintenance and the presence of hindu crematorium in the rear makes it very uncomfortable feel. needs to be better maintained and better crowd management which seems to be increasing by the day.

in my opinion one of the best things to do in kathmandu. loads of small and bigger tempels and shrines in a large but not to big area on top of and around a hill and surrounded by monkey filled trees. for better understanding take a guide. best to start from the bodhanath(big stupa) and end at the crematorium. the complex is half hour walking from bodhanant and the two are easily combined in one day. get to the crematorium just before sunset for the best vieuws.

it was my first time watching an open cremation! an eye-opener indeed. if you do feel comfortable, this is a truly unique experience. sadness, curiosity, worried, all sorts of feeling inside me that i can't simply describe! the temple is only for hindus so we could only peek from the outside!

just a short taxi ride from thamel. haggle down to 500nr. or pay a bit extra and gave the taxi wait around. also just a quick trip from the burning ghats to boudhanath. so do both sites in the one hit.

on the bagmati river, which feeds into the ganges and is similarly a hindu holy river, the abiding memory for me was witnessing several cremations one morning, up close on the riverbank. note that the r1,000 entry fee only applies if you enter on the cremation side, not if you view the temple grounds from the opposite riverbank. and guides may start chatting to you without first asking for money and only later ‘suggest’ you give r1,000-2,000. the central temple cannot be accessed by non-hindus, only the surrounding buildings. well worth a visit if in kathmandu.

it was my dream ti visit kathmandu for 'pashupatinath ji' darshan, historicle temple with encient dezine, a large 'murti of nandi ji' out side the 1st face of temple is much attrective, we visit in the time of evening aarti there and it was wondring moment for us(me and my friends. you ha e to do four side darshan of the repmle because lord shiva murti is four side facing here. i always recomand every one to visit once in his/her life. jai bhole nath

pashupati nath temple is the point of convergence of hindus worldwide and has been attracting people from around the world and all religions, creed and dogmas. it is a place which has witnessed all the change that has come to this country and has continued to remain the same. this is one of the biggest attractions of kathmandu.
very well maintained temple. entire visit is very well regulated. pious place with amazing ambience.

it was a wonderful experience to see a hindu cremation. however, the so-called sadu or highest priest, they ask for money after you take picture with them. and the tour guide of the area was the worst, he would keep on pestering you to give more tips. i mean after preaching all about good karma and bad karma, where it went?

this is the sacred cremation site for the nepali people. they believe that the body needs to be cremated within 24 hours and the ashes pushed into to river in order for their souls to be reincarnated. this was a heartbreaking, somber and private event that is done in such a public way. i stood on the banks of the river crying as the smoke and ash from the fire swirled around me all the while women hawked their jewelry, trash was carelessly tossed in to sacred river and priest charged 100 rubies to have their pictures taken with tourists. but alas this is their way, their customs and their culture. not worse, not strange or bad. just different. and i was humbled to be allowed to witness it.

it feels a bit intrusive to be present at the cremation ceremonies - but it seems the locals are ok with that. the kids playing in the water next to the burning bodies, running troops of monkeys, stoned sadhus, vendors selling souvenirs, funeral chants - it all felt a bit surreal. the entry fee is quite steep (1000r), but it seems to be a norm in the popular kathmandu sights.

big temple with big territory. hinduist temple inside forbidden for non-hinduist visitors. but there are a lot of other features there. one of the most important is place for cremation on the river. you can see how smoke with spirit flow to the sky. be careful, some extraordinary people ask you to make a picture with him, and after they ask about fee, not so expensive, 100 rupee, 1$. place to be!
the temple is much organised now. darshan is easier and faster. it doesnt take much time as local people tell u
holy and internationally famous temple.very neat inside. had clear darshan of lord pashupatinath through all the four doors.within the temple complex we had darshan of bhairaveshwar and nagaraj.
beautiful temple to visit and walk around the area , had an very good guide who outlined all the history for us

i've see the reviews that complain about the price and the people and the funerals.ok. yes, you are not allowed into the temple itself, which is for hindus only, the only thing that i find negative about this is that i asked my guide how do they know i'm not hindu, and he said because you're white, which is wrong on so many levels. however, people complained about 1000 rupees price, well book a private tour then. my guide was amazing and explained everything in great detail and it was fascinating. people have also complained about the burning of bodies, why are you in a place of culture of not to appreciate the differences? the whole ceremony and the traditions behind the funeral and the funeral pyre was very interesting and not disturbing, in fact, the whole process is very beautiful in its own way. the monkeys are so cute, the whole site is filled with beautiful buildings and get your photo taken with a priest, it costs about 100 rupees, a steal for a great photo (and a blessing). well worth the visit with a great guide

a jyotirling temple connected with kedarnath darshan and to be done after it. the pagoda shaped temple building houses many other deities around it and has 4 gates to seek lord shiva's blessings from 4 different directions. the cremation pyres outside the main temple near bahati river is a unique sight and effectively connects the last rites with life and almighty. the river has been polluted beyond limits turning it into a nulla. something needs to be done to restore the pious nature of river. plenty of hotels around and the nearby international and domestic airport make it very easy to visit.

the entire complex is quite big but it's its actually easy to get around it. and don;t get impacted by how not exciting it looks from the airport road - once inside, you will experience a lot! we took a guide who helped us to understand more of these important rituals and was also helpful to negotiate with the locals who either wanted to sell us something or needed a gift for photos (mainly applies to the holy men). we spent about half a day wandering around after which we walked for 30mins to the boddnath stupa for another half day. that way we explored hindu traditions in the morning and buddhist traditions in the afternoon. that was an amazing day!

very special experience to come here in the evening around 6-7pm when they hold the ceremonies and cremating of the bodies. lots of people all lined up on the steps to watch, and 3 men perform a ceremony with bells and incense, then each family take their dead to the river (apparently to see if they come back to life), and if not, then take them to one of the many platforms for cremating. felt very privileged to see such a sight and apparently it is much better / more atmosphere and more going on in the evening.
i made a visit to the holy place in the evening and the araati activity with hindu devoteess cultural dance was astounding

the whole visit is a lot more organised now, with tourist ways in. watch out for the touts, as your ticket entitles you to a guide. if you want to take pictures of the carefully posed yogis they do expect you to pay them. there always seem to be cremations going on and so if that is what you are going to observe then you will probably see this occurring. used to be lots of stalls with piles of coloured power in the shopping area, but only a few stall have this now. used to be such a good picture opportunity - but don't worry they are plenty more things to take shots of.

a beautifully conserved ancient unesco heritage site that must not be missed. no leatherwear of any kind is allowed within he temple. take time to go round the various shrines and temples within the complex. one may be able to see "holy men" smoking hasish in little alcoves or even out in the open. do not miss the giant "nandhi" walk behind the temple to see the corpses being cremated along the bagmathi river that runs alongside. the accompanying ceremonies and rituals are interesting.

ancient temple, untouched heritage and rich culture. all in one place. you will get to see great examples of architecture and native flavour. do visit in the early hours to get an uninterrupted view of the deity. photos are not allowed, hence, do not have much to share.
