the entrance fee of rs. 1000 (as expensive as that for durbar square) is a bit of a rip-off as only hindus can enter this temple and other hindu temples around. i was stupid enough to ask a question and was told to buy a ticket. there is a nonzero chance that one could sneak in without paying the fee. because the ticket checking is far from diligence and was totally “government work”. though i couldn’t enter the big pashupatinath temple from the main entrance, i climbed the hills surrounding the temple and saw quite a bit of it. in fact, one could enter through a side door or two. from those doors, i saw everything in the courtyards outside the main temple. but the most interesting part is of course the burning scene outside the temple. walking towards the bagmati river where the burning took place, people approached you only to volunteer their guide service (only rs. 100 or 200) and nobody asked to check my ticket. i got quite close to the actual burning and asked a guy whether they were burning was an animal and he replied firmly that it’s a human corpse. i then walked over the bridge to “appreciate” the corpse burning across the bagmati river. i took pictures for the same corpse burning from both sides. a little while later, i noticed a hand coming out of the straw covering the body and the fire was probably too strong for people to try to put the hand back to the burning straw. i was just observing with curiosity. nothing seemed elegant to me but just nasty and even a bit obnoxious. i am typically not fond of ceremonies, esp. those after death. but from another angle, it’s similar to incineration but this is just open-air 😁 walking away from the burning scene, there were rows of shrines and a few temples before one found roads with traffic across the bagmati river again.

the pashupatinath temple is perhaps one of the most visited attractions in all of nepal and especially significant to hindus because of its deity, lord pashupatinath. this temple complex is on unesco world heritage sites's list since 1979. hence, we were mentally prepared for extremely long queues since many people had experienced this in the past. however, to our good luck, since we went here late in the evening around 7pm, there were barely a few people milling around in the main temple and we got to go straight to the main altar and view it from the outside while taking our time to spend a few minutes in silence and prayer. we then also visited some of the smaller temples right beside it and these seem to be steeped in legend and history. the most impressive aspect of this temple was the massive nandi made from bronze along with the dozens of lamps, sculptures and idols that complete the visual palette along similar lines. we did not get a chance to explore the surrounding temples, ashrams, forest sections and pyre sites along the banks of the sacred bagmati river but the visit to the main temple proved to be unique in energy and visual appeal. we also appreciated the service offered at the entry gates for all tourists who come here to have their footwear stored in bags and returned to them after their visit for no charge at all. the lane leading up to the main temple is thronged by vendors and shops selling all the usual paraphernalia and artifacts belonging to the hindu deity.

a unesco site, a large complex of buildings/temples you also get an idea of what devastation the earthquake caused in 2015. worth going just to see the range of people like the holy man. your hotel should be able to book a accredited guide, which you will need.

so for a first time in kathmandu and if you have a taste for the macabre, yes, it is worth a visit. but the stench of the burning bodies can be brutal. climb up the stairs on the opposite side of the river and go look at the monkeys play instead. also a fantastic view of the city from above, including the boudhanath stupa! the price you have to pay as a foreigner is off-putting and for this place, not worth it.

we stayed for the whole afternoon. i had seen a cremation in a small village on a river. this is full on ritual. my local friend asked if this went on in my country. i told him this would land you in jail!

pashupatina shiva temple complex is completely fascinating. a guide is highly recommended here. choose your guide carefully. better to go inside and walk around a bit, the right accredited guide will find you! our guide was a young, charismatic and very educated young man. the information he gave us was extremely interesting and we felt comfortable to ask him anything. you are in a crematorium basically so it can be quite sensitive. we paid $10 for our guide (1 hour) they will ask for $30 at the entrance have since been told it is best to attend early morning or evening prayers here. bodnath is an easy 20 minute walk from here.
every day in 7 pm aarati is done in eastern part of pashupatinath temple in bank of bagmati river. this is evening worship of god shiva in pashupatinath temple.

this temple is a must-do for anyone in kathmandu, but don't expect huge, impressive monuments. it's a sacred location and a bit of a trek (recommend a guide for tourists) from thamel, but it's something you'll be glad you did an fought the crowds for. guides will give a good overview of the history of the place and the development of the whole complex, plus will make it easier with entry tickets, but it's not difficult if you want to go it alone with a lonely planet or guidebook. pashupatinath is a hindu complex for cremations and worshippers and those dedicated to the faith, so tourists are generally outnumbered by local and visiting hindus - please be patient and considerate of them when you visit, as they're possibly there for a funeral. bodies are prepared and cremated in the complex, in the open and in full view of tourists. respect their procession, keep clear and keep photos to a minimum and from a distance (if you really need to take them at all). bodies are cremated and collected, but ashes from the pyres are pushed into the river. there's plenty of smoke and debris around the complex too. the complex is full of history and has been added to over the years, so there's a variety of smaller temples and monuments, and you could spend either hours here or wrap the whole thing up in 30mins - depends how much you really want to see. on entry there are the standard street vendors and beggars as it's a main tourist hot-spot, but they generally don't hassle as much as those in thamel. there are plenty of animals too, ranging from cows to monkeys to the always common dogs. take note however that the sihks who call this place home will really hassle you for money if you take their photo. some are genuine, some are not, but they all look the part. make sure you get a price up front if you want to take their photo because they are relentless if you do it the other way around. they make for great photos, so if you can get them at a good price it'll be worth it.

hindu temple to shiva, with cremation ghats along the river. . rishi's smoking hashish all day, and many elderly residents. only hindus are allowed in the main temple, but the grounds are really interesting, not to be missed.

pashupatinath is a big hindu temple, and it is always crowded. but the tourists, who are not hindu, cannot enter inside. there is a small hill on the opposite side of the river, and from this place you can take a nice pictures of the temple.
it was a privilege to watch a very public cremation ceremony in this sacred place. a very sympathetic way to honor the dead.

visited the pashupatinath temple. dedicated to lord shiva, pashupatinath is one of the four most important religious sites in asia for devotees of shiva. built in the 5th century and later renovated by malla kings, for tourist its pilgrimage place we did pooja lord shiva there and sat for long time.

i took my family back to see this on this holiday, it is a realty check on what life is, and what happens after. i would recommend a mask while sightseeing, the air pollution is terrible and the cremation fires add to this. tip to pay 10 dollars and get a guide
the most famous and big temple is pashupati nath which is situated on the banks of the holy river bagmati.it is god shiva 's temple.shiva is biggeat god of hindu people.

during my visit to the pashupatinath temple complex, there was much to see and experience that was one of the highlights of my journey through nepal. i learned that this temple is dedicated to pashupati, another manifestation of the hindu deity, shiva. this manifestation of that of the "lord of the animals." representations of shiva were found in the phallus statues that were located in the shrines along the hill at the temple complex. as one of the holiest hindu sites in nepal, only hindus are allowed in the actual temple. however, there is much to see around the temple complex. there is the bagmati river, along which funeral pyres burn while family and loved ones mourn the passing of the deceased. there are monkeys galore as well as sadus—holy men, some of whom solicit photographs, for a small fee. they can be identified by the brightly colored clothes and painted faces. i found this to be more of a somber place than some of the other temples i visited in nepal, but a great place for reflection.
i strongly recommend you stay for the evening arathi. this was an amazing experience: watching the ceremony, as cremations occurred on the ghats.

pashupatinath is a famous sacred hindu temple dedicated to lord pashupatinath and is located on the banks of bagmati river. this temple is full of sadhu's (baba) meditating and people performing their daily rituals.
very near to airport lots of devotees coming from all over very old holy place what ever you wish in this temple you will get it

an interesting place to visit but not for the faint hearted as you will most likely see - and smell - bodies being prepared for cremation from washing of the feet and face to the placing of the body on wood and the rituals preceding the cremation. would recommend viewing this with a local person or guide - we had ram with us - as he explained every ritual and its significance.
the temple has its own religious importance, however the maintenance is not upto mark. the approach road is not in good conditions.
its amazing to be a spectator to this way of celebrating life and death. magnificent temples, lots of monkeys, special place

it was an interesting experience. some parts of the temple were under construction but could still give you an amazing learning experience about life and death and hole existence . it has a very strong energy vibe and is better to have a guide with you for a better understanding of every thing you see in it.
this is a must visit for anyone going to kathmandu. the feel is great & the atmosphere needs to be soaked in.
sprawling disorganised site. highlight seems to be the ghats - if you like that sort of thing! beware of the monkeys and the sadhus
of you want to fully understand the local culture it's a place where you should definitelly visit. very beautiful architecture and interesting traditions.

our city tour visited the temple and he explained that approx. 50 cremations occur here every day. there are platforms lining the river where bodies are cremated taking 5 hours before the ashes are pushed into the river. as we walked around to the main temple area, the guide explained that a person over 77 yo was being prepared for cremation. that age signified a special ceremony with music and much adornment with marigolds on the body. there were about 200 people lining the bridge and surrounding area and our guide said it was ok to take photos. we felt privileged to be able to witness this special ceremony. the family then wait in a nearby room for the process to complete. it was more emotional than we expected but well worth the visit.
pashupatinath ,for hindus bigger and popular temple.god shiva stayed in there.every morning mny many prayers was pray there.if you want to know hindus culture and relligion you must go there.
very very interesting place ,old place ,many many temples are there. one small river bagmati is near by there.i look surrounding there.i see some people's death body in firing .

for hindus, pashupatinath is like must visit place, if in nepal. in-fact my trip to nepal was centered around visiting the temple. it was morning time when i went there. there was divine peace and serenity everywhere. the temple complex is an architectural masterpiece. built in 15th century, it truly deserved to be in unesco's world heritage site. the best part is the idol of lord shiva. i was eye struck to see the beauty of idol. highly recommended for someone visiting nepal, irrespective of religion.
i'd come here again. when i went there, you can see live cremations. pretty intense. very interesting.

very interesting place to visit. the culture deals with death very differently than westerners do. we were able to see a cremation and a holy ceremony which occurs a few days after the cremation. interesting experience.

the temple serves as the seat of lord pashupatinath it is one of the most sacred sites in the hindu religion . it seems more like a sacred city- a collection of temples and ashrams built over the centuries along the banks of the sacred bagmati river , all in all pashupatinath comprises 264 hectares of land including 518 temples and monuments . there is so much going on . so many people going about their devotions , others going about their work . it has an unequaled vibrancy . there is a heart and soul to this place . in among the whirling mass of people there are about 200 monkeys going about their business trying to avoid probably the same amount of dogs going about their business . there is a prevailing ambience . there are no cross words spoken . there is no animosity . there are pilgrims from all over the indian sub-continent , i saw a smattering of japanese and chinese monks and some noble tibetans.even some exalted brahmin priests from kerala . there is even a hospice where people come to die and the dying is such a ritual as the passing on .at any given time there were at least three cremations going on .along the banks . the family mourners dressed in white with their heads freshly shaven . a man slowly got into step beside me and in a completely disarming way became my companion and guide for the next three hours . he made sense of the whole experience for me . he was quite surprised when the sadhus welcomed me with open arms and made a space for me on their mat and insisted i join them for kirtan . i felt the ground buzzing underneath me . some-one produced a chillum and they were lovely about me not having a big blast . 25 years stoned was enough for me . they were a delight . we chanted and we chatted and we looked around . then my guide took me off to meet baba an aesthete who had the longest dreadlocks i have ever seen .they were longer than he was . he was in his 80's but spritely and sharp . we got on well . i was guided here there and everywhere - do not wander around on your own you will miss so much . as i fell through the gate at the end i felt completely different from when i arrived - something had shifted deep inside . a priest had blessed me and tied a new kalava round my wrist . my old one tied on at a snake temple in the thar desert , rajastahn had finally fallen off a couple of weeks before . it will attract the blessings of the deities and protect me . i felt better .i thanked my guide and he thanked me and off i went to find my driver . do be respectful and not get too gawkish taking photos of cremations - it is bad form . if you don't want to upset people taking their photos ask them if it's cool . what an experience .i shall never forget those few hours in such a holy place .

this temple complex is fascinating. we spent the better part of an afternoon here and had a local tour guide take us around and explain aspects of the site. this was a really good idea, especially in terms of understanding the cremation area as well as the area where bodies are washed in the river. it was a solemn and thoughtful place, so allocate time to sit and reflect. we also found the late-afternoon light to be lovely in terms of photographing parts of the site. definitely make time to visit if you're in kathmandu.

bit confusing to begin with as we went up the stairs (the entrance on the left into the temple is for hindu people only). wandered around the bushes for a while before heading down the other set of stairs to where thy hold the cremations. a lot of pushy sales people and 2 holy men who walked straight up to my sister and i and started putting flowers and things on us and asking for money. very cool place to see though, lots of monkeys playing on the stairs. perhaps get a guide!
pashupatinath is one of famous temple of lord shiva.must visit place.very historical place.it's a divine place for hindus.

we are hindi family .so we like this temple pashupatinath .we was gone in early in the morning from hotel for pray .we do shiva 's puja in pashupatinath. it is big opportunity for us . thank jaya pashupati mahadev

if you know nothing about the hindu religion then a few hour here will put you right! on entry a guide will accompany you around - take note this is not part of the entry price! expect to see a wide range of really interesting cultural activities including public cremations - where you are welcome to view the entire proceedings - although the way the fire is started may not be to everybody’s liking. really fascinating place - easy to spend 2 hours here - the souvenir salesmen are a bit of a nuisance though.

whilst there in the afternoon we witnessed many cremations in progress and at different stages, which our guide explained to us. these are ancient and traditional rituals and one wonders how long it will go on - ashes are swept into the bagmati river.

interesting place despite the 1,000 rupee entrance fee. very touristy and people asking for money. fake/ very decorated baze's or holy people posing for a picture.lots of monkeys as well. saw their burial ritual. lots of temples. huge space. decently priced items and clean bathrooms. it was worth seeing for the experience but wouldnt go back. also get a guide to have a better understanding of their culture and not just take photos. i would recommend this over thamel, kathmandu and patan durban square.
whether a hindu or not this series of temples are fasinating. well worth spending 2 hours looking around and soaking up the ambiance of this very special place. worth the 1000 rupees entrance fee.
many many nepali and tourist people go there. it is hindus biggest temple. very ancient temple. shiva's temple.
this is a must see if you really want to get a better understanding of nepali live and death. walk up the hill to see many shrines. only down-fall. too many hawkers.

absolute must-see if you are a hindu and shiva worshipper! if you buy the abhishek ticket for ~750 inr in the state bank counter just outside the gate (near the golden nandi or bull statue), it allows expedited darshan for 6 people. well worth it. beautiful 4-sided idol that can be viewed from all 4 sides, n/s/e/w! beautiful temple.

you will find here an universe dedicated to the passage to the next world. the locals are incinerating their passed away relatives or they are giving offerings to various deities. definitely a must visit place when in kathmandu. entrance fee 1000 npr. in october 2017.
even after so many centuries and devastation by fire and earthquake, this mandir is simply mesmerizing. the artwork can make your jaw gasp in wonder. very well maintained and clean. a must visit spot
a must visit for every hindu traveller to kathmandu. the temple is a fountainhead of spirituality and history.!

the complex is huge and has many temple belonging to various gods. the main attraction is the shiva linga. it is an unusual looking linga. people from all over the world come to worship. it is for only hindus. please dress decently here.
this temple is one of the famous in kathmandu valley , nice sport to look for the hindu cultural program ,nice to be there
be there at the time of aarti.its overwhelming; witness the arti by the river from the temple; though not well maintained, the temple complex is one of the most sprawling

the compound is interesting and bustles with activity. non-hindus are not allowed to enter the actual temple though. (i wondered how they determine who is hindu and who isn't, but i guess that's besides the point.) from the outside it's possible to get a glimpse of the giant bull inside, but that's about it.

this temple is a very famous spot of kathmandu. visitors must be aware that only hindus are allowed to enter the temple complex. housing centuries old statues and carvings of hindu gods and goddesses, it is a visual delight as much as a spiritual one. the best time to visit would be before 12pm when all the gates of the temple are open.

the most holly place in nepal, where all the afterdeath rituals take place. please be respectful to what you see, don´t walk where you are not alowed to, don´t take pictures of what you shouldnt - it is always better to ask. entrance fee is 1000 for tourists and you can spend here at least one hour, but if you want to get a bit more of atmosphere, stay longer

one simply must not skip this important aspect of hinduism, here in nepal. the place offers amazing 'aarti' session around sunset, and one can also witness bodies being burnt on the other side of the temple. the stark difference from our ways to bidding farewell to the dead soul will surely intrigue you into understanding the meaning and significance around the same. please also be careful not to give any donation to the naked 'sadhus' or men imposing as the authoritative and concerned people out there.

the most fascinating and holy place in kathmandu. we respectfully observed cremation preparations (processions, cleansing of bodies), and actual cremation rituals. beautiful. so different from western practices.
access to the inside of the temple is restricted to hindus only.pashhupatinath is called god shiva temple.best time to go is in the morning.

worth viewing but feels like you are intruding on a family's send off of a loved one via public cremation. surreal comes to mind. but the historic temple buildings are amazing to see and if you don't mind fending off holy men wanting to touch you and then ask for rupees spend an hour or so there.

i thoroughly enjoyed my time here and despite not being an 'attractive' temple it's very spiritual and a very important religious place for hindus. i recommend visiting around 5 so you have some time to look around before the music begins at 6 in the evening - this happens every day and really made the experience for me. it was quite odd yet moving to experience a cremation on one side of the river with singing, dancing and a celebratory feel on the other side as the crowd sang songs worshipping shiva. an incredible unique place well worth the high entrance fee

i've visited this temple compound during my one day guided tour around 4 major tourist attractions in kathmandu, all in unesco heritage sites list. temple itself is open only for hindu believers, so i couldn't enter, but i heard guide explanation about the facade, and history of the temple and its importance in hindu religion. temple is on the bank of bagmati river which itself is considered by hindus and buddhists as a holy river as continues to ganges, and purifies the people spiritually. that's why many hindus are cremated on the banks of this holy river, near the temple.
access to the inside of the temple is restricted to hindus only. otherwise, the temple complex is open to all.

this is a favourite place of mine when i visit kathmandu. world heritage site visit in the daytime so that you can have a good look around, if possible return in the evening. at 6 pm every night a prayer ceremony called ‘arti’ takes place, it is a moving experience and i wouldn’t miss it, take a guide to explain things to you cremations take place along the river of this temple, some people may be disturbed by this but it’s part of their culture, just be respectful and don’t let this put you off
my suggestion: go first to pashupatinath temple and then for lunch and couple hours to boudhanath. the experience here is quite strong - it is not a tourist attraction, it is real life.

we were a little shaken to be taken 1000 rs out of our pockets for a visit here. this is- even for westerners- something we hardly ever pay for sightseeing, keeping in mind that we were not even allowed (as non-hindus) to go into the main temple. at durbar square it is different because it is obvious you make a contribution to the rebuilding after the earthquake (it^s also 1000 rs there) a little disturbing or funny is also, that the "sadhus" almost get a little angry if offered "only" 50 rs for a foto (they just sit there and smoke all day. well. it^s your choice i guess but i don^t quite approve.) nonetheless, if you take your time to stroll around and take in the atmosphere, you feel that despite the commerce this place is special- especially with the cremations going on of course. bear in mind to pay respect and dont shove your camera lens into the ongoing cremation- stay away.
the temple is one of the famous temple in asia . the best time to.visot is early morning to avoid rush . lot of gift and prasad shops are also available . beware with fraud stone shops .
this is a shiva temple. from the moment we stepped in, the sacred feeling and divine enthusiasm took over us. would be great to visit during shivaratri :)

death and mourning are out in the open at this huge hindu temple on the outskirts of kathmandu. the inner temple is closed to non-hindus but the area across the river is open to anyone. you can almost always find at least one cremation underway, and you may be able to watch a family say goodbye. the corpse is brought down the ghat to the river's edge where close family members anoint it with the holy water. afterward, the body is carried to a brightly decorated cremation platform. the eldest son (if there is one) lights the pyre and is expected to spend 13 days in mourning at pashtupatinath. the ashes are swept into the river, which flows to the sacred ganges. there are smaller temples all around where "sadhus" pose for photos, monkeys clean each other, and the homeless can find shelter. not to be missed.

this is a very special place and i advise anyone visiting kathmandu to make a stop here a priority. this is a holy place where the devout come to die, be cremated, and have their ashes washed down the holy river into the ganges and eventually the ocean. it's believed that having your remains burned here virtually ensures you will be reincarnated as a human in your next life. i was told that something like 35 cremations happen every day. people come and pay their respects to the deceased and witness their bodies go through this final transition alongside curious tourists and onlookers alike. it's very moving to witness the final rituals and send off of the body and soul in such an open forum.

this is truly a bizarre but fascinating place. this is the main hindu temple in kathmandu. there you can see monkeys, a temple that foreigners have limited access. i pondered the meaning of life and death there. in one instance the son of his deceased father, started a fire on yhr corpses forhead. i was told that watching or taking photos was considered fine. i walked by another pyre on a different day and the skeleton more or less exploded. don't mean to be grim but it makes you think about life. another thing was to watch a yogi lift a 30 pound rock with his penis! i've been told the japanese like this. it was not that awe inspiring. once again, he told me i could take photos and video and so i did. this area is truly crazy and unique, in a good way.

interesting visit. people let you wander around freely, although this feels a bit awkward because dead bodies are being cremated. just be carfull with unofficial guides picking you up at the ticket office.

this visit was completely unique to anything else we had done before. we hired a guide, and he explained to us about the life/worship temple before experiencing the other side of life. after walking around, he then explained to us the cremation process, and what there was to it such as sprinkling of rice, milk, etc. depending on the circumstances. there is nearly an ongoing duration of cremations taking place here, and as a tourist, you are able to view this process from a respectful distance. there are many sabhus here that are more than happy to have photographs taken, but expect a small donation in return. there are also many monkeys that hang around the premises, and they appeared a little more aggressive than in other places, so just be aware. we would definitely recommend a visit for such a unique experience as this.
very impressive! main spot in kathmandu where died people get their last attention! take some moments to relax and watch these tragic moments

one of the highlights of kathmandu. beautiful, ancient temple in a serene location where bodies are cremated by the river nearby. for hindus, the shiva idol is part of the 12 jyotirlingas, being the top half of the same idol that's in kedarnath. the temple is well run. non hindus cannot enter inside.

this feels a bit wrong to call this place an attraction, it isn't an attraction. it is a very important part of the nepali people's circle of life. i took a small group of teenagers as part of an expedition to nepal. i went thinking we were intruding on a very personal event. it was an amazing experience, i could have stayed there for hours just sitting quietly and taking it all in. we watched the whole ceremony from the body being carried in wrapped, the deceased sons having their heads shaved, to a well alight fire, to children in the river looking for gold teeth or jewellery. the holy men were there, it was not touristy at all. i would love to return again .
it is very interesting place .you can know and see hindu cultural in pasupatinath.you can cremation cermony in there. it was intersting eatching the temple life.

the visit to the cremation site could be somewhat disturbing if you are not hindu.so you need to be aware of this. locals and guides do not pay but tourists are expected to pay the entry fee, and please remember unless you are hindu or local you cannot view the interior of the temple. walking around the grounds and seeing the cremations is definitely worth it.

first of all it is a very important place both for hindu and budist people. unfortunately not hindus are not allowed on the main temple, but you can enjoy the view from the rivrside to people on the other side, in the temple. and you can visit small shiva temples on the open riverbank - each is housing a lingam, a fertility symbol. and you can see how people are embarked to their last journey in fire. great place to visit

i find this whole scenario disturbing. you pay to get into this world heritage site, and immediately are hounded by guides, that will continue to follow and harass you despite saying "no" multiple times. the place is filthy with trash as well as monkey, dog, and cow excrement. the holy men hanging around the temple seem to only be there to charge money for photo ops. the grounds around the temple are not that interesting, and only hindus are allowed into the temple, which is understandable. i traveled with an american born indian hindu and he was only allowed in after intense questioning about his family origin in india, and after entry, he discovered they were charging money for blessings inside the temple. the fact that people come here to witness the cremation ceremonies is the most disturbing thing to me. death rites of loved ones as an attraction that people pay to see while on vacation? just strikes me as twisted and sad. go to may of the other great sites in kathmandu instead, like patan durbar square, bodinath, the monkey temple, etc.
this is an old complex requiring a good bit of walking. you cannot access the temple but can take photos outside. guides are available for hire.

i strongly recommend visiting the place to get a unique perspective of how hinduism treats death- and therefore life. this place is not to admire beauty, architecture or to debate if it meets the unesco standards as other reviewers argue. the place shows you how some people deal with death , say goodbye to beloved ones and move on with life. i recommend taking a good local guide to get the explanation of the rituals - otherwise you might get a superficial interpretation of what you see

we entered from the south and paid the $10usd entry. we were immediately set upon by tenacious vendors. several cremations were underway. we wandered a bit and departed to the north. we noted that visitors entering the north portal were not paying an entry fee. all in all we only spent a half hour here.
at times confronting, always intriguing. a fabulous insight into this most religious of hindu sights.

a really incredible place to go watch the cremations, locals and guides do not pay but tourists do to view this, and please remember unless you are hindu or local you cannot view the interior of the temple. walking around the grounds and seeing the cremations is definitely worth it though i would have loved to see more of it.

being able to watch families cremate their loved ones was insane. the whole experience was mesmerizing and definitely worth a visit if you're in kathmandu. only problem was the beggars but didn't detract from the atmosphere at all.

this was probably one of the most interesting sites we visited. as you learn about much of the hindu culture surrounding death performances. i would recommend this as an important site to see in ktm as it helps you learn about the culture and you get to see the process of cremations being performed and meet some holy men who have devoted their lives to god.
watching the families wash the dead was an unusual experience, especially seeing the number of bodies on the steps. the surroundings were great to walk around.

pashupati nath temple is the highly regarded hindu temple of shiva by all hindus round the world. the temple itself is one of the ancient temple in the kathmandu city. although the entrance inside the temple premises is restricted to hindus only but the ancient architect of the old structures can be observed while walking around outside the temple. the crematorium at the bank of the holy bagmati is regarded as powerful place for performing the death ritual and cremation after death. watching the rituals and the process of cremation from the other side of bagmati river and by contemplating on the truth of own's life, which is impermanent by nature will give you insight that it is worthless to lead a life suppressed anger, greed, hatred and aversion.
it was intersting watching the temple life, families around, departing their lived ones, the costumes ond the wide gats. one if the more interesting places in kathmandu.
its a tempel, and as a non hindu you are not allowed to go in. the cremation ceremony is very interesting and gives one a felling of the natural path of life.

it is a ancient hindu memories temple.pure nature , religious place.when i reached there i fell heartly. to see many things .some people praying ,there is hindu people died there body was firings. it is peaceful place.a great time to visiting this sacred place.

we had darshan of the lord 2 times during our visit and spent lot of time performing pooja and darshan of the lord. and able to have darshan of aarti being performed on the banks of bagamati river also. we saw almost all the temples of the lords and time spent was more than 2 hours still feel more could have been spent.

is there anything better than seeing bodies of deceased family members being lovingly washed before being burnt & ashes scattered in their religious river? it sounds morbid, but - it's an experience you just have to witness to see the love. painting a picture of the complex - naked boys backflipping into the religious river, cheeky monkeys stealing dry biscuits, discourteous holy men charging for photos from tourists, pleasant women blessing visitors with red rice tilak blessings, tourists wandering around wondering where they can spend their hard earnt holiday dollars & loving family members watching on as their loved ones are washed & burnt. it's a memorable experience that'll burn deep in your soul.

it breaks my heart to say this but this place could have been far better maintained and managed. being such an old place of hindu worship, i expected this to be a bit cleaner and a bit tidy probably. anyway it is still worth a visit. it is better to carry something to cover your nose from time to time as the smells can sometimes be exhilarating.

i went in with a lot o excitement knowing that many claims this as the largest hindu temple of the world and is itself a world heritage site. i was almost shocked at seeing how poor-managed the entire place was. even if i try to keep the smell aside, the things spread here and there just makes it sad. whether or not the world's largest, i assume this temple could use a much better cleaning from the nepalese government.
definitely worth the visit coz of place's heritage value and holy vibrations.management can be better, stay away from pandits to perform paid rituals.no cleanliness at all.
pure and religious place. expensive foreigners. most of it got destroyed in the earthquake so still under renovation.

my wife and i were really surprised to see how filthy and chaotic everything there is. the buddhist temples were for the most part very well maintained and clean, not the hindu temples, and this one was no different. i went to use the free toilet there and it was filthy beyond and description. of course we are glad we went due to it's historical value, we decided not to go inside as there is plenty to see from the outside, but, it's really a huge culture shock.

probably you should be here to know the bitter truth of your own life. you feel heart pounding, when you see dead pure bodies lying and getting burnt. smokes along with red flames grows to the sky and you feel the reality, keep pondering silently about your activities. you calculate your behaviours, your opinions,your moves and your attitudes. sit opposite to the cremation sites alone and sit silently for an hour. you can see your body burning and but only your soul intact with you. then and there probably you could feel the time you spent recklessly without any calculation. the main objective of life i gurantee you that you will learn: live peacefully and let live other peacefully. i have learnt : no worries in life because everything goes and fades by time. your loved ones may leave you. but you will have your soul. so nevery try to pollute it other materialistic things like sculptures depicted on wooden wall and stones are awesome. try to find the meaning once you see it . google it and you will have knowldege real field trip knowledge. dont do: donot try to feed monkeys otherwise they wont leave you if you want to feed them just watch how experienced local people do that. love animal but donot make them dependent on peoples offerings. you need a whole day minimum to look around. go and look in every corner of place because there are so many untouched corners where you get great arts.

i never understood what they were about, but it is fascinating to know that people live(d) there. my guess was it is how the used waters from laundry and the like were evacuated. i beg to be taught otherwise, if i am wrong. in any case, it is still fascinating to see how "the other half lived", to quote the beatles. it is still nicer than what we have in north america. once again, i was there before the earthquakes
this place has huge religious importance but not well maintained as per the hype. beware of some pandits who walk around you for tips.

i paid for the ticket to get in and it's just like we're just passing through and my guide was just explained about the heritage history of the site (what a waste of my money/energy to walked esp. so hot).and i said to him, i want to see the open-air cremation and he says, tourists are not allowed (which is a lie cos i checked online after) i think he was beginning to feel tired but i paid him for my guided tour to get to see them etc. (+tip at the end - mistake!). interesting place!
best temple. you get nice 360 degree darshan. lot of monkeys. dont take your camera as its not allowed to take pictures inside. great experience
if you travel in nepal, it's a must-visit place. you will see how nepalis think about their life and death. it might even change how you see things in your life!

i was glad i heard a lot about the temple and crematoria before i went along since, even with the background knowledge i found it a quite confronting experience. the temple itself is off limits to non hindus. i found the most interesting part of the visit was the banks of the river where the cremations take place. for someone from a non-religious western household, it's a very different experience to see death being handled so openly and publicly. in australian, ceremonies are usually far more private, with most of the preparation work done behind the scenes. the way it is a normal part of the nepalese culture was interesting to see - there were local kids diving for coins in the water while bodies were being dunked in to be cleansed, as well as families sitting on the opposite side of the riverbank having a picnic while watching some of the ceremonies. i was glad to have a guide for the experience. i usually decline guides, but had heard they are good to have to explain the ceremonies. i didn't have to search for one, since one greeted me at the booth where i paid my entrance fee. the guide took us to a quieter spot on the opposite side of the river, from where we watched one of the cremation ceremonies. he explained each part of the process, gave interesting cultural, spoke to me about some interesting nepalese history and answered my many questions. i paid him 1,000 rupees, which seems to be the going rate and i thought was very reasonable given he spent almost an hour with me, gave some great insights and so patiently answered my questions! for me this was one of the highlights from my visit to kathmandu.

i regret that my visit was very brief as i was on a private tour with a driver and covering three other tourist sites. it's best to come early in order to cover the whole area. it's both very interesting and totally different to the west. the entrance fee is 1000 rupees. beware the self licensed guide at the booking office! he stands there and behaves as though he is part of the pashupatinath temple but infact he is not. he starts talking to you and follows you in and later informs you that you can pay him a contribution for his efforts! and even asks you for your ticket!

inspite of the fact that this place is a unesco site, the upkeep and maintenance does not really tell so. it is sad to see temples with such heritage and importance being kept so badly. if one wants to understand the temple better it is always better to engage a knowledgeable person who knows the history very well. only then will the visit to the temple be useful. there is so much to be told as a review will not just suffice. however in a nutshell this is one of the places where the pandavas went in search of shiva seeking salvation. the name of the temple comes from the fact that a cow (pasu) used to offer daily prayers by offering its own milk over the shivalina. the shivalingam is suyambu, and there are four faces that darshan has to be performed. the four face darshan is available only in the morning after 9.30 am. one can wait till late evening 7.30 pm once again to see all the four faces. generally there is a huge rush to see this darshan. there are several small shrines inside the temple complex. most important among them is the mukthisthal. according to the tibetian/nepalis custom in the yesteryears a person who wishes to attain mukthi will come here and merge with the river flowing below the temple, in the presence of the guru. even today you see people performing this in a slightly different way, where the final rites are performed on the river side in a particular way. the temple is a very powerful one, provided one is receptive to the energy that is available. this is a tantric center and is indeed very powerful when one sits in deep meditation. one can definitely feel the raw energy in the place. there is a rudraksha tree inside the temple complex that should not be missed. rudraksha is considered as the tear of lord shiva. the rudra abishey is also very famous here. you would need to hire a priest to perform this in the morning time. better to go with a know person.

being one of the most holy places in nepal, if you are in kathmandu you have to go. the place is unlike the other tourist sites in kathmandu has a very local feel to it. no souvenir shops. a lot of people. you see some tourists but with a lot of with beggars, people who seem to be living there, locals going for worship, locals going for cremation of their deceased with mourning people. it is very unique. i did not explore the whole complex but i just sat there in awe watching how completely different the culture is. i witnessed a couple of cremations from the beginning of washing the bodies' feet in the river to the set up of the cremation site to the actual cremation. it was definitely food for thought. i don't know about the rest of the complex but after an hour just sitting there watching i felt so heavy i needed to leave. not in a bad way but in a i-need-to-digest-all-this way. i would definitely go again because it is a completely different feel than anywhere else i saw in nepal. entrance fee for foreigners is npr 1,000. worth at least an hour in my opinion. i did not have a guide.

the temple is a must visit place. though if you are not careful you will be taken for a ride by the guides there. do select your guide carefully. it is not necessary to give offerings at each idol of the temple.

the pashupatinath temple is a hindu religious site, where the dead are cremated. it might sound morbid, but i felt honored to be allowed to see how the local people honor and mourn for the deceased. the ritual ceremonies and the site itself leave a lasting impression on anyone who visits there.

lovely ambiance and good support from the temple authorities. what i really liked was that the temple priests did not accept any cash but advised me to put them in the hundi. that is a welcome approach
this was part of the many other temples and places that i have visited. very significant visit and eye opening.
nepal is a cool place to visit with lots of natural resources. pashupatinath temple of lord shiva is most religious place in nepal. good country with cheap travelling and stay.
it was pretty cool but the smell of burning flesh was a little off putting. but still a must see if you want culture.

is the most important and revered temple in nepal.it is close to the airport.beside the religious value,value,there is a huge bronze bull (nandi)facing the main diety(shiva).the carvings are also interesting and the whole complex is definitely worth the visit.
typical hindu temple and many places are under renovation. you may spend 1hr within the large temple premise.

great place. it's huge and it quite fascinating to see rituals that are so different than your own. you see the dead having their feet washed, then cremated. it's all very public with an audience. very different to a western funeral. it is interesting to just sit and watch people interact with each other. soak it all in because it's amazing what you witness. we saw kids torment the resident crazy lady and then have her murderously run around after them with bamboo sticks. kids swimming in the god awful coloured river, men coming in singing and dancing while giving an offering to the gods then taking a dip in the river. amazing stuff. if you want a guide, make sure you agree upon a price before you set out. my mistake by not doing that and having to cough up 2000npr per person just for the guide, ontop of the 1000npr entry fee for foreigners. my advice, don't take a guide.

the diety is said to represent the head aspect of shiva. the legend goes that when pandavas chased shiva who in the form of a bull was trying to escape from them, the body exploded. the hump fell at kedarnath while the head fell at pashupatinath. situated on the banks of bhagmati river which is the ganges of nepal, it reminds you of varanasi the inner sanctum of the temple has four doors, the west door is mostly open while the remaining doors open during specific hours. if wanting to do an abhishekam, you can get a ticket at the sbi bank counter in temple premises. don't go by touts who say that you can't get tickets for the same day. you get priority entry from the east gate and see the diety at close quarters and the chance to do aarthi. like varanasi there are cremations happening at the rear of the temple along the banks of bhagmati river and also an evening aarthi by riverside. there are many small temples within the complex. photography is not allowed once through the main entrance of the temple. entry to non hindus is restricted.
very energetic temple that takes one away to a diff world. easy access and very well managed. early morning visits highly recommended.

this is basically a crematorium. it looks like a river with kids playing but if you look closely the heaps of wood burning on the sides are actually bodies. it's surreal. kids swimming in the river while families wash their dead relatives in the river. fascinating.

this gate between live and death is very disturbing but a must see as we now know. we went there by coincidence because we gained some time when we took the plane from pokhara to kathmandu instead of the bus (saved us 6 hours). to see the public incinerations and the closeness between the different groups of believers, priests, corpses, beggars, monks, apes and tourists is really distressing. the practice of mourning that is so different to the western habits, the burning dead bodies, the smoke the sorrow and the misery.all this is totally different. but this place is the center of hinduism and very holy. we left it with a deep thoughtfulness.
its ahuge temple complex and a must see. they offer goat sacrifices at certain times of the year, and a lot of blood can be seen during such a sacrifice. its a beautiful temple, and a must see.
its truly magnificent, and steeped in history, a must see, very close to the airport in fact. very huge complex, and very old too.

the pashupatinath is one of the twelve jyotirlings of lord shiva. pashu (animals) pati (owner/minder). it is very scared for hindus, however be wary of the priests and the minders in the temple. it is also not clean or well maintained.

this place is very unique and interesting. but unfortunately partially destroyed by the earthquake. the admission price is quite expensive. the guide price is also quite expensive. shadu will ask for 10usd every photo with him. i came with 3 colleagues, i spent about 120usd. quite expensive.

must for hindus - those love shiva will definitely rejoice with devotion visit in morning hours @ 10;30 you will have complete puja from various abhishek to mangalaarathi - evening 7:30 also advisable during aarathi
its the temple of old lord shiva. its really nice temple which i the most important for hindus . wonderful to be there .

this is the most famous hindu temple.we worship the god named "shiva ji". people have lots of believe in this god.and behind this temple there is a river it's called "bagmati" and this is the place to do funeral as well in hindu style it's called "dahasanskar". so you will fell peaceful when you visit this place.
for hindus this temple is the most important place to visit, like mecca for muslims. you can see cremation also, i came with an experienced guide, much better

despite the main temple being only open to hindus, the whole area is a place of wonder. from the cremations in the river side to the monkeys in the hill, pashupatinath is another one of the "must see" of kathmandu.

we pay the crazy 1000 rupees fee. the place is pretty amazing. we get there by 6 and we can experience one cremation and the amazing dancing and music show across the river. this made all worth it. it happens every day from 7pm to 8pm aprox. every body crazy dancing and screaming with priests holding big fire candels. very cool. regarding the cremation, everybody knows is a public place and that they will be people taking pictures and all. the family pay a great deal of money to do this ritual and they are perfectly aware of tourists and all. if they want a private place there is one. but here is open for public and every body knows it.

very very old lord shiva temple, it is really very beautiful and spiritual place, for hindus it is one of the most important place to visit, it is said that lord shiva is present in active form in this temple, this temple has a very beautiful bharavnath, ganpati and hanuman idols, even there are so many small shiv lungs all around the main temple.

i had mixed feelings about visiting this place. it was such an educational experience to learn about and see first-hand hindu death rituals. being from the us, where grief and the whole business of death are intensely private, it felt intrusive to witness what we saw here. i still feel it was one of the most important parts of our visit and i'm thankful for the experience. we visited during tihar in 2014 and again in 2016. tihar was indescribable. the day we visited pashupatinath was the day they worshipped dogs. there were dogs lounging on the stairs with tika on their foreheads and garlands of marigolds around their necks. we saw people being cremated on the banks of the bagmati, and learned about the two sides of the bridge. life and death are linked here, and it was beautiful to see another culture's handling of this inevitable part of life.

sad sad day for my friend, as her father passed away due to illness so he was taken to the crematorium, a newly built but in dire straits as all things are in nepal. not very clean, out of 2 one not working and one has to wait for more than an hour to get a turn. parking area poorly maintained and very very sad to see such conditions of the new crematorium dunno where all the funds go?

pashupatinath temple is one of holy places for hindu and it's one of 12 jyolingas. it was sad to see how poorly temple was maintained. no signs of directions within temple. temple premise is not at all clean and it was stinking. it was devastating to see such a holy place in this condition. hope nepal tourism will do well maintenance for their leading attractions.

this has been the highlight of my visit so far simply because i got to learn so much about hinduism and also the burial practices. i would strongly recommend going with a guide to provide you with some of the context and also to explain in particular what you can see unfold before your eyes. whilst the temple when approached seems a bit chaotic and dirty and in some areas affected by the great earthquake of 2015, it is worth holding judgement until you get to the banks of the river to see the throngs of people either sitting down with the brahims to honour the parents who has passed in yesteryear or those people who were there to bide farewell to loved ones who had recently passed away. the burial rituals were very visual and telling from the washing of the feet of dead with the holy river waters, to the preparation of the funeral pyres on which the dead where then cremated on the banks of the river. this was a very visually rich scene and at times quite sad as you saw family members breakdown as the weight of the loss of their lost one became too much i was thankful to have the guide unpick all that was going on and also position this with the context of the hindu religion do not miss this!

be here and connect with the almighty ! the premises is wide spread and you can visualise the greenery, the hills. once inside the temple, there is a divine connect.there is a peace of mind.surrender yourself, forget all worries.what an amazing journey of self one goes thru.
this is one of the major attractions in kathmandu valley. temples, surrounding are beautiful. please visit this temple and experience this holy temple with lots of things to do. i loved it.

the place is really very dirty and not maintained properly. its an important historic place from the perspective of nepalese history but the authorities need to pay attention to the cleanliness of the place. a very holy place for hindus. you can also see the burning ritual of dead bodies behind the temple.

as you enter the temple you are stunned by the imposing structure and the practices. i was fortunate to be there during the evening arti time and felt elated witnessing the arti. as you go round the temple, at the rear end the river bagmati flows and on the banks, the dead ones are cremated. it is mystical that though there were couple of bodies being burnt, there was no associated odour. it was only the fire and smoke and nothing else. as you go round the temple you see the suryanarayan, kal bhairav temples before being blessed by lord pashupathi nath
this is a greatest hindu temple, hindus from all around the world come for worship here, you can see what happens after death here, live cremation taking place here.

good to be here. and it is one of the top tourist spot in kathmandu. their are few option available to reach here. one is public transport which is very cheap and second by taxi. taxi's are little expensive.

we visited this site late on a thursday afternoon at the end of august. it was the most expensive of the world heritage listed sites in katmandu to enter, but well worth the expense. the visit gave great insight into the hindu religion and their circle of life. it was beautiful to observe the prepartion of a body for cremation by a family and interesting to learn about the caste system and cremation for hindu followers. you need a few hours to truly visit this site. we did not use a guide and traversed the site comfortably ourselves. be aware that potential guides and shopkeepers are very persistent on the site. a must-do location in kathmandu.

the temple had a lot of importance in my religion and i had a very serene and happy experience. lord pashupatinath was very gracious to grant me a patient darshan and was blessed by the priests there. the only thing i would like to request the devotees as well is to try to keep the place clean- while the temple authorities do try their best- we can also do our bit.

we went there as two families with the youngest being 8. we had a tour guide from our hotel, which i highly recommend so they can explain the cremation, shrines, holy babas etc. it was very humbling and yet awkward to watch families mourning a loved one and prepare them to be cremated. the grounds are lovely to walk around and you will get harassed by vendors etc. there are monkeys all over, my sons best experience was when a monkey stole his coke bottle! definitely recommend.
must do when you are in kathmandu. strange place but very positive. we had very inspiring conversation with some local teacher. funny monkeys all around the temple.
a great time in visiting this sacred place lots of arts crafted everywhere idols and statues be aware of monkeys around, they won't attack you but might steal your sweets

you can see traditions of burning dead people. we hired a guide there and he told us that you can pay whatever you want at the end. the enterence ticket was 1000 ruppies for each person and each of us paid 1000 ruppies for guide. i think guidance was helpfull for us since we where not familiar with hindus traditions.
we witnessed 2 funerals taking place the day we came. it was humbling to witness another culture/religion and their grieving.

pashupatinath pashupatinath is very much a revered site, sacred to both buddhists and hindus. it is also a cremation grounds where cultural gatherings transpire.which includes regular ritual bathing. this spot too is the home of nomadic yogis,many of which favor these grounds for the meditative atmosphere evoked by this pilgrimage locale. one can request the renunciants to pose for photos.and often they are keen to demonstrate hatha yoga postures known as asanas. one time i was taken aback by a small group of snake charmers who were playing diverse hypnotic tunes on their folk instruments, much to the delight of all assembled. the gyrating spectacle sent a distinct chill up my spine as several of the six foot long cobras slithered in my direction! hence i beat a hasty retreat with my camera and tripod. i was prepared to keep the tripod legs fully extended to ward off the creatures who for some unknown reason seemed to share a growing fondness for my presence there. my retreat was successful.and i did not look back! on he a subsequent visit i asked some local man about the charmer entourage. he mentioned that this group was eventually banned from the site, most likely for safety reasons. yet today the wandering sadhus, decked out in their exotic finery, placid cows, and lively monkeys all seem to adore pashupatinath. as an aside, proper etiquette is to be sensitive about taking photos of the cremations taking place. instead you might consider purchasing some local arts and crafts accoutrements such as miniature prayer wheels,rudraksha neclklaces made from seeds,.and the like. whatever transpires, enjoy that which is both rare and beautiful.
the manner in which the temple is managed is pathetic. the pandits are rude and despite standing in serpant ques, you may end up not getting a dekko of deity
one of the oldest temples in nepal, pashupatinath temple has a unique grandeur attached to it. go around the aarti time to witness the whole experience.

i have been to this temple twice and it was a very good experience to be there, we went in the afternoon time when it was sunny and our feet were burning with heat, morning is the best time to visit actually, but afternoon the rush is less hence we had finished with our pooja immidiately. the place requires lot of maintainance as there is cow dung spread every where.

it was very scary to see burning body but it was very different experience for us. the surrounding area and view of temple are excellent. the only disappointment was it was not allowed to non hindu so we could not go inside.

it is an interesting temple to visit but it is a bit in your face with corpse burning on the riverside. it is dirty and muddy (as expected during the monsoon) but saw a couple of thefts and be aware of people trying to scam money of you. it is quite expensive to get in for foreigners 10 us $ per person and almost impossible to let to walk around alone so be prepared to pay for a guide.

i visited a lot of temples and this one was the most memorable and special. i actually coughed up the $10 and got a guide, well worth it, and stayed until the aarti ceremony at 6:30/7pm - definitely worth waiting to see the ceremony!

only for hindus thus temple has an amazing feel about it. a lot of hindus visit everyday to worship. it's a lovely place to visit in kathmandu but the only issue is only hindus are allowed inside the temple.
pashupati nath temple was very pious place, you feel positive energy when you be there, being a hindu at least once you have to visit in your life time.

its a highly spiritual place but be prepared for lot of litter n filth everywhere. the dead bodies are cremated here. you can actually see all of it happening. if you are not a hindu, you cannot enter the main temple but you can still see it from next door temple. becareful while taking pix of any saadhu here. they ll ask money from you after you take their picture. according to our licensed tour guide, most of them are fake saadhu, but not all of them.

a cremation ghat is abutting on the temple. entire temple is utterly filthy with rotten flowers and stench everywhere. but, this temple is supposed to be very powerful. so, i would say you avoid all the rituals and drama and just sit in a quiet place for some time and leave.
world heritage. you cannot enter into the main temple if you are no hindi, but it is possible to visit the rest of the area and even see the cremation ceremony near the river

it's refreshing to see such a big temple that is still used. it has the antique look and everything, but is still crowded with people that came there to worship. despite many tourists, this doesn't make it feel like a tourist spot. the temple along the river is just beautiful. a bit sad, that non-hinds can't go inside. but you can find spots with a nice view of the golden pagoda and the bull from almost all sides.
its a magic and a pure temple of lord shiv, i was having back ace since 7years but after been there my ace has gone off. so pure and sacred. your wishes comes try. must visit .

during my visit to the pashuptinath, i took time to go to the the river and witness the rituals involved in preparing a body for cremation - and the placing the body on the pyre. it was an extraordinary experience.

people with belief in god - must visit for a puja - not necessary you have to pay and give puja - a darshan is also worth it - have to travel through polluted streets of kathmandu but once you reach it is just the temple visit
a divine place full of positive vibes. the temple needs maintenance and cleanliness but it still is beautiful and calm providing peace to souls.

this famous ancient shiva temple is visited by thousands of devotees everyday. a high security, huge premise, various on going rituals will amaze you. due to continuos offerings the premise is bit dirty. overall impressive & must visit!

the pashupatinath temple (nepali: पशुपतिनाथ मन्दिर) is a famous, sacred hindu temple dedicated to lord pashupatinath and is located on the banks of the bagmati river. this temple is considered one of the sacred and oldest temples of hindu religion. this temple complex is on unesco world heritage sites's list since 1979. this main temple is built in the nepalese pagoda style of architecture. the deity here gained great fame as 'pashupati' the 'lord of all pashus', which are living as well as non-living beings. one must know the various timings of worship so that you would not miss the darshan of lord of all lords mahadev. inside the area of main temple only hindus are allowed & it's a no camera & no mobile zone. one can also travel outside the pashupati area, guhyeshwari temple (lord parvati- saktipeetha), crematory area in the bank of the bagmati river, outside the main temple there are so many other famous temples to be visited. you can also buy some bananas inorder to feed the darling monkeys residing inside or outside the temple areas, and biscuits to feed the faithful dogs outside the temple area. by doing so, you will be surely blessed😇😇🙏🙏

this is an amazing lord shiv temple ,it shows you the the creation of god ,the surroundings are also nice ,you should visit on evenings and weekdays as weekends crowd will be very high in nos great temple with really huge following. recommended to visit during evening so one can see the evening grand prayers done traditional way!

the temple site and the chance to visit this shiva holy site is a wonderful experience. but, as usual, humans have their own way to make this experience self-serving for themselves. how? priests want money to do your prayer. there are many temples inside and at each temple, a priest is there who points after you pray to give a donation. there are people there who will give you a menu of prices for various levels of prayers you can do starting at rs 3100. thankfully, since only hindus are allowed in tthis temple site, only the hindus are fooled not foreigners. but wait, some will tell you that foreigners must pay rs 1000 to see the cremation area. this is wrong.no fee is needed. i am a hindu with christian (white) wife so i had to separate from her for a while to do this visit while she waited outside. also, as a foreigner, you will get your share of people trying to sell you something or beg for money. i gave openly but others should be aware.
i been here at that day where the nepali women used to celebrate there some specail festival ,its crowdy but i am happy to see the way they celebrate ,
such a nice to be here, we were so curious to know about the hindu culture. also this place is listed on world heritage side so we were so nice to be here .

planning to have dinner with a friend who took us to pashupatinath temple - auspicious occasion of ganga aarthi. lifetime opportunity of witnessing the whole ceremony of this festival standing with a crowd of locals

pashupatinath temple is one of the main hindu temples in kathmandu. incorporated into the temple grounds are the ghats (cremation grounds) where corpses are publicly burnt up to this day. while respectfully watching the fire consume the bodies take the opportunity to contemplate your own mortality and resolve to live a good life. the temple grounds also include various fertility lingams and hermitages of so-called 'holy men' who seem to be more interested in tourist cash than nirvana. a little upstream from the main temple complex there are two caves purportedly those of the great buddhist mahasiddhas tilopa and naropa. next to these is a modern 'holy' man's hermitage who captured and caged a wild monkey and keeps it in awful conditions. very sad and most unholy.

pashupati nath is hindu temple in kathmandu city, it is just 3 kilometer from kathmandu. and around 7-8 kilometer from tribhuvan airport. i visited this place with my family. you can eaisly get hotel near by this place is just start npr 500
it is a pitty that if you are not hinduist you are not allowed to come in inside the temple o ly around the temple but they make you pay 1.000 nepali rupiea anyway 😠

we enjoy visiting cultural/religious places and were looking forward to this. unfortunately we were very disappointed, the entrance at which our taxi driver dropped us (i believe this to be the main one) involved a walk through a dirty, smelly path. got to a point and just had to give up and return to the car. went to the boudhanath temple next and loved it.
one of the four most important religious sites in asia for devotees of shiva. it's an oldest hindu temple in kathmandu.
you will actually feel the flow of positive energy at this place. ancient sacred placeamong hindu pilgrimages. one must visit this place if you are in nepal.
indeed the temple visit and lord shiv pashpathinath divya darshana from all four sides with aarathi made us very happy,

i was deeply moved by this place. i hired a guide and this is well worth the money. i watch a family washing the feet of their relatives and then crossed the bridge to the cremation site. at first i was shocked by the process and then realised this was far more honest than the sterile way we reality death in the west. i was deeply moved by the cremation which was not ghoolish in any way. although the place dealt with death, i have never been to place sinfully of love and life.
went here to get some positive feelings and god's blessings. it's a huge temple with so many gods inside. shivalinga is the main god there. lovely place and really worth visit!

haarati is one of prayer or puja. puja or poojan is a prayer ritual performed by hindu to host, honour and worship one or more deities, or to spiritually celebrate an event. when i visited pashupatin temple, i saw that prayer. and i am bery lucky to see that kind of prayer. this temple is hindu temple which has many stupas and in the evening, you will see this kind of prayer, during the sunset untill end. pashupati has a holy river besides and there are many monkey too.

the temple is ancient and needs a guide to explain the story behind it. offers great views of the bagmati river. not a place fir the faint hearted as rituals if the dead being carried out continuously. cellphones can be carried but must not be used. the 4 doors of the temple ooen in the evening during sunset and the deity can be viewed from all 4 sides.

being hindu one must visit and one should do abhishek. one has to take ticket from bank in previous day so one should do that to have puja. it is jyoti ling and lird shiv has four faces. it is v nice and god is live.

we were very surprised to witness actual hindu cremations. we learned the cultural significance of cremation, how bodies are cleansed, purified and prepared for this ritual. it was a very personal experience to witness the hindu farewell to loved ones. we met babas and received blessings at this place. there are people who try to sell you things, but they're not especially intrusive- just firmly let them know you're not interested and they'll stop. overall, this was a good experience; a reminder that death is a part of living-
beautiful and powerful temple.something unique here.i havent seen in any other temples.i really felt excited exploring.worth it

in agreement with all other reviews, a visit to pashupathi mander is a must-do when you are in kathmandu. it shows the cleansing and cremation rituals in hindu culture, which you are able to follow and see from the moment a dead person is brought in (they are brought in all the time as in hindu culture, a deceased person needs to be cremated as soon as possible), cleansed, wrapped, burnt, and his/her ashes swept in the holy river. apart from the cremation, there are several small temples and stupas, dedicated to shiva, and the domain is quite large. you can easily spend 2-3 hours here. recommended to have a good guide to explains you everything that is going on.

very famous and popular destination for hindus as once in a lifetime every hindu should go here. all major char dham darshan which is situated in the four corners of india will be completed by doing this alone temple. lot's of small and medium size temples present in the area of the main temple. temple has four gates as representing four dhams. this temple included in to unesco world heritage site.

welcome to lord shiva home.pasupatinath temple in kathmandu,nepal its a magical and very holy shiva temple where u can admire a shiva lingam.among other unique things. the temple is quite crowded at certain evening times.so its better to visit it early in the morning.although at 6.30 there is a aarti taking place in the banks of river bagmati right at one side of the temple. i suggest u all visit it slowly and with enough time cause each corner is symbolic and unique.specially if u are a lord shiva devotee. then late in the evening.take a deep breath and enjoy the beautiful aarti and bhajans music.its recommended to stay till the end .for about 2 h cause the place is so enchantting and magical that u wont feel like leaving. keep in mind that u have to go bare foot inside the temple though there is a place outside the temple you can leave your shoes.and also. be ready to see lots of monkeys.roaming around like home. in a word.pashupatinath temple is a must to visit in kathmandu.lord shiva will welcome you so be prepare to have his darshan.

1000 rupees per foreigner. for this you cannot enter the temples. on arrival when we were told this price, we jumped on tripadvisor to see if it would be worth it. based on the reviews we chose not to pay this ridiculous price and instead wandered around the site. we enjoyed some buildings from further away and walked down to the river to see some burning pits. however not long after we had walked down to the river we were chased by a security guard who demanded to see our tickets. decide for yourself if you want to go, but we think 1000 rupees is a ridiculous entry price without being certain that all the money is going towards restoration.

this is one of the 12 jyotirlinga which are considered the most revered of shiva temples, this one is outside india. temple is not overcrowded nor dirty, at times there will be a queue, but mostly it's difficult to offer puja. it's a temple complex, in which there are many, many small temples, some inhabited, some vacant.the earthquake has damaged some structures, but main temples are more or less intact. temple is situated on river bagmati, and beside the temple, there is a cremation ghat. on the other side of river there are numerous temples on the slope of a hill, some populated by sadhus. great experience, not to be missed.

as it is famous for hindu pilgrimage, however, this temple is great to see for visitors, located on the bank of bagamati river. there some crematory on the bank of river where hindus will be cremated after passing away.
kathmandu is really very dirty and this temple is not an exception. i was so disappointed to see this temple was also dirty, smelly and chaotic. it was not a pleasant trip for me.
this is the very old and holy temple of bhagwan shivshankar.you can visit early morning and perform abhishek.one must visit pashupatinath temple in nepal

it was a spur of the moment decision to jump into a taxi from thamel and see pashupatinath temple. the cab cost 350 npr. there was still a lot of damage to outer buildings from the earthquakes but it is an impressive site. it's a complex of many temples surrounding the main temple. as a non-hindu you cannot enter this temple but you can glimpse through the 4 main gates. for hindus, cremation here means an escape from reincarnation and entry to the afterlife. however, for such a morbid, yet spiritual place, there seemed little sense of occasion. i would highly recommend taking up the offer of a guide when you buy the ticket for entry - and a guide certainly will offer his services! entry is 1.000 npr per person. the same was demanded by the guide, but a bit of haggling will reduce that. take the chance to photograph the sadhus. these are religious people who have renounced worldly life, yet have a price for appearing in photographs! the bargaining started at 2.000 npr. monkeys are in abundance at the temple but don't get too close to cause alarm. for those short of cash for this holy place, there are cash machines conveniently located in the complex. after spending a good couple of hours here, you can spend whatever money you have left in the hundreds of souvenir stalls approaching the complex. in summary, this was a moving experience. i've never witnessed a cremation before. yet, religion seemed to take second place to money.

a very old shiva temple and consider as important as jyotirling. there are two queues formed. if you are in hurry, you can stand in front queue itself which is generally shorter. this queue remains on back side but barely 5 ft behind front queue but it had raised platform to do darshan so view is almost equal.
