pashupatinath temple is not only one of the most revered sites for hindus, but also a must-visit site for everybody who want to see the hindu funeral rites and aarti, the evening prayers at the bank of holy bagmati river. the evening prayers are solemn and full of celebrations at the end --- the musicians and priests sing the hymns, the devotees chant along swaying their bodies to the tunes of the mantras -- and you feel like you're approaching salvation! it's so soothing and satisfying experience! then you see the realities of life -- dead people being burnt on pyres! you'll feel like there's no need to amass wealth 'cause at the end everybody dies!

in my nepal visit, pashupatinath was not included in my list of desired places to visit, from the net i got many negative reviews about that place. some said they took too much entry fees, some said they didn't allow to enter the temple to non hindu visitors. but my taxi driver told me -"if you like to do some photography, you must visit this place." when i enter the area i also realize it. first of all, i surprised to see that actually now the authority didn't take any entry fees from anyone. the atmosphere is different. you can see cremation is going on, just walk through the bridge & take the stairs & it will lead you to a different zone where you can find hundreds of small old temples like buildings & many sadhu baba's. recently famous hollywood movie dr. strange shows some view of this place, shooting also done in the temple premises. this place should not be missed if you travel to kathmandu . p.s. due to extensive dust of cremation, smoking ganza by shadu's & air pollution you should wear a mask in face.

pashupatinath is one of the biggest hindu temple in nepal, lots of festival is celebrated here like teej,shivaratri, shavan somari. lots of people across the globe come to visit pashupatinath in shivaratri! and shivratri is said to be one of the biggest festival of lord shiva.

the temple is a must visit for hindus due to their faith in shiva. the temple complex is really huge . however the main temple and area around it is limited. as you enter you gets to see a kalaeidoscope of 'sadhus' dressed in 'bhagva' clothes and painted in an interesting manner . it is like any other hindu temple. the holy and pure vibrations are tainted with noise, crowd, dirt around the temples and commercialisation.

my husband and i visited the temple and ghats and enjoyed learning more about the hindu traditions. we witnessed a couple of cremations but were horrified at all the tourists taking pictures! some going directly up to the deceased to get a photo! one girl even taking a selfie! please be respectful when you go! how would you like it if it was your relative getting cremated! also be prepared to be pestered by guides wanting money and women selling jewellery who will follow you unless you are firm with them. also the sadhus will ask for money to take their photo.
a must must visit to each and every hindu pilgrimage or tourist in kathmandu. you will feel the entire journey of human life by blessings of lord pashupatinath.

the venerated hindu temple of pashupatinath is well worth a visit when kathmandu. just beware the fake baba's (holy men) who encourage you to take their picture then try to charge up to $25. if you would like a photo just be sure to set the price first which should cost no more than $100 npr ($1 usd)
the holy hindu temple. this is a must visit place. as a foreigner you'll get to know and see the hindu culture. this is a must visit place if you are in kathmandu.

it was nice seeing actual cremation ceremonies performed in the temple. this was definitely one of my highlights in kathmandu. guides are available upon ticket purchasing. you may hire them if you wish to know more of the history and traditions of the nepali people.

so amazing to observe this solemn ritual. when a hindu person dies they are almost immediately brought to this site and prepared for cremation. if no one is ahead of you, it's immediate. if the spots are full toy get in the queue. unlike other reviews, we did not pay a fee.
come here in the morning/early afternoon to witness public cremations and interact with the sadu's. they will glady go on a picture with you in exchange for some money.
best place fir shiva divotee huge nandi and 8 mukhshivlinga darshan in unforgettable peacefull structure is not damaged in earthquack

came solo. hired a cab from thamel and visited pasupatinath. hired a guide during the trip and he explained everything that's happening in this area. learned some about how buddhists cremate their dead love ones under buddhist religion. the place is actually a crematory site where dead bodies are wrapped in a clothing, placed in the platforms, burned for 3-4 hrs. only the son or male relative can burn the body. females aren't allowed to stay near the platform. some men live in caves near the area and believed them to be holy.

one cannot review a holy place. but having said that it's a beautiful ancient temple , and we were glad to have had d opportunity to visit it. it was on my mother in law pilgrim list for a while and the main reason for visiting nepal. we got an abhishek done at d temple. but one needs to be careful n ask all d details of d dakshina as well as the actual site of d pooja. we also attended the evening aarti by the river bhagmati which was a beautiful experience aswell. there is also a shakti peeth close by, guhneshwari devi. it's situated along the river as well. would highly recommend it for any pilgrim visiting the city. only hindu's are allowed inside the temple, but saw a lot of tourist clicking pictures by the gate. one will find a lot of animals roaming around the temple goats , cows , dogs.a rare thing for nepal temples i have been told. the reason being most temples have animal sacrifice ritual there. this is the only temple where animals are not sacrificed. reason being pashupatinathji is considered as patron god of animals.

the temple includes main shiva temple and hundreds of other hindu temples. at the bank of river, there is an early medieval buddha temple as well, which is not seen by most tourists. the cremation which happens throughout the day is part of their hindu vedic cultures. highly recommended place to visit in kathmandu.
maintained by nepal government , needs more maintenance. a must visit temple once you are in nepal - a pride of nepal. local market around the temple have lots of local artifacts to choose from.

well, first of all you pay 1000 rupees for this hindu temple but i can say that definetly worth it especially for westerner. there is an intense smell everywhere of the temple. you can just sit there and watch the rituals or take pictures with hindu priests!

the most popular & inspirational place in whole of nepal kingdom, the temple is of great faith and significance to hindu devotees. one could take darshan of 4-facet 'shiv ling' - a unique temple in itself. it's a huge campus with lots of mini-temples encompassed. photography inside temple is strictly prohibited. try visiting on days other than saturday, monday as it is over-crowded. also visit before 13:30 if you wish to take the darshan from all four windows.

oldest hindu temple of city where lord shiva is established, this temple has good scenario from front & sad moment at back of the temple as it is known as aryaghat being holy place many people are burn here acc. to hindu religion after their death. next stay away from monkeys.

it was one of the finest temple i've ever been,firstly because nepal people are really good with hearts so it creates such an environment for which you will fall automatically and secondly the architect,which is marvelous.lot of birds around ,sadhus performing their own meditation.they have evening ceremonies too which is again worth watching.so overall experience was amazing and would love to visit again.highly recommendable try to be there on full moon,they have special ceremonies for it.cheers fellas \z/

pashupatinath temple has both historical and cultural significance to hindus across the world. the temple is said to be the oldest hindu shiva temple in the world and has exceptional carvings. only hindus are allowed in the temple. there are several temple stores outside that sell small shiv lingala as souvenirs. offerings of sweets, fruits and flowers are made. the temple opens in the morning and the evenings.
i feel very relaxed to sit beside the river and enjoy the evening prayer at this place. amazing feel.

entrance fee is np1000 per person. you may hired a guide with cost of np500 (optional) be careful with fake tourist guide. from my review, there a few cases of fraud. there are about 20-30 bodies burned at this place, so you may have high chances to see the ritual from the beginning to the end. you may not need tour guide at this place if to observe the ritual. be careful if you decide to take photo with the sadhus (kamiri). they invite you to take photos with them, then later ask for unreasonable token (np2000 for each sadhus). if you really want to take photo, pls ask them the charge (unless you don't mind to pay). according to the guide, normally sadhus ask about np200-300 per sadhus. after that incident, i don't have trust to this sadhus already which claim to be very 'religious'. after all, this is must visit place. suitable for everyone.some guide also may ask for your entrance ticket as the ticket is valid for 1 day. probably they try to resale.

one of the great religious place of kathmandu nepal. it's a world heritage. their not allowed for all religious. it's a really amazing place and peaceful place. their many people are sing a god vajan and many people's are dancing also. this moment is very enjoyable. it's a nice place for visit.

a nice place with beautiful corners to take pictures, very dirty and the river also, if you are not hindu you are not aloud to enter but still you see much, too many poor people asking for money, but still very colorful and by the way on your left side at the entrance a shop selling original natural colors for painting, an amazing variety, thank you suman. !

the place is really nice, but doesnt worth to pay to visit the inside, unless you are really keen to see the dead bodies burning by the river. otherwise you can watch this from the wall behind the temple. inside beside the river with the cremations there is another hindu only small praying temple. you can go in if you are white, i entered with a nepali friend, since i am latina i managed to get inside without talking at all, pretending to be local. inside there is different colorful monks keen to take pictures with you. dont give them money! or at least no more than a couple of rupees. there is prayers in the evening when the cremations are done.
this temple complex is on unesco world heritage sites's list since 1979 considered one of the sacred temples of hindu faith
the temple is huge including its surrounding compound. its mostly close in afternoon.check timing before you go.
the temple is great but the premises is maintained really bad ! the river looks like a drainage ! dirt everywhere! it's a world heritage site and it needs immediate help.

totally not worth the trip. there was little redeeming about this poorly signed and dirty place. visitors should be aware that the temples do not allow non hindus into the temple proper. perhaps the inside of the temple is far superior.
although they charge the tourist a high entrance fee in a rather agressive way, it is a very original site with funeral-bodyburning, monkeys, holy men and a very beautiful temple area uphill.

me and friends went to visit here. however the taxi driver left us at the entrance for hindus. do not approach this area for the hindus, as soon as you do we were told we could not enter but we had to pay a fee of 1000 rupees each and that he will take us to the correct entrance. we just agreed as we didnt know where the other entrance was. so i suggest if you are in a taxi make sure they take you to the non hindu entrance. it is just around the corner anyways, if not. but please make sure to go to correct entrance as there is no one to charge you there. the fake 'official' guide took us around and spoke briefly about what happens and then told us not to cross the bridge as that is the hindu side and then he flashed a fake lanyard so quickly. it is a fake. do not trust this man. because when we gave the money we said "so where is our ticket" and he said we didnt need one, and we said "but what if we get asked" and he said we wouldnt so dont worry and he left. so do not trust anyone who approaches you. however, it was a great experience anyways and i still recommend people to go. rant over.

i loved pashupatinath temple and the surrounding complex. watching the cremations was fascinating and, oddly enough, didn't feel super intrusive. a guide approached us on the bridge to explain some of the ritual behind the cremation (significance of flowers, colors, etc.) - we ended up hiring him for 700 rupees because he was super knowledgeable. the tour was short but nice to contribute to the local economy and get a bit of information. there are no maps on site, which is kind of annoying. we spent a while floundering around, just trying to figure out where to go, as there is a hindu entrance and another entrance that takes you across the river. to get into the general admission entrance (we'll call it), you have to take a right before entering the main gate. then you cross a bridge and you can view cremations from the other side of the river. if you walk up the main stairs, you can have a look into "deer park", where you will see monkeys climbing up the gates and trees to jump from a few metres high into the puddle down below. i could have watched them for hours. it made me so happy. worth the 1000 rupees! i would definitely go back. and great photo ops, if you're into that kind of thing.

this is the most important hindu temple in nepal with access restricted to hindus only. non-hindus are not permitted inside. the temple is next to the bagmati river where you can see the deceased being cremated. my tour guide mentioned that about 100 bodies are cremated each day. monkeys can be seen around the river. you can cross the river on nearby footbridges to see the back of the pashupatinath temple. hindu holy men can be seen and be photographed for a price. there isn’t much shade around the temple so a hat, sunglasses, and sunscreen are highly recommended.

the main attraction and well-known holy place for the hindus. a large pegoda structure is present inside the main temple area. one can do many types of pooja here. lot's of ancient hindu temples are placed near the main temple. a large nandi idol is present here. photography is strictly prohibited inside the temple area.

beautiful and very ancient temple on the banks of bagmati. a must visit place for devout hindus and non-believers alike. lovely architecture with a distinct nepali style and form, a visit here uplifts your spirits in many ways. huge campus with many small shrines dedicated to other gods of hinduism. loved it every bit!

this is a large hindu temple complex, which unfortunately does not allow non-hindus to enter. thus all you can do is walk around it. the rear of the temple backs up to the bagmati river, where there are cremation pyres. when we visited, we perched on the far side of the fairly narrow river and watched transfixed as a number of cremations and attendant ceremonies were performed. definitely worth a few hours.
you didn't go to kathmandu if you didn't visit pashupatinath temple , it is as simple as that . pahupatinath temple is the spiritual and cultural hub for hindus all over the world .

one of the biggest temple complexes that i have visited so far. huge golden ( brass) bull facing pashupatinath,hanuman, vasuki, unmatta bhirava, suryanarayana inside and guhyeshwari temple behind the complex. beautiful temple dedicated to maheshwara as pashupatinath on the banks of vangmati river( known as bagmati river) is extremely peaceful inspite of huge number of pilgrims rushing in, to get a view of the lord. best way spend 5-10 minutes peacefully in front of the god is to secure a ticket for rudrabhishekam which happens from 9 am. they have limited number of tickets, so try to get one, a day in advance. the nepal bank in front of temple is where you pay about 5000 nepalese rupiah and get the ticket. evening arati gives one more chance to see the god. ( very crowded again). another thing not to miss is ganga arati at vangmati river right behind the temple at night where you can feel the vibes again. guhyeshwari is shaktipeeth behind pashupatinath temple. few minutes by walk or taxi. one has to visit the temple to feel her presence there.

this is the only shiv temple wherein it has 4 doors and 4 face of shiv ling, this is the holy place for hindu and only hindu are allowed inside the temple. you'll find lot of external tourist visiting this place this is located near gaushala and must visit place

this is a sacred hindu temple, thus only nepalis and indians are allowed. indians sikhs, jains are also allowed. muslims, christians and people from any other nation can visit the outer complex of the temple by paying 1000 nepali rupiya. the temple is beautiful from inside and has many statues. temple is visited by many hindus to complete their whole darshan of a single statue. as the head part of god shiva is in pashupatinath and body is in badrinath. the temple is open from 3:30 am to 12 pm and then opens at 5:00pm. there is a "mahaarti" at 7:00pm.

it was our 1st experience to see the cremation ceremony for hindu. a mix feeling for us to see the family of the dead were mourning, yet we were very curious on the ritual process. it was 5 burning rituals on the time and day when we visited and it was really put a mark on our mind, to experience this kind of sacred rituals.

the biggest drawback of the place is that there is a lot of people pushing you to buy their goods. i know that this is common in the country but in pashupatinath we found it exceptionally pervalent. the same applies to the tourist guides. one guy joined my wife and me saying that he would show us the route and then he stayed with us for another hour without asking us if we actually wanted to hire him. we saw it coming so we offered him money before he asked for it, however the entire situation was quite awkward. other than that, this magic and at the same time bizarre place is definitely worth visiting - a lot of holy and sacred places, sandhu men, cremations, monkeys and roaming bulls.

it is definitly important hindu temple and very interesting place to see. we could not see better the temple beacause we are not hindu but that is ok and to be respected. the fact that there are a lot of very pushing people around who disturb you and follow you to sell the things and offer guide service even when you politely reject for a lot of time made us felt not so secure.

we visited pashupatinath temple hindu religios temple devotees of lord shiva.so beautiful place. we got chance to view ritual funeral of dead body. it was really different i never saw in my life before. attraction of monkeys was more funny. be careful of monkeys otherwise they can robbed your bag .

it was a great experience visiting to pashupatinath temple. i saw more monuments rather than other places, and the amazing thing is that there were more number of sadhus rolling in the streets, i came to knew that they were the main believer of lord shiva

a religious place for hindus. jai jai shambho! pashupatinath is revered as one of the holiest place of hindus. non-hindus are not allowed in the courtyard of the temple. devotees from nepal, india throng to this temple, especially during maha shivaratri, teej. it is believed that darshan of pashupatinath is must for getting blessed after indians visit char dham. lately, it has started evening aarati at the back side of pashupatinath on the bank of bagmati river. it relaxes whole body and mind. here any religion people can come. a must visit place.

temple is a certain and, is usually the start of journey for kathmandu visit. highly recommended to visit. offer prayers and feel blessed. police are foreign friendly and provides necessary guidance and instructions.

we went to the temple as the first thing to visit kathmandu and were there in the evening. it is indeed a very good experience and after a darshan of the deity, we sat down for some time to aborb the peace in the air of the temple. it is one of the most famous temples of the hindus. we were also there for the aarti in the evening when the 4 doors of the temple are opened and we had a closer darshan though there was a lot of jostling. at this time it is avoidable to bring small children as there is quite a lot of pushing and shoving. we also went on the banks of the river bagmati behind the temple where there was a musical program which was really nice. again a crowded place but worth the visit. as far as money is concerned we had to pay the priests for a 2 second extension to our darshan of the deity but it was just an odd 200 rupees. a must visit for all hindus visiting kathmandu.

i had no idea they burned bodies at the ghats when i visited this place. it's quite expensive to visit (as it should be) but unfortunately non-hindus are not allowed inside the actual temple. i think this place is still well worth the visit as there is not much to do in kathmandu. you can easily spend 2 hours here. be prepared to be approached by "tour guides" that are not really necessary.

famous all over the world, and heard about a lot. once when i visit there first i see the many priests or baba are there and a lot of people's are blessed by them.it very good religious place who believes in god.

we visited as a group of 4 from uk. wow what an experience and so glad we did it, by our pockets were rung dry so be prepared! (backpackers on budgets) the actual experience was great and insightful with a new understating of the hindu belief and culture. entrance as 1000rupee each, we then were given a guided tour which what what we assume the 1000 each was for. turned out not and has a very awkward experience although learnt alot from the guide. we offered a 2000 tip to which he refused and demanded the cost as minim 4000 usually getting 8000rupee a tour. hard to believe. this is after our photo with the sadhu men and told to pay them 1000 each! i don't believe any of this excessive amount was required but trying not to pay it was resulting in a very tense atmosphere. all in all be prepared to pay a minimum of 3000rupee per person. a very educational trip and glad we did it seeing all the offerings of animals was amazing. you have to go if ever in kathmandu. hope this helps prepare any other westerners visiting as we were not prepared enough money wise! x
wow! a must see area. the guide explained the history of the area and meaning of the buildings.fascinating!
this is most revered temple of lord shiva in kathmandu, hindus worship here.nepalese solmenise low cost marriages at this temple

this is one of the most sacred hindu temples of nepal. pashupatinath temple is located on both banks of bagmati river on the east side of kathmandu. pashupatinath is a prominent temple dedicated to god shiva. it is the site of hospice care for the elderly where it is believed that the spirituality of the location can improve health to the point that they can return to their homes. it is also the site for the crematoria when the dead are properly tended to guide them to the afterlife. the main temple of pashupatinath is a building with a roof with a golden spire. as you walk around the complex, you’ll head toward the bagmati river where you’ll encounter the crematoria and the hospice areas. some locals call this a “monkey temple” and we found they are plentiful. however, this is a much more sacred site and reverence is the key word. we also recommend a guide. i’ve read some reviewers saying it was a waste of money but we hired a car and driver for $80usd for a half day tour. the guide knows so much more than your typical guidebook and will answer your questions. they have to go through training. we used pradip lama (pradiplama@hotmail.com) as our guide and he was terrific.

i simply enjoyed being near the sanctum santorum, the main temple has 4 doors in 4 different directions. there is a pooja system. the priests are honest they perform the pooja and take you around for darshan at the predecided rates displayed at various places in the temple. a must visit for those who would like going to ancient temples of lord shiva.

this place is incredible. pushupatinath is an extremely important part of nepal's culture. bring eyedrops and remember you are in a sacred place. people are surrounded by and constantly experiencing death here. that being said, it would be fine to bring the family as long as everyone is respectful.

entrance fee 1000 rupees; expensive. first time seeing open air cremation. we were able to partially observe the process of preparing the body to the cremation. climb up the steps to view the inner temple which is open only to the hindus.
another historically very interesting temple to visit and walk around - take your time to enjoy the ancientness of the place!

there was an on going puja so the place was crowded. 3 cremations in progress at the same time which can be a bit overwhelming for a first time visitor. our indian friends got to enter this sacred temple and get a blessing . don't be surprised if you are accosted either by monkeys or a bull looking for food

our kathmandu day tour took us here (and it is my second time, as it's pretty much in all the tours). it can be confronting for westerners. there are a lot of people here(excluding the tourist contingent), poverty, beggars with deformed and missing limbs, and cremations in full view. it's just the way life is here. monkeys play in the bagmati river below the cremations, and the ashes are simply shovelled into the river to make way for the next one, adding to the polluted state of the river. there are sadhu here (holy men), who will pose for photo provided they are paid, though one guide told me that they are fakes (frauds dressing up for the tourists to get money for the photos), but i don't really know either way. hold on and go and experience it - it's nothing you'll have seen before in western countries.

pashupatinath temple has its own unique beliefs which makes world hindu to believe in mahadev. centrally located and nearby from international airport, this place is one of the major tourist attraction and pilgrim tourism. evening time to be seated for aarati and have a a blessful time, it brings you a true peace of mind.

this is an interesting place to visit. unless you are hindu you are not allowed inside the temple but that does not detract from the overall experience. you will get to experience a lot in a small area from the beggars and hawkers as soon as you enter to the architecture and destruction from the earthquake to viewing cremations on the river. this is not for the weak and some people on our tour were disturbed from this experience, but that is why we travel to learn about other cultures and their way of life. be sure to have some small notes if you want to take a photo of the holy men, they will want money before you take the photo.

a very special and world heritage site. it's a temple complex near the river where the dead people were burned and the ashes is thrown in the river. a very holy place for the buddhism people. we should respect this place very much. the people which getting here have lost a member of family.

wow! i am tempted to describe this as mind bending, it is so far from my experiences. we were lucky enough to visit the temple on a holy day, which made it very busy but also added more atmosphere. the holy men, the cremations, the animals in the river, people in their finest lining up to go into the temple, the women burning wicks, people handing our drinks and roasted barley and so much more - it threatened to overwhelm the senses. one of the most amazing things i have ever seen.
must visit when in kathmandu. great mix of faith and reality. do visit also the kal bhairav and kaliyuga temples nearby.
bholenath.located centrally at kathmandu. darshan is available from three side of temple and one is reserved for paid darshan. a temple having samshan ghat(buried ghat) in its campus.
i was here 28 years ago and cycled there to have a look. it was nothing like this time .saw three funerals .what a window to an amazing culture.

you can walk just in front of the entrance of the tempel and take a peek inside. the only thing you will see is the big bump of nandi the bull. take some time to look at the people walking in and out the tempel. walk aroud the main tempel towards the gaths, cross the bridge and get ready to see some live cremations. people are tollerant here. you can take pictures of the whole ceremony. but be awere! when they start the cremation the face of the deceiced is visible. not for the faint hearted! or children.

the temple is in bad condition owing to the improper management and poor habits of most devotees visiting this place. the main shivling is kept open only in mornings and evenings between 5.30 - 7.30 pm. i visited in afternoon around 2.30 and was disappointed as the shivling was closed. visiting this temple is considered very important in the hindu community and hence i had to visit this temple again in the evening just to get a glimpse of the shivling. there's a ghat just behind the main temple where bodies are cremated openly, so stay away from the smoke. there are monkeys, lambs, goats, dogs all over the premises. the shoe counter can get full and you'll have to keep the shoes outside at your own risk (not to worry i guess). there's also a pigeon feeding place in the premises.

a dream to visit for many years. you find your mother nd at peace as soon as you enter the premises and feel relaxed once you have darshan. no long waiting a no pandas and a magnificent feeling of being so close to bhole baba.
the only reason to go to this temple was it's rarity. long queues and very unhygienic ghats is what you will get there.

we also visit this point on our sightseeing tour in kathmandu, booked by the travell agency gret vision & treks & expedition in karma travellor's hotel. (directly inside the hotel) very nice. perfect organisation of the team. thanks to shankar and his team. the service support is really fantastic there. this is a very interesting view point.
it is a holy temple having good atmosphere and environment the temple was not quite clean but it is a wonderful place to visit

visited temple and had good darshan. quite crowded in the evening during aarti time. better avoid it. lot of guides available to explain the history of the temple. can be used if needed. well maintained.

lord shiva is present here in grand form, chief preist here is from south india telling us the great national integration process followed by our ansestors has four doors in four directions showing four faced linga darshan, destruction of earthquake is still visible, cleanliness needs big improvement not only inside the temple but around also, trust is requested to play important role in improving the society life and mandir life
large temple with site of crematorium at rivers edge. interesting experience of taking care of their dead next to a large peaceful are full of temples and monkeys.
a simply divine place and is taken as a dhaam for hindus. lord shiva s power can be truly felt here looking at the old heritage temple
well kept, clean, no hawkers, nice to spend any amount of time you want for peace. the lord dwells here
if you reach kathmandu you can not think about come back without see lord shiva. its true a dam true.
pashupatinath mandir is the nation god of nepal. pashu means animal and nath means leader. this is the reason why all animals are got freedom in temple as we as in nepal. jay pashupatinath

this place has amazing aura. the moment you step in or move around.there are several shiv-lings in complex each must be having it's own story.carving's are breathtaking. there is big nandiji as you enter the temple. then there is a very long trishul of bhole baba ! word of caution stay away from pandit's approaching you fro puja .instead near entrance there is state bank of nepal, here you can deposit money and get puja done at jyotirling officially. different prayers start at 9:30am. if you go for official puja you will get more closer and longer darshan of bole baba ! there are different puja's available. jai bhole !

people told me about the big aarti ceremony they have in evening. so i went to catch this aarti ceremony. heard it starts at sharp 7:00, but strangely that day it started late by almost 40 minute. there are two aarti which goes on simultaneously. one in the temple and outside across the ghat. the setup of the outside one is strange enough. right below is a holy river, where people come for hindu ceremony before cremation is done. so one side there were people coming for this process and one place there is aarti going on. you kinda get a mixed feeling, and that too vibe is very strong. one more strange rule - white skin is not allowed in the premises of temple.

had a spiritual feelings when we entered the temple. without any words or instructions my inner journey had started looking at ancient temple, construction and feeling the long history of the famous hindu temple. must visit place for everyone. but cleanliness level should be improved a lot which can be done but need to change the mindset of the people. cleanliness is next to godliness!

even after more than two years of devastating earth quake, the debris were still not removed. it is supposed to be the richest hindu temple in kathmandu but hardly anything is done to keep it neat and clean. it was raining the day we visited and we were suppose to remover our shoes to enter the main temple, it was really difficult to walk bare feet as the floor was not clean, it was bit slushy also. anyway, it's a must see

much talked about from outside nepal as prime attraction. but the maintenance is poor. bagmathi rever on the rear looks pathetic. non hindus cannot access inside temple though they are charged npr1000 as entry fee. no footware, phone, camera inside temple campus. storage facility for footware, camera and phone are available but may be crowded sometime to make the deposit. it is good to make photos etc before depositing and leave them in your car (not on unknown taxies) with the driver acknowledging it. hundered of domes with hindu deities but no information displays around. pasu pathi nath. pasu (globe) pathy (the leader /cammander) nath (dwelling place). hindu religion believes brahma is the creator of the lives on earth, vishnu is the protector of the lives and shive (pasupathy) is the destroyer of lives once their time on earth is completed. even today deadbodies are cremaated on the banks of bhagmati river at the rear of pasupathynath temple with the belief departed souls will reach heaven direclty abode of shiva. it may rain time and hence good to have an umbrella

the best time to go there is at afternoon so that you can see the artworks in the walls of the temple in daylight and evening rituals just after sunset. it's a quite big and old complex but the craftsmanship in the walls and ceilings of the temple is really stunning. you have to enter into the temple without shoes but the complex is quite dirty with mud and urines of goats.

our visit to the pashupatinath temple was an amazing experience. on the one side is the ghat where you can see bodies being cremated & on the other side is the temple complex. since it was raining, the walk was dirty with the stone walking paths full of slush. the temple itself is impressive with doors where u can see the lingas. worth the visit but something needs to be done about the general cleanliness.

so here you are at the temple being a 6'5" white westerner. if you can take the smell of burning bodies, you can watch cremation taking place here. otherwise the best thing to see if the inside of the temple. if you are hindu. be ause otherwise you're not allowed inside. but don't sweat it! have fun with you being the tourist attraction instead. yes you'll have countless questions from people taking photos with you near the entrance to the main temple! haha!
amazing experience getting to watch the funeral pyre and the aarti at sunset however its a shame you can't see the actual temple and the river itself is horribly polluted.

pashupatinath is the most important shiva temple in nepal, but it's main shrine can only be visited by hindus. non-hindus can visit the rest of the complex including the cremation grounds. it's important to inform people about this cremation area, as not everyone is prepared to see death so close.
lord shiva's shrine and most preferred reason to visit kathmandu. i like the place most. must visit place.
the place has an important part in hindu mythology. the threshold from inside is very divine. however it is not clean enough. best time to visit is 9 in the morning.
one can meet these fascinating holy men, sadhus followers of hindu deity shiva in the premises of pasputinath temple, kathmandu!

i thought i may be uncomfortable visiting, given that people are cremated at pashpupatinath and i didn't want to encroach on people saying goodbye to their loved ones. however, this was not the case - it is culturally very different and it was actually a very peaceful place of worship, reflection and community. i would recommend going at sunset to catch the music and lamps, and to hire a guide to clue you into everything that is going on. one of my favourite places i visited during my stay.

i went here because of the cremations, which was really fascinating to see in a morbid way; but we also had someone guide us through it and found that we learned so much about the hindu religion by visiting this place. just take note that there are lots of people there who will ask you for money to take a photo with them.

a significant temple to visit for hindus, the sprawling temple complex is also a unesco world heritage site. a visit is accompanied with the usual crowds but leaves one in thrall too of the amazing number of shiv lingas installed there. apparently the complex has over 100,000 shiv lingas. the complex is dominated at the entrance by a giant statue of nandi. a spiritually satisfying visit. in spite of the thronging crowds, the process of darshan is hassle free and without the expected discomfort. a must visit if you are in nepal.

my friend and i visited this temple on april 4,2017 as part of our tour in nepal. it is considered the oldest hindu temple of kathmandu. countless smaller temples were constructed around the main temple on both banks of bagmati river during the last few centuries. it is a unesco cultural heritage site there are numerous legends, connected with the construction of the temple. the most famous one claims, that the temple was built on the site where shiva lost one of his antlers, while he was in the guise of a deer. he and his wife arrived to the bank of bagmati and amazed by the beauty of the site decided to change themselves into deers and walk in the surrounding forests. it was an excellent experience. a great place to learn about hindu customs and funeral rituals. the main temple is restricted to hindus only but the rest of the sprawling complex is open to anyone. the upper river banks are for high caste cremations, and the lower banks are for low castes. for foreigner the entrance fee is 1000 rs approximately 8€ or $ 11. it worth the visit.

this place is out of this world and there is a reason why the main temple is for hindu's only. i visited on the day of budhdha poornima and there was a special tantric puja held with around 86 dishes made for the lord pashupatinath. i felt so overwhelmed and sacred just to be present at that moment . hinduism is an old religion with many layers and requires deep knowledge and will to understand. i was fortunate to see the sacrifice as well. this was the most authentic temples i have visited. faint hearted should stay at their hotels and enjoy as this is not a park it is a place of great religious significance. please don't expect it to be pretty as this a place of lord shiva who is the god for all and every living beings and being who lived once. om namah shivay!

had great experience to visit kathmandu. if you are planning to visit nepal plan for atleast 4-5 days. apart from kathmandu there are several nearby places to visit like bhaktapur city, pokhra. in kathmandu you should visit pashupatinath temple, shanga, syambunath temple, bodhnath stupa. pokhra is a very beautiful place. people must go there and see snowy white mount everest valley from there. you can enjoy various adventurous sports there. to begin your journey by road or train first reach gorakhpur in india. from there you can hire a taxi or bus to sanauli border. it will took 2 hours to reach border. then from border you can hire a cab or bus to reach on top to kathmandu. it will took around 10 hours. roads are really very bad so carry your scalf to get rid of dust. on border only you can exchange your currency into nepal rupess. 100 indian rupee is equal to 160 nepalese. do bargain while buying things.

we went their in the evening. it is very big temple .magnificent surrounding. in hindu religion pashupatinath temple is very significant. after kedarnath (chardham) one must complete yatra by going to this temple. which i did and happy. head priest came late so god arti have took time .for darshan.it is hard to get in, unless you have pandit. their is no systematic darshan. you have to push each other. avoid pandit. just prey to god for everyone peace. temple should do something to have systematic darshan.

this is a unesco world heritage site and a holy hindu temple. i was not satisfied with the level of preservation and cleaniness for such a place - they do charge us$10 for entry. it is old and has huge religious significance for the hindus - they still creamate the bodies of the dead there and it was quite a confusing thought to have bodies being ferried in amongst the crowd of tourist. do take a hike to the top as it is quieter and a bit cleaner. i would also suggest a tour guide as the place would make more sense. i was snapping pictures of a cow and was reprimanded by someone - not sure if i was correctly reprimanded but i stopped.
nepal´s most hestrorical temple called pasupatnath temple.all the way from india and nepal also foreigner country´s people used to come here.because shiva god called god of god .
this is the place for hindu. who worship lord shiva and can be a great place for those who loves different temples, idols and many more
all of the babas are really great. it is a beautiful place to meditate as well as just to visit. huge turnout for holidays.

pashupatinath temple where a celebration was going on so it was very busy, but in a good way. lots of locals in celebratory mood! a guide took us round to the back of the temple (only hindus are allowed in the temple itself) to the sacred river, next to which there were open air cremations going on. in nepalese culture, they bring their dead to the closest temple within a few hours for cremation, usually with fire, followed by many days of celebration. we saw families bringing their loved ones to the river to wash their feet, covered in red, yellow and orange (to signify fire) before being placed on the funeral pyre.

as someone interested in culture and religions this location was the highlight of my visit to kathmandu. in typical nepalese style it is confusing with no instructions for foreigners but find a little wooden hut and pay 1000npr and get a guide. our guide took our tickets after we had used them in order to get some sort of payment (maybe?) but we also tipped him. our guide was extremely knowledgeable and took us everywhere (except the main temple which is for hindus only). the highlight of the visit itself was witnessing the cremation of an ex-gurkha. as the family attended to the cremation the last post was played by a group of gurkhas. we saw very few foreigners on our tour of the site and felt immersed in the culture. near the temple there were many hindu pilgrims very happy to be there and taking lots of photos and this contrasted sharply to the scenes of cremation which were ritualistically formal and somber with family members fully involved with proceedings. not at all like "hands-off" western cremations. the whole place was an assault on our senses. it was a hot day, there were fires for devotion, groups of hindus in religious services, goats and cows walking about unhindered, lots of monkeys (don't touch!) and pigeons (also having a favoured status). there were strong smells, funeral processions, statues and shrines to different gods, fabulous architecture, sadhus (our guide called some of them fake and others genuine). also many people just hanging about soaking up the atmosphere and having picnics. this place was authentic and was a good example of lived contemporary religion rather than a relic to the past.

in varanassi india we saw the raw deal. here we were on the other side of the river and had a excellent view of all the happenings at this crematorium! what a privilege! it is respectful, and done with dignity! we sat there for more than an hour and experience the hindu way of cremation! we had an excellent guide which told us exactly what was happening and why. the temple itself could only be seen from the outside( only for hindus) - but was beautiful! not to be missed at all!
fascinating place - photographers dream. with children it was quite a profound experience but very worthwhile. non-hundus are not allowed to enter the temple compound but plenty to see outide.

i keep visiting this place with my tour groups. a nice place to know about hindu culture. but makes me a bit sad. not by looking at cremation but things happening around. lot of tourist take photos of cremation which is a disrespect to the local culture and family who lost their loved one. its not entertainment. tourist are allowed here so that they can know more about the culture and tradition. lot of hawkers who keep walking around u trying to sell stuff. authorities should come up with ideas of keeping the bagmati river(temple is on bank of this river) more clean so that it doesn't get polluted. ppl dump many other trash else then ashes in river, this is not good. they need to work on it. it is lovely place to learn more about hindu culture n traditions followed in nepal. few sadhus are around here who want to get clicked and will charge u for it. so if u want a good shot, pay them a little bit n get it. non hindus are not allowed in main temple area but can walk around other parts of temple area. it have a entry fee of 1000npr for foreigners.

i went to the temple hoping to learn more about hindus final resting place and instead it was like entering hell. i i cant find the words of how disturbing and creepy the visit was. the extremely poor amputees begging for money, the fake tour guides, the perverted "saints" sitting their naked for people to take pictures with them, the locals with their families eating and going about as if any regular outing, the horrible smell, the dirty river with monkeys and people bathing in it! and the worst part was all the bodies burning! it made me sick to my stomach! avoid at all cost!

i am from the same city kathmandu where i have been living and the temple pashupatinath is located. i have visited the temple (which is also an world heritage site of unesco). i have got a chance to explore series of temples and monuments in the surroundings. it really has a massive history, culture, art and architecture. i am really impressed.

the entire area has been cleaned up and being remodelled to facilitate easy movement of visitors and pilgrims. the temple visit was smooth and hassle free thanks to the streamlined systems and procedures when human interventions are regulated and disciplined. the age old temple can be visited without any jostling and shouts! i hope the riversides will also get facelifts. the parking facilities too need attention.
it's a well known religious place, but not well maintained or organized. the statue is seen fleetingly & from a distance.

a very good temple with great nepali architecture. inside the temple we have both lord pasupathinath and lord bharaiva. a must visit place and people who wish to complete their 12jyothirlinga yatra / char dham yatra should visit it to complete the sampoorna yatra

the place is so big you have to make sure you had the right shoes. this is where the hindus cremate their bodies and let it flow in the river. they have a special temple too but you had to be nepalese or indian to be allowed inside. toilets are horrible hope you dont have to use one.
a good shiva temple. crowded. poojas conducted well. had a nice dharshan. shivalingam has five doors to be seen.
it's a very interesting place to visit but i was unfortunate to go on new year's day when it is packed with pilgrims and difficult to enter and walk around. very beautiful though.

we got to visit this temple on our second day of our 4 day tour in nepal. it was absolutely fascinating. much further in, our guide pointed out the river, rather dried up due to lack of rain. further down.a cremation was going on. quite a surprise, but still this being a holy place--we saw all sorts of human endeavors--from the celebration of life, to the release into the next world. it's nepal. and at the least, it makes for incredible stories.

i'm not a religious person, but enjoy visiting religious places for their architecture, carvings and the peaceful ambiance. however at this place the cleanliness needs to improve a lot. the vendors and priests also keep disturbing all the time. the only good part of my visit was witnessing a group of people singing prayers and dancing.

before entering there were some very sick people begging on the road. entrance fee is 1000 nrp. a guide showed up immediately though did not present himself as a guide. as he spoke good english and seemed to know his stuff i allowed myself to be guided, which i'm glad i did. probably not a place for sensitive people, but a powerful reminder of death and everything's impermanence. definitely an experience bringing deeper insight to hindu culture.
it a miracle place ! , believe dat nepal is a hindu country ,, '! religious people must visit dis place once is lifetime .! god n god

this was by far the most interesting sight in kathmandu. this temple is dedicated to shiva and is fronted by a huge terrace where there is a warren of activity. you will find cows and calves strolling, packs of monkeys running around, flocks of birds, throngs of barefoot hindus coming to warship, lepers and the sick sleeping or begging for money at the edges of the entrance, sadhus (ascetic, hindu monk-like characters) sitting around or making money from tourist photos. the sights and sounds here are thrilling. only hindus are allowed to enter the temple but once you walk across the bridge to the other side of the river, you can get a great view of the golden center of the temple as well as the crematoria below the temple, along the horribly polluted river. the bank of the river opposite the temple is beautiful, with small shrines containing lingam/uni statues lined arranged in linear patterns that produce beautiful imagery. sadhus were sitting around and chatting, waiting to make money from tourists snapping photos of them.
a palacios religious place to see. the deity of god shiva with four faces in four different directions is worth watching.the temple complex has many more small temples to see.
a great experience to see hindu practices being performed. a cremation was going on when i visited. the temple itself was beautiful and very unique to see.

when we first arrived we almost walked directly to the funeral pyres. luckily we realize something not so right and manage to u-turn. then we reached the entrance and been asked to buy entrance ticket. at this moment, a tour guide approached us and start to bring us around. at first we denied his service but then he told us that we no need to pay him for anything as his services was included in the ticket. after the tour, he asked us to give him our entrance ticket and walk away with it. we guess he took our ticket and sell to others tourist to gain earning from it, that is how the free service come into.
pashupatinath is one of the most holiest place for hindus. it is among world heritage site ranked by unesco. mostly people from india visit this place. people has great faith for pashupatinath.

the pashupathinath temple was said to be built by the licchavi king supushpadeva. later in 1099 king shivadeva rebuilt the temple. later in 17th century king bhupendra malla again rebuilt the pashupatinath temple. as per the early story gouthama buddha performed a penance sitting in the middle of burning fire which is called as panchagni.goddess parvati was happy by this penance and advised buddha to install a shivalinga on the banks of manimati river. buddha installed a shivalinga which was called as karunikeshvara. later the goddesses built a golden temple for karunikeshvara and the place was called as maheshvarapuri. as the centuries passed the temple was decayed and shivalinga was buried under the soil. kamdhenu used to come to this place and anointing the shivalinga by its milk. the local cattle feeding people (pashupalaka) found this miracle and they saw the buried shivalinga. they started worshipping the shivalinga for the welfare of there cattles and their families. then the shivalinga gained the name as pashupathinatha- protecter of cattle feeders. here we can see a unique form of shivalinga that we cannot see any where. the linga is around 3-4 feet height. it is having five faces which are called as sadyojata (bhumi-earth), vamadeva (water-jala), aghora (fire-agni) and tatpurusha (air- vayu). the fifth place is called as ishana (sky- space), this is facing above. we can see unmuktha bhiarava temple, vasuki nagaraja temple, mukti mantapa, santhaaneshavra mahadeva temple in this complex. there are thousand shivalingas in this complex where we can see 525 shivalingas. behind the temple bhagamati river flows. there is a ghat-crematorium where every day the bodies get sacrament. behind the temple there is a hill where we can see guhyeshvari temple. guhyeshvari is known as maha shakti peetha. the temple complex takes us to thousand of years back we can feel the journey in the time machine. i never forget the visit to pashupatinath temple. the pashupathi nath temple is a place of nath cult. this is yet another big story.
not so interesting. you are not allowed to visit the main place if you are not hindu. we just see the temple & the ghat from top.

if you have done with all 12 jyotirlings then if you do not visit pashupatinath, it will not be considered as accomplished. otherwise also, pashupatinath is must visit place. the ambiance, closest to himalya makes this shivling very special. the atmosphere is very serene , in air you literally you van hear omkar. on the banks of bagmati river this temple is unique presence of lord shiva. atheist must visit this place, they may not realize god, but at least their soul will have some thing to start believing in supreme authority. its great experience. must visit

this is one of the hindu's most holy temple complexes. they charge visitors into the complex. it's about 10 quid which i thought expensive. each day there are cremations going on at the side of the river. please be aware this is all in full public view. they bless the bodies near the water's edge and they are carried along the river and put on a pyre. the family then light the pyre, ashes to ashes. it's extremely humbling and sometimes hard viewing seeing families in such emotional turmoil all out in public. there are a lot of people begging and lots of homeless people around the temple. all trying to make a tourist dollar. the complex itself like most of kathmandu has been badly damaged by the quake. there are also lots of "holy men" around, they are all fully body painted, if you take their photo they will demand money from you, be warned. really worth a visit though as a westerner, opened my eyes.

famous about this temple monkeys are everywhere and cremations happen by the river. monkeys are mostly harmless and not wild. watching the cremations is very strange experience you can watch very close and see the body parts either and you can smell burning flesh. so it is interesting but definitely not easy, be consider before you visit. photographers can catch very nice captures. not appropriate for children.

i went there in the month of february, a few days before mahashivratri. the temple was nearly empty with enough time to take darshan. we could spend plenty of time in its vicinity, see the sadhus sitting in the temple premises and soak in the spiritual atmostphere.

this is a vast compound of buildings which hosts one of the holiest sites for hindus. the $10 admission fee does not give you access to the temple itself, which is unfortunately off limits for non-hindus. but you are here to witness the unbelievable (for westerns) open air cremation rituals, which happen around the clock. yes, of course it's off-putting for the faint of heart, especially when the wind blows ashes everywhere. lots of monkeys (plus dogs, cows, sheep) but also holy men/beggars who are happy to pose for you if you spare a dollar.

the title of my review is true, but i should add that when you are a foreigner (white skinned), they charge an entry fee which is quite high. while one of my friends is a beef-eating afro-american, and due to his skin color he could enter the holy golden temple, which is accessible only for hindus, me, who is a vegetarian and practice yoga, was barred. i don't like the discriminatory rule, at least it should not be based on skin color but some other criteria. but anyway, i felt elevated and overwhelmed by the air of this ancient temple complex, which reminded me somehow of varanasi in india.
this is one of the 12 joytilrings of shiva ( main seat of lord shiva) that is why so many devotes visit this temple.
this place is good for those who want to do pilgrimage , other wise as usual this temple is crowded and dirty , long ques,
this is the place must visit. hindu holy temple with long history. feel the reality of life to see died people burning there. only wish make clean and make more manged.

i visited lord pashupathi temple on my birthday and it was one of beautiful shiva temple i have visited so far, four faces of shiva and thousands of shiva linga could make you think about our ancient people architecture. people also really nice and explain about history of the temple. one of the must visit temple for shiva devootes.
it was a unique experience, spent half day visiting the premises with the sight of yogis and religious people and ceremonies everywhere.

if you are in kathmandu, this place is a must for visiting. you need to be very open minded about the place and can be off putting to some people ( lots of rubbish, dirty rivers etc.) there is an expensive charge for tourists to help restore damage temples etc. and its worth it. this is a sacred place where the locals cremate their lost families, and the ashes are washed into the river. yes you can watch the ceremony.

many call kathmandu, the city of the pashupatinath and therefore, one of the holiest cities in the world. this amazing heritage is a hindu marvel. hundreds of temples of all major gods grace the main temple of lord shiva, the most revered and storied god in hinduism. the temple is a architectural marvel as well with its famous doors and pagoda-styled roofs. the temple area is huge with multiple hidden temples and places. one really needs to have enough time to explore the entire area. the river that flows beside the temple (bagmati) is cleaner than it was and steps have been taken to stop pollution. the only downside to this immense heritage is the fact that entry to the main temple premise (although there is still much much to see around despite this) is allowed only to the hindus. as all global religions market themselves and invite conversions, hinduism shies away from such exposure and therefore, entry is restricted. i really don't know how to comment on this but the temple is still visible mostly from outside its walls. the evening aarati (prayers with music and oil-lights) in the temple is a delight as well. something definitely not to miss.
it a place for pray to god. is a very nice place . always nice place for pray to ggod , wow too much monkey,,, too much temple,
it's an ancient temple of lord shiva mainly. it's a beautiful spiritual place. only hindus are allowed to enter the temple premises.
a wonderful place to visit as a hindu. i went at 6:15am and it was lovely and the best time to go as it was quiet and the temple was not too busy at all!

i always had a thing in visiting temple. and this temple there something special about pashupatinath, it's one of most important place for the hinduism. the place full of people with devotion sending their prayer, and sadu and family having burnt ceremony for the loss one. the place quite huge and unique and its a some iconic scenery with all of those activities around. people give offerings in the formed of fire, somehow its transformed some energy. unfortunately the temple itself only for the hinduism, but tourist could still enjoy the view by sitting in the bench and immerse with the surroundings, lots of pigeons and monkeys too.
a visit to pashupatinath temple is an unforgettable experience and will be one of your highlights during your stay in kathmandu.
would probably be nicer with water. we visited in january 2017 and earthquake repairs are still taking place.
lots of people in my tour found this very fascinating, but i was turned off by watching all of the cremations.

totally rip off! they charge you $10 pp for a place that really looks like . i visit this place 25 years ago and in those days it had still the mysical vibe but i am sure that not one dime of the 10 bucks you had to pay was invested in the compound! beter invest your money ($2.50 entrance) in the boudhanath temple complex! worth it!
biggest temple in the world. temple of lord shive. it is blongs to hindu. bank of the bagmati river.better to go in the morning and evening.
pashupatinath is one of holiest place to hindu . i suggest everyone to visit this place once in their life time .you will definitely find peace and spiritual growth .
located in the banks of river bagmati, it's one of the religious hub where a hindu should atleast visit in his lifetime.

pashupatinath definitely gives you a nice insight into hindu culture, but it is one of the least exciting things i got to do in kathmandu. you are not allowed into the main part as its so sacred only hindus (and buddhists as i found out) are allowed entry. it is interesting to see the surrounding, especially the ghats by the river where cremations regularly take place. i would try to go, but dont be sad if you miss it. if you do go, be sure to include the amazing boudhanath stupa into that day, as its very close (i walked). for that, you have to walk up the hill across from pashupatinath. there you can also see a beautiful maze of little temples in a small forrest.

enjoy the shiva ceremony just after dusk to enjoy this holy site. non hindus cannot enter shiva temple, but enjoy the ceremony from the steps above the bagmati river.the ceremony added to the mystical and religious ceremony of the funeral pyre rituals we observed. we worth the visit and experience.

just be careful, it is a typical tourist trap. when you go in, and if you don't look local they ask you to pay $10 per person at the enterance, and then a guide shows you away and expect more money. but, the guide was great, the visit was fascinating including watching cremation, if you are interested, it is just there in the open. you will not be able to go into the temple, as with other hindu temples.unless you are hindu.
the place gave me a feeling like some sort of extra dimension or sth. there is also place where people burn dead. there is vajans and enchanting, bagmati rivers, temples and the mesmerizing vibes.

pashupati temple is the place to go in your life and after life. you might recon " after life" people use to be taken there after there death . pashupati use to be visited even to get blessing from lord shiva for long life. its place to transform your life physically and spiritually pashupati nath bless you all

extremely expensive for no reason. the guard did not let me in to see the temple side because only hindu people are allowed. i am white, so apparently he did not believe that non-indian or non-nepalese could be hindu. he literally pushed me away and shouted at me until i left. unbelievable.

extremely expensive, not worthy a visit. they did not let you see the temple if you are not hindu and even if you are but you are not nepalese or indian, they scream at you pushing you to go away. this happened to me. why? white people can not be hindu?!? shame.

for a non-hindu its treasures are hidden from view unless you are interested in burning bodies. that's an overstatement perhaps as some of the architecture in small temples and idols are nice. it's a very holy place for hindus. wish i could have seen and learned more.

hindus send their loved ones here to be cremated. it is exceptionally surreal to be standing across the river with hundreds of other people taking photos of bodies being washed, pyres built and then everything set alight. very reflective, but a must see on your tour of kathmandu
this is where one is brought to be cremated. the whole process is quite interesting from being washed in the river to seeing how the pyres are assembled and the fire started.

this was not a very good experience. pay the 1,000 rupees entrance fee as a foreigner and then you can't actually even get inside the courtyard of the temple, much less the temple. terrible waste of time.
as a hindu must visit place, its call jyotirling and we got the darshan of all 12 jyotirlings at one place, evening aarti behind the temple of baghmati river is also spiritual feels.
very interesting place to visit. you can learn a lot about the culture of nepal and the cremation. the complex is big and worth to go on top of the hill.

this place is filled with history . i highly recommend that you go early in the morning . like 6.00 am the streets are less busy and the views are unforgettable specially if you want to take pictures.

pashupatinath temple is the major hindu religious place to visit in nepal. you can find very clean an d peace environment here. most of the visitor who come to nepal they use to visit here. i found it as very much incredible place among other tourism destination of nepal.

the pashupatinath temple is located on both banks of bagmati river on the eastern outskirts of kathmandu. its one of the most pillars of scared spaces for hindu worshippers dedicated to lord shiva. the architecture of the temple is worth a visit ad if you visited the ganges river in india this temple site is similar. we visited in december when the indian currency was changed making it difficult for indians to access their money. while the temple still attracted thousand of worshippers nepalese told us there were far less pilgrims then other years only believers of shiva are allowed into the temple. there also is a entrance fee which is much steeper for tourists. the dead are burnt and the ashes committed to the river. the sacred space is a pace where many people around the world (older, the sick) etc come to die. if sacred spaces is for you don't miss pashupati this sacred hindi temple is also mystical in a spiritual way.

good to see this place. a bit odd though so be sure not to bring children. there are aggressive monkeys. we saw a monkey bite a man in the arm as a warning. lots of dead bodies being burnt everywhere along the river. it's all part of the hindu religion so it's expected but just be aware in case of shock. hindus can only go into the temple not tourists. the overall area is ok not too impressive and lots of people wandering the area with mental illness. or guide was with us and was good.

with family on the nepal tour we visited the world famous pashupatinath temple. there are some timings of operation as when we reached it was around 3:30pm and waited for 30 mins for darshan as it opened at 4pm.only hindus are allowed here and no photography allowed. you can buy offering from outside and carry them along. the good part is you won't find any pandas around as it is common in india.
wonderful temple .a must for everyone to visit during your visit to nepal. without pashupatinath visit your travel to nepal will be short. such a peace and holy place.

we did not enter, as the entrance fee for foreigners is soo high! the other stupas are for free or a little fee (250r), i would recommend going elswhere. i can not give informations about the interior.

i'd give it an excellent note if not the price of the ticket which is 1000 nrs (10 usd) for a temple that you can't visit inside (hindus only). it's a beautiful complex, that's sure, but for the money you pay you only get access to a terrace which offers stunning views of the complex and that's all. little bit expensive for a viewpoint. especially if you compare it with bodnath stupa which costs only 250 nrs. so the place itself is wonderful, but the charge is far too high.
hard to stay in this place because it is a place where the body of the people are burning. if you feel a little bit sensible you must visit around the bagmati river the temples.

it's definetely interesting to see the rituals from such a different culture in front of your eyes. but having said that, there is nothing else to see - as the main temple is closed to non hindus. the 10 usd entrance fee is way therefore way overpriced. so, unless you have a particular interest this one could probably skipped.

crowded, but a good place to visit while you are at kathmandu the temple closes at around 2 and then reopens for an hour at around 5 so plan accordingly. the first time we visited, the main temple was closed and we had to visit again to see the main temple.

amazing temple and holy energy. some people dressed like sadhus are only scheming to get tips and money. but there are plenty of holy women and men at the temple to share positive energy. visit this temple and be blessed.
must see once in your life time. one of the greatest iconic temple of the hindi religious. i've been there due to ancient design of art. can't believe what a marvellous architect in history.
pasupati nath is very beautiful temple .it is hindu temple .big temple .gdo shivas temple .there is small jungle .small river .
interesting temple and area. cremations happen in the open air on gats, not at all gruesome. the area is quite large and easy to spend an afternoon looking over the whole area.

it is a temple different from the typical ones you would have seen in india - both in terms of architecture and approach to worship. more open and spacious and less pressure to move on from the sanctum. in the early morning and late evening, this can be a very different experience, setting your mind at peace.
a loxation that makes you question the meaning of life and death at close quarters. go see for yourself.a must visit place.

we visited this location. the history and cultural significance is something to be noted. we felt uncomfortable and "guilty" to do there during the most difficult moment in some peoples lives - - the passing of a parent or loved one.but to them this is tradition, it is expected and they achieve calming due to the reincarnation aspect. visit here but respect. we were there on a sunday in march and they offered up 5 different animal sacrifices. definitely not for everyone body but again this is their history and culture.

crowded. dusty. filled with beggars and the people who make a business out of hindu death rites. as a hindu born atheist this place reminds me of everything that freaked me out at temples and made it an unpleasant experience. visit if you have the kind of macabre curiosity that feeds off watching the rites for the dead. otherwise give this place the miss in baulk

most hindus in nepal and surround countries, try to visit this temple at least once in their lifetime. the day we visited, was a good day on the lunar calendar for the ceremony for boys becoming men. there were several young men with shaved heads wearing yellow cloths. goats, sheep and monkeys wander among the people. non-believers cannot enter the temple area but there is are so many other things to see. the most interesting for us, was the funeral ceremony and cremation. the day we were there, there were two bodies prepared for the cremation. it really was lovely to watch as the sons and male family members lovingly washed the feet, head of their departed loved ones. they were covered with cloths of red and yellow design and sprinkled with flowers and coins as offerings. this is also the area where the shadhus are.they are pilgrims who have devoted their life to spiritual searching. quite an interesting group! if you take their photo, you will be expected to give a tip.200 rupees (which is about $2.00). this is one way that their sustain their chosen lifestyle.

this lord shiva temple is the seat of 13th jyotirlinga, complementary to jyotirlinga situated in kedarnath. the temple is situated in a large area where a large number of hindu pilgrims come to have 'darshan' of lord pashupati. whereas at almost all the temples in kathmandu, one has to pay entry fee, this temple is open and free for all.

unfortunately the day we visited this temple it was raining and just had a quick visit .no cameras allowed hence cant take pictures.the nandi is huge .what i didn't like is no proper signage to guide a tourist to the proper temple where the deity is .luckily we had a acquaintance who took us to the temple directly else its very tricky .quite a big temple .

i wont go into the details like entrance fees, where you can and cant go, historical significance etc, these have been well covered by others. what i must tell you is that you will get a very different view of death and the whole cremation process here which may challenge your outlook. people bring their newly deceased relatives here within hours of their death. they are carried to the cremation platforms and burnt in a public display. monkeys wander around, people sit and watch, holy men pose for photos, tea and coffee are sold, schoolchildren walk past the bodies and homeless kids dredge the river with magnets to look for small change and jewelry from the remains. it is not the sterile, impersonal goodbye that we are accustomed to but a busy, involved, raw process with a defined beginning and end. i was totally fascinated.
its so religious place a hindu great temple is so interesting jogy baba and cremation things ,its great culture of hindu people s.

visited this site for the cremation of those passed on and witnessed an actual ceremony. it was a public display of a very private matter, the death a loved one. keep an eye out for some of the buddhist who beg for money, this is not a reflection of the true level of buddhism for hindus. worth the visit to understand a little of the great culture of the country.
the place is a cremation center and a hindu temple. if you are not hindu, you are not allowed to visit inside the temple. but you can witness the cremation process of dead bodies.
oh unimaginable temple .big temple which is ancient period made by king .there is a small river bagmati.many small temples around in pashupati .nice

we came here with a local guide. we had been to varanasi and had that experience. this was similar. it is still a place to be respectful of the deceased and their family. we did not stay long, but did experience a small amount of knowledge about the nepal culture and their way concerning life/death processing. between india and nepal, i have had a lot of contemplation concerning this subject. it is most likely to have that effect, which is one of the great things about travel!

travel can make mind outstretched. the oldest and holiest hindu shrine in nepal "the pashupatinath temple" located on the bank of bagmati river is a world heritage site enlisted by unesco.the water flowing along the temple of bagmati river is considered as holy and eventually flows into ganga. only hindu and buddhist are allowed in a core of temple. although it was a heartbreaking fact, the beauty of temple premises made to overthink what the beauty of core might be. i witnessed a funeral ritual according to hindu tradition: the fire, smoke; sadness and cryness of relatives of the deceased made be realise the pain when u loose your beloved one. it was a learning of a cultural difference and a beauty of tradition.

celebrated maha shiva ratri at pashupati temple , was one of memorable time with shiva prasad. every year try to go and celebrate maha shiva ratri . world heritage side , tradition hindus culture and beautiful kailash kut , mahadev temple !

i love visiting pashupatinath as it feels very authentic. as a foreigner, there are portions that you are not allowed to enter but overall, there is so much to be seen here. i have visited serval times and there is always cremations occurring, which did bother some people, both because of the smoke and the visual. i find it fascinating. i do recommend a guide, not that you cannot find your way around, but without one the begging here is almost unbearable.

a religious temple for hindus. one can find a peace of soul, and a mind at the same time. a must visit place, in order to know the religious culture, & traditions. and, also to know about different numbers of deities in hinduism. from, pashupatinath, you can head towards guhyeshwari temple. after this visit, you can head towards bouddhanath temple too. a must visit while in kathmandu.

ugly, dirty and uninteresting if you don't count the live cremations. not much to see except beggars following you and touts trying to make a sale. to be avoiedi as there are far more interesting and cultural places to visit without desperate hindus following you until you give in and pay them something. repellent.
this is a must see in nepal. the negotiations and ceremony that goes into the cremations. loads of interesting locals wants a dollar for a photo.

it was absolutely fascinating. much further in, our guide pointed out the river, rather dried up due to lack of rain. further down.a cremation was going on. quite a surprise, but still this being a holy place--we saw all sorts of human endeavors--from the celebration of life, to the release into the next world. i don't think it's for everybody, especially the squemish--but hey, it's nepal. and at the least, it makes for incredible stories.
