great place to visit for its relgious/spiritual and cultural customs. get a guide to explain the various temples and customs for cremation, funeral and grieving process. a lot of saddhus (spiritual hindus) live or wander there and it is common to donate money to them but it should always be a voluntary thing although some appear to be trying to purposefully capitalise on the influx of tourists.
a holy site where the dead pass on to the next world. leave the camera behind, and go with the expectation of other worldliness.
if you want to look nepai culture you can be there, you can see different kind of people in different way and lord shiva with other gods .
interesting because this is one out of 5 locations hindoes have to visit in their live, you hindoe funeral, you see hollies, an old (wo)men home founded by mother theresa.make sure you have a guide!

first, it's a special place for the locals. it was a new experience to see their traditions and costums. and we were to varanasi in india. shame that the place is dirty! very dirty to be frank. also, all the disrespectful (stupid!) tourists who were there only to take pictures. what's so exiting for you when you take a selfi with a corpse burning?! at least in varanasi ppl respected the customs and didn't take photos of cremations. but it felt strange to be there. to you (future visitors), see the place . yes. but let your cameras in your bags.

pashupatinath temple is one of the most important temple to visit for the hindus. it is believed that the god of destruction lord shiva resides onto this temple. the temple area is situated near the holi bagmati river. the condition of the river is not so good but campaigns relating to clean the river is ongoing. temple is situated in the peaceful area around a forest. the whole pashupati nath area is listed in the unesco as world heritage site.

divine a must place to visit for hindus , don't buy anything from outside as they are not offered to the lord shiva , for all types of puja there is a nepal sbi bank in the premises of the temple it opens at 08:30 am and the puja is offered .
pashupati nath is temple of lord shiva.although it is not among 12 jyotirlings,but equally famous,important and popular among hindus.you feel very good after visiting the temple.

the atmosphere is completely different to the other temples i have visited. the area is large. entry around 10 usd. it's on the riverside and has a special atmosphere because of the funeral ceremonies. you can only enter the temple if you are hindu. it's a really impressive place that you have to see and feel.
we started our nepal trip after paying visit here. it is a ancient temple of lord shiva. in the holy place you see a huge statue made of bronze.

for any traveler i guess visiting places has to be meaningful. not only wanting to see how wonderful the place is but the history behind it gives us an idea about how civilized and amazing the place was in the past. pashupatinath temple and its surrounding has been and still a heaven for anybody. the architecture, the monuments, the buildings and moreover the religious value adds to why 1000s of people visit this place everyday. just to be in the vicinity gives you the tranquil feeling. sitting by the holy river bagmati and staring directly at the temple is just breathtaking. take a walk around the area and one shall immediately find themselves at peace with their inner self. there is so much to do once you get here. if you are in kathmandu please take your time to visit this place. look aside the chaos and the eerie cry of the families of the deceased, by the holy bagmati there is more to it. even the dead find themselves at peace when they come here so imagine what a living human being could seek within.

as a non hindu, i did not find this place appealing or interesting. even as a western hindu, you will not be allowed into the main temple. instead, for a $10 you can watch cremation from across the holy bagmati river, a river so polluted i do not understand how it can be considered holy and treated with such broad civil irreverence. i would not visit here again.

it is interesting to visit and see how other religions deal with death. you will hear a lot of crying and screaming but it is normal for burial procedures. this probably will make you sad. do not stand in the smoke as it comes from the burning of a dead body and it smells bad
pashupatinath is one of the great hindu temple in nepal. shiva is main god inside the temple. its really attraction place to visit.
major hindu shrine in kathmandu place of pilgrimage for hindus from inside and outside nepal place of cremation you can see the funeral pyres

this is where many of the people of kathmandu come to cremate their deceased, and if you stand on the river bank you can see the ceremonies and cremations. a very atmospheric place, well deserving of its world heritage status. must-see.

pashupatinath temple is a hindu temple. this temple is frequented by the locals and it is most popular. there is a crematorium within this site. they burn dead bodies along the river. it is very interesting to visit here as one can see how the people worship and offer their offerings. there is an entry fee to the temple. this temple is very busy .

went here a while ago. what to say - home of lord shiva. went as part of a tour group and was priviledged to perform a havan here. beautiful temple - one of the most revered temples in hinduism! love it.!

you may find it unbelievable but this temple is one of lord shiva's largest in nepal and hindus are cremated almost every day. we watched two cremations along the river. i do have photos of the corpses but out of respect i will not post them, there is no smell of burning flesh as the body is covered with spices and herbs and this and the charcoal smell are more evident. walk around for more than an hour, climb and see views from terraces and give a few rupees to the quiet beggars along the paths. only hindus can go into certain temples. there are numerous opportunities for photos. sadhus are willing to allow a photo and none asked for rupees; they simply smiled. we found this the most interesting place in kathmandu.
you should see it! where they burn their dead bodies. very imirtant experiance. and beatiful buildings!
it's one of the best shiva temples i have ever visited, very systematic, we paid through shrine bank that gave us satisfaction

a great hindu religious site. no 'pandas/priests' to pester you. only a few steps to climb unlike many hindu temples. a short walk from the car park. puja offerings quite straightforward via a short queue. temple closes at 7 pm.

pasupathinath temple first appears to be normal when u enter as just an another hindu temple. but the ambience, pasupathi, bunch of temples, open cremation and the scent in air, baghmathi river, kala bhairava all makes u to travel somewhere inner. u understand or reveal or identify something from that. it is not by bhakthi. so it is still claimed as one of the holy, ancient and main temples of hindu religion. feel it.

we visited this temple on 11th march 2017. its an awesome temple with unique shivling hosting a huge idol of nandi in the entrance. bhagmati river flows behind and makes the temple even more beautiful. photography is prohibited inside the temple but can take photos before entrance. shopping around the temple is for reasonable price. shop owners are eager to accept indian currency and no need to convert inr to npr in nepal. who ever visits kathmandu should and must visit this temple. it took 20 min for us to have lord shiva's darshan. it takes around 2 to 4 hrs for over all visit depending on the rush at the temple. as the photography is prohibited inside the temple i am uploading the photos taken outside in front of the entrance

a must see world heritage site that features the place of cremations. you cannot enter the temple unless hindu or buddhist. but the grounds are full of additional building sites, a small museum and an opportunity to think, reflect and stroll.

we opted for a guide when visiting pashupatinath and it was a good decision. we came around 6 pm and on one side of the river it was a ceremony celebrating life and on the other side families where cremating their loved ones. the guide gave us good information as to how the thinking behind the ceremonies were and what everything meant. a very sacred moment and i felt a bit more in love with life afterwards.

the temple is worth a trip to kathmandu alone! monday is best avoided if idea is to get peaceful darshan. the rudrabhishek is not like indian temples ,it is about a 10.minute exercise and the receipt is for 1100 nepali rupees but the sanctum is truly awe inspiring, one can see the deity but not touch it. mobile phone can be carried inside though clicking pics is prohibited inside the complex

very interesting place, this is the varanasi equal in nepal - everybody from sadhus to tourist peacefully together. just remember to be respectful, since there are crematations and funerals going on all the time. not recommended for young children.
pashupati nath is a hindu temple of nepal , it is located in capital city of nepal , again i will visit this temple

the temple has beer built in the eastern architecture has the shiva-ling that has doors on all 4 directions for darshan (viewing). the doors on the east and west are supposed to be more auspicious and are always crowded. the temple premises has numerous other smaller temples. on the back side of the temple is a river, which had transformed into an extremely polluted still water pond, besides which corpses are burnt. not a great sight. non hindus are not permitted inside the temple premises.

buying a ticket for 1000 npr means that you can enter only the main part - bridge across the river and stand on the opposite site from burning places. but not being a hindu, you´re not allowed to go inside the main temple. and be aware of all the "holy men", they´re asking a lot of money for taking photos with them, could be aggresive.
if you are hindu one must visit this holy temple, where all your sins are washed away. shivaratri is the best day to come, but expect a long but well organized line.

its a historical site, however, it is dirty as hell. the ghat next to it is worse. probably ranks very high for a believer in religion but for an atheist like me, who just go to see a place for the attraction of it, it does not makes much sense. however, it is listed as one of the must visit. also the place is crowded. but morning views apparently are good.

as soon as you enter the temple premises, you are out of this materialistic world and get connected to the divine power, that is running the universe. the atmosphere, the essence, the people take you back to ages, this temple has preserved its existence, as it would have been in the past . truly a spirit satisfying and unparallel experience.

this is a historically significant place in the local culture. visitors should be aware that there are many open air cremations going on down by the river everyday. this may not be something many westerners will find appealing. it is, however, quite interesting from a cultural standpoint. entry fee to this area was 1000 rupees, about $10 usd.

this temple dedicated to shiva is animated and crowdy. shiva's followers are really intense. worth a visit. you can not entrer this temple if you are not hindou but it is possible to take photos. worth a visit if you visit kathmandu.

visited more than 3 times ,very holistic and spiritual place .architect is more ancient ,temple behind bagamati river which nullify and remove sins of soul . i need an opinion on emergence of temple ,if anyone aware plz share on this.
stunning. i highly recommend it, and if you can have a guide, get it! it is the most amazing place i have seen in kathmandu for now.

fire in his head, the sun and the moon his eyes, space his ears, the vedas his speech,the wind his breath, the cosmic universe his heart, from his feet the whole earth originated. verily he is the inner self of all living and non living . hail shiva" creator and destroyer " amazing place to explore the complete hindu culture,deities and legends related to hindu religion. hindu crematory, ancient temple.

as others have said, best to bring a mask if going near the cremation area. be careful not to photograph any of the holy men who can be quite aggressive and are usually stoned. some damage here but plenty still remaining.
this temple is good, the structure and building is very old but the management is not good. tourist must aware of beggars.
it's such a holy temple for nepali. i love the part of many pigeon that gathered together and some people feed them and then they're flying here and there. got some beautiful picture here.

best temple in kathmandu. a unesco world heritage site. they allow that you watch the cremation ceremony after the death of beloved ones of hindu community. it is in fact very sad to see that the people make their last duties to their beloved ones. i felt somehow guilty to watch them in atead of sharing the pain. but somehow it is a very different experience. all around the temple there are beautiful pagodas and on every corner there arr baba hindus which are considered as sacred. a must see place once arrived in kathmandu.
it is very best place to visit, it is one of the famous hindus temple in the world.it has many different temple around.

can a cremation site be a highlight? we somehow thought it was. it is a strange thought that you take pictures of a cremation . until you notice that family members of the deceased are doing it as well. the entire atmosphere is sobering, rich and religious, yes we found it truly unique! an absolute must for every kathmandu visitor!
lots of interesting temple buildings. the main reason to come here is to see the cremation area on the river bank. fascinating, especially if you have a guide who can explain everything.

after walking a short distance from the gate you will walk along the bagmati river and then you will see the smoke coming from the cremation ghats. you get an up close view of the cremation process and i would suggest bringing a mask as the smoke changes direction frequently. this temple to shiva draws plenty of visitors and is a must see.

it was one of a good visit here. we were approached at the gate by a guide named richard. i must tell you how awesome he was. he spoke very good english very clearly and of course his history knowledge is really great. learned a lot from him. we were worried how much would the guide cost at the end of our tour as he seemed very professional. but he only charged npr1000 each and it was pretty worth it. but be a little careful while taking photos with the sadhus. they ripped us off npr1500 just for few photos. i'd rather pay that to the guide! my husband gave npr500 and they said more so i took npr1000 when one asked for another 500. he then said he had change. when i handed the money over he said that's it. but well, its a once in a lifetime experience and i guess as tourists, sometimes we dont have much options in a foreign land not knowing if we had rejected, what would happen. but overall, was good to learn. beware of the dust and smoke from cremation thou.
i loved this place. one of the most sacred hindu temples of nepal, pashupatinath temple is a huge place with monkeys all around. worth a visit!

one of the most sacred hindu temples of nepal, pashupatinath temple is a huge place, with a lot of religious constructions and temples (some reserved to hindus only). along your walk, you will probably see traditional cremations.
this is not something you see every day for sure but i am privileged to have seen. the day i visited had many cremations taking place in public view a place of peace.
a must see place in kathmandu. you will learn everything from here including life, death, spirituality and strangely i was fascinated with the public cremation here.
interesting place, but private. worth a visit, but don't hang around or take photographs. unless of course its of the holy men.
very ancient and very old and worth visiting, a good feel overall to be in the place and very peaceful worship, good to go in the evenings.
a very famous temple and very ancient and must be visited by everyone, a good feeling when there is aarti in the evening and be careful, there are tons of monkeys here.

the day i visited was a holiday for hindu people & so the site was very busy. there were a number of cremations during my visit & in some ways i did feel as if i was intruding at an important time for a bereaved family. there were many priests & disabled people sat waiting for rice to be given to them. it was a very colourful sight, this may have been because it was a holy day.
this temple looks really ancient and kept really dirt. the entrance fee is high that i think because tourist can not go inside the real temple.
pashupatinath temple located on kathmandu city is the best destination for any tourists visiting nepal because of its best religious tradition and awesome speciality
had great experience of kathmandu valley sightseeing including the pashupatinath temple. such a great place. rich art and architecture.
pashupati nath temple we belive and very much trust .we are here every year for shivaratri we love to come every yeat for pray pashupati wiil full fill our wishes.thanks god bless ua always.
a trip to kathmandu, and for that matter, nepal, is very much incomplete without a visit to the hallowed pashupatinath temple. experience the divine.
if you are in kathmandu, i suggest to you go to pashupatinath temple where you can see religious things, historical things. i visited pashupatinath when i am in nepal every time .

there are crowds, there is filth, and there is death.but there is also life, vibrant in divinity, ritual, and faith. being at pashupati is an intense experience that truly revives your spirit and physical senses. the actual temple complex may not be as large and wide-spread as many of the temples in india and east asia, but it fills your mind. you emerge anew. the government and the administrative board really needs to pull up its socks when it comes to the general upkeep and maintenance of the temple grounds. this important centre of hindu divinity deserves a lot better.

located on the east side of kathmandu, pashupati̇̄nāth is a 50 minute walk from the centre of a 15 minute taxi. we were stopped at the entrance and as foreigners had to pay 1,000rupee (about £8) for a ticket each. it wasn't clear where we were allowed to go but a man from the ticket office appeared next to us and engaged in conversation asking where we were from etc. he explained the cultures surrounding the cremation and then beckoned us to the other side of the bridge. he spent about 90 minutes taking us round and it was unclear if he was a guide, included with the ticket or just a friendly local. he was very patient and there was no rush and we bought him a drink at the top of the temple. i tipped him (i thought generously!) at the end but he made it clear he wasn't happy with the amount given which was quite embarrassing; he latched onto us we didn't ask him to! aside from this the temple was absolutely fascinating and it's little wonder this is a unesco site.

prepare yourself and possibly eat before you arrive here. it is a must see. it is one step closer to understanding the hindu rituals and coming face to face to there ceremony of death through cremation. i would recommend this one over the one in varanasi! it is absolutely intriguing. make sure you are respectful.
i was amazed my their rich and welcoming culture. just around this temple is whole another world. you would have plenty to learn about culture based on hinduism.

visiting here i had the chance to observe 3 traditional hindu cremations, which were both emotional, and fascinating. the site is full of interesting people and buildings. a must visit site when in kathmandu
ew are camming fram malaysiya we did really good shivs poja in pashupati nsth not so croudy nice mamage and good time in temple

ew ar reslly happy to see shiva ratry and nice time with to menny munks and gat blessing fram god shiva they did well managr to menny people more then one million people we must came back next year to

had been to pashupatinath in 2014 and 2016 ! it's awesome ! and great architecture ! here funerals are carried out everyday . you can just sit and watch funerals in a respectful way . get a guide if you want information about culture , religion etc :)

i love the hustle and bustle that surrounds a temple. i love to see the devotion of the people and to experience a new culture and a new way of doing things. i purchased an offering before entering the grounds to the temple. we didn't go in to the temple itself. we stayed on the grounds and took pictures and then our guide helped us with a puja. we then walked to the back side of the temple where the crematorium is located. it was fascinating to watch the care and gentleness of those preparing bodies for cremation. i loved being able to be a spectator and to have a small glimpse in to how a loved one is honored and prepared for a loving end from their worldly existence.

pashupatinath temple and burning ghats are beautiful, spiritual, and extremely moving, all at the same time. it is important, in my opinion, to remember that there are families of people who are cremating their dearest relatives. the amount of grief, some days, is palpable. as you walk around, you will observe the saddhus who sit and witness the cremations and are always willing to be photographed. one saddhu with whom i spoke, told me he had not cut his hair in 45 years! there is a row of lingams/yonis that is truly something to behold. it is a place of fertility on the banks of the bagmati river, which is sacred to hindus and buddhists. pashupatinath is a rare, unique, and beautiful experience.
don't miss the place to visit, culturally beautiful and feel the real cremation ceremony everyday from the river banks but they should clean the river. worth to visit the place.
we have a very good times in this area because of the siva tatri to menny hindu munks call sadu nice decure clean and organized good and historical palace

visiting pashupatinath is like nothing i have ever experienced in my life. it is a holy place, where hindu cremations are occurring daily. we went on a monday evening, and many families were there to take place in the funeral rites of their loved ones. it was incredible to observe these rituals in a respectful way.
we would like to thank alpine event management malaysia for organising a perfect prayers for us here.
old temple and the famous one in nepa. during earthquake in nepal, temple was safe due to lord shiva power. not a single crack insert inside the temple.

really beautiful architecture.so much to see with the funeral gnats.if you can get a guide then do so.so worth getting the information about the culture,religion etc from a local instead of just walking around clueless

i hired a guide to wander the complex and it was useful to have this local knowledge available plus explain the hindu faith in greater detail. non-hindus can not enter the main temple however, that is the only restriction. seeing the bagmati river site where the cremations take place is interesting. as a westerner i'm used to the more closed approach to death and seeing a more open, public ceremony is interesting. i didn't find the experience macabre or feel a voyeur. who is to say one approach is better than another? we come from the earth and we return there. in this case as ash. i would recommend anyone to visit.
i went pashupatinath temple when i was in nepal. i found it is the holiest place for hindu people. i suggest if you want to learn hindu culture then visit pashupatinath temple.
loved it. once u visit and take darshan of lord u will be filled with spirituality and love. the people were very lovely and honest.

一定要请景点的专业导游讲解，许多印度教的文化体现在烧尸、告别仪式、建筑、河流以及生者在对岸祈福的过程中，印度教徒对待生死的态度绝对是一种文化震惊，当然还有成群衣着褴褛、吸食大麻、试图脱离尘世生活的印度苦行僧。 gonna to have a professional guide to explain all the scenes you've seen there. the interpretation will raise so much of your cultural and religion background knowlege to get the most cultural shock of this incredible place!

this hindu temple is one of the most fascinating experiences in kathmandu. there is one area that is restricted to non-hindus, but don't let that keep you away. there is still much to see. if you happen to be there while there is a funeral/cremation ceremony happening, you may want to bring a face mask as the smoke can get quite heavy. a must see!

we are fortunate to visit lord pashupatinath temple and mesmerised by the aura of the temple, supernatural, phenomenally godliness experience. i wish almighty give me strength to fight the adversity if this world and mark some good signature on this world.

its an incredible place with lots of emotionally intense moments when families are saying goodbye to their loved ones.its the second time i went and i like it most of all the temples to visit.ignore the people who want to sell you stuff .they can be annoying but its how they live.and the sadhus there also try to collect some money off you .dont let this disturb your experience,,,,

visited this temple in october 2016. met an excellent guide who was helpful and did not try to up the cost of his services. be respectful of grieving families as this site is the major funeral site for hindus in kathmandu. well worth a visit talk to the guides and establish a price before committing.

a world heritage site apparently. fascinating place to walk around. a guide is probably necessary to help you understand what you'll be looking at and to make sure you don't miss anything. i would say it's a must do.

this sight is located by the bank of bagmati river incluides many temples and monuments. when we entered this area, we had to pay 1000 rupees each. we hired a guide for the same amount (1000rp - 45 min-1h) he talked about the temples, hinduism, buddhism, folk customs, architecture and the legend of the main temple, totally worth it! we watched a preparation for cremation from ghat which is a viewpoint for tourist from adjacent side of the river.

a trip to nepal wouldn't be complete without visiting pashupatinath temple. it is the most important temple in nepal, hindus all around the world visit it all year round. that's why there's always so many people, but it's worth the visit. one can learn so much of nepali culture through this place. warning; it is not for the faint of heart!
i always take my family to pilgrims to this place.though it's crowded but still like to go there and pray.

best experience of my life, felling so much blessed after visiting their, only indian and nepal people's are allowed to enter in main mandir. no leather things allowed inside. high security of police and military their. many shops were located near about the temple either of food or religious things to buy.

if you are interested to watch how the local people burn the bodies of their relatives that passed away. please don't take your children, it also includes a hindu temple and you have to pay 1500 rupee or 15 $ as entrance fee. the place is not clean but it is a very important part of the local life.

among the largest temples in nepal, this temple frames an important religious value to the hindus. the nandi idol is huge! the infrastructure is again very poor by the authorities. watchout for the monkeys!
this is another great way to spend the day. great bathing pool and historical buildings. fun to explore the city with the restaurants and shops. a half day or less is all that is needed.

the pashupatinath temple is extremly fascinating for a good look into hindu culture in kathmandu, and if you have the time, it's worth the visit. but the last few years the price for entering have just gone up, and it is getting rather expensive (1000 rupees)

this is the temple of lord shiva, thousands of travelers visit every day here. if you are hindu you can go inside the temple main area also. you wan watch various traditions and culture of hindus around here.

(1) what a wild concept: charge foreigners an admission fee (1000 rupees), but deny them access to the temple. (3) aggressive macaques monkeying around. keep yr things secured.

if you go to kathmandu and do not visit pashupatinath temple, your visit would be incomplete. a historical monument, much revered by hindus from across the globe, is on the banks of a small river. the main temple is in tact but scores of small small temple on the other side of the stream are practically in ruins. however these are a "must see". many miss this aspect as just behind the temple is a cremation site where one would invariably find a cremation taking place (one avoids going on the other side). overall a good tourist spot plus a religous monument.

do not get a guide at all cost. at. all. cost. guides are out for money and will pretend to be your friend and ask for any amount you want. guide will bring you to "holy men" who will have a picture to be taken and request for 20usd. guide boasted to us that "holy men" will even charge up to 60usd for white tourists. guide then brought us to another set of "holy men" who will "bless you" for another 20usd. honestly do not know what their religion it is to. it is honestly a beautiful temple filled with tradition and heritage so do not let a guide ruin this experience like it did for us.

one of the highlights for hindus and the real hindus can even enter the centre of this place, which westerners can not join. to pay 1000nrs per visit is ok, but no too nice. you can walk around and see old temples and you can watch to the cremation of the dead bodies here

one the most holy places for hindus and only real hindus can enter the centre of this place. huge area full of old temples and the cremation of the dead bodies you can watch here very good. wonderful place !

for me, the pashupatinath temple was a most impressive sight was. this is also known as the crematorium. it may sound morbid or gross, but in the presence of people caring for, and venerating their dead, i felt an empathy for the mourners, and a glimpse of something sacred in their lives.

i hated the tourist experience. as much as i respect the religious aspect, 1000npr for that experience (or should i say inexperience?) was steeper than the himalayas. i saw little. non-hindus are not permitted in most parts. in fact even western-hindus are not permitted in many parts. without clear signage, expect to walk beyond the boundaries and get shouted at. it is embarrassing and shameful. if you open your doors to guests, be clear. i was massively curious and ultimately disappointed. i'm not religious and this was the equivalent of opening a christmas present, only to lose the ability of eyes, hands and ears to know what the gift was. also, for the whole of the paid experience, i was tailed by several beggars. i did not enjoy it.
this is a temple about 6 kms away from the city. this temple has several layers and uniqueness is its golden roof and silver doors.

whereas temple itself is open only to hindus, the cremation ghats nearby are easily viewed from across the disgustingly polluted bagmati river ( a stream in january). cremation ceremonies can be viewed discreetly.

visited the pashupatinath temple as part of a kathmandu day tour with himilayan spirit. this is a beautiful and very holy sight (from my understanding- the most holy hindu sight in all of nepal). our guide was great at explainingall the cultural traditions to us, otherwise i think it would have all been a bit confusing. during our visit there was a hindu funeral taking place on the river bank- very fascinating to see. i would recommend that you combine this attraction with the other main attractions of kathmandu in a day tour (monkey temple, patan durbar square and pashupatinath.). enough to keep you busy for the full day and experience nepalese culture at it's best. we chose himilayan spirit adventures and could not recommend them highly enough- by far the best value for money (~65usd per person, lonely planet advertises one for the exact same things which is $95usd)) and included all entrance fees (which themselves equate to about ~30usd), a very informative and friendly tour guide and a driver with a nice, modern car. could not recommend them more highly.

did not enjoy my visit to this temple. very expensive entry fee of 1000nes. for this they don't actually let you go inside just the grounds there are lots of bits that tourists aren't allowed into but none of them are signed so when you walk in you get shouted at aggressively by nasty security people. there are lots of beggars and people harrasing you inside so you can't even really sit and enjoy the atmosphere.
for those who wish to understand nepalese culture, this is a must. the open-air cremations are unusual for westerners to experience, but it's an integral part of nepali hindu culture.
pashupatinath temple is a place of pilgrimage people of hindu only. we had a bad experience about this temple was that no one other than nepali & indian can't enter the main temple.

maybe we just aren't extremely cultured, but after a day of looking at amazing temples, this site left us underwhelmed. it cost $10 a person to get in, and once you're inside unless you're hindu you can't even see the main temple, which i assume is gorgeous but we never got to see. the area is surrounded by a crematorium, which leaves you feeling like you're intruding on something. it was a pretty view from the top, but after seeing other sites i just didn't understand the hype on this one.
watching the dead delivered to the sacred river, prepared for cremation, was for me a moving reminder of how we all end. i am grateful for the time spent there.
one of the oldest hindu temple in kathmandu, i visited alone and had a good time spent over there, saw some of the funerals taking place near the bagmati river.

our guide to this place helped a lot to explain what the structures meant and how visitors ought to conduct themselves. certainly the religious symbols made better sense after the trip, as aided by the leisurely walk in areas where there were not many tourists.

i'm not quite sure on what to say when asked about this place, and i feel bad for rating it a 5 star due to the circumstances but it is really something i'd advice you go and see for yourself. the experience was moving and upsetting but extremely interesting and insightful.

it is a prayer area where one can pause, reflect say a silent prayer or two and seek blessings. very popular to locals and visitors. pertinent that you observe the sensitiveness regarding attire, slippers and mode of dressing. avoid the shoppers and others trying to make a quick buck from you. stay focused always.

this shiva temple is located by the side of bagmathi river. entry to the main temple is restricted to hindus. this temple is perhaps one place not affected by earth quake.it is a massive complex and one cne can see lot many shive lingas in one place.by the side of the temple there is a creamation grounds where dead bodies are burned and ashes dumped into the river which made us feel little disturbed.this is a heritage site but needs more maintenace. this is a must visit temple when in nepal.photography inside temple is not allowed

visited two weeks before the famous pashupatinath temple at kathmandu. still we can say devastated structures outside the main temple entrance but i believe no nature fury can cause the damage to the main structure. it is magnificently standing and as soon as we entered evening aarti was going on and first had glimpse of shiva at the main entrance. positive vibes and feeling of fulfilment upon seeing the lingam. waited for some more time so that all the other doors gets opened and in line went and completed seeing the lingam from another 3 sides!
one of the best and larger shiva temple in around the world and to menny historical things are there any manja around and manley to
great temple with really huge following. recommended to visit during evening so one can see the evening grand prayers done traditional way!
a big hindu temple near airport. this is my first time to see cremation in the open area near a stream.
it's also known as head of 12 jyotirlings in india.its a holy temple of lord shiva.you can also do special pooja in 1100 npr which is worth.very nice and calm feelings from the area.

this historic temple site is protected for a reason--it is simply awe inspiring. while the many temples should be enough to keep you interested, i was most fascinated by the cremation ceremonies happening along the river.
holy & i suppose the most popular place - but very shabbily maintained - specially when it is said to be the world heritage (as we found it written)

if you are a hindu then your dream will not fulfill unless you visit 4dham one is pasupatinath so indian people dream is to go and worship pasupatinath as india primininster and president was here to worship tooo.

this is one of the most sacred shrines in the world and sits on the edge of the bagmati river where they carry out cremations along the rivers edge. it was a very moving experience and makes you more culturally aware.

i always have a dream of visiting pashupati nath temple. and its like blessing from shiva himself to fulfill my desire. the whole place and surroundings are really charming. only thing is the shopkeeper who sell puja materials are charging a little more (i believe not sure). else everything else just so good.

of course, this temple is beautiful and a must-see in kathmandu. however, the brahmin priests will start doing poojas, or prayer services, for you without asking and then ask for astronomical amounts of money. if you try to give them a few hundred rupees for a five minute prayer, they will give you guilt trips and demand more. i felt less spiritual and more like i was being exploited.

definitely an interesting facet of the hindu culture and a must see if you can handle the cremation - as it is literally happening right in front of you and can be confronting. i chose not to take any photos out of respect, but others, mainly tourists, were taking photos - so i suppose it is up to your own personal ethics/morals as to whether or not you choose to take photos of the ceremony.

this was an amazing experience and one i would recommend to anyone visiting kathmandu. non hindus can't access some of the areas but we could see pretty much everything from the other side of the river. it is a very spiritual place but also seemed to be a place of celebration. have your photo (for a small cost) taken with holy men who (we were told) enjoy smoking a bit of hash - they did seem mellow. you have the privilege of viewing cremations taking place. it is an experience i will never forget.

if you are a bit squeamish then don't visit. there is a big hindu temple there and outside on the river bank are some large stone platforms where cremations are conducted, after the cremations the ashes are pushed into the river. we went in january which is nepal's winter but there is no or little rain (the rains come mostly in the monsoon season) and so the river is not flowing so it is a dirty charcoal colour. there are many other buildings and small temples to look at. you could easily spend three hours there.we teamed up with a local guide at the entrance, or should i say he teamed up with us, but it was really worth the tip we gave him as he went into great detail about each building plus some history. i do wonder though about rituals still carried out today which to me should have been dispensed with centuries ago, for example there was a young goat tethered outside a small temple and when we inquired as to its purpose there we were informed it was to be slaughtered as an offering to the gods.

this is one of the most renowned hindu temples in south east asia. it lies on the bank of bagmati river. visitors witness the journey of life and death at this place. there's no words to describe the experience of visiting pashupatinath temple. highly spiritual place for people who believe in spirituality. inside the temple people aren't allowed to take pictures for religious reasons. for the same reasons visitors must take off shoes and deposit it at shoes safety vault free of charge before entering the main temple premise. in the past the main temple was accessible for hindus only but not sure if the same rule applies or not at the moment. this is one of my most favourite places to visit when i am in kathmandu.

very nice temple, and huge permises, lots of mandir inside the main temple compound, if you are not hindu then don't go there, only hindus can enter the temple. i suggest you to take a guide or pandit/pujari. they will explain you everything.inside u can't click the pictures. temple has timings so check before visit.

i really like the pasupatinath. it is god mahadev temple.hindu called him shiva also.this is old and ancient temple.area of pashupati is very big .hindu people died body is firing there.there is a river .near by pashupati is a big jungle.i liked .

we realy felt immersed in the indian religious culture while visiting one of their most sacred temple in nepal. although the temple itself is off limits for non-indian people, you can see the temple and rituals from outside the restricted area perimeter. we could also observe a cremation ceremony. quite a cultural experience

we always loved to visit pashupatinath temple simply because of the positivity it bring in you. the day we landed in kathmandu we visited the temple in the evening but unfortunately it was half past six and doors were closed. we got the coupon for rudrabhishek pooja from the bank in the premises for the next day. this was a pleasant surprise as during my earlier visits we had to give it to the priest, performing the pooja, which used to create some sort of chaos. this time it was very well managed. although there was some construction going on for the approach roads which created some unpleasant sites.but it was very satisfying visit.
there's something about this place which makes you feel calm and composed after each visit. you could feel the pure aura around this place. beautiful place for soul.
when you feel you need inner strength,please visit pashupathy nath temple-its a great place to visit and you can feel the power of god by yourself. great place to visit.lovely place.

one of the best siva temple in the world is the pasupati the best thing in side is good management and nice clean
unfortunately restricted to hindus only. this treasure needs to opened up to the outside world really. could be better maintained and kept cleaner. access needs to be wider and beautified.

unfortunately, non-hindus are not able to enter the temple, which is apparently the 2nd most sacred site in hinduism. the crematorium, though, is fascinating. i wasn't sure whether i really wanted to witness the cremation process but it was so interesting and moving. we stood on the opposite side of the river so as to not feel as though we were intruding on the grief of the mourners.
you can see the majesty of lord shiwa.! beautiful hindu temple. the main temple is open only at certain times. but lots of mini temples around. beautiful architecture.! must see in kathmandu

first place where i saw only hindus can enter.some where i felt happy as they are keeping the hindu culture alive , but soeme where also felt that, how other culture will get to know about hinduism. nice place must go, burning ghat is just near you can easily see the bodies.

only hindus can enter the actual temple, but you can walk around the area and visit the burning ghat. takes a while to process the fact that you are seeing bodies being cremated in front of you. make sure you show respect, don't take photos and don't stick around for too long. i personally didn't find this temple particularly interesting, but visited it anyway because of it's historical significance and world heritage status.

the entrance fee is a little high and it is simply not worth it. you go up a lot of stairs and finally end up with a lot of monuments that were affected by the earthquake. in addition that is where incremation takes place. not an enjoyable experience.
open cremation of dead bodies was culture shock for me and sadhus (holy man) smoking marijuana was so interesting.
don't pay the 1000 rupee fee near the main temple. you can see the vast majority of the complex without paying that fee.

very interesting place to people watch. a little raw experience when watching the cremation of the dead and cleansing of the bodies, etc. unfortunately as i am non-hindu, i couldn't go inside the template, and so would have love to.

a must see ,go early as it gets busy especially in the walking season take your camera plenty of photo opportunities

though the temple is crowded with people pushing for darshan, the site of god in his form is heart warming. places of destruction still remaining following the earthquake. no proper road with chaos at the entrance. the experience of the divine is never less.
its unbelievable site i don't have words to describe.very good management security and everything but be care full of monkeys.

a compelling and somewhat sobering place. pashupatinath is not as touristy as other sights, but there were visitors. the burning ghats are sombre and the place has a strong spiritual sense. the architecture is old and striking to view. there are a hatful of touts and be wary the sahdus will ask money for photgraphs. a must visit if you have an interest in the hindu rituals in nepal.

pashupatinath temple, this is the one of the biggest hindu god shiva's temple, in the history, hindus have more than thirty-three million gods and godin. but there are main three gods brahma, bishnu, and maheshowra. brahma is the creator bishnu is the protector and the maheshowra is the destroyer. therefore in pashupatinath temple visiting many hindus pilgrims people either from india. it is listed in unesco heritage site best place for the historical and cultural place to visit.
like watching life nat geo documentary. you can see the funeral rites from beginning the prayers right up to the funeral pyre.

find sumit kharel to guideyou. friendly lical guide with good englush. he knows a lot and explains all traditions and cereminies. entrance ticket is 1000rupies and lasts one day. it is worth a visit at night when they perform light ceremonies.

find sumit kharel to guide you. he can explain rituals and traditions very well. entrance ticket is 1000rupies and it lasts for a whole day. we have been told that at night there are nice light ceremonies to see but we have not had the chance yet.
it was memorable visit at pashupati nath mandir, arti was good, each hindu must visit here once will be kathmqndu

this was one of the most interesting places to visit. i recommended you do this tour with a guide as the hindu culture needs to be explained to understand the significance of the cremation ceremony. there are quite a few stairs to walk up to the top of the area but well worth it. at the top there is a deer park but no entry to this but it is significant in hindu scripture. the temple can not be entered but there is a lots of stories attached to each small temples.

the temple of pashupatinath is dedicated to lord shiva, the guardian deity of nepal. pashupati means the lord of all animate beings. this sacred place lies on the bank of river bagmati and this area is called deha patan. with the main temple in the pagoda styled roof, it houses the jyotir linga / illuminating phallus which is surrounded by many other temples dedicated to different gods, goddesses and deities. there are two important moments in the temple: morning and evening aarati (offering the sacred candles to the deity and blessings by the priests). on the bank of the river the final death rituals are conducted. on the other side of the river are the temples dedicated to lord rama and walking up the slesmantak forest you will visit the temples of gorakhnatha, hanumana, ganesha and the vishwaroopa dedicated to the universal representation of lord vishnu. on continuing the descend you will visit the other important shakti temple dedicated to guheshwari. from here you can continue your journey on foot the the stupa of boudhanath! namaste
a divine feeling all around the premises of the temple an amazing architecture of old era and full of divine belief!

we went there when it already dark, and it is really good. they also have a good policy not to let tourist to entered the beautiful main temple. but you can go around to see the cremation ground and see the aarti, lingams, sadhu's dwelling, and dont forget to go to surya ghat there are some caves that related to naropa and tilopa, a buddhist yogi teacher or marpa who teach the tibetan saint milarepa.
cremation - body burning ceremony was so intense - makes you to thinks about life twice. temple on up of stairs was great.

this is one of the holiest hindu temple in south asia. this is a cremation center as well. full of religious activities. a must visit when you are in kathmandu. you are not allowed inside the temple if you are not a hindu.

pashupathy is anothe form of shiva. this is one of the oldest and famous temples in kathmandu which attracts tourists, devotee and also people with interest in history. a must visit when u r in kathmandu

one can travel to pashupati nath temple in the evening to have greater look. before aarti it is worth to see how they give purification bath to lord shiva (shivling). one can see from all the four directions. worth to enjoy!

one of the major holy place for hindus. the temple surrounding is open for any people to visit but not the main temple area. all the religious hindu come and go a lot in this temple. it is believed that before starting your holy visit to any of the holy places around the nepal or india , you have to initiate the workshipping in pashupati nath and same for the completion of the holy visit around the places.

this might be alittle too much for the faint hearted, because of the cremation.but i guess we all come to contries to know more about their cuture and this is one of the places to do so.unfortunately the best bit is unaccessible to non hindus.once you enter the pashupatinath temple , time stops and it has a super charged vibe.must visit

apart from being a somber place (as in: in-your-face-reminder that death comes for us all) - this place and the surroundings felt like one humongous garbage-heap: plastic bags everywhere, food left-overs, garbage - i've seriously never seen that kind of scene anywhere (and i live in a country where sadly many people throw their empty bottles and garbage out on the roads on a frequent basis. :( ). this place really left me deeply shocked.
one of the largest shiva temple in all around the world and must visit and to meny this to see around like hindu munks

a solemn and somber tuesday. i witness three cremation. a big burly fair skin man, a petite small size lady and a medium size man sharedthe same final destiny. death stings! the grief was indescribable as the son laid the father's body to a final rest. for the hindus, the karma and reincarnation worldview is deep and entwined with their culture. where does the soul goes? must go to understand the nepali rites of passage.

the entire are covered by the pashupatinath temple is massive. it plays host to virtually every major ceremony in the lives of hindus - naming ceremony of a child, marriages, cremations and post-death ceremonies - making it a hugely significant temple. entry inside the main temple is restricted to hindus only, and even as hindus, it's not very easy to get the darshans from each of the four 'dwaars' (doors). there will be a lot of pushing, shoving and jostling for space, all of which has to be managed while taking good care of your belongings. we were lucky to have gotten the darshans from each door of the main temple and then we roamed around the other temples surrounding it in a relaxed manner. if you hire a guide, they will explain the significance of many of these small temples and the stories surrounding them. more often than not, there will be a cremation ceremony in progress at the crematorium sites behind the temple along the river. it is a huge place of great religious significance and worth a visit. the huge crowd does make it a somewhat chaotic experience, but a worthwhile one too.
temple is in heart of city with vast campus,little walking is needed,darshan from four sides are nice,aarathi in evening is colourful event with ambient,must visit place

its an important pilgrimage for hindus - but that aside its an interesting place for everyone to see. loads of great photo opportunities with the sadhus of the temple with their matted hair & interesting clothing. it gets quite crowded, so avoid if you do not like crowds. from the temple you get views of the bagmati river & the crematoria - very interesting. has to answer a lot of existential questions to my little 7 year old post our visit here :)
an important place in the hindu religion. a guide will explain many things you see. be sure to negotiate the price and do not pay per person.
pashupatinath, or pashupati, is a hindu temple on the banks of the bagmati river in deopatan, a village 3 km northwest of kathmandu. a must see attraction

not sure how to rate this place as it is a crematorium and not really a tourist attraction by any western standards. you are not allowed to visit the hindu temple so you are there only to witness well organized cremation ceremonies. everything there feels almost like a business from fake sadhus to self appointed guides making this experience not really a solemn one. a good way to get a glimpse on how hindu religion deals with death.period. nothing really touristy about that.

a strange combination of religious funeral site and tourist attraction. entrance fee for non-saarc ("western") 1000 rupees or about 10 dollar. for that price you can walk around pashtupatinath indefinitely. and it's a big place! there are multiple temples and religious buildings within the park and hills, and also a deer sanctuary. know that some temples are destroyes though and others are only accessible for hindus. still definitely worth a visit though. the cremations seem to take place virtually everyday and you are bound to witness one if you stick around the river area (the ghats). when you go further up one of the hills you will find the more quiet parts, where monkeys and local shiva devotees go to hang out. one of the more interesting places to visit in ktm!

this is the cremation area where the dead are brought and burnt before having their ashes go in the water. we went with our nepali translator and it was super interesting to spend some time there and get good context on the practice and actually watch the full process from the sidelines.
it was a beautiful place but most of all, i experienced the sense of peace and tranquility. the highlight was being at the community centre nearby where mother teresa used to served.

this place is ok if you are into hinduism and pilgrimages. and interested in seeing live cremations. unfortunately the main temple complex is not open to other castes besides hindus. . some good stone carvings and sculptures are avail in the outer premises but if you really want to soak in the ambience would be during the shiva rating festival, usually late feb or so as per the hindu lunar calendar. the coldest night of the year and fires are kept burning 24 hours to keep lord shiva warm and company him this cold night. bhang or ganja (hashish) is avail for followers and sadhus , sages and smoking this is legal inside the premise this night. best to wistful in the late afternoon just to relax, take in the ambience and people celebrating. . morning can be too caloric. .

a must-see for all travellers who want experience historical and religious place. be careful when you are taking photographs. you are not allowed to take photos of cremation. and you will see babas there. they are not camera friendly. or you can seek their permission to take photos of them.
a must see experience, because you are dealing with death and human emotions it's probably not for everyone.

there were lubes of devout hindus in large numbers. very poorly maintained and cleanliness is missing. but for a hindu it has its significance, and the fact that it is over crowded dies not interfere with devotion. once in a life visit is recommonded

one of the big siva temple in around the world and nice historical temple around and good memory around inside temple
it's the best and most sacred place of worship for lord shiva . it has one of the beautiful architectures and lot of carvings around

as you should visit the temple as-in at noon time to get the darshan, so you can get the time to see temple propely

we came to nepal for a wedding and so going to the principle cremation site on the river was an uncertain choice . but we were glad we did ! this place is steeped in culture and religious symbolism and although you aren't allowed into the hindu temple you still see everything from walking around it and observing the process from the opposite side of the river . we appreciated having a guide to inform us of the whole ritual and to indicate when it was appropriate to take pictures of the holy men etc i found it all a moving emotional experience and would allow time to reflect on the scene before you

i could have spent the who day here,. such an interesting place. we watched a family preparations of their loved ones body prior to cremation and then also the cremation. also to see so many people come to the river to wash and pray and give offerings, it was so lovely to see. an experience i will never forget.

this temple has cultural importance for local people and also hindus all over the world. but it has suffered damage in earthquake and is under reconstruction. the temple premises and the approach road is not at all clean. very filthy place. there are guides in the temple who start telling you about its history even if you haven't hired them. we met such guy and made it clear in the beginning itself that we won't be paying him anything. he agreed and continued telling us the history and other information about this temple. once he was done hw started asking for money in the name of god. this doesn't leave a good impression on tourists.

visited the temple last week , its a must visit place for every hindu once in a lifetime. darshan starts at 4:00 am till 7:00am in the morning and 5:00 pm till 7:00 pm in the evening during this period you can also attend the arti.a big nandi statue disrupts the view of the actual idol from the gates. the entry is only for hindus i have heard but was not asked my religion at the entry.the temple complex is very large but except the main complex nothing is properly maintained which is more or less with every place in kathmandu. would've been very happy if the surroundings were clean & which can be done with little effort from the temple authorities.
temple in center of huge complex,god idol is visible from all the four doors and priests are from costal karnataka of india,evening aarti is very melodious and eyes filling

oh really nice hindu biggest temple called pashupati nath.it covered big area.there is a river called bagmati .when hindu people go to that temple before swimming in that river.it is god shiva temple.when people died that body is firing there.

locals free, all else charged $10 to $15 and could still not get in past the temple. wonder what they do with all that money as nothing appeared to be maintained and trash, trash everywhere. i enjoyed the surrounding local culture much more and was able to see the womenfolk gathering to give food as part o f their blessings.
this isn't for everyone. this is the temple were the local people cremate their loved ones. i was so fascinated on the burial rituals. i could stay for hours. again, not for everyone.

the entrance of the temple has a huge archway with the name of the temple inscribed. inside is a huge nandi and then a smaller nandi. a few steps are there to reach the sanctum of the temple. there are four doors so this helps easy access into the temple. worship of the shiv linga is done in this temple. the linga here has four heads facing the four directions and the fifth being the linga at the top. every evening there is an "aarti" which is offered to all the four sides of the linga. in the morning the temple opens at 9 am and the "abhishek" is done. at this time the linga can be viewed in its natural form. there is quite a bit to see around the temple. there are several lingas and smaller temples around. approximately one hour is required to look around. photography inside the temple premises is prohibited.

this is quite the experience. we follow the christian faith and it was very interesting to see how the hindu locals practice their faith.there is a lot of history here. the human cremations was a very surreal experience. make sure you spend some time chatting to a holy man. spend time here, don't rush through this one.

its a great place, the culture and the tradition are reflecting the spirituality culture. here you will came to know the meaning of life and death. shadus are reflecting the power of devotation for the lord shiva.
doors of the main temple opens at 5:30 p.m. in the evening. recommended to reach 15 minutes early since lines builds up.
we organized temple and famous with locals and tourists alike. doors of the main temple opens in the morning and in the evening at 5:30 p.m. and aarti happens at around 6:30 p.m.

one of the many must see religious sites in kathmandu. always interesting to see other religions at work and to understand just how different this is compared to traditional western religion! the dignified expressions of faith shown amongst the clamour of the crowds of fellow worshippers and tourists shows strength of conviction.

a huge and absolutely fascinating temple and grounds. while you can't go into the temple unless you're hindu, going down to the river to see the cremation ceremonies is very interesting and peaceful. worth the price of a guide for sure.

we had a guide take us there (through plan holiday's) and explained all the ceremonies going on. it was amazing to learn all about the culture and ceremonies taking place. i would highly recommend having a guide there to explain everything you are seeing and explain what all the buildings are. this is the first thing that made me fall in love with nepal.

little talked about amongst backpackers, but this is a must visit in kathmandu. a genuine experience and way to experience hindu culture. disappointed to see other tourists so openly photographing burning bodies and grieving families up close however

the temple is a sacred hindu temple on the bagmati river which is full of rubbish and very polluted with ashes from burnt bodies. people sifting the river for jewelry. a charge of 1,000 local currency is to be paid to see the dead bodies being burnt and the wailing families. the outside area badly damaged with earrhquake of 2015 is very diry with beggers and tourist stalls. if you are pushed for time, read on the internet and give this place a miss.

visited this temple which was unaffected by last nepal earthquake. we were there to pay our respect and specially for me before i went to do my three passes trekking. the temple is located right next to bhagmoti river and is a sacred place for hindu devotees.
pashupatinath temple is famous [lace to know about the hindu culture . many baba and monkeys around .
