interesting area but it's very crowded and tourist driven. you can see where dead are cremated and sent down river. pricey compared to other attractions.
this is my first time visit hindu temple and know about hindu culture . very interesting place. cremation, prayer. must visit

one of 12 jyotirlingas, this 15th cent. temple is a unesco world heritage site. photography is not permitted within the temple premises. there are many temples and shrines in the inner courtyard and outer complex. the main temple has nepalese pagoda style of architecture with cubic constructions and carved wooden rafters on which they rest (tundal). the two level roofs are of copper with gold covering. it has four main doors covered with silver sheets, a gold pinnacle (gajur) and two garbhagrihas. the mukhalinga is 1 meter tall, has faces in four directions and a silver yoni base bound with a silver serpent. the faces represent various aspects of shiva - sadyojata (aka barun), vamdeva (aka ardhanareshwor), tatpurusha, aghor & ishana. entry is permitted from west entrance to hindus only. the other 3 entrances are opened during aarti only. rituals and viewing from 5am – 12pm and from 5pm – 7pm. abhishek is performed from 9am – 11am. buy ticket at sbi counter for npr 1000 for rudraabhishek. abhishek is done based on the direction the mukh is viewed. if your receipt says as eastern direction then one needs to stand in the queue at the eastern entrance, wherein the priests will do the abhishek for the eastern mukh of the shivling.

we visited pashupatinath temple grounds after doing everest base camp. entrance fee was 1000.00 pp and non-hindu are banned to visit pashupatunath temple, but there was plenty to see on pashupatinath grounds. we saw nepalese funeral which took place across the river - members of family were burning the body - it was strange to see someone's legs sticking out and realising person's body is getting burned. ashes were swept into river. in mean time kids were in the river looking for valuable items which were put together with body. overall it was good to see the place.

we went at sunset which was a perfect time to go as it seems as the sun starts to set over kathmandu giving a glorious view. we went with the mindset of not getting a guide, but after we go a little lost we decided to pay a small fee to a gentleman with very good english who had started speaking to us upon entry. i have to admit that this was absolutely the best thing we could have done with our money, the man took us around the entire complex, taking his time to tell us all about the intricacies of the hindu religion and the cremations. we both learnt a huge amount about the temple, and nepalese culture in general and it was only a small fee in the grand scheme of things. if only i had his name to tell you.
its great place for all hindu community. god shiva is greatest god in hindusim. the art, history is amazing.

pashupatinath temple is most visited temple by hindu and other tourist. it is rich in art and most of the tourist who visit nepal will definitely visit it. i normally go there once in a year and enjoy the art, festival and culture. highly recommended.
being able to watch real hindu funeral ceremony from the beginning to an end is something unforgettable for a common european.

the pashupatinath temple itself is not open to non-hindu people, you can only look at the outside of the entrance, but the most impressive things happen at the riverside where the crematory ceremonies are held. off course it feels very strange to watch people mourne and grieve about their lost loved ones, but for them it is part of daily life. they are not offended if you stay there to watch, as long as you behave in a respectfull way. it is truly impressive and something we will remember for the rest of our days.
i am maybe a bit squeamish but walking around a temple area breathing in the smoke of burning bodies really didn't do it for me. add in the many fake holy men asking for money and i was glad to leave

kathmandu is on of the big population residence place . when you are in kathmandu do not forget to visit pashupatinath temple , the temple is very papular , there are many people from the morning to evening . there is place called ariya ghat where born the death body after people died in kathmandu . . this is hindu religion to burn the death body near by river . there is a bagmati river too . pasupathi is popular of god shiva and parbati . every body are there to pray these god . i like the place crowd of people and sadu are there .
this is a unesco world heritage site for good reason! the most memorable location from my first trip to nepal.
very special place not just because of the beautiful buildings but it was extraordinary to experience life and death from such closeness.

this is a must visit temple for hindus. unfortunately only hindus are allowed inside the temple. whether you are a believer or not, the temple and surroundings is worth the visit. if you are non hindu, you would need to purchase a ticket to get in to view the surroundings, not the temple. also footwear is not allowed so be prepared to carry them with your or leave them in the car. of course there is a shop where you can leave your shoes ( no fees ) but choice is yours.

i am not a religious person but i make it a point to visit key religious places in any city and it was given that i would go to see pashupatinath temple in kathmandu. in a distant corner of city is this famous temple of lord shiva. it is world famous and one of the key tourist attraction in city. people in india recognises kathmandu with this temple. it is spread in large area and is very serene. the temple inside are very serene and you get feeling of calmness as soon as you enter in the premises. i was pleasantly surprised by the cleanliness and the way people organise themselves and how the things are organised. the priests are very friendly and do not force you to donate or they do not miss guide you. they are indifferent whether you donate money or not. this is not true about temples in other parts of the world where the worst experience is getting rid from greedy chase of some false preistman. in nhps opinion this is the high point of this temple. on down side, i was disappointed that even in today's time it reads that only hindu are allowed to enter in premises. i am sure lord shiva would not have differentiated by religion of the person. it is high time this age old tradition is done away with. the complementary part of this visit is a river side behind the temple and you can see age hold hindu traditions including cremation of dead bodies. a must visit for all and hope that non hindu will also be allowed to enter in the pashpatunath temple premises, hopefully very soon.
i know the reality of the hindu religion its unique myth. pashupatinath temple is temple of the shiva and shiva is god of the all animals .hindu culture is amazing religion .
good to go around 1030 am, and if you are lucky you might get all the doors open to the main deity. do not forget to walk the numerous siblings assemble

a little difficult to navigate around but worth the effort. what a jaw dropping experience. unfortunately the temple is only open to hindus so we couldn't access this part but the rest is without doubt the most surreal experience. daily there are open air cremations, like statues, we stood and watched the deceased being washed, swathe in flowers and cremated. there are signs of earthquake damage however there is still enough to see and fill half a day. lots of friendly people that turn out to be guides that would like payment. we had a guide that proved very knowledgeable and gave us the background to the proceedings. lots of monkeys!

simply put this is one of the most intense places to visit in the world. one can find peace at this wonderful world heritage site. every time i have ever visited kathmandu, this has been one of my major stops and one of my frequented sites. this place opens the mind and the heart.

this is a large temple complex and has extensive grounds inside so be prepared for some walking. we went in the morning and there was huge amount of activities going on. by the river is where families come to conduct cremation, funereal or mourning rituals and these are fascinating to watch. tiull also see a lot of temple monkeys biding their time for the offerings which keeps them very well fed. as well as real or fake shamans dressed up to get money for getting their pictures taken and persistent jewelry vendors following you around. plan for at least two hours. this temple is almost within walking distance from dwarikas hotel but taxis are easy to get.
well worth climbing the steps to visit this very holy place, the views fro the top are amazing, not so sure about all the monkeys!
the place to be once in lifetime for all the hindu people from around the world. and a place must visit for any tourist visiting kathmandu.

the holiest place in kathmandu for all hindu's. you have to pay 1000 nrs entrance and you're not allowed to enter the main temple if you're not hundi but it's worhtwile to go their and visit the other temples. from there you can go up the hill and walk down the others side to boudhanat stupa; on teh way you will pass holy men, stupa's temples and monkey's

the complex is very impressive but it remains weird to look at these outdoor cremations. but in another way it is also exceptionally beautiful how they deal with death in nepal. lighter entertainment is provided by the (commercial) sadhus and the ever present monkeys.

impressive,marvelous and worth a trip to kathmandu. spend a day there and your life will be different after. unfortunately entering the hindu temple is forbidden for non hinduists. beware of tourist scams, guides, souvenirs and similar. wonderful!

absolute highlight of my time touring kathmandu. we watched a cremation ceremony from the bank of the river and really got to absorb the religions and traditions of the nepalese. highly recommend - very intense but beautiful place.
this sis hindu temple but very nice to see cremation about the hindu culture many monkeys also here i like it
definitely one of the most interesting places in ktm valley if you want to witness hindu rituals and culture.

a small group of us went in a luxury minibus to visit pashupati and boudhenath with birendas company nepal tibet tours and treks also know as visit nepal tibet. we had a very informative guide who also took us to the ashram for the elders next to pashupati where we listened to men and women chant and laugh in the sun. this was a highlight .there is a donation box to support them as well and we were told the eldest is 100 years old and all the people have no family to care for them, which is rare in nepal. the temple was beside the river and many female sadhus lined the entrance adorned in bright gold and orange salwars. there are beautiful grounds and deity sculptures lining the gardens above. a beautiful colorful reflective day in kathmandu.
beautiful and scary at the same time. just to sit and study people in this fantastic place for as much time you can.
a beautiful place to visit alongside the other local temples- beautiful brick work- only slightly damaged by the earthquake

i have come here more than 5 times. every time it looks new to me. animals are free to roam in the temple. i think the name pasupathinath is apt. one thing i like is on the 4 sides of the temple . there are entrances and you can see the shiva linga from all those. a must watch place. during winter, be ready for a frozen foot. when you remove your shoes and move toward temple . your feet will become frozen 5 centigrade is the temp (december)

one of the seven unesco world heritage site in kathmandu valley. such a beauty among all the hindu temples. shivaratri is the great hindu festival here occurring in the beginning of the year but i was here during the diwali which also is a important for hindu families. the temple is not far from kathmandu city, better to take a taxi than walk. the entrance to the inner temple is forbidden to non-hindus, but you can enjoy the view outside. maybe the best overview of the complex is from the terrace on the wooded hilkl across the river.
only the hindu can enter temples. the area is vast but the most touching is observation of cremation ceremony at the river. it is worth a few hours.

the main pashuppatinath temple is closed to non hindus, but you can enter the ram temple aram janaji mandir and the lakshmi narayan mandir, that lie next to it. the main draw i guess are the ghats next to the main temple, on the banks of the river. there is a lot of action there with all of the cremations and preparations for cremations going on. you can get a view of this from the ram temple terrace on the far side of the river. beside the ram temple are the obligitory sadhus, posing for photos on the payment of cash. if you want to make more of your trip here and have more time, you can take the path up the hill past the ram temple and head for the gorakhnath temple. it has some damage but it still worth it. if you head further on and down the hill you come to the shree guhyeshwori temple, but it is closed to non hindus. there are a million monkeys along the river bank here. fee to enter this complex is rp 1000.

an intriguing, but sobering place. beautiful temple and lots of cool stupas as you went up the hill, but stark to see the dead bodies lying on the other side of the river being cremated or getting ready for it. you could see, smell and taste it. meghan did not like the energy there, which i agreed with mostly. pretty different type of scene--not the most uplifting but glad we experienced it as it is a critical aspect of hindu and nepali culture.

i was been pasupati temple .so so imagined places .very ancient place.it is temple of mahadev.when hindu people died his or her body firing there .there is a river called bagmati.many moneky is there .nice

i went nepal with my husband and my little son and we did some tours around kathmandu valley. it was fantastic trip for us. a sunset view from nagarkot was so precious. we saw beautiful mountains very clearly as the weather was clear. after this we also did a package trip to chitwan national park. everything was perfectly organized. we would to thank ms tara and her entire team who made out trip unforgettable. now we are thinking to do small trekking in our next visit.
beautiful sight in the evening - go at sundown. also possible to watch or join in with the open evening service. it costs 1500r to get in but this is a price i was happy to pay

shiva, his trident, his drums, his bull. the site is enormous, and rightfully so. according to the puranas (ancient indian texts), nepal is where shiva would go for retreat, smoking ganga and meditating. in february, the nepali people come here to celebrate shivarati. everybody - thousands - fill the site smoking hash and performing rituals. there are many temples at pashupatinath. there are many temples, big and small, but the most important is the main temple at the west entrance. it is shiva's temple, but for the sake of not having their religious ceremonies bombarded by tourists, only hindus are allowed in. the strangest, maybe most shocking sight of all is the cremation ceremony along the river, which is done publicly. the bodies are placed on pyres while the attending family members participate in a sad ritualistic goodbye as tourists pass by and watch! a part from the intensity of burning bodies are gangs of monkeys (some not so friendly), but worth some cool pics. there is still a lot of damage to many of the smaller temples, some under repair, some not at all since the 2015 earthquake, so be careful.

beautiful and different. would try to get a guide for explanation of religious significance. unfortunately it is hard to believe people can not keep a holy place clean of liter and the petty pushy people selling trinkets and pictures with the monks.

“prayers, cremations, grief, re-birth” we felt uneasy standing across the bagmati river while observing various stages of wood fired cremation pryres. we felt like intruders as devotees bid farewell to loved ones. we felt more at ease walking about the ghats, one room temples that often serve as housing for the sadhus. we were told that cremations continue ‘round the clock. the actual temple itself is off limit to all non-hindus. yet, there is something very human and fundamental here----we live, we love, we die. but if there really is reincarnation, then just perhaps…

worth a morning of afternoon visit, this is where cremations are held and is a revered area with many shrines and temples. walk over the hill and back round by kailash hill for views across kathmandu to the mountains.

it was a wonderful place to visit. there are temples everywhere and so the monkeys. and it was also my first time to witness a public cremation. my nepali friend told me that they burn the bodies of their loved ones so their soul will go directly to the heavens. such a moving experience.
went here as part of a few days sight seeing around a 3 week trek. really interesting sights and sounds, definitely worth a visit.

we are overwhelmed to visit one of the most sacred hindu temples of nepal. pashupatinath temple is located on both banks of bagmati river on the eastern outskirts of kathmandu. this temple complex is on unesco world heritage sites. temple courtyard has 4 entrances in all directions.the western entrance is the main entrance to the temple courtyard and rest three entrances are only opened during big festival. most important practicing hindus and buddhist of indian and tibetan descendant are only allowed into temple courtyard . practicing hindus of western descent are not allowed into the temple complex along with other non hindu visitors. sikh and jain groups are allowed into the temple compound if they are of indian ancestry. others can look at the main temple from adjacent side of the river and has to pay a nominal fee of $10 (1000 nepali rupee).
if you want to see how the locals worship, it's a must see. it's a huge temple with many different buildings. there's an admission fee, if you are not a hindu or you don't look hindu.
very interesting to learn of cremation philosophy and ritual. was happy to hear that the entrance fee went to social assistance for the elderly/people in need.

after a very good trekking to the everest base camp what a better experience than getting to know the rich hindi culture of nepal in kathmandu manifested all the way through the beautiful and enigmatic buddhist gompas and chortens (temples and shrines). now in the center of the country we could enjoy the old temples, actual monasteries, markets (unbelievable freak st), towns and cities with arun, guides in mountains and streets. for a flavoury meal, at the new orleans cafe in thamel one block away from our cozy mandap hotel and for a pleasent afternoon, a drink at the kaiser garden all in thamel, the tourist zone. a really recommendable four day visit to kathmandu.

everything is very nice. the river needs to be cleaned. in my view geriatric home should be maintained as soon as possible, it has been suffered from earth quake. this is creating problem to the old people. as well as foods for monkeys should be prepared so as they don't have to roam her and there.
must visit place in nepal. it is such a wonderful and must visit place, if you are devotee of shiva. jai shambhu
a must visit place-it has certain vibes which you feel when you offer prayers before pashupatinath.

this is a very holy hindu temple attracting large numbers of hindu people from all over nepal and india to visit. it is also the main place for cremation of the deceased in kathmandu. here you meet holy gurus and witness the daily procedures of taking farewell of family members and friends who has passed away. this is for many western visitors quite unusual to take part in but a normal and important ritual in every nepali's daily life.
you will see how locals treat their dead. be prepared to see pain and tears. avoid entering the smoke!
just a place to see shortly but really incredible. to vieuw of the same city one temples and crematorium
the recent earthquake has adversely impacted the temple but it is visited by many. the repair work is on but slow. only hindu are allowed in the centre of the temple

there is something so overwhelming about visiting pashupatinath. upon paying your entrance fee, a local will most likely offer you a guided tour for which you may tip at the end. it is worth having the various parts of the temple explained and the insight into the history and rituals is invaluable.
very exotic. a lot of monkeys jumping next to you, sadha people sitting at ghats and burning men doing their job. take some water with you, in october its very hot

this is a huge hindu temple in kathmandu which only admits hindus. there are lots of animals inside such as cows, monkeys, mountain goats, pigeons and other birds. the temple itself is really old. one can view cremations from the viewing gallery and can even go down the steps for a closer look. foreign tourists have a view from the other bank of the river. time your visit so that you can attend the evening arati - it's supposed to be great.
holly temple of hindus,so many temples within the temple complex,one guide is required.must collect rudraksh which is the main prasad of lord pashupatinath.
the temple has a divine power which you feel as you enter it. the bagmati river aarti and it's chants fill your soul with divine love

perhaps the oldest temple of shiva that i visited, a rare place where the temple is on the banks of river bhagmati,with dead bodies being creamated just adjacent to the premises, famous lighted lamps "aarthi" during evenings,the shiva linga pooja being performed by priests from karnataka,india,with no animal saacrifice within the premises. that is done at parvathi temple away from this temple. d'ont miss the rare evening sunset over the temple with splendid play of colours on the western sky

the pashupatinath temple is one of the structures that withstood the earthquake. my guide toured me around the area.it's a huge place that is spread out between the sacred bhagmati river, unexpectedly there was a couple of cremation services that was going on.seemed to be a normal process that i found people around were just comfortable watching this holy ceremony, the family members of the deceased and the visitors /tourists like me. this is a hindu sacred temple so only the faithful are admitted and on the opposite of the river is the place for others to watch. aside from the on going ritual, one has to look around and be amazed.pashupatinath is a collection of beautiful stupas in different dimensions and weathered finishes and the hindu temple is an imposing but beautiful sight. it does not matter if you are for or against the purpose of the site.pashupatinah is fascinating!

i hate to have to pay the 1000nrps to enter. don't think that this is fair and cannot be sure where money is going. having visited hindu temples in india for many years and never been charged, it goes against the grain to have to pay such a large amount to enter here. once in as a " non hindu" one cannot enter into the inner shrines. having said all that , it is a living place of worship not a museum piece and one can see different aspects of the hindu faith here. life and death exist side by side. the sadhus are always interesting, even if some of them might not be all they seem. worth a look if you have to time( and the money)

remnants of the earthquake present everywhere but signs of a people rebuilding. a tour guide helped in describing the historical significance in the surrounding features. there was a lot of military walking around and some areas did not seem safe from the leaning bricks and shaky structure. worth the visit.

this is one of the most interesting sights in kathmandu. extremely colorful. you will see the preparation of the bodies for cremation, the ceremony carrying the body to the burning site and you will see the actual burning of the bodies. there are naked sadhus walking around, there are holy men doing readings and giving blessings. there are beautiful temples, most of them you can not enter unless you are buddhist but just looking outside can give you a good idea. bring your camera because wherever you look there is a photo. you can combine this with a trip to the boudha stupa. the walk from one to the other is very interesting and takes you through some lovely temples.
a very intersesting place to visit, expect kremations, monkeys, a lot of poeple. get a local guide to tell you a little about the place.

pashupatinath is the main temple of nepal as lord shiva is the god of nepal. its vwry fascinating place one should go tjere and take blessings from lord shiva when in kathmandu.its a interesting excursion for foreign tourist aswell as they will see many activities going around the campus of temple which will bring them by surprise.

i visited here on 29th oct. this is one of the twelve jyotirlingas of lard shiva. its a unesco heritage site. situated on the bank of bagmati river where the cremation also takes place. it has 4 entrances. the nandi statue is huge. you can monkeys around the temple. the temple is open from 4am to 7pm, however, the main temple is open 4am to 12noon, 5.00pm to 7.30pm. location is near the airport. need bit more cleaning in and around the temple.
what else could have been better this! yes, lord shiva's - pashupatinath temple. is the only temple where you actually experience the divine,power of lord shiva, here's to everyone! a must must visit
this is a huge hindu temple in kathmandu and a must see. non-hindu's can not go into the center but you can visit all the surrounding grounds and see most of it.

i visited pashupatinath temple, 15 days back. since i was staying in dream nepal hotel and appartments bim, requested me to visit the sacred destination in kathmandu valley pashupatinath stands in centre of town on the bank of bagmati river,it is a square, two-tiered pagoda temple built on a single-tier plinth,richly ornamented gilt and silver-plated doors are on all sides. i recommend everyone to visit kathmandu valley n visit all sacred holy places over there. beautiful scenario
what a place . wonderful. nicely arranged lines. gets crowded at times. better go early in the day as traffic is less during early morning.

the place is hindu oriented, situated at the west of thamel and the traffic was heavy when we went and returned to thamel. the entrance fee is 1,000 rupees. i have to declare that i am not a hindu so my comment that if your time only allows you to visit one place, go monkey temple might be viewed as biased. no doubt, if you want to find out more about hindu, it is a place to go.

we want here last at the end of a long day and i'm glad we did. the atmosphere from the opposite bank to the cremation pyres is hushed, almost reverential. it's hard not to be affected especially when you see a corpse being laid out by the river ahead of the cremation. well worth continuing over the bridge to the smaller "vip" area best viewed from the top terrace. it's a bit of a climb but we'll worth it for the views down the whole river section. the monkeys provide some well needed relief.
very much satisfied with monteresa trek expidation. nepal. for sight signs around kathmandu. & other trel like mukthinath & kailash manas trek.

we visited pashupati nath on dassera day & we were lucky to get darshan very peacefully since it was off season & most of them were away celebrating the festival at their home. pashupati nath is one of the jyotirling among the 12. there is a magnificent statue of nandi in the temple. you can get amazing bead long necklace in different colours which is traditional accessory of nepali women.

the history, culture and customs here are something else! the rich customs including the cremation in the hindu custom can be watched up close. our guide was incredibly informative and was able to answer every question.

this is a famous hindu temple by the side of bagmati river. every years, tens of thousands of devotees from india pay visit mainly during the time of shivaratri. entrance to the main temple isn't allowed to non-hindus given the historical reasons, however, i always see foreign tourists watching and photographing from another side of the river, mainly witnessing the cremation which is performed by the side of the river.

middle part of town bank of the bagmati river with golden roof view of pasupati is majestic because of sivas power i feel when i was in front of temple totally different. security is really kind and responsible for hindu won culture. i am glad to meat main priest it was my good luck.
pashupatinath temple is one of the biggest temple. in this temple we can see the lots of shiv linga as well as many goddess statue also. evening aarati was awesome to watch and singing over there.

its one of the most sacred,visited and known hindu temple of lord shiva in the world. its only allowed to hindus to enter the temple but all other religion followers are allowed to visit upto the gate and also watch some religious ceremony from the other side of the temple. as bagmati (sacred river)flows besides the temple, evening rituals (aarti) are performed along the river and has a beautiful and serene atmosphere. you need to reach a bit early to find a good spot to view the aarti and the temple premise is big enough to take good time if you want to explore full. it has lots of other temples nearby like guheshwari (15 mins walk) etc. lots of devotees visit the temple and it has enormous crowd during special events,day, month for eg. during shivratri and shrawan and also on mondays. hence, pls plan accordingly. temple remain closed during noon time.

the ancient pashupathinath temple is close to the airport, and is one of the most iconic attractions of kathmandu. the ancient shiva temple is grand, and there is also the attraction of daily evening arathi along the river. bodies are cremated close to the temple as well as part of the rituals conducted at this temple.

a great golden temple built in 15th century is a meeting place of hindu religion. it is one of the most sacred hindu shrines dedicated to lord shiva. the temple extends to a huge forest place with thousands of smaller shiva shrines around the place. located on the bank of holy bagmati river. the ghats are famous for hindu final rites for cremations.many hindus are cremated along the bagmati river bank everyday. a must visit place to see the lives of hindu devotees in one of the world heritage sites. hindu sadhus live in different sattals( small houses) and meditate in different forms.some are even aghori babas. entrance only for hindus in the main pashupati temple premise but one can visit to see the lively activities everyday.

i wanted to encounter the last truth of life and was there in the temple premises. the final truth revealed in front of me and i could look inside myself for a moment. now from the viewpoint i could see the golden pagoda of the pashupatinath temple with an assembly line of the smaller temples made in the middle age. as the time passed and was evening, the temple started sparkling in the lights and also with the evening spiritual music. a place where you see only the temple but encounter the truth of life ! dont miss in your trip to kathmandu.

we visited the temple in the late afternoon and it was fairly quiet from a tourist perspective. there were some cremations in progress, so be aware that this happens in full sight and that makes this temple unlike others you will visit. the place was pretty clean, but beware of the monkeys, who will try and lift things off you. also be aware that the "sadhus" (holy men) will want money to have their picture taken.
the area for cremations was interesting, but the surrounding temples were not in great shape. it was worth a visit, but nothing amazing. there were hundreds of red macaque monkeys.
it's one of the jyotirling and most respected temple of lord shiva in nepal. one must visit this whenever in nepal
it is at the bank of bagmati river. only meant for people with very high religious thought. evening aarti is good.

beautiful site where one can feel the culture and religious element. only drawback about this and similar sites is the fine thin line to make tourism sustainable without endangering the religious aspect.
the temple has given a divine blessings to me when i visited there. beautiful place & very close to kathmandu airport.
good locetion really intrested palace in kathmandu and historical nile and heritage palace and magic pawer amreally gating by gad shiva

this is where bodies are burned for the funeral process.it is a nice area,not all along the river.you can climb up the hill and go through the woods.along the way you'll find multiple shrines,it is a very nice walk.it takes a short time maybe half an hour at leisurely pace,wooden area,very pleasant.

the temple grounds are so so and foreigners are now charged 1000r to enter. way overpriced, but if you want to see cremations and don't know how to enter from the back side across the river, you don't have a choice. but to be honest, it's worth $10 to witness how they send off their dead, as well as walk around some of the temple grounds. near the river is where you want to be. the rest is not very interesting. the main temple is for hindus only. there are lots of sadus here, all wanting a tip for photos and such. they add a colorful back drop to the grey temples. monkeys are plenty. it's a good place to spend an hour or two. it's a cleaner, more organized version of india's varanasi. the cremations take place 24 hours a day.

this is one of the best attractions of tourists in nepal.indians find this similar to many such places here like banaras,tirupati balaji and many more.we were taken here by localite who also works here as volunteer and so he explained it in detail.we had darshan from all the four gates.a must place for visitors.

we all know about this place and i was lucky to visit it twice within 6 months. never been able to see all 4 ends(open gates) of the main temple but the feel of the lord itself gives the ultimate satisfaction. ghats and the river are not something i will prefer to visit but its a must to go place.

when we search for peace and prosperity, we all have common question, who is superpower. to feel the peace i always visit one of the best sacred place of nepal which is pashupatinath temple. place is having rich religions value and historical architectures apart from baghmati river adjacent to the temple. this place having cremation centre to realize your final destination.
the beautiful place with lots of religious value.when i entered the temple was stunned by the area it covered and the monkey but after the puja i was at peace.happy place

this palace is considered to be one of the famous and holy places in nepal. if you are going to study the hindu religion, its birth and all the aspects of it you will frequently hear and see pashupatinath temple because according to their religion it is one of the sacred places. that's why if you visit this place there's a lot of people praying and meditating. aside from the historical architectures and temples here, you will see a lot of birds, cows, and monkeys freely living with the people as if they are part of the human race. truly amazing!
pashupatinath is one of the best and famous shiva temple in around the world and historical heritage and cultural palace in kathmandu

a short taxi ride from central kathmandu, pashupatinath is a large complex of temples, with monkeys darting about all, that gives a fascinating taste of nepal's hindu traditions. the 1000 rupee cat to enter does not allow non-hindus to enter the main temple, but views within can be archived from the hills surrounding. beware the touts and guides and sadhus who will ask for contributions for any device rendered and just wander the site, cross the river and meander the peaceful parks behind (which are also the starting point for another 20 minute trek that will take you to the boudhanath stupa buddhist site).
it was dream for me to visit pashupatinath temple. one of the thirth place according to hindu religion.
be prepared for an emotionally moving experience if you are fortunate enough to be there during cremation. i have never seen or felt anything quite like it and will never ever forget it.

what a surreal experience. apart from the sense of peace and tranquillity that this temple has to offer, the lack of commercialization makes this a very pleasant place of worship. no one hounding you for money, no one pushing you around asking you to hurry with your worship and a general sensibility amongst most devotees lets you be with yourself, in the presence of the lord, even in a large crowd. monkeys, rams, calves, dogs, pigeons roam about freely in the concourse thus making it such a beautiful sight to see. it screams of peace and oneness with the divine. one of the most peaceful experiences that i have had in a long time. it could be a little cleaner though. having said that, i travelled during the monsoon and soon after a sort of a downpour - so expecting the place to be dry was a little bit of a stretch. a must place to visit.

went there as a devotee of lord shiva. at first visit at 4 pm tyne temple was closed and i was told that the doors will open by 6 pm. later went there, and was lucky to get darshan within 15 min. but the problem is upkeep, security etc unlike the more famous temples in india like tirupathi etc. the view of baghmati river and the burning bodies at the banks was tantalising and reminds us of our limitations and the truth of life.
visit to the temple was great, and the maha aarthi that happens around 1830hrs in the evening daily is a must see experience.

pashupatinath on the bagmati river is where many nepalis are cremated and for those dying live out their last days. it is also home to colonies of monkeys and a few sardhu's (holy men). it is not for those who wish to see neat and tidy and golden temples. it has its own holiness.
very soothing n soulful place where happiness and sorrows lies together. u can fel he cycle of life.

we have been at the pashupatinath temple. sumit joined us right at the begging. the entire exprience was amazing and a lot because of him! sumit explain about everything and more in a great english. we highly recomend to go with sumit! do this tour only with him! it wasnt the same without

best is sunset- non-hindus not permitted in primary shrine but no big loss. colourful pilgrims and sadhus. cremations at the gahts along the baghmati. don't miss the climb to the top to mrgisthali for earlier mandirs (temples) many still unrestored from the '15 earthquake. peaceful sunsets too. one of major temples of shaivites in all the subcontinent. worth the trip to nepal alone.

this is one of the world heritage site located about 10 min drive from tribhuban international airport at kathmandu. very well maintained and clean. one can visit the temple without any usually hassled by touts. the temple is located in the bank of river bagmati. you can spend some good time in the temple premise, it should take about an hour or so to go around the temple properly. special puja can be arranged on prior bookings. saturday and monday are usually most crowded days as locals visit the temple on these days. usually the temple is open for darshan from 7-11 am.

one of the holiest place for hindus. non-hindus are not allowed to enter the main temple premises but there are a lot of other places to see here. there is a stairway leading towards another temple just behind the pashupati temple. climb the stairs and you will find a place to sit and enjoy the view. it is just wonderful to just sit there and gaze at everything thats going around. the place si crowded in evening and on holidays. climb to top of the stairs to see some artistic temple with religious significance. the 2015 earthquake did quite a lot of damage to the temples but they are still standing. note : try and go to the temple in evening(just before sunset) and ask anyone for 'evening aarati'. it is done everyday and is one of the hidden gems in kathmandu. a must see.
the famous temple of mahadev in kathmandu which is worshiped by thousands of people every day. you will find almost all the hindu goddess in this temple.
one of the largest big famousu shiva temple call pashupatinath is really great dursan i did not much croude i really like shiva tempal

if there is god that god is in the nepal mountains, the himalayas, the annapurna, daualagiri, everest or anywhere but not at pashupathinath temple. i am very sorry and disappointed at the sorry state. the people who smear it with chemical (kumkum, burning oil soot etc .) have ruined the natural beauty of the temple. we say godliness in cleanliness but never practice it

one of the 12 jyothilingas (sacred form of lord shiva) - this temple is said to house the lord - who is the protector of animals (pashu - animals pathi - lord). while monkeys roam around freely here, cleaniness is totatlly lacking. unless you are very religious minded, better avoid
this temple is very big and near the bagmati river see about hindu religion but river is not so clean
a very holy place for all hindu people. better to visit here either early morning or in the evening. if you visit at afternoon time you will see the the people burning death bodies and monkies

a good place to visit if you're hindu. you get to enter the main temple complex which is extraordinary. otherwise, you only get to work around the temple complex, or walk a little further to the river to observe the hindu cremation rites. watch out for the persistent guides. you don't need a guide to walk around. take your time, get lost, and explore the way around yourself.
temple of pashupatinath has richness of religious fervour filled and a visit gives you divine experience.

om namah shivaya, the description of lord pashupathi nath is mentioned in the harappa and mahinjodaro civilization pashupati nath is the protector of all. best time to visit isearly in the morning. contact a day earlier and book a panchamrit abhishek pooja. you will get a proper receipt and next morning the pooja abhishek will be performed in front of you. this is considered as one of the most holy places in hindu religion and a must visit during trip to nepal. the architecture of the temple is beautiful. temple is made od stone and wood. during the massive earthquake in nepal not a single damage was seen in temple. people of all ages can come to the temple, there is a giant nandi and bhairav temple .behind the temple flows bagmati river where dead bodies are cremated.the pandits come from rameshwara south of india and never ask for any dakshina for quick darshan etc.
the pashupatinath temple is a must visit place. there is a statue of a giant nandi facing the main deity of pashupatinath. there are innumerable small shrines in the complex
me and my friends been in tempae is really nice shiva temple is very good lice manage not to croud to menny more another tempal around
a good place to visit for all hindus. ancient old age temple and well maintain and taken care of . no hassle or long que every one get to pray.
we visitedpashupatinath temple as this is the most sacred temple of bhagwan shiv and every hindu should visit this temple
i loved this area. so different from tamale in katmadu. more cultural, spiritual, architectural. great vibe, food, buddhist infuence which i love. definitely a resist for me.

pashupatinath temple is the holiest place for the devotees of lord shiva and hindu. it is very famous and sacred temple where hundreds of devotees come to worship lord shiva. your trip to nepal will be just incomplete if you miss visiting this holy place.the holy field of this place makes me visit this wonderful temple by bagmati river every week.

hindu pilgrimage site, the bagmati river goes to gangis, hindu does believe after death the body goes into five elements such as fire, water,air, earth and sky. after 84 millions reborn they get human beings or gods.

we met the temple with small daughter it was amazing for her and for us but some scenes weren't appropriate for young children and people afraid of dead bodies. not sanity but you must visit. so wearing a breath musk is recommended.

as per my experience nepal draws visitors who come for a diversity of reasons. some are lured by the call of the mountains and seeking to climb or trek in the himalayas, others are intrigued by the culture and the famous city of kathmandu, and yet others come hoping to find some sort of spiritual awakening. nepal can be an adrenaline adventure, a cultural eye opener, or a life-changing experience, depending on the itinerary and mindset of the traveler

the best place that i liked in nepal the pashupathinath temple. old historical temple a must visit when you are in kathmandu. a huge nandi in front of the temple. probably the biggest one that i have seen till date. very good atmosphere very peaceful and invites you to go again and again. gives me great mental peace on visiting this temple.

the temple is dedicated to lord shiva , the minute you entre the temple gate you do see the pashupati entrance from a distance, the walk from the entrance to the main tempe is really nice with interesting views and architecture. as you enter you see a big bull (nandi ) a bronze statue facing the entrance of the temple. the temple would be really an attraction if it was kept clean as i ket seeing people either spitting or there was cow dung. we could see loads of monkeys strolling the temple premises. so if you are on a short visit and have an evening to send you could do pashupati temple , its worth a visit.

the best time to visit the temple is when all the four directional doors of the sanctum sanctorum are open. as i understand the timings are 9 am to 11 am and 5 pm to 7 pm. the visitors do not have to fear from pursuing pandas (self proclaimed priests).

this temple is worth to go! as a foreigner you have to pay 1000rupiees to get in (approx 8-9euros), but the temple area is beautiful! if you go there early you get to see cremation ceremonies on the banks of the river, sadhus (locals dedicating their life to meditation) and many many small temples. the biggest templeyoi can't unfortunately access unless you're a hindu, but you can see it from outside.

wow. even as i try to recall all that i observed and saw at this temple site, i still can't seem to take it all in. i am not a hindu, but i do respect everyone's religion, so i entered this site with an attitude of a "learner". first and foremost: if you are a "westerner": someone or anyone who has not grown up in a traditional "hindu" or buddhist worldview but now claim to be a "hindu" or a follower of hinduism; do not claim that until you have personally come and observed the daily rituals of traditional hindus. don't just make something up and call it hinduism. come and see real hindu worship, death rituals, ceremonies and true hindu faith in action before you simply "claim" to be a hindu (i'm looking at you, hollywood hipsters). i saw death rituals and families seeking to find peace for their loved ones who have passed on. i saw sadhus trying to impress me with face paint and strange antics using their "private parts" (i can't go into more detail here). i saw women seeking "identity" by asking these sadhus to help them have children. i saw people worshipping idols, gods and statues. a mass of humanity seeking peace. i heard chants and laughter; crying and small talk. i smelled the burning of flesh and the rancid smell of sweat, trash and monkeys. this experience is simply overwhelming.

pashupatinath is the oldest and most well known hindu temple in kathmandu, nepal, situated on the banks of the bagmati river. it is a unesco world heritage site, which means you will get charged 1,000 rupees ($9.50 usd) to visit. only hindus are allowed to enter the actual main temple but you can walk around and enjoy the sights and take pictures. you can get a really good panoramic view of kathmandu here as well. myself and three others from my home stay visited here and were immediately approached by a youngish nepal tour guide. we saw temples devoted to shiva, hanuman the monkey god and several cremations happening along the river. the hindus have a much more pragmatic view of death; it's just another part of life. there are specific rituals start differently depending on the age, gender and caste of the person who died but everyone ends up as ash in the river. our guide said it can take two or three hours for a body to burn completely. we met a group of sadus hanging out around the temple who speak english and will ask for money in exchange for blessings or photos. i'm not sure if they were legit or not but our guide assured us that they use the money to buy food for themselves and the poorer communities in the area so i obliged. on the other hand, i was really annoyed by the vendors that kept approaching me and trying to sell me crap that i didn't want or need. our guide also took us to a nearby elderly complex where mother teresa used to work. the old ladies and gents were lined up for their lunch and had colorful laundry drying all around courtyard. in the center of the courtyard was a temple with a bell that one old woman rang incessantly for several minutes. at the conclusion of our tour, we tipped our guide and headed in the direction of our next destination. a black temple dog with white paws that we called puppy escorted us as far as he could but had to leave us and turn back when we got to main road because that turf apparently belonged to other dogs.

it is a famous, sacred hindu temple dedicated to pashupatinath and is located on the banks of the bagmati river 5 kilometres north-east of kathmandu valley in the eastern city of kmt. this temple complex is on unesco world heritage sites's list since 1979. innermost part of the temple is only reserved for true hindus! all the rituals are active and alive. crowded always! a few remarkable scars of 2015 earthquake are evidently still there. one interesting and love to observe is a lot of vendors along the entrance way to the temple. all have their own peculiarities, colours, fragrances, movements, sounds, etc.

my visit to pashupatinath was made all the more worthwhile by experiencing it with a guide - sumit kharel, who i met at the entrance of the temple complex. sumit was very knowledgeable, friendly, good-humoured and spoke impressive english, and i learned a lot more than i would have if i had explored the site alone. the pashupatinath temple complex is a fascinating and extensive place, and it was enlightening to have a local explain the various hindu customs and rituals of life and death unfolding before our eyes in an engaging way. sumit was also kind enough to show me around the equally impressive boudhanath stupa, a towering buddhist monument. if you want to gain valuable insights into this wonderful spiritual place, its history and its culture, i highly recommend hiring sumit as your guide.

i have travelled a lot around the world and unfortunately this is my worst rewiev ever! this is absolutely tourist unfriendly and racisstic place ! they would try to rip you off on many ways so: dear future tourist please read: entrance fee is 1000 rp (10$) and be careful because they would just start follow you to "show you the way" and start guiding you without asking you anything ! be careful about that and always know what are you dealing with. also, there would be some people who will present themselves like " students who would like to talk with you to improve their english"! don't - it's a trap- they will rip you off! there are some kids also who will come to you and pull your hand asking for money. last but not the least: don't take photos with the "holy" people unless you wanna pay another 1000-2000 rp! before taking photos they would tell you that you can pay how much you want (it's donation) and after taking photos they just tell you the price. shame on you! how come is that " holy" and modest life when you earn in 5 seconds as much as someone earn for 1-2 days actually hard working! shame on you! place is interesting to see the way they cremate dead people and to see a lots of monkeys but is also quite devasted during earthquake :/

with its ancient temples and history, pashupatinah is, without any doubt, the best site to visit in kathmandu. i often don't use tour guides but i was very glad that i approached sumit to ask for some guidance. he was so kind and relaxed and decent that i felt at ease immediately and requested that he give me a tour. sumit is highly knowledgeable and he will sincerely want you to have a good experience without rushing you through the place. he will take you to all the corners of pashupati and tell you stories so fascinating that your mind will literally blow up. this is a site that you must visit with a guide. sumit's english is great and he is highly knowledgeable and decent. he will also be very open to talk with you about many aspects related to the nepalese culture. i would not be surprised if you end up asking him to take you around to other places around the city. sumit eventually gave me beautiful tours to boudhanat, tamel, and bhaktapur as well. all very impressive, but my favorite will always be pashupati. don't miss this beautiful site and contact sumit to have the best experience!

if you go to pashupatinath, you should not miss a chance to meet great guides for this astonishing heritage. when i visited there, i was trying to avoid having a guide because i was visiting other places and i had been spoken by many people talking to me to take their guide just as any tourist spots are. but i met a man named ram having very sincere attitude and was apparent that he is trying to give a good experience in this great world heritage he was grown up with. during his guide, he was making best effort to tell me many things and to take me to the monuments and the view points as i told him what i expected for, and took care of my safety from possible monkey attacks! at the end of the guide, we talked about our lives and it helped me so much on making strong connection and imagination on the life in nepal. i could not forget this beautiful experience and visited here again with my friend, and met ram's friend sumit who was also a man with impressive will. i respect them and it is not too exaggerating to say they showed me not only the beauty of nepalese culture but the people's mind. so, i tell you "you cannot miss it!"

as an american traveling in a foreign land, it was easy to see the poverty and struggles of others but it was not until i met my friend ram (tour guide) did i understand the stories of the people from this country. we backpacked through nepal this summer, when i arrived at the temple i met a nice tour guide who spoke great english, his name was ram he grew up around temple, he told me stories of his personal experiences about life in nepal and of the recent earthquake. he was so eager to tell us about his city and he was eager to learn about where i came from. this inexpensive tour was so valuable i gained so much knowledge from this experience. i highly recommend everyone visiting kathmandu to take the tour and learn about the rich history and culture of the nepalese people. ram will answer any questions you have about the temple and he will get you up close and personal with everything going on at the temple. ram made my trip to nepal a very memorable one. thank you ram! i hope to visit nepal again soon.

sumit has been my guide for a day, and finally became my friend. more than explaining what's pashupati nath, things we do there, and why we do them, he drove me in motocycle to buddhanath -where, once again, we taught me a lot- and to one of this authentic cafe nobody but locals are used to go, to pay me a tea. because i told him i wanted to see more and especially authentic places. sumit's english is very good. sumit is also very funny, receptive, and passionate about this place and its history. he really likes what he does : because even though he always repeats the same things and answers the same questions, it's always done with different people, and that's what, with sharing his own culture, he especially likes with his job : my point is that you will feel you are not the only one to spend a good time there, and that will make you even more happy than if you are with a guide who just does his job to earn his life. in a word : if you want to learn everything about this place, but also about the nepalese culture in general, i really, really advise you to ask for sumit. he'll be more than happy to help you, and so will you.

(:

depending upon when you visit, you can experience so much, not just the "place", it's architecture and it's heritage, but as a truly visceral experience into the culture of this remarkable city, it's inhabitants, their religions and customs. come on the right days during festivals, and the colours will amaze you with all the beautiful women dressed in their finest colours. pilgrims come to pray at the temple. families performing the last rights and cremation ceremonies . it all happens here, at pashupati nath. a visit to pashupatinath on monday. every monday in the nepali month of july ( by the nepali calandar) is celebrated especially by the hindu women, who dress in their brightest clothing, mostly red, adorned with plastic bangles on their arms, getting henna applied. here to go to temple and pray for long lives for their husbands. pashupatinath is also home to the cities main cremation site. tho's families with enough money witll cremate their loved ones on a traditional wood fire. there is an electric crematorium close by for those that cannot afford the more traditional ceremony. loved ones feet are lowered and dipped into the holy bagmatti river one last time, sprinkled with the holy water, adorned with fresh flowers and then placed upon the traditionally arranged stacks of wood. if you are a tourist here, please respect the families and what they are going through, you are a guest in their private moment. stay across the river , take photos with discretion, and respect. while it may seem like a tourist attraction, it is real life. this is also a very large temple area, there is an adjacent deer park, and you can walk completely around this place in about 1 hours time. many many steps if you want to circumnavigate the entire place. there are also many "holy"? men, in various places, they seem to situate themselves in conspicuous places closest to where the tourists are. they are 99 % naked, covered in grey ash. yes, you can take pictures if you pay! why else are they there? still interesting, and free to look. there are a dozen ways into the temple grounds, go with a guide, and they will take you through the main gate so they get a cut of your admission fee (very pricy for nepal, i think 1000 rupees depending upon what day and how greedy they are at that moment. don't pay it, go around, find your own way in, they are just pocketing the money. this is free to everyone, else except tourists, so ignore them if you can, keep walking, say you are on pilgrimage, whatever. and of course, you can always pay. but. that still won't get you entry into the temple itself. want access, ? you must be hindu, at least for the day, yes it can be done, just go it alone, not as a large group if you really want full access to this incredible place. also, if you come, get change from one of the change people just outside, they will give you a handful of 5 and 10 rupee bills to leave at all the many important sites, where if you want to wax hindu you must leave an offering.
one of the beautiful temple i have come across. don't miss the evening aarti. you can the dead bodies being cremated at the banks of the river bagmati at the bottom.
jay shambho! must visit for realization of reality of life and transformation. at the same time visiting kirateshwor mahadev temple, cosmic temple, and guheshwori temple is meaningful.

i was travelling with my wife and the purpose of my trip to nepal was to visit the pashupatinath temple. we could visit the temple twice in two days. booked for a puja which was performed at 9.00 in the morning. we had darshan from all gates. this is a major pilgrimage site for hindus. it was a beautiful experience. the duration there was done very smoothly unlike you do at any shiva temples in india. credit goes to the team who does the temple management. people are very cooperative.

i had missed visiting the pashupatinath during my first visit to kathmandu. hence during my trip in may 2016, i had decided that i would not miss visiting one of the most holy temples for hindus. the temple, a unesco world heritage site, is very well maintained. once you enter the temple, there are many shrines dedicated to lord shiva, including the main temple, a site where there are supposed to be 555 shiv lingas (did not count, though seemed to be a fair number). after exiting the temple went to the shore of the bagmati river right besides the temple where here are some smaller shrines. also, one may see a number of cremations taking place at the site. overall, the place just calms you down. a divine experience indeed.
this is really good to know about hindusm.this is very ever to learn about hindusm.deffinaly like it.i will recommend you you.and don't miss this place to visit in nepal

this temple was very busy when we visited and entry to some areas was restricted to those not of the hindu faith. the cremations pyres were somber to observe and photos were frowned upon- naturally. very spiritual place

felt the divine power , being inside the temple. it was our first time to visit pashupatinath, and the experience was overwhelming, i strongly believe one can't visit shiv, unless he calls, after my several visits to nepal, shiv finally called me this july to visit him. the main shrine has four dwars and one must take darshan from all direction. the temple has many small shrines for ganpathy, hanuman and others. make sure you visit all of them. going around the 108 shivlingams are very special , so don't miss it.

we visited the temple early in the morning . in the hindu practices before visiting temples one should have a shower and if planned to offer puja then should fast untill the completion of puja. before entering the temple complex there are shops on both sides of the road . flowers and essential things for puja are available there.we entered the complex bare footed keeping our shoes in a place . in addition to visitors there are hundreds of pigeons ,giant goats and lot of monkeys.as we walk past the exterior we saw shadus sitting outside. till the gate of main temple complex photography allowed after that not. lot of people are queued to have darshan and offer puja.beside main temple there are temples of small deities.people are offering oil lamps. in the east of the temple river bagmati flows . from the temple plain itis 100 to 150 feet down. on the constant cremation fire is on.in the evening there are aratis onin the temple and in te far bank of bagmati . in the tranquil atmosphere music and religious dance is really wonderful and fills one`s heart.

the sacro sanctum, or the main idol is a stone mukhalinga with a silver yoni base bound with silver serpent .the lingam is one metre high and has faces in four directions .these faces represents various ascepts of shiva; sadyojata (also known as barun), vamdeva (also known as ardha nareshwor), tatpurusha, aghor & ishana (imaginative).facing west, north, east, south and zenith respectively representing five primary elements namely earth, water, air, light and ether.[10] each face has tiny protruding hands holding rudraksha mala on right hand and a kamandalu on the other.unlike other shiva lingams in india and nepal this pashupati shiva lingam is always dressed in its golden vastra except during abhishakam, so pouring milk and ganga jal is only possible during abhishakam through the main priests

i chanced upon a couple on the mt everest flight couple of weeks ago which was pne pf the highlights.of my two week sojourn to nepal. as i didn't have any other activities for that day, i gladly joined them for the city tour they booked with their driver. our first drstination was the pashupatinath temple, one of the many unesco world heritage sites in the capital city of kathmandu. i gladly shelled out the equivalent of about 10 usdollars to ger inside and was toured by our friendly driver who knew a lot about thencity and culture that hewas like a tourist guide as well. once inside the sprawlibg area, you get to see a very unique custom hindus have of cremating their departed ones. i won't share too much details only because i might give misinformation-but suffice it to say that all of us were very curious.

pashupatinath is a hindu temple area built on the banks of the river where cremations take place. it is said to have been built on a sacred site where shiva and parvati mated in the form of animals. it is a highly worshipped place and many big and small temples have been built over the centuries. the damages by the quakes are relatively minor. really worth a visit.

i have visited the temple last week. only hindus are allowed to enter in temple premises many other temples are in temple premises also such as bhairav temple. really a very attractive and holy temple. i extremely recommended to watch this place.

it was the first visit to nepal and since it was a business trip didn't had much time in hand for sightseeing. but pashupatinath temple was un-missable. it's very close to kathmandu airport. now, if you are visiting here, make sure you have at-least an hour's time just for main temple courtyard. it's almost nothing like ever seen. from main entrance, you can only see a huge bronze nandi's back. since camera not allowed, this is the only view you can click of the inner part. there are many small cupolas around with idols in the courtyard. there are lot many steps where you can peacefully sit and even meditate. the main temple itself is amazing. probably completely made of wood and stone, with beautiful stone and wood carved figurines of devatas. the two story building with these features is mesmerizing. the view of the main shivalinga is through a very small door. take enough time to look at it if there's not much crowd. the inside is only lit by diyas. behind and around the main temple are other structures with idols. just behind the temple and downwards flows bhagmati river and there is also an area for cremation of bodies. on the opposite side of the river, again there is a line of beautiful small cupolas in front of which aarti of bhagmati river is held. somewhat similar to ganga aarti in kashi. from here only you can click the proper pics of main temple building. overall it's a very soulful and peaceful experience especially being among the old and beautiful structures. you may take a guide too but i was not having much time so left the option. lots of monkeys around! careful while carrying the prasad packets.
found this place very interesting and informative, lots of heritage and culture and simply takes your mind back to this place where it begain

non hindus have to pay 1000 rupees but are not allowed inside the main sites. this is on top of the 1000 rupees return taxi fare. unless you are very interested in watching the burning pyres next to the river i would suggest the buddhist sites at swayambhu and boudha are far better excursions.

ended up spending about 2-3 solid hours here. make sure to walk around to the riverfront. we almost didn't! it isn't clear that you can walk that way because the ground is all muddy and not paved, and sort of a mess (maybe due to last year's earthquake, but i'm really not sure). it cost us 1000 rupees each for entrance. you can't enter the actual temple unless you are hindu, but the cremations are going on 24/7 and being able to watch this process from across the river was pretty incredible. the ritual has so many steps. it was all performed in a way that made me feel like i was watching a celebration of life, rather than watching mourning of a death. it was beautiful and completely different than anything i had ever experienced before. be careful of the hippie priests hanging around, they lure you in for photos and then ask for steep donations in return! otherwise, visiting this temple was a highlight of our trip to nepal.

pashupatinath temple is the most important temple dedicated to lord shiva in nepal and one of its most scared hindu temples. located on the banks of the holly bagmati river (which much further down flows into the ganges) and at about five km from kathmandu, pashupathinath is the place where hindus from both nepal and india come to die and be cremated, in the belief that dying in pashupathinath guarantees their rebirth not as a worm, a pig or a dog, but as human, irrespective of the misdeeds in their lives. you can, of course, observe the bodies being brought to the cremation ghats, the oldest son pouring some incents and holly water from the river before lighting the funeral pyre, then ashes being discarded into the holly baghmati and further down the river kids playing into the water and women doing their laundry. here, death is not the end but just another step towards nirvana. the temple itself (available only to hindus) is a masterpiece of hindu architecture and, if you are interested in religions, in other ways of thinking of life and death, then you may not like to miss a visit pashupatinath. .

the best time to go is 5:30 pm. go for the interest of the cultural differences, explore the temple grounds, and stay for the aarti ceremony, which starts at 7pm. you will want to be seated by 6 pm for the ceremony. i think it lasts an hour. it was definitely an authentic experience i truly enjoyed the aarti. seeing the burning ghats and basically "attending" a hindi funeral was quite powerful and felt almost intrusive; however, coming to the temple and watching the "funerals" is what many people (including locals) do. oddly enough. i never hired a guide, but wish i would have. apparently one of the mother theresa homes is nearby and part of the guided "tour." i have found temple guides to be very hot or miss. when they don't know information, sometimes they make it up. it is best to check with your hotel and see if they recommend anyone. also, taxis in nepal are crooks. be prepared to haggle and stick to your guns. i am hoping ola cabs or uber will come to give them competition.
gud and best historical places to be in nepal pasupatinath gives u a pleasure of everything combined peace,nature,mind and spiritual excercise.

in a total disappointing tour this was the place which makeup my wastage of time and money. a great temple and place of worship. non hindus are not allowed but if you are hindu you must go and seek blessings of lord shiva at this place. that will help you in your future lives for sure. as lord shiva has promised all that who ever come and pray here will never be reborn as an animal.

old hindu temple is a must visit place for all hindus. temple boasts of a central structure which houses the four sided shiva linga. guards control the crowd. while support staff present in large numbers, the are still needs more cleaning. not withstanding, a must place to visit. do pick up great bargains on rudraksa and potas ( glass beads) outside the main complex.
wonderful experience, a must place to visit to all hindu devotees. other communities can take a overlook of the temple from other side of the temple. peaceful place.

pashupati temple is beautiful, with lovely grounds especially at sunset, but only hindus are allowed in the temple itself, so many westerners and tourists will not be allowed in the temple proper. there are some scammers around as well - "shamans" who dress in full traditional attire and demand money, then try to "curse" you if you don't give them a donation. these men are not genuine, and you should not feel pressured to donate to them, even when they hold swords to your throat. just be firm and walk away.

not at all what i was expecting. so much culture but not prepared for the cremations happening everywhere. a guide nominated himself to us, he was at first annoying but then it was good he could share his knowledge. freaky to see dead bodies being cleansed in the basmati river, the smoke was from the burning bodies and it was all quite confronting. there was a funeral taking place, the body lit from the mouth to burn is not for the feint hearted to see. not a place for children who are not prepared for what is happening. such a memorable place to see and it's a must see, just be prepared for a confronting experience

it's an interesting place, but if you are not hindu, you will not be allowed to actually enter the temple. they will allow you to walk almost to the door and take a picture, and then they tell you about a "back door" where you can see everything. not so! you can go around the back and see some cremations, but you still cannot actually see anything inside the temple. considering the price (about $10) this made me more than a little frustrated. furthermore, despite saying so on the sign, locals do not have to pay. so, the only people paying for the temple are the ones who can't even go in! also, guides will chase you and try to tell you about the place even after you say no, monkeys are aggressive (i accidentally looked at one as i walked near it and it ran toward me to attack, but stopped when i turned my back to it), and lots of people will try to get money from you in some way or another. i give it two stars because you can walk around outside and there are some interesting things to see, but i would never go back and sort of felt that i had wasted my time.

interesting to experience. take local 'guide' to hear interesting tidbits! (we were listening to dude explaining pre-cremation process - very interesting!)

the entrance fee to this temple is 1000 rupees for foreigners. when i went, i saw many people just walked in and out of the temple freely but as soon as i entered, a "tourist police" stopped me and said i have to pay entrance fee. when i asked why the others do not need to pay even though it says so on the board, he merely said because i am obviously a tourist. so tip to avoid entrance fee would be to dress local and look local maybe. i point this out because i see many people walk in without paying. anyway, the temple itself is very interesting - many stories, histories to learn and things to see. there are two places where they burn the dead bodies and usually they burn about 2.5 hours to 3 hours depending on their sex. there are fertility temples and an old sacrificial temple too which is no longer in use. a man who works there approached me as i was sightseeing the place and started giving me information about the places, i really appreciate the info he gave me and while i did not ask for his help, i decided to tip him anyway when i left. instead of being grateful however, he lamented the tip was too little. the tip was definitely not little for such a short time of "service" but his response gave me a sour feeling throughout the rest of my day. hope others wont have similar experience!
