the entrance fee was a high 1000 rupee. but it was worth it since the money will be used for maintenance of the place. there will be some local tour guide that will be very friendly to you and start guiding you. be sure to ask how much before proceeding. i opt not to have a guide. there's some temples that are only accessible for hindus. first stop i went all the way up the stairs to some monuments that look like ancient lost city. there's lots of monkeys and a deer park. heading back down to the same path i went up just now, is the cremation area near the river. you have to walk past the cremation ceremony to reach the end where you'll see narupa cave. it was closed , you can only peak from outside. from there you walk up the stairs all the way to what i call the highlight of the place. it is an open field, but looks like a scene from the movie sound of the music. great atmosphere for love birds enjoying the view from any of the edge. buy some snack there from one of the many sellers, and pick a spot free of cow dung, and just sit and relax with the cooling wind and sun on your face.

we visited this temple after seeing the four other world heritage sites in kathmandu, and this really impressed, even after the others. only hindus can go into the main temple itself, but it is part of a massive complex, and you can see a huge amount without going into that main temple. one of the things we found really fascinating is seeing people washing their family members who have died, and performing ceremonies to cleanse (spiritually) their bodies in preparation for cremation, and then the bodies being burned on funeral pyres. being a westerner, i was initially taken aback by this all happening out in the open and felt like i was intruding by watching it, but our (hindu) guide happily explained to us that they are happy to have people see it, that this openness is part of the religion, and that they actually believe it brings more luck/goodness for the spirit of the deceased, the more people who watch it. definitely something i think is worth seeing, to see how different cultures approach death and its rituals differently. do watch out for the touts though, we had one lady follow us all the way to our car trying to sell us necklaces, even though we'd already bought some!

pashupatinath a sacred hindu's place, which is one of the oldest and biggest hindu temple in the world. you can enjoy its surrounding by seeing the old architecture and structures however some of it has been devastated by earthquake. we ca see cremation ceremony as per hindu religion which we can see live. be careful with monkeys if you holding something on your hand.

this temple among-st the most important for hindus apparently. the temple itself is spectacular and there is a river behind the temple, on the banks of which the dead are cremated. this is the only place in nepal where the priest almost forced us to donate money. "you have to give 100 ruppes for this." for whatever he has chanted. we never asked for his services. what a shame !

it's a divine temple which stood untouched in a place which was devastated by a deadly earthquake. always an amazing experience to visit this temple. participation in evening aarti is an experience one should not miss. it gives you an opportunity to get blessed.

you cannot go into the temple if your jot a hindu, but you can walk all around it, and the interesting part is the cremation ceremonies along the banks of the bagmati river. it is definitely a unique experience, and a cultural experience.
one should must visit while travelling nepal ! as it is religious place dedicated to lord shiva. :)

it is a one of the most holy pilgrimage site for hindus - dedicated to lord shiva. the main temple has gold plated roof and silver on the floor. only hindus are allowed inside the temple. however, there is a nice spot at the bank of river bagmati, from where tourist can have a nice view of the temple and surroundings. cremation of deceased bodies takes place at the bank of the holy river bagmati, believing that the soul will then go to heaven.

the crowds were ridiculous on shiva's birthday. millions of hindus everywhere. it was awful, almost a nightmare. i had visions of my wife being fondled in crowds of indian gang rapists. i don't know what else to say.
this is the great palce to visit where we can hindu cultures n rituals.also we can see there crimation

as a hindu i am much fond of this place as it brings me a enormous amount of pure energy and devotion while visiting pashupatinath.the amount of devotion towards this place from hindu people is extraordinary.the main problem that i see pashupatinath is that it is very much unsanitary and polluted and respective personaas well as government should emphasize in getting the place upgraded for revival and increasing of tourism in the place.

beautiful hindu temple from 5th century on the sacred bagmati river where hindu's from all over the world bring their dearly departed family members to cleanse them in the river as preparation for their departure into the after life. the bodies are then cremated on the banks of the river. just be aware of con artists pretending to be hindu priests collecting money from unsuspecting tourists. this is a must to see when you are visiting kathmandu if you want to experience the hindu culture.
situated in on the bank of holy bagmati river, this temple is one major tourist attraction of kathmandu. open crematory is the one which may be new things for western tourists.
interesting to see, lots of monkeys around, you need someone to explain the things you see to get the most out of your visit.

definitely worth the entry price, and to get a guide to talk you through the tour was great as i was told many things about the history and goings-on that i would otherwise have no idea about. an eye-opening eperience to see beggers sifting through human ashes while fresh bodies are prepared for cremation.
a mixture of feelings as you enter the temple. it s a very big area where weddings are celebrated next to cremations and where yogis find a place to live. a must go.
a places where you can sit cool down your system and seek blessing from the almighty.wish the river flowing near to it had been kept and river front developed to make it beautiful.
lots of monkeys and few things to really see as mist parts are closed to the public or hidden behind tents until they are fixed
never seen anything like it. highly recommend. smells a funky sometimes but yeah really interesting. some dudes will try show you around if you pay them.

one thing that really intrigued us when we visited this temple were the corpses lined up at the river bank, which we later saw were being cremated. we were able to witness a little bit of the ritual/practice later. people seem to be fond of spending time near the bank of the bagmati river especially in the afternoon, as we saw many of them just sitting there.

there is a lot to see here and it is worth taking a guide with you to gain an understanding of the rituals and spaces in which you are walking. the cremation of bodies by the river is very interesting and there are a lot of temples up on the hill. very interesting spot to visit.
cremations are done here next to the river, it can be a little too much. there are monkeys everywhere and it is a little bit dirty
definitely a must-see while in kathmandu. the place has a very special spirit. take a guide with you to understand the hindu rituals!
not sure how you rate a temple. all i can say is do visit if in kathmandu. serene environment. keep in mind clicking pictures inside the temple area is not allowed

first it looked like not much worth for the 1000 rupees of entrance fee - but when you walk around, there's actually a lot to see. the cremation of bodies by the river is, of course, very interesting and when a climb up the big stairs, there are a lot of temples up on the hill. this is definitely one of the most the most interesting sights in kathmandu.

it is a holy place for hindu relogious , it is a hindu temple in the centre of kathmandu, only hindu peoples are allowed inside the temple without carring any leathers items like purse shoes belts etc , very nice place of olden age.

located north east at the bank of bagmati river in kathmandu. pashupati nath mean lord of beast, this place is very much popular for cremation in puplic. every day many death people are brought by family for final rituals in human life that is performed with great honored. only hindus are allowed to enter the temple but other can have good time walking around and watching cremation. beautiful place to learn lot about life.
i would recommend this place to see not only a temple but some of the local traditions too. popular with locals too

it is a beautiful place to visit and my guide told me that this is the most sacred hindu temple in the world. i loved the surroundings but the place was very dirty and crowded. why do not the government clean and properly manages the place?
only hindus can enter the temple, temple is historical and very old. this place is very crowded. the cremation area is just behind the temple and its doorstep to enter heaven as per hindu believe.

pashupatinath temple is one of the holiest hindu temples in the world. i was very fortunate to spend time there with a nepalese friend who explained how, in the past, the while building by the river was to house those who were near death. it was like a hospice with doctors and caretakers. as the person came close to taking their last breath they are wrapped in cloth and taken to the river where their feet are washed (the river flows eventually into the holy ganges river in india) while family/friends chant until they pass. the hindu belief is that how we leave this life is how we enter the next, so they want the person to leave with only peace and thoughts of god. today people are sent to hospitals and so do not come for this until they have died. after this ritual the body is cremated. there were many cremations going on while i was there. if you have time, it is a beautiful place to sit, observe and contemplate how spirit moving into the body, living in the body and leaving the body are all natural phases of life to be embraced. we were there in late afternoon and for the beginning of sunset. i am not certain about this, but i believe it is the best time to view the cremations.

being a hindu, i was quite skeptical visiting yet another temple, not expecting it to be much different from the ones i had seen back home. but the architecture is quite different, for similar to tibetan/east asian style pagodas. there are many hawkers selling ceremonial-peripherals and souvenir items on the way to the temple. once at the temple, you will be asked (if you are a hindu) to remove your footwear and place it in a temple bag that will be kept safely in a shoe rack and a token for the same handed over to you. all non-hindus can walk right up to the entrance of the gate and hindus can proceed inside. there were idols of various deities inside and the prominent sight here that everyone on trip advisor seemed to be raving about was not quite pleasant/interesting (in my humble opinion) to watch. the river where the cremations were taking place was quite polluted and it was sad to see that nobody cared to clean it up.

this place is truly spiritual. seeing the shrines and cremation spots was unlike anything i have ever seen before . certainly different for westerners to see. the way the local people deal with death is truly fascinating and i would encourage people to get a guide who will explain the meaning of certain functions in the cremation procedure and how relatives grieve for their loved ones. my guide talked me through the steps involved in the process and i was lucky to witness actual cremations taking place. it was truly moving.

this is a place where people bring the dead for cremation. the process is actually quite interesting and unlike anything i have ever visited before. it is outdoors. out of respect for the dead, we decided not to take photos. entrance is 1000 rupees. there is a temple where only hindus can enter. we spent about an hour here.

i had been here on a previous visit to nepal, however i accompanied a friend and although i find it quite an oppressive place i also find it quite calm. we had a guide that befriended us at the entrance and this was worth the few hundred rupees we paid at the end to learn about the temples, steer us away from the monkies and protect us to some extent from the hawkers and beggars. always be aware of your surroundings if visiting here

the visit to pashupatinath temple always brings in me a very blissful and tranquil experience. this is one one the most ancient and sacred hindu temples in the world, a unesco heritage and is especially very crowded on mondays and certain days in a month per lunar calendar. only hindus are permitted in the actual temple premises, however, there is a lot of fascinating and mysterious culture to explore beyond the actual temple premises. the temple lies on the banks of holy bagmati river (like the ganges in india) where the many hindu rituals including cremations take place. sights of sadhus dressed in colorful yellow and orange robes with ashes and long hair and beard, signs of worldly renunciation are common in the temple area; there are also a number of ashrams and 'bhajans' singing groups of people along with numerous temples and shrines in the area that adds a sense of spirituality in the region. the view of the temple, flocks of people paying homage in the temple, the sadhus, the rituals, the river bagmati, the architecture of the main temple and other temples - all of these add bliss to the environment. the view is most fascinating from the east side of the temple across the bagmati river, where there are places for people to rest and experience the beautiful ambiance.

this is another unesco world heritage site, a must visit place when in nepal for cultural experience. you get to experience and learn about the traditions and religion. wittness live the processions and ceremony.

pashupatinath is the cremation area and temple along the river bhagmati. although you can't walk through it as you can in varanasi, you can observe the cremations more easily from the bridges over the river. the temple is viewable from a distance although you can only enter if you're hindu. taxi rates are about three times higher in katmandu than they were when i was here two years ago, so visits have to be better coordinated. entry fee here is about $15.
a must see on your list of places to see around kathmandu. have a great time walking around and taking in all there is to see.

it may sound morbid to anyone who hasn't yet been there, but it is strangely meditative and calming sitting on the bank of a low dirty river watching human bodies go up in flames. be sure to set aside at least a good half day to explore and absorb pashupatinath. follow the staircase that leads up from the east bank of the river to find some of kathmandu's best relaxing trees and a good view of the city, watch out for monkeys (i have a friend who got randomly attacked), and walk around the old city part too. can be linked with a walk to boudnath. would give 5 stars, but the 1000 rupee entry fee is a little embitteringly steep, especially if you're spending a while in kathmandu and end up having to pay it several times.

various legend exist about the temple and origins but visiting this really old temple which is one of the important temples / place of worship for hindus. it has a ghat (cremation and prayer area for the departed, with a part reserved for royalty) one can see people from all over the country and india offering prayers.

of all the temples we visited this was by far the best. the entrance fee is high at 1000 rps but there is so much to see it's worth it. once inside we also hired a guide which was a good idea as he explained all the gods and temples really well. his name was diamond (like the stone). we were told all about the cremation ceremony's and it was the best cultural experience we had during our trip. there are also lots of monkeys to see.

this temple is one of the "must-see" experiences in kathmandu, even more so if you haven't seen varanasi in india. however, it presents the tourist with a few ethical challenges that i wish i'd had more time to consider before visiting. you will see families mourning. you will see the dead being prepared. you will see cremations on the banks of the river. you are allowed, even encouraged to spectate. you are also allowed, and even encouraged by guides and local "holy men" to use your camera. i ended up taking photos, but i was acutely aware of the grief of the families on he opposite bank. just something to consider. a key detractor are the monkeys who are a pretty militant lot. it is easy to walk into or be surrounded by a full-on monkey brawl. these are not placid monkeys found elsewhere in the city so exercise caution. i am not sure if the combined effects of being covered in smoke and monkey pooh make up for the novelty of this site - i'll leave that to you to decide.

the spiritual journey to new heights. on of the most ancient temple and a must to visit. it carries a great legacy of lord shiva temple. often flooded by tourists, i felt a bit crowded but this place has one magnetic aura i felt.
one of the important place for hindu and interesting place for me. their culture is just surprising.

its like another planet or era, from one side you are admiring the spirituality and amazing architecture, from another you feel empathy for the inevitable poverty of people and believers living in the area. the babas, are definitely the highlight of the monasteries. their eyes sparkle when they talk about simplicity of life.

people seem to just hang out and wait for a funeral procession. it is really interesting to see the body come in, people secure it near the water, wash it and then burn it. i would highly recommend seeing this site while in ktm
partial damage to temple but open for visit, hindu temple 5k from kathmandu valley. a must when visiting kathmandu

this was one of a few places i definitely wanted to visit in kathmandu. holy and world heritage site with a remarkable atmosphere. there are lot of cremations undergoing throught the day, so be prepared for a typical wood burning smell (i expected something even worse). you also have to pay an entrance fee - 1000 rupees, so quite a lot, but its worth to visit this place, as you can see the whole ritual of the last farewell from the distance including burning the body and mourning - not something you can experience in the western world.

i went twice to this place as i was meant to go there. its was on my way to mukktinath n kailash where we spend 2 days in kathmandu. its breath taking temple as i can feel lord shiva's presents there.a huge brass nandi welcomes us.we had a god darshan blessing from the priest there. you also can see death bodies been cremated here at the bagmati river which later meets the holy river ganga.you can also see many sadhus seeking for liberations. you can take photos with them but not free you pay.but i was scared even to see them.

be prepared to take off your shoes and walk barefoot. the temple is open only to hindus,and tourists and they are averse to muslims entering. it is a symbol of strong hindu faith and interesting to see even cremations going on inside.

the temple itself is only accessible by hindus but everyone is able to answer the whole site. it's worth getting a guide (i paid 500 rupees) as you will learn a lot about the site and the rituals completed as part of the cremation process. it's sad but also beautiful how people care for the dead. if you want to take photos of the holy men you need to pay a small amount. a good guide will tell you who are the real ones and which are fake. definitely visit but be prepared as i saw 6cremations while i was there.

usually tourists have the chance to see at least one open-air cremation. the majority of religious rituals are culturally unusual and even mind-blowing for westerners, but probably the most culturally unusual thing in pashupatinath is the specific smell of cremated bodies. unlike any expectation the smell has nothing in common with the smell of decaying flesh, but rather reminds the smell of clabber mixed with different spices.
be careful of monkeys, dirty. not much to see around except for archaeological importance. nearby food not available.

it is a great heritage site. very good art crafted in the temple. many interesting small temples are in around. a culture of dead body cremation in the permises of this holly sight makes bad smell and smokey environment. it is a bit difficult to digest. however, it is good place to visit at least once in life time.

my husband and i were very intrigued by the cremation process at this temple from beginning to end. families hire men to wash the bodies in the holy river and prepare them to be burned on cement slabs over open fires. once the bodies have been cremated the ashes are pushed into the river. we had never witnessed anything like it before and found it impressive.

this is their special and most scared temple , though very poorly maintained . you will have monkeys all over place and people will surround you for pooja. you have to park your vehicle almost 400 meter away from temple and walk down to temple.
the temple is very famous for the indian diaspora, and you can get a feel of indian hindu tradition in nepal if you decide to see the place

pashupatinath temple complex in the kathmandu valley is a fascinating and significant shiva landmark and a pilgrimage place for many hindus. the main entrance is bustling with stallholders of all kinds, including one very talented watercolour painter who creates evocative scenes of this ancient city. the many sadhus wandering the grounds are always interesting to see and the ghats on the river, where cremations take place all day are a humbling reminder of impermanence. there is a park and seats a fair way above the ghats where people can sit and watch at a discreet distance. you may also get into conversation with a local about the religious significance of what is happening. many temples and statues are also dotted around the complex.

one of the very important shiv temple in the hindu nation. our visit was so precious that we were able to see the chardwar (four door) darshan of this special divine place. here the shiv has four faces and you get rare opportunity to have all four face darshan. we felt divinely blessed to have this darshan during our visit to this temple
amazing place to visit! a must! you can not enter the temple, if you aren't a hindoe, but you can see beside the temple. take a good guide, who gives you a good feedback.

very good wives from temple. we visited temple for three times every time it was different experience. i started loving and believing even more. that god exists. thanks god to give me opportunity so we got the pleasure to come and have darshan of yours. har har mahadev, dev dev maga dev dev. om namah shivay

this holy temple of lord shiva is situated very close to kathmandu airport. if you are on a short trip to kathmandu, will recommend visiting the temple while you are going to your hotel / office from the airport directly. temple closes around 6 in the evening

this temple is a place where the families of those who are dying or recently died find a place. staff assist the family in the cremation process and also provide a hospital of sorts for those dying to come. across the river from the cremation pyres many people come to sit and watch. it's a fascinating process, meant to be observed with dignity and silence.
a holy place but very crowded.there are a thousand of lingas inside the temple which is worth seeing.

the history of the temple is very rich and its very beautiful. hire a guide for a perfect tour of the temple "parikrama". the temple has a unique combination where apart from seeking blessings, the temple also provisions to carry on last rituals according to hindu religion.

pashupatinath is a shiva temple and a major pilgrimage site for hindus. there is the main temple and many smaller temples on the grounds. the structures straddle the bagmati river which is ritually pure and holy but terribly polluted. there is so much going here it is advisable to read about the site before visiting. people line up to get inside to worship the linga which is the representation/presence of lord shiva. this is open to the hindu only so unless you are hindu you cannot enter. i have met people in india who on the mention of nepal say that they hope to make a pilgimmage to this temple before they die. on the ghats or platforms on the river side of the temple cremations are constantly occurring. this may be shocking to people from developed countries but it is great example of how close the nepali people are to the rhythms and cycles of life. i remember one visit where cremations, children playing in the river and women washing clothes were taking place within 100 feet of one another. there is a also a building on the upstream side of the river. it is called a nursing home by locals. families bring people about to die here and the people who run the place are good at knowing when death its imminent. individual are brought to the river's edge and placed on a slanted ramp with their feet in the waters of the river - the belief is that if a person's feet are in the bagmati's waters at the moment of death s/he will be liberated from the cycles of reincarnation. there is also an old age home and a small infirmary run by the nuns of mother theresa's order. cross the bridge and walk up the steps to the smaller temples and you will see numerous sadhus who are devotees of shiva and make various claims about their diets and abilities to perform various feats. pashupatinath is a kaleidoscope of activity - a must see.

pashupatinath temple darsan is like dream come true after log time plaing. it is best temple for me . truly blessed after darshan of lord shiva here pashupatinath temple is must visit for hindu divotees.

had a very different experience to visit this temple. considered one of the biggest and oldest temple for lord shiva. devotees from all over the world can be seen in large numbers every day. a quick visit is worth. felt little uncomfortable with the cremation area.

there is so much there to see wiht so much significance, pay the extra $10=$20 for a guide, you will learn a lot more than just wandering around. don't be surprised by the cremations going on right in front of you with spectator seating.

we have heard about the hindu rituals but this was our first encounter, an eye-opening experience. the funeral and cremation processes were done according to nepalese traditions, there were no crying and howling for the deceased, showing that the nepalese had their own understanding about death. other sections were off limit to non hindus.
i visited pashupathi nath 15 days before earthquake. such a holy place. but very crowded. i liked the thousand lingas temple inside the pashupathinath temple

pashupatinath has not been damaged much by th april 2015 earthquake. it is very interesting to walk around the area alongside the bagmati river. the temple is napls most important hindu temple. the sadhus will have their photo taken foor a small amount of money. non hindus cannot enter the main temple.

we walked from boudda to pashupatinath in about 30 minutes. we went there from the hill side passing the river and some small buildings looking like an older temple, where a lot of monkeys lived. we passed a guard, where we paid 1000 rs each and got a ticked. the path from the hill side can be a bit steep at som places. when we arrived to the crematorium a funeral ritual just started. it was really interresting to see the hindu culture, but also a bit stange to be watching as a tourist. it's different from our culture, to burn dead family members outside while watching, so some people might find it a bit macabre. but it was a very peacefull and quiet cermony, which gave us a good insight in the hindu culture. if you want to take pictures of the painted men sitting outside the temple, they will charge you.

it is a magical place where one can sit and think about life and death at the very same moment. best moment to visit in our opinion is just before sunset. having been more than once to varanasi (the indian holy city on the banks of ganges), we can definitely say thay pashupatinath has kept its authenticity better with time passing. you will not be bothered by touts: the only "tourist attraction" there seems to be "businness-sadhus" and it's up to you to give them a few rupies and have a funny photo together if you want.

we visited this during our nepal trip in december 2010. the pashupatinath temple is a very popular sacred hindu temple dedicated to lord shiva(pashupatinath) and is located on the banks of the bagmati river 5 kilometres north-east of kathmandu valley.this is a unesco world heritage site. photography is not allowed inside the temple complex, which is very well maintained. we helped by a priest did small puja inside the temple premises and the priest was happy with whatever we payed at the end unlike some temples in india. only hindus are allowed to enter the temple premises. must visit when in kathmandu.

quick tour to pashupatinath temple in nepal ,its very close to tribuwan airport and can be easily visited in short interval of time i had been helped by my cab driver as well as a garland selling lady-manju didi here to see the place, do buy genuine roodraksh here,if you find one
pashupatinath largely escaped damage from the earthquake and is certainly worth the time to visit. try to go there without quoting monty python "bring out yer dead".

pashupatinath was a very interesting place.this is a magnificent pagoda style temple with great architecture and wonderful metal doors. only hindus are allowed to enter the temple, but even views from across the river are not bad. it is a place of sadness it is where the hindus bring their dead for cremation.you can spend quite time on shelshmantak jungle.

pashupatinath was a very interesting place. it is a place of sadness it is where the hindus bring their dead for cremation. it is also a very peaceful place the river running tru it. i had a local guide to get the full history well worth a visit.

really interesting and historic site of hindu temple. its where bodies are cremated so be careful where you photograph and indeed go if families are mourning. we had a guide which we often don't but worth it - as always sort price out. also take some smaller notes for offreing to sadhus and pics with them. from here its an easy walk to bodnath temple its around 20 mins walk- keep walking up the steps and then left at entrance of temple over the bridge and straight on through the town - just ask if right way

this is a magnificent temple with great architecture and wonderful metal doors. only hindus are allowed to enter the temple, but even views from across the river are not bad. there is a strong aura of death around the temple with its cremation ghats facing the river

i hadn't been to pashupatinath for many years, and went only because guests in town had put it first on their list of places to visit. i am so happy that i went. the bagmati river, which runs through the temple complex was running nicely, although it was filthy as always. we went on a nepali holiday, and there were many families visiting the temple, dressed in their best. at the main shrine, which we, as non-hindus could not visit, we could hear the sound of a lively bhajan, or religious song. being sung. as dusk fell a huge troop of monkeys came thundering past, the big alpha males standing guard to make sure all the mothers and babies made it safely past the humans. all in all a lovely visit, which i would highly recommend to any tourist.

everything about pashupatinath surprised me - the size of the place, the throngs of people, the emotion, the respect given to the dead in the cremation ceremonies. it was a complete eye opener; one that i will never forget. you must visit here to get a truthful experience of nepalese culture.

came with a few friends to visit the place before our flight out of kathmandu. outside of the temple is crowded by locals trying to sell stuff and push to be hired as tour guide! there are so many monkeys running around. you can take pictures of the monkey but can't cross them. they can be very aggressive. following the smoke to the entrance of the temple. for foreigners, we will have to pay 1,000 ruby ($10 usd) to enter which is one of the most expensive one in all religous and historical sites in nepal. there are many ceremonies and cremations along the river which you can observe and taking pictures. i did not take pictures of these ceremonies out of respect of the death and their family. it is interesting but to some, it may be a bit disturbance to see bodies and smell of burning flesh. there are many sadhus hanging around the temple and calling you to come taking pictures with them for a fee! they are a bit creepy though! overall, it is interesting place to visit and observe the local rituals. you might want to wear a mass for the smoke and watch the direction of the wind!

we went her twice on our trip. once during the day and once at night for the aarti. both times it was so peaceful. it is beautiful and the vibrations/energy here is great. the evening aarti/prayer is not to be missed. the best i have experienced thus far. i love this temple and plan to go back here again. note: no photographs allowed and i do think its best that way so you might want to leave your camera behind and just enjoy the temple.

the condition of such an important shrine will sadden you. it appears the victim of mismanagement more than earthquake. still, there are touching moments as the devout practice their faith and you can feel the reverence afforded to this temple.

awful feeling to smell the death, i was unable to clean my nose and lips for long hours. i understand them and respect but as a european i was unwell when i saw / smelled this. my stomach was upside down. otherwise it's a nice holy place.

one of the most important holy temple for hindus. you will feel like being in a real lord shivas place. its a great place to be and also symbolize nepals national glory. you can find hindu funeral which is done with hindu rituals. its simply a place where you should be when you are in kathmandu. one of the most valuable asset of nepal.

must stop by here when you're in kathmandu. foreigners may be asked for 1000 rupee entrance, depending on how noticeably 'foreign' you are. many people travel here (some by foot from india) for pilgrimage; often, deceased are cremated here. can do a quick walk-through or sit for awhile with a book or people-watch.
every time i visit, i am amazed by this "living" temple; people are using the site for daily life all the time. the juxtaposing of life and death rituals side by side is a must see.
we visit this place at morning. it was crowded. this old temple has great historic & religious value to hindu religion.

oh! there is new things face to face i see. openly dead bodies burnt around the perimeter of temple. while other pilgrimage busy to worship. it is expensive for ticket, 1000 rupees. sense of hindu books and history are reflecting in the live.

a real insight into the hindu religion and their customs after death, hiring a good official guide is well worthwhile as he can explain what is going on and take you to areas you think you can't go to if you are not hindu,

fascinating place to visit. beware of the presence of a guide who suddenly offers to help you. our guide was brilliant and very knowledgeable but foolishly we did not negotiate a price first! despite this i would recommend a guide to learn about the place and its history. we felt that it was slightly voyeruristic to watch families say farewell to their love ones and then put them on a wooden pyre to burn but our guide said this was not a problem.

my wife and i recently visited this hindu site with our himalayan social journey guide and 4 or 5 other westerners. this was a site where cremations are carried out by the river each day. we watched as funeral pyres were constructed out of wood and butter and then saw a number of bodies being cremated on their journey into the next life. we also saw a number of ambulances bringing dead bodies to the site for cremation. while at first i thought that this site might be confronting i was intrigued and surprised to find that i thoroughly enjoyed it. this might sound macabre but i was impressed by how open the whole experience of death was for these hindus. this is one thing in the west that we try and hide away from our minds, each other and our kids and it was so refreshing to have it out there for everyone to see. i guess i felt the same about a number of things with respect to nepal and kathmandu. our world in the west is sanatized in many ways. a great example for me was smell. on my return to australia and then heading back to work on my first day i tried to take notice of the smells that i encountered on the way. it was all so sanitized and i got a whiff of my deoderant and really not much more. this was totally different in nepal as you are constantly bombarded with the amazing smells of life including the good ones like food and wood fires of many types and some of the not so good ones such as burning bodies, dung and decay but which when added to the good ones make up life. so come and visit this hindu site to open your mind and visit nepal to re-awaken your senses.

many temples, ghats for cremation on bagmati river, try to be aware of ceremonies and being respectful, gratuities for photos of people expected, remember, nepal is a poor country, plenty of monkeys and cows

this is the site on the outskirts of kathmandu, by the river, where human creations occur on a daily basis. while somewhat sobering it is interesting to see the death rites of another religion and culture.

we walked a cross to pashupatinath temple from thamel on a bright morning and paid for our tickets and walked into the grounds. there were hundreds of hindus going into the temple but as non hindus you cannot enter. so we walked the grounds and then went across to the opposite bank to view the cremations as a number were taking place. it is a solemn experience and fascinating to watch the rituals of the families. there are also lots of sadhus (holy men) who will pose for pictures in exchange for cash and watch out around the entrance as there are lots of 'guides' who will take you around for over inflated prices!
every time when i am here in this place makes me to forget what am i. just sit and feel the place sound of mantra and chanting of bells

this was my second visit to pashupatinath and i came with some reluctance. on the afternoon of this visit, nine cremations were going on so you could watch the funeral rites at different stages. this is not a place to come if you are squeamish or have recently had a death in the family that you want to avoid being reminded of. on the other hand, the practical steps that families make to send their loved ones off, the swiftness of the process, and the sweeping into the river of any remaining ashes has its own reassurance. as on my first visit, i spent most of my time on the hill on the other side of the river from the main shiva shrine. there are lots of charming small temples dotted among trees. teams of children and young people come to practice martial arts. there are friendly dogs. be careful with the monkeys, which are used to being fed by visitors and can be pushy. on this visit, there were fewer sadhus (holy men) asking for photos in exchange for money.

if you have seen the movie 6th sense visit this place and understand why creation is important in our life. this place is full of everything this, happiness, sadness, spiritual and more, visit this place to understand life better
we spent half the walking amongst the buildings that had an interesting insite of the people of nepal.
for religious travellrs ; its a must to visit place . from the cultural point of view, you will know that the old time rituals and traditions
this is one of the most important hindu places of nepal and gives you an intruiging insight of the religion and customs.

i do not think ,i will.not see anything like this for the rest of life. it waa totaly weird but good experience. i shared all this ceremony - burning dead boddies - and leaves the ashes to the rivers and praying after them. the monk was so interesting . i think paspupatinath temple is the first place in nepal for visiting.

pashupatinath is a revered deity and a very important temple which every devotee of lord shiva seeks or aspires to visit and worship. this temple admits only hindus and has large parking areas on all sides. after walking through sprawling walk ways, one enters the main gate where celestial bull, nandi's backside is seen prominently from outside. once inside, there are serpentine queues after which one enters crowded and lowly lit passages around the main hall which is even smaller. place around has dozens of smaller temples but all around monkeys scare you. so do crowds jostling, burning offerings to lamps. all in all, one feels such an important temple must be kept cleaner and flow of visitors directed if not controlled better.
the steps of the temple area along the river were an amazing display of ritual, tradition and local culture.
this is a really fascinating temple on the banks of the river. very much worth a wander round, very much worth having a guide, it's a really interesting insight.
if your vist day was the day ,have a chance to see and understand the ceremony of death people preparation.

to start with pashupatinath is lord shivas temple. itz huge and definitely a place to visit when in kathmandu. the temple itself is beautiful. lots of worshippers n preachers. no cameras allowed in the temple. but the outside compound is also very beautiful. itz one off the best temple i have visited.

i have visited pashupati nath temple, it is really amazing, inside the big temple area there are many many others gods, i saw main shiva god inside of temple from all doors in my visit, i feel peace of mind after darshan.

it has nice surrounding, with monkeys, goats and cows. but some people seems to take advantages by using children to beg. so be prepared to be surrounding by beggars in many forms, youngsters and olds, yogis and all.

visiting pashupatinath and its cremation part provides an insight into the most difficult time of our lives. you can see the whole process from the preparation of the bodies until their complete burning. it's a very spiritual place and it's better to be ready for what you'll see. worth to hire a local guide to show you around and explain what is happening. the entrance fee is 1000rs. from pashupatinath you can walk up to bodnath stupa.

this is a beautiful and famous temple of lord shiva. you can find the pigeons (a lot of them) flying around the area of this temple which gives an even great view to the place. in this temple nobody is allowed to touch the linga of lord shiva as it is believed that anything that touches the linga will turn into gold (a myth!). anyways a very peaceful and soulful place to be in kathmandu.
darshan at pashupatinath temple is like dream come true. it is best temple. truly blessed after darshan of lord shiva here!
place where you will get peace of mind and .pashupatinath temple is must visit for hindu divotees.

being an indian,i had heard a lot about this place,and this was the first place i visited when i was in kathmandu.went there around 4 pm,what hit me was the melancholy with the burning bodies and mourning families.photography is prohibited,and the main shrine was closed.apparently they only open it once a fortnight.the crowd depends on what time to visit,and also the days of the week make sure you visit the adjoining hilly area where you can enjoy some nature sans the smell of death.

a short ride from thamel district (on the road to the airport) interesting buildings and to see the cremations along one side of the river and the families sitting eating and offering prayers and gifts on the anniversary of their dead.

i'm not sure how to rate this. i was unprepared what i was going to see even though i had read about it. it has left an imprint in my mind, the wrapped up bodies, grieving families, the ash in the air, the garbage, the heavy heavy feeling of death. this is how i felt as a foreigner and a non hindu. i would never take children. i paid the entrance fee, but left after about 10 mins. i won't forget this place. my rating says average but i need a whole different category.

this is a very popular hindu shrine and worth visiting if you are in kathmandu. in the morning there are lot of rush but if you visit very early in the morning or around noon the crowd is less. i visited the place at 12:30 and there was no crowd. photography is not allowed inside the main temple. there is a cremation place on the bank of the river which is visible once you are inside the main temple premises.

reach there early - it will be really clean, no crowd, prayer can be offered in peace. the main temple opens at 8 but by the time it opens, other smaller temples can be visited. also don't fall into the trap of numerous shops trying to sell you puja material at a very steep price, especially fake rudraksh beads.
i visited here during the teej festival and it gave the temple a special atmosphere. please don't take photos of the cremations it's disrespectful!
non hindus are not allowed and had to pay 1000 npr just to see main temple, how ever the cremation part was something to see.

"pashupatinath temple", a unesco appointed "world heritage site, is an absolurely must see in kathmandu. as the holiest shrine dedicated to lord shiva in nepal, the main temple is not opened to non-hinduists. visitors are allowed only in the outer areas. the atmosphere of death is present at almost everywhere. on the "ghats" of bagmati river (a tributary to the ganges river), cremation rituals are performed continuosly. ailing hinduists come to die here, living their last days on the temple's premisses. misteries and faith! moments of silence, meditation and respect. be aware of monkeys (they are all over - keep your belongings safely secured, as they are not at all reliable), fake saddhus (the "ascetic yogis" always ready for "paid" pictures with western tourists) and street vendors (free souvenirs do not exist in anywhere in the world). i visited kathmandu last february, nearly two and a half months before the terrible earthquake that devastated nepal on april 25, 2015. as a lord shiva miracle, "pashupatinath temple" has suffered only minor damages and is normally opened for visitors. enjoy!
an interesting insight into funeral rituals of the hindus, you definately need a guide. really interesting and a must see.
pashupatinath temple in kathmandu is a hindu temple. the major siva temple in nepal.a must for hindu, but not compulsary for others religion.
i only visited at teej, but it is a great location for seeing and experiencing festivals like teej, when women gather to celebrate and dance and the entire place becomes a beautiful sea of red saris!

apart from religious value, if you're a photographer then this might be your spot. you can take slow shutter snaps of pigeons, different geometrical structure all around the temple, different types of people with different color can give you better portrait too, blur/movement images also comparatively easier to get here. and yes, there's monkeys too! just be aware as there are many clever guys roaming around who can impose you to buy something, talks with you nicely, give something like gift then demands for money, offers sight seeing guidance and may ask higher price. sorry to say, i got this situation in a hot and full of crowd day. i dodged, bet you can too. ;-)

most quite, calm and high energy place which i ever visit. all time when i'm in nepal i'm going to that temple. just to think, relax. may be kind of meditate. there two parts which is divide by bagamati river. one part where is shiva temple - if you are not hindu, you anyway can't go inside, and place for cremation. where is very hard to be. and another part with stupas. bright side. it's like hell and paradise. full power! i can recommend to go there at night with many reasons: 1. there no people, no tourists, no sellers, no fake-sadhu. just almost nobody. dogs, monkeys and may some cremations. you can seat and think about eternally. 2. at night time visit of pashupatinath is free )) enjoy!
one of the most eye opening experiences, dispalying the futility of life and efforts. this will change you.

pashupatinath temple is oldest shiva temple worshiped by hindus. it is situated in such a on the bank of bhagmati river that, the temple didn't get damaged during recent earthquake in nepal. the shivling has 4 faces and can be seen from all the four sides. hindu rituals are performed at the temple and at the courtyard. it has beautiful architecture. worth visiting during trip to nepal.

pashupatinath temple is very famous among hindus, where hindus come and working shiva in pashupatinath temple. behind the temple where you can see bagmathi river and where before burning the bodies, the dead person is dipped in the river then taken cremation. during monday's a big ceremony done behind the temple where various priests handle lights in various styles. it is worth watching. inside the temple, watch out the monkeys. while we were there, we watched a monkey snatching a women's purse and taking out the phone and dropping others on the floor and climbing on top and holding the phone on the mouth and trying to catch things thrown by others expecting the monkey to drop the phone. people making calls to the same phone and the phone ringing, but the same money did not even drop the phone. so, be careful with your belongings.

located on the bank of bagmati river on the eastern outskirts of kathmandu. the temple is dedicated to god shiva and is important for hindu and buddhist cremations. their last journey traveling the bagmati river which meets up with the ganges river. the smell of decaying flesh mixed with spices and reeds is overwhelming (wear a mask). colourful sadhus wander around - it is worthwhile tipping them to take a photo. they are there trying to acquire liberation from the cycle of death and rebirth by meditating. a definite visit.

huge temple grounds and lots to see, like funeral ceremonies and people living on the temple grounds. definitely worth a visit but a bit pricey, 1000rupees per person. also, rubbish everywhere doesn't make it very appealing.

one of the dhams to sure visit. its really huge and very beautiful. the car parking is very near to the temple it was sad they did not allow even a car with handicap person in but anyways to my luck there was not much rush the day i went so i took my automatic wheelchair from parking point to the temple. had very nice darshan. if i could walk would have gone around the whole temple surroundings which had fantastic architecture but they did not have any ramps so it was not possible. but yes they give preference to handicap people the pandit and all have a way to make us do the fast darshan.

the holy & historic place where you can feel the last truth of your life as me tweeted after visiting there"yesterday i went to pashupati for funeral & came back ,thought on mind someday i ll go & only others ll return back.". but its really beautiful with all pagoda style lord shiva temple & more scenic view of river bagmati & shelshmantantak jungle.

this great historic site (and many others in kathmandu) are actually hostile to foreign visitors. an expensive fee is charged, but foreigners are not allowed to enter the temple premises. atheists, agnostics, buddhists, christians, muslims, and other nepali and indian nationals are welcome. but not devout hindus from other countries. it is a shame that hindus from other countries are banned from a hindu temple.

if you interst local culture and being familiar to funeral of indian people; this is the best palace for this purposes.there are many funeral services in this temple. they wash the body of dead person, by water of baghmathy river ( as a holy river )and then burn it totally. non indian people only can see funeral and different small temples in front of pashupatinath temple and they were not permitted to go inside the temple.

if you love taking photos, love quirky stuff (like varanasi india but on a much much smaller scale). you can see cremations, photos are allowed. you can wonder around and just sit and watch, there is so much to see if you do this. take a stroll up the steps to some lovely temples, loads of monkeys around. easy to get there via green sahja yatayat bus about 20 rupees. well worth the trip, entry is 1000rs which is steep but as i like this sort of thing it was ok to pay this amount.

this temple holds a special place for its religious value for all hindus. the temple is situated just 5-6 km from kathmandu airport and is a part of a modest compound. you can easily walk down thru the lanes to reach the main temple witnessing the last rites and rituals being performed across the river running beside the temple. we went to this place in december of 2014 and it was a nice sunny day.the temple compound is well manged and clean. there are a lot of monkeys (really a lot many!) inside the main temple area. but they do no harm.:) you can hire a temple guide who explains the history and significance of this place and the rituals. the one we found was quite reasonable and we felt much more aware and contended after he showed us around the temple. overall we had very good darshan and felt accomplished!

beautiful temples and old temple grounds. main temple cannot be entered by non-hindus. the remaining areas were filled with history and culture, everything was outdoors. there are many smaller temples to see and live hindu cremations were going on while i was there near the stream/river. there are sadhu's (hindu holy men) asking for donations to cleanse your spirit and other sadhu-types offering astrological charts or readings for a fee. there are monkeys roaming around and a lot of tourists and religious visitors. it was worth the trip to see it all.

this must be mind-blowing for us, westerners, and we might just be like oooo they are burning bodies non-stop and its so filthy and smells and weird. but in fact its a holy place, people from all over nepal and india come here to spend the last days of their ilves and meet death. very very interesting and amazing!

this is a must see site, it is a very large site with numerous temples. a guide is suggested, as the history and explanations for the temples, customs, etc. was very worthwhile and didn't cost much. we did see cremations taking place which was mysterious and creepy while at the same time awe inspiring. this is a unique and special place.

one of the most sacred , historic protected monument which is absolutely blissful , neat and clean temple in kathmandu. hats off to the maintenance team for maintaining this wonderful temple, i went there on 25th august and due to it's overall attraction i was forced to comeback on 2nd september before boarding my flight to chennai via delhi. situated in the heart of kathmandu and easily accessible from all parts of the city. must visit for all age groups when you are in kathmandu. when we sit in silence after doing all the formalities, we will be absolutely peaceful and feeling relaxed. the whole atmosphere is full of positive vibrations and bagmathi river adds to the beauty of this glorious place. thanks a lot for everything, loving you so much, peacefully yours. s m bega raaj. happy travelling. 🚗 ❤ 💘 ❤ 🚗.

the home of lord shiva and centrally located in kathmandu photos are strictly forbidden so dont try to sneak any this is a place of worship to be respected - i visited prior to the earthquake in april

pashupatinath is one of the 12 joytrilinga of lord shiva ( i hope i am correct). i have visited the temple before the earthquake. outside of the temple you will find verity of "rudraksha" and it is believe that one can find original ones here, provided you know how to identify. one advice is that if you don't know anything then don't purchase such things. thank you.

this temple has a wonderful history and is the main attraction for hindus. the place is vast and shoes removal is compulsory (as is customary in most temples) but sadly the place is not clean. it is better to have a pair of disposable socks to be worn for the visit and then thrown away. preferably allow yourself 60-90 minutes to visit. not sure if i would like to visit again !
pashupatinath temple is the holiest shrines of nepali people and respected by both hindu and buddhist located by the bank of holy bagmati river. where in the world one can see the the ritual so live.

temples territory is surrounded by small and large statues of hindu gods and goddesses. the crematoria is also placed on the bank of holy bagmati river. the place which has both religious and cultural importance is a worth place to visit. along with the view of temple we can also have the view of the deads being burnt according to the hindu ritual. plenty of monkeys in and around the area make the trip amazing.

pashupatinath is the holiest temple for hindus. the main temple premises is reserved for hindus only so if you are a non-hindu, you can not enter the main temple premises but it does offer a lot. for hindu, early morning is a good time to visit and enjoy the religious feeling. for non-hindu, an hour before it gets dark may be the best time to witness the beauty of the temple from across the river, and then the aarati - lighting of lamps - in the early evening, something you shouldn't miss.
beautiful temple, with order in the apparently chaos around. best tiem is the evening aarti at 7pm.go a little earlier as it gets dark fast and might not be able to see the temple in detail.

i highly suggest going here. the crematoria here is something that's emotional that's hard to replicate in any other place hit the ancient stupas everywhere with the large number of pilgrims definitely make this a must see.
only hindus are allowed. remarkably old architecture. you can book pooja in a day advance only starting from ncr 1100. one must do
if you are visiting nepal, it's a must visit.you feel inner peace after darshan.if you can, attend evening aarti (evening prayers).
this is the best place of mine, spiritual, religious, and circle of life. i spend 2-3 hours at bagmati river bank, where you can see everything, beginning of life to end.
visited this temple two times during my stay. om namashivaya. very holy & divine place. gone into spirutiality.

a must visit when in kathmandu. the place is charged with energy as you are surrounded by all the five elements - earth - fire - water - sky - air. the burning ghats bring you face-to-face with mortality and yet there is a serene and spiritual feeling the moment you enter the complex.
all hindu must visit this temple at least on time of there life time. lots of visitor comes from whole nepal and india.

it was incredible to see the history and the ongoing cremations of the dead. however it costs 1000 rupees to enter and they do not allow into the main temple. on top of this, people harass you and try to sell you merchandise.
an interesting place to visit - beware the self-appointed guide who told me that 2000rp was not enough at the end.
a major part of this temple is a burning pyre for humans. while foreigners are excited at the prospect of clicking pictures, pl. understand the sentiments of locals who come here.
the building was beautiful. the funeral just beside the river, so you can see many people come to the funeral and burn the dead body . it was very interesting.
there is no question of dislikes. just be there, its something like when you are hungry you want to eat, so its all about your spertural hunger.
this temple is very old and has mythological importance. for the benefit of devotees from various parts of the world there need to be more cleanliness and more signage for guidance.
a place to check your relations with death - would be certainly a shock for european traveller - must see and if possible meditate there

i was very disappointed in this site. it's the most expensive ticket in kathmandu at a whopping rs1000. however, there really isn't that much to see. perhaps you just go for the atmosphere. there are many, many mini temples all over the grounds, some up very steep stairs, some in amongst tranquil gardens, some along the river bank, but they all look very similar and after a short time i had a sense of "seen one, seen 'em all". down by the river side, you may catch a funeral taking place and it's best not to photograph this activity. sitting around the temples are holy men in full make up and 'costume', but rather cynically, it appears they really are there just to have their photos taken with tourists and charge for the pleasure. i can't imagine the archbishop of canterbury sitting outside westminster abbey in his full regalia charging for his photo. alas, the most interesting part of the complex is not open to tourists or non-hindus. a massive, resplendent, golden temple structure sits separately and only believers are permitted to enter. even if you somehow convince you are a follower, you still are not permitted any photography inside. you can however climb above the compound, up some steep stairs and you can then get a few snaps inside the grounds, but the view is still restricted. for the price of this complex, i was disappointed. there are far better and far cheaper options in kathmandu.

on my way to kailash manasarover yatra i had a two day stay in kathmandu.i visited the pashupatinath temple.the temple was erected in the 15 century by lichhavi king.the richly ornamented pagoda houses the sacred linga or holy symbol of shiva.pashupatinath temple is located on both banks of bagmati river .the main temple of pashupatinath is a building with a bunk roof and a golden spire.it is a cubic construction with four main door ,all covered with silver sheets. only followers of hindusim can enter the main temple, but all the other bulding are available for foreigners to visit.along the right bank of bagmati river numerous platforms for funeral pyres are built.the cremations on these platforms are a common activity everyday.it is also very common to meet sadhus in pashupatinath temple.sadhu are wandering ascetic yogis, who are trying to acquire liberation from the cycle of death rebirth by meditating.it is belived that those who die in pashupatinath temple are reborn asa human,regardless of any misconduct that could worsen their karma.if you are attacted to the places where death can be felt,consider pashupatinath as your first destination.it is a temple with special atmosphere of death, death is present in almost every ritual and every corner of it. om namah shiva.

i loved seeing the evening aarti here in pashupatinath. it was one of the most beautiful i've seen in nepal/india and quite memorable. the architecture was fascinating and the campus spans far and providing lots of space to roam and explore. i also observed my first cremation ceremony here. so many small buildings and sculptural elements. very unique, a definite must see.

very holy place and must visit for all hindus every year. wash away your sins and thank god for all his help. avoid buying anything here as they will crook you. wear rubber slippers, do not carry any leather. catch hold of a nice old pundit who will show you around. tip him 100 nr at the end. avoid those elaborate 'poojas' as it is just a 'con'. no photography permitted inside. beware of the monkeys that snatch your prasad etc. spend a good one hour here. carry a lot of change to tip at all the prayer points. ( bundle of inr 10 notes fine). buy a basket of flowers and coconuts @ nr 100/- before entering.
beautiful temple! a must visit when travelling to kathmandu. a trip to kathmandu is incomplete without going to these beautiful temples.

an atmosphere you wont find anywhere else! so much going on- singing, drums, burning bodies- lots of pilgrims. this is a good place to see the law of nature and culture of the indian people in nepal. instead of the going to varanasi in india, this is a place to visit on a much smaller scale.
pashupatinath is god father of nepal. it is really a heart pleasing experience to me visit such a holy place. you will really found your self tense-less as the sound of bells will touch your soul.

the place of bholenath.everyone should visit this temple.very religious place for indians.you will feel really good if you visit this palace.it will relax your mind, body & soul.i am feeling lucky to visit this place.om namah shivay.har har mahadev.
assuming that it survived the earthquake this is a well worth while temple to spend some time viewing.
visited a temple after five years and i thank god mahadev for the darshan. powerful and inspiring if you believe in almighty.

construction started on this temple in the 7th century. it sits on the bagmati river in nepal and is one of the holiest sites in the hindu religion.the entire complex has 500+s other shrines and smaller temples. it is guarded by nepalese police. pashupathinath admits only hindus. it is encased with silver walls and has a gold pagoda roof the complex is a world heritage unesco site. gat's jut out into the bagmati river.these are used by a dedicated cast to cremate the dead. similar in fact to the use of the gats in city of benares on the ganges( india) the cremations are a sobering sight and the river looks polluted but this has been a center of the hindu faith for many centuries,as such will remain for many more.
this is one of the most religious destination for believers of hindu religion. the temple is situated on banks of the river. it makes you think several centuries behind

the first time i came here i was expecting a little bit more from this place. i heard it from a few travelers on what is done here, where families would come to burn the deceased members body and into the water. i visited with a friend and entered through the wrong entrance. we ended up watching the ceremony. the part i did not enjoy was seeing dozens of foreigners snapping photos of this from the distance. if it was my family i would hate people taking photos of this emotional time. entrance fee was 1000 nepali rupees too. quite high in my opinion. average price for an attraction in nepal for the entrance fee is usually 200-500 rupees
of course nice, like most of the temples around kathmandu, but the vibration here was not so high like in other places. it worth anyway to go.
loved visiting this hindu temple and took around 1,000 photos , watch out for monkeys and local tourist guide, it's only 4 kms away from the main town and taxi fare is around $250 rupees.
visiting nepal and not seeing this temple of lord shiva, impossible. very old and magnificent, but needs more maintenance.

the biggest temple of lord shiva in kathmandu. the architecture of temple is excellent and attractive. it is said that once in a year, the king of nepal comes for pooja and use the paras mani. that day, the temple is closed for common man.

this is like stepping into the most amazing assault on your senses ever. pay for a guide and let them tell you all about the place. watch the bodies burning by the riverside, the kids swimming and diving to collect gold from the teeth of the burned corpses, the colorful sādhu sit and smoke marijuana, (sanskrit: साधु sādhu, "good; good man, holy man") is a religious ascetic or holy person. have a picture taken with them and donate to the pour. walk to the shopping area and bye some religious paraphernalia. if you do one thing in kathmandu do this!

one of the greatest religious place for hindus, visiting pashupatinath is considered equivalent to visiting kedarnath & you can stay in dharamshala , it is govt. subsidized & walking distance from main temple.
personally i can't get my head around cremation ghats as a tourist attraction . . . . if you are happy with that, then it's a fascinating place

our first full day in kathmandu, we visited this temple and it was a great way to learn how the people of nepal live their lives around what happens with the temple and their gods. i liked being able to see how they ritually went through the cremation of the dead and how each person handled it. this temple received some damage from the earthquake but not as much as other places. however, i did not like that there were locals every where in the temple who had set up little places to sell things and were constantly going up to tourists trying to sell their items. it just didn't seem very sacred to me. but it could just be how their culture operates.

this is the most holiest site in nepal. this was the place where lord shiva took incarnation of an animal (all that anyways you can wiki around). this temple only allows hindus to visit, don't understand why the restriction. it is very peaceful inside and really beautiful, the roof was made of gold they say now not sure but surely the color is golden still :). apart from the main temple there are many other temples also and you can also directly go to the bagmati river from the temple itself. non-hindus are allowed to visit temples on the other side of bagmati you can see pashupati standing in all her/his glory. should go there at least once if you are in kathmandu.

this is the most important hindu temple site in nepal and is also a unesco world heritage site. many fascinating things to see and well worth a visit. many smaller shrines suffered considerable damage in the big earthquakes. i don't know how long it will take to repair things, but this is a unique place in the world.
i love this place, lots of people were word shipping lord shiva.and hundreds of other temples in pasupatinath temple

this is place of our history which proof that how we love god but these can b visit by only hindus. so if you are not then please be careful don't tell your real names tell other names this is just of their privacy but i must recommend that please visit it & blessed by th god.but please don't take photos inside the temple other wise the people & police of their will beat you so don't do. but please gooooo
felt extremely sad for people who were stuck between rituals and beliefs. the sight of corpses lined up for wash and funeral was very sad

i really refresh the environment of pashupati temple.pashupati temple is one of the great hindu temples. many artful structure of the temple.i really refresh when i went to the pray time in pashupati temple. and i went to the mrigasthali and brindaban.many visitors are visiting thmple.i really impress.when i will come back in nepal again i will go again the pashupatinath temple.

this is one of the main temple of nepal. whenever i visit the place, i feel free from stress and any type of anxiety. there is something in this place which brings peace in my heart and the sorrounding here is very peaceful and beautiful however the place is sometimes over crowded during the festivals.

this temple was really great to explore! it was fun because they also had all the wild life. heres a few tips for non local travelers 1.) beware the monkeys! :they are not nice and will steal things;clothes, snacks and gagets 2.) fake religios people : we went with locals who knew all the ins and out and there would be people at the temples who pretended to be things they weren't. they would dress up in very cool traditional (looking) outfits and offer pictures but then would want money. it is a scam . 3.) cremations :being a temple they do have real ceremonies and such there including funerals where they would burn the body's in the open then dust the ash off into the river. so watch out for smokey areas cause your probably breathing burnt flesh and to be respectful and not intrude / take pictures of these events because it is someones family member. but its truly amazing to experience this place the list off good is just way to long to rite out :)

i wasn't sufficiently prepared to visit a river with funeral pyres on either side. every culture does things differently, but here i felt a bit of an intruder. my local friend assured me that it was ok, but i was not comfortable seeing people in distress. there were several several holy men willing to pose for photos.

i have been to pashupatinath temple twice in my recent visits to nepal, but unfortunately i have not been able to actually offer my prayers to lord shiva as the main temple was closed on both occasions. it is one of the few major hindu shrine temples where non-hindus are not allowed. the 1100 shiva lingas are a major attraction and you get peach doing puja to hanumanji. i'd like to go again at lord pashupatinath temple. a must visit for all nepal visitors.

this is one of the most sacred places i have ever visited, i was lucky to be able to enter the premises on a sacred day. however very crowded, it was beautiful to see all the women in red, fasting, dancing and singing for their husbands. it is a place of fertility worshiping. it has opened my mind for traditions that are unconceivable on the western world.
a place not affected by the agonies of nature, robust and traditional. if its upkeep could be guaranteed many more visitors could come

if you know and understand hindu culture then you may appreciate the grand pashupatinath temple of nepal. the river beside the temple and cremation ghats remind you of varanasi. tourists from other faith may find it amusing and disturbing. go there with an open mind.
pasupatinath dedicated to lord shiva.photography is not allowed inside the main shrine.must visit temple.

most tourists getting into kathmandu, will make a visit to this temple considered a very holy shrine. the temple is built centuries ago and is evident from the structure of the building. this temple has restrictions like no photography allowed of the main shiva temple where the shivling is. more so, you will need to keep your footwear at the designated place and walk to the main temple area barefeet. in case of rainy days, this becomes a little messy. the holliness of the shrine has been well accepted by hindus over the years. the positive vibrations of the place is well evident and you will find it quite tranquil in here. after the massive earthquake this year, the temple structure has stood still even after 380+ aftershocks! that is quite a marvel. but on the flip side, which is also normal, is that tourism has been hit in this country and my last visit there hardly saw much visitors from abroad.
the most holy and spiritual place. glade to visit it in my life time. the environment and the spirituality wondered me.
we were approached by a guide and -- for once -- took him up on the offer of a tour. we were glad we did. there is so much here that we would not have understood on our own.

for.hindus .a great place to visit.but remember u cannot offer prayers directly to sacred shiv ling.no photography inside temple is allowed.rest i would recommend to take darshan from left side as it will give much less crowd ,although we visited on saavan first monday.and it was jam packed.cleanliness is an issue but historical importance overwhelms it.
we absolutely enjoyed visiting the pashupatinath temple of lord shiva. an immensely satisfying and peaceful experience!
it bring us to know ow nepali after live, visit and try to see something different what your experience in your country

very good temple, its a heritage temple of lord shiva. people from around the world visits this temple every year. it is very near to the nepal airport. even one can walk from airport to temple (around 10 mins walk).

pashupatinath tampel was relations with hindus people ,nepal india and other country.we can saw old imoprtant shiva stachhu ,monkey bagmati river phavcha dival shiva we can learn to hindus culture , saddhu ,hindus death culture ,watch near foriest ect.
this was an interesting place in jan 2015, i hope this fountain survived, as it shows so much of the old city .

we visited this famous temple upon completion of our ebc trek in nov 2014. the complex is huge and clean unlike other religious places. photography is prohibited beyond the entry gate of main temple. the typical pagoda style hindu architecture attracted me. the unique lingam with face deity is hugely popular. i don't know how but i recognised the place where i was laid down as a child of 15months [ mother told i was suffering from high fever and suddenly got cured after visiting the place] when my parents visited the temple 28 yrs back.

one of a few temples where entries are restricted to hinduism only. situated at outskirts of kathmandu it is one of the most important temple of lord shiva and believed to offer moksha to people who die here and hence attracts old age hindus from across nepal and india too. the river bagmati flows at the side of the main temple and is an active hindu cremation place. most of the priest are from south india. apart from religion i found it a great piece of ancient hindu architecture. the main temple & the nandi outside are crafted very nicely and indicates highly developed architecture of those period. apart from stone crafting the wood work at the temple is excellent. the silver, copper and gold crafting too adds to the beauty and indicates to wealthy temples of medieval days. a nice place to be religiously or beyond. for non hindus too the site offers good insight to history & architecture and they can spend an hour or two outside the main temple. the market outside offers handicrafts but i suggest darbar square as a much better place to shop these article.

like most hindu temples this one has the usual grime, cattle, holy men and cremations but easily pashupatinath is the standout for its rich history and the fact that it is among the most revered holy hindu spots on earth-

after powerful desaster , mostly old city was fallen down but i wonder how is possible 100 of years old temple standing without any damage . i start to believe more on some hidden power . you must visit once this amazing place .

you should go if you are in kathmandu - no doubt. the best place to see it is from across the " river" (very little water flowing). sit in the steps there and enjoy the place. you have a full view of the temple, and of the burning sites at your left. a place to reflect on life and death.

as you walk down pushupatinath temple there are so many different smells, sounds and sights. a place where monkeys and cows roam freely and families say goodbye to their loved ones. i felt very humbled and emotional watching people carry their loved ones to the pyre. i got quite angry when i saw some tourist filming and taking pictures, i thought it was quite disrespectful! i would recommend you visit but please respect the dead and their families by refraing from tweeting/facebooking/instagraming pictures of the funeral site.
watch as families say their final goodbye to their loved one. what a cultural difference from the west. admire all the little details on the stone carvings, just marvelous!
