kompleks swiatynny, warto , w silver temple old men house full of smile quiet old people,,,,alsko one houre for pleasaure and photos.also crematotium , hindu temple, beggars /over 100 / ,special place.very special

as a solo female western traveller i was glad i hired a guide there, a young university student took me around for an hour and i paid him 1000npr. i would not have seen or understood half as much without him. money well spent! the crematoria ghats are fascinating. non hindu's are not allowed in the temple. be prepared to pay to have a tikka administered or to have a photo taken with sadhus. many beggars which can be intimidating.

it is arguably the most important temple in the kathmandu. read up on the net before you go, so it all makes more sense. we didn't see the crematoria. but the temple inside is very pretty and very intricately carved. and there are a lot of sadhus around - and none asked for payment for pictures! photography is not allowed within the temple complex, but you can carry your camera in and keep it with you. just be careful where you step - it is the temple for the lord protector of cows. so there are a lot of cows. and hence cow dung. every where.
it was totally hindu temple but so much interesting to learn about hindu religion & their worship way, lots of place to visit, area is bit not so clean,
we visited on the last day of a major festival, so it was almost too crowded to move. there was so much to see, though, that i'm glad we didn't pass on this.

really don't get why non-hindus are not allowed, a bit discriminating i would say. however we could walk around the outside perimeter but did not enjoy seeing other tourists taking pictures of cremations. how would these people feel if strangers were taking pictures of treir granma's funeral?

whilst i appreciate the religious significance of the place, it's a total waste of time for non-hindu's. i was erroneously told that i could enter the temple but, as wih many other hindu temples, that wasn't the case. all i got was a fleeting view of nandi's backside. add the racist 1000 nr entrance for non-asians and their morbid fascination of intruding into others' grief (through disgracefully taking pictures of cremations) and you'll understand my contempt for the place. i absolutely hated it!

fascinating place, although the fact that non-hindus cannot visit the inside of the temple is unfortunate. the site is in need of care and upkeep which is surprising considering there is a 1000 rupee entrance fee, and while i willingly paid the various yogis to take photos of their colorful outfits, the place starts to feel like a bit like a run down tourist attraction as opposed to a religious site. still well worth the visit especially as a counterweight to the more sedate and elegant buddhist temples.

this is a very large hindu temple and unless you are a hindu, you would not be allowed to go into the temple. for europeans, there is a fee of rs. 1,000 per person and if you are from a saarc country, you will be allowed to go in free of charge. although the temple is out of bounds for non-hindus the rest of the premises houses a lot of things that might be of interest to you. this place has a lot of examples from classical hindu architecture and there is a bit of a climb to get to the top of this place as well. you will have to cross a bridge and the view here is extremely beautiful although the river itself is not that clean. however, you will not be able to enjoy much of that beauty because of the sellers who try to get your attention and practically stalk you! when you get to the top, you would be able to find a quiet corner and enjoy a bit of peace in the midst of small shrines which are scattered all over the place. if you go up here, you might want to be careful of the bulls and the monkeys.
it has a lot improvements there since last time, last time when i went there it wasnt maintained well but this time it was really appreciatable, and yes be careful of monkeys too

this was expensive in relative terms and you are hassled at the gate to take on a guide. if we had more time, i would have negotiated one, but we only had an hour or so. was interesting but beware of all the hawkers!

pashupatinath temple is one of the holiest temples in the hindu religion. flocks of devotees throng the temple and it is said sins are absolved after a visit. the pujas thaat are conducted here are full of rituals. the temple is adjacent to a river but it has not much scenic beauty.further only hindus are allowed into the premises. it is a must however for hindus visiting nepal.
when you are have different culture and beliefs this place is a bit shock. our guide well explained the place and crematorium area, i feel lucky that i don't have to see that.

pashupatinath is one of the biggest hindus temple in the world. the supreme holiness of the site stems from the shiva linga enshrined in its main temple. it expresses the very essence of hinduism as pilgrims, priests, devotes, temples, ashrams, images, inscriptions and cremation ghats intermingle with the rituals of daily life, all sprawled along the banks of the sacred bagmati river. the temple’s origins are obscure.

pay round ten euro. u allowed to watch from the other side. not allowed to cross the bridge to the temple. can be shocking burning people. i feel like outsider watching private things. but the feeling to experience this is very special. be respectfull with taking pictures.

very strong and magic experience, must see place in kathmandu.everytime i am comming back to see ,,little varanasi,,if you have more time, you can spend more time in this place and visit forest and places without any tourists.
make sure this is on you list of temples to visit. the hindu architecture at this location is fantastic.
very different experience for me personally. will remember it forever. don't forget to see the deity from all the entrances.
definitely my favourite temple to have visited! there was so much going on, and so much to explore. very much worth a visit

traveling is about getting up every day and seeing something you have never seen before.pashupatinath temple and bagmati river cremations is certainly in that category! i was surprised that it did not make me squeamish at all. it just seemed so natural, as if it happens everyday.which, of course, it does. it was just sooo interesting! of course there are monkeys and cows and the sadhu holy men are wonderful photography subjects,even if you do have to pay them. it was absolutely something i would not have wanted to miss, one of my kathmandu highlights. we did have our own guide with us, which helps make it that much more interesting, besides the fact that you have someone to answer your questions!

we paid the $10 entrance fee and gained a wealth of knowledge as our tour guide (who met us there) explained what we were seeing and answered our many questions about the culture and religion we were seeing in practice. we're not allowed inside the temple, but there's so much more than just a temple there. if you have an attitude of respect and are there to see the architecture and to better understand the people you are living among while in kathmandu, this is a necessary spot to more fully comprehend the practices of the people. i would also especially recommend this place to those who practice a religion other than hinduism. many conversations will arise from what your group sees and experiences there and it will give you a heart for the people.
you pay too much to see the temple and carving bot you are only allow to go next to the rive side to see the crimination. it was written on the gate that it is allow for hindus only.

a very holy place and therefor europeans are not really needed there. coming from an other country you have to pay 1000 rupies (pretty much) and then you are not allowed to enter the inner temple if you are not hindu.
the entrance fee of 1000 npr shocks but is it worth! 50-60 dead bodies get burned there daily. special atmosphere!

this is a very interesting location where you can witness the ritual cremations that are happening throughout the day. late afternoon as good as the lights comes through the drifting smoke. remember to respect the event that are happening her and watch the monkeys

the temple lies on the bank of the bagmati, the holy river (as per the hindu legendary book)s. we have to walk up few stairs to main temple from the river side, but this gate remains closed often, so the visitors should have to check in through another gate. this is the temple of lord shiva, one of the universe himself for the hindus,as this is the god of all the living things including the animals and human beings. open cremation can be seen close by the temple, where almost every moment we can see at least few dead body being cremated. to the opposite, cross the river we can see stone made shivalayas, each of them contain one shiva lingam . the mirror window is interesting. we can chat with the sahus (followers of shiva), and take photo graphs (of course, you need to pay, as they are the modern sadhus). most of the things are interesting beside, the bad smelling from the river, hassle of the local vendors and the high fee for the foreigners are not that acceptable for most of the visitorts

very crowdy place.we hardly could have darshan in minites. pushing and pulling the thiefs take undo adavantage. we lost uor money trvel papers. still shivaling is unik it having 4 mukhas an important jyoitirlinga.the only out side of india.the ghats on bagamati river is good but not clean. typical nepalian stracture

i visited pasupatinath on my way back to hotel in thamel after visiting bodnath. taxi costs around rs 200 from bodnath to pasupatinath, but you can also walk if you want to enjoy street scenery of kathamandu. my taxi driver offered to guide me around temple for free if i took his cab back to the hotel, but once i pay the rs 1000 entrance fee, one of the guide there told him to leave since only official guide is allowed. the guide insisted to use his service by telling me many spots inside the temple are holy and i need someone to guide my way through. well, i do not have a choice. inside, there's nothing much to see, no cremation, and only sadhu are the main attraction. plus point is the shelter for elderly accross the temple's main entrance, i was there to take some picture but end up talking with doctors from india who is doing voluntary work, really enlighting. btw, i end up paying rs 1000 to the guide :( so better negotiating the price upfront if you are followed by guide, and keep small rupee notes in your pocket.

this collection of temples is massive and impressive. as everything in kathmandu, it is a bit dirty and you will find stray dogs, monkeys and of course cows. i had never seen a cremation and found the whole ritual, from arrival of the dead person, washing, preparation and movement to the pyre fascinating. the entry fee is 1000 rupies, which is a lot for nepal. children don't pay.

this is a great temple and worth visiting, but the admission is pricey at rs 1000. since when you pay at the gate, a guide will follow you. the guide fee is not included in the ticket, but he never mention it. if you are not satisfied with your guidebook, it is not so bad to hire him, though.
it was written in the main gate that only hindu may enter.i jut look outside the temple.many monkeys were around.the river looks dirty.

one of the oldest temple. there house lots of small temple inside the premises. the best place one can get rudraksha. one should do the pooja. a u r allowed to do pooja inside the temple in front of the pashupatinath.
so many hindu people to visit pashupatinath temple.specially in shivaratri,many sadhus & pilgrimage tourists on que to visit temple.i heard every bhakta has to smoke to make happy for shiva.

i visited this temple at 1997 , i stayed with nepali family for 2 weeks and we visited pasupathinath temple twice. it was my first time to see how bodies are burned , i would not forget it. great experience but not for soft hearts :)
very much enjoyed visiting this priceless treasure. the architecture is beautiful and the sense of peace is inspirational.

if you are going to worship, then it is a typical hindu temple with all the good and bad that comes with us. as far as the structure goes they have some beautiful carvings, if you get time to stop and see them it is quite pretty.

a taxi dropped us off and we kind of "fell into" hiring a guide. no mention of fee, etc. in retrospect, however, i don't think i would have gotten much out of the place just wandering around. explanations were necessary. just negotiate up front on the price. it's worth the visit since you will see cremations going on, monkeys having a blast, shrines and statues, a very hindu site.

located on the banks of the bagmati river, the temple is just outside of boudha in the outskirts of kathmandu. one can see devout hindu pilgrims visiting the temple daily as well as funeral pyres of local and not so local hindus. the local sadhus or babas are very nice and interesting to talk to and will do their best to answer questions. the milk baba is especially friendly and world famous. shivaratri, the annual celebration of shiva is a crazy and jubilant event that is not to be missed if you are in the area. all in all a worthwhile place to visit, and one of the few major hindu temples that even allow non-hindus inside.
if you go with devotion - just that is enough. however like most hindu shrines - this could do with a lot better facilities management.

i had no idea what i was visiting.but our guide told us it was a cremating temple and showed us the cremating site (up to 45 a day) alongside a river that flows to the ganges.i could barely handle the smell of burning hair (and please no jokes about bbq).our guide (beware of probin (sp); he overcharges) showed us where the rich people are cremated (up stream) and then the boys looking for gold (yes, the gold teeth from the rich cremated).we crossed the bridge to view a series of lingam altars.thus the theme of life and death.
it was really a divine treat to visit pashupatinath temple. really felt some strong force inside the temple premises. do remember to visit parvati devi temple nearby.

it was the first thing we visited and it blew our minds. we witnessed a ceremony with an animal sacrifice, wondered around the temples and spent there a long time just watching the different ceremonies and rituals happening at the same time. you can take a bus from ratna park to pasupathinath temple. that enhaces the experience for sure!

this temple is very pricey because of the cremations. we were fortunate enough to see a rather fancy one, but after about 15 minutes the ceremony had ended and we took to wandering the site. the temple itself is underwhelming and probably not worth the visit. if you've seen hindu cremations before or are not interested, don't bother visiting.
excellent experience.not crowded but people should be allowed to stay inside a few more moments.little bit more cleanliness desirable. but in devsthan always such things happens. location divine.

this is a must visit for every person coming to kathmandu. the temple is huge & it takes a while to visit all the shrines inside the temple. beware of the shops close this temple as they will charge you quite high for normal stuff.

ashupatinath temple one of the most sacred hindu shrines in the world, pashupatinath lies 5 km east from the city center. the richly-ornamented pagoda houses the sacred linga, or phallic symbol, of lord shiva as well as the noteworthy gold plated roofs and silver coated doors. this is the abode of god shiva and is the holiest of all the shiva shrines.religious pilgrims and sadhus, like the one pictured here, travel all the way from the remote areas of india to visit this sacred sight, especially during shivaratri (the night of shiva) that falls between february/march. even though these devotees have denounced worldly possessions, each carries a sadhu id (identifications card) to freely cross over the border between india and nepal.chronicles indicate pashupatinath’s existence prior to 400 ad. devotees can be seen taking ritual dips in the holy bagmati river flowing beside the temple, also a world heritage site. the crematorium is just outside the temple and it is a dream of almost every hindu to be cremated by the side of pashupati aryaghat after their death.

took a friend who has never been to varanasi where we were able to watch the entire ritual , from family gathering , grieving and support , to the preparation of the funeral bier and the actual burning. the experience is a quieting one, making way internally for consideration of issues of life and death which was a gift, as it seems as though in the west we run from the calm consideration.

this is a very good temple of lord shiva. it is spread in huge area. apart from main temple, there are many small temples inside the campus. it is being told that bhim ( one of the pandavas in mahabharat) wanted to worship lord shiva and he was searching lord shiva in himalays. to take test of bhim's devotion and to hide from bhim, lord shiva hided himself in the look of a buffalo. but, bhim identified lord shiva even in the form of buffalo. then, lord shiva (in the form of buffalo) tried to hide himself by going beneath the ground(land). but, bhim caught tail of buffalo. the place where bhim caught the tail is presently known as kedarnath (one of chardhams of hindus) and shiv linga in kedarnath(india) is rear part of buffalo and the front part (face side) is in pashupatinath, kathmandu, nepal.the shiv linga in pashupatinath is having four faces of shiva on four sides of shiv linga(east, west,north and south). the devotee must perform darshana of all four faces. there are many brahmins available in temple . devotees can perform "pujavidhi" through them with nominal charge @ rs.311/- . overall it was very good experience.

located 30 minutes from the heart of kathmandu is this hindu spiritual temple(s) & cremation site. it can be very confronting for people who have not seen an open air cremation on a funeral pyre, let alone 10 in a row all burning simultaneously. it is sombre and reflective, yet also a fascinating step-by-step peak into the hindu culture and tradition of cremation and and celebration of life. as you walk through the grounds you are exposed to every aspect of this process and as unusual as it may sound, photography is welcome, common and in some cases encouraged. there are many iconic sadhu's (holy men) lounging in hidden nooks and prayer areas in the buildings surrounding the pyres, they come complete with their long dreadlocks & glazed eyes, particularly the younger ones. take a little time to do a thorough walk-thru if you're chasing a photograph of an older 'wise' sadhu, if you look closely and explore the myriad of mini temples and surrounding buildings, you are bound to see some extremely photogenic locals! these amazing characters won't talk but many will pose for photos and welcome the opportunity if a few rupees are on offer. (always show respect and ask first or point at your camera and nod, watching for their reaction) there is also a cornea depository bank just behind the main cremation area, it is the very last stop in a long list of processes and rituals. the spiritual river trickling through the grounds and separating it into two distinct sides is the iconic bagmati. this section flows very slowly during much of the year and with all of the activities on the banks and never-ending dumping of ashes, it is nothing short of putrid and foul. it remains however, a highly recommended spot to pay a visit to while in kathmandu. you will leave with a deep appreciation for the fascinating and ancient processes that occur in religions around the world. it is one of the most significant hindu temples of lord shiva in the world that is open to the public. (**note: a few specific areas are off limits to women and non-hindus- signage is posted**) allow for 2 hours. entry fee in effect. taxi from thamel = 25 minutes.

unusual site seeing experience. being able to watch photograph smell cremations. amazing photographic opportunities the seemingly infinite 15 votive shrines. photographed next to 108 year old sadhu with his five foot dredd locks rapped round my neck. down side tourists getting to close to the cremations they should show more respect.

i like the way they cremate the body on the river bank and it's open cremation is highlight of this place. there are many platform for higher cast and to lower cast people too. i disliked the things that they have not keeped the place clean even they charge $7 per person entrance fee, so i would say they should keep everything need and clean with that money.
temple and surrounding are dirty. i do not know why temples are kept that way! i am a hindu too. i do not mean to offend anyone in nepal.
the entrance fee is 500 rupees.it really worth the money.you'd better there in the evening if you want to have a glimpse of the death ceremony.don't worry about the smell, it is not smelly at all.
very interesting hindu site with temples and river. location is close to the airport must visiting when in ktm

me and my wife visited the temple in the morning,already a lot of devotees there,at the entrance u can see lots of pigeons being fed also number of sadhus.hindus alone can enter the temple at the entrance a big nandhi statue (bull)and a small nandhi statue ,made of some yellow metal the main deity has four faced siva,there r 4 entrances.there r some other deities around.don't miss 365 lingams,local people worship pne lingam eveyday ,they say the hindu creamation site is also near by.if u r lucky u get a rudracha garland.u will experience lot of inner peace.it is near katmandu .so u can visit easily

this is a very surreal experience. it is difficult to process the experience in western norms. american grieving process is very antiseptic. this is (in our experience) a very strange process. the river where ashes are distributed is considered holy, but to the observer is horribly polluted, complete with trash, livestock wandering and dogs. bodies are cremated on pyres located above the river and ashes spread into the river below. families of the deceased gather with the body next to the river prior to cremation. there is a building located next to the temple that functions like what we would consider a hospice. when a person is deemed terminal and near death, they are brought to this place to die at a holy site. it is a place to be respected and one needs to be cognizant of this and act accordingly. you will be set upon by very aggressive folks trying to sell items on the way in and out. they will follow you relentlessly until you finally reach your ride to leave. i would recommend this to anyone visiting kathmandu, but be prepared for an experience the likes of which you have never seen before.

things are quiet organised, one can even book different poojas a day in advance. suggest you to take a guide , their knowledge is good and they are helpful and not expensive. foreigners are not allowed in so no point wasting money there, suggest a little hill lock next to the temple for a view.

entering pashupatinath is an inspiring experience. it is a sacred place, where cremations take place. you can just feel the atmosphere and looking at the ceremonies is an amazing experience. i would like to point out that you are looking at funerals, so be respectful of people, i think you do not want that people come and go, and take pictures of your dears' funeral.

the most powerful temple for the hindus across the world. this site brings tourists from all over the globe due to its stories & also the work of art couple with the archeological value. its a world heritage site.
we had a nice darshan of lord shiva in the great temple. when entering the temple by the river side, we could see some volunteers cleaning up the river and the banks.
pashupatinath temple is a unforgetable place in kathmandu. there, the nepali culture shows its strong aspects, in all senses. if you go to kathmandu you have to go there.
interesting to see but hard to find that it was so dirty even bearing in mind that this was one of the most sacred places.

some interesting stupas and ancient hindu sexual relief artwork to be seen. also this is where local people have cremation ceremonies for their deceased. i felt like a douche watching this as a tourist, but it appears as though it is a common place for lots of people (locals and tourists) to hang out. the mood was not somber or formal. lots of people, beware pickpockets. also watch out the fake holy men who will try and charge you to take a picture
its wonderful visiting, simple and no hangama around temple. te moment you enter temple premises guides will surround you, no need of guide. you visit yourself.

we went to discover this holy hindu temple with its historical details but not allow to enter as it's reserve for hindu unfortunately. instead, we were assigned to watch a funeral service of hindu rites at the opposite stand. though we never seen one before which could 'excite' ones, it should be a private affair and respect the departed , not expose to the public. worse still we have to pay double for entrance fee (was up after its new year day). this is like stealing from the dead and am sure they can rest in peace!
the most important divine place in kathmandu. entry fee is high (nrs 1,000/-) but worthy place to see the hindu traditions in practice in a single place. sadly, non-hindus are not permitted here.

it was a rip-off at 250 nrs. i don't mind supporting tourist attractions, but there is a limit. everybody else walked in for free. they stopped us (westerners) to extract an entrance fee (apparently needed to pay for all the fee collectors). the site has not benefited noticeably from the fees and generally offers little to see.
for one i have visited many temples and this by far was one of the cleanest. only hindus allowed in the main temple, for the blessings but the area around is very nice. great architecture.

go with plenty of time, it is a temple complex and crematorium. i sat for hours and watched a single funeral progress from washing and mourning to enshrouding to being set on the pyre and burning. probably not for everyone. for me this was a rare glimpse into a very important ceremony. highlight of my trip.

it is just amazing to be able to see history as such still standing. the artistry and the architecture of the buildings are awe-inspiring. the bathing area was pretty cool and you could go in too! you get a feel of the culture and that you are standing in a historical place. definitely worth the visit.
it was a great to be experiencing the different culture in this part of the world, the place is dusty but still good for a day's trip.
take your time to walk around and get the feeling of the place. i can highly recommend to hire a guide for a few hours since there is many things happening in the temple area.

we visited the temple in kathmandu prior to departing for our khumbu trek in may 2013. i had read about it before coming and was very curious to see what it was all about. most interesting was the religious cremations of hindu loved ones through the complex. 24 hours after death, those being laid to rest are brought here and placed upon concrete tiers lined next to a small river which is supposedly sacred to all hindus. after a brief ceremony the dead are cremated on the tiers in public with their ashes being spread amongst the river. incredibly serene and interesting,this temple was probably my favorite side trip while in the city. the architecture of the complex is also very good and the sight/smells are unmatched. beware of the entrance as their are many oxen and cattle roaming freely as well as vendors haggling for a sale. once you enter take in the sights but if you want to take pics i recommend walking to the back of the complex and up the hill overlooking the temple as there are many others doing the same and it is more respectful. there are also a few asthetics within the complex willing to do a photo op for less than $2 usd if you're interested. it will cost you $1000 rupees o get in or about $10 usd. check out my pics and enjoy!
it shows the real life and that life goes on. very sereen atmosphere. impressive to be part of a very private occasion. it brings death near to life in the most positive way.

be sure and get one of the locals as a guide. i used a 1-armed man of about 40 that was fantastic. this site is extremely large with a lot of walking to do but well worth it. wear comfortable shoes and be ready to go up and down. bring water with you that you know is clean. so much to see so be sure to set aside several hours. well worth the visit.
i was sorry i could not see the temple inside and also other areas. but it was an interesting experience.

visiting pashupatinath gives rare glimpses into the end of life that is so hidden in western society. take a guide so you can get a deeper understanding of the place and the many kinds of rituals that occur there.

thought i'd be afraid but i wasn't. in fact, this was the most memorable part of our trip. the cultural significance of this site is astonishing. i was in awe and ended up staying almost 2 hours instead of the 30 minutes i thought i would only manage. we had a local guide and driver with us which i highly recommend. the smell can be overwhelming at first so i stayed high up on the bridge. bring money to tip the babas if you want a picture with them.

this was a great experience! we loved the magical feeling of this place. it is like entering another world.being greeted with "would you like to see some burning bodies" i was a bit reserved but i am so glad i visited! having a glimpse into a different culture, different burial ceremonies, ancient temples and beautiful surroundings.it made the visit go by too fast and even the heat didn't matter much. highly recommend visiting the place.
make sure you stop and watch the public cremations - it's quite acceptable for members of the public to observe what we would usually consider a private ceremony.

this is one of the holiest of hindu sites (only hindus are admitted to the inner temple), to western eyes, where loss and grieving are private matters, watching cremations taking place out in the open is quite raw. but you should not be deterred by this as the architecture is well worth seeing.

if you come hear for an experience similar to what you get in holy cities such as varanasi then expect to be disappointed. the sadhus (or holy men) are fake and are only after money. there are kids on drugs (glue sniffing) and the river is almost dry. the guides will take you in the daytime - but that's pointless. you must go in the evening around 07 pm. for the place to be interesting. and if you've not been to varanasi then you will probably get a kick out of the area.

we arrived today at the temple and got spooked of by the enormous entrance fee. a staggering 1000 r. to enter a sacred hindu site. if you are backpacking that could take up a big part of your budget. and if the money would all go to restoring and maintenance of the place. but as with durbar square you know that's not what's happening the first way of avoiding this is to get involved with one of the dodgy "tour guides" that offer you second hand tickets that where purchased in the morning and valid for one day. they will resell the ticket for half the price including their guide services. we where quite put off by the whole situation and decided to take a stroll around the area. whe ended up on the other side of the hill where a small trail leads you up the hill and along the fence for the temple grounds. you walk along a nice park where people play cricket and picknick in the park. there is a small set of stairways which lead down to the cremation area, you end up somewhere that feels behind the scenes of the temple. from there you can stroll around and leave the same way,
overall, a pretty disgusting place. the river is a tiny stream flowing through piles of garbage. the temple grounds are filthy, the burning bodies, animal sacrifices, etc., do not add to the charm.

according to puranas lord was discovered by a cow at this place and hence it is called as pashupatinath. this temple reveals that lord siva not only love to human beings but animals too. pandavas were searching lord siva during there way on heavenly abode but siva disguised himself in the form of an animal but recognized by bhima and he tried to catch him but siva disappeared,this incidence relates to kedarnath ji at uttrakhand and the mouth of disguised siva appered at pashupatinath temple at kathmandu in nepal. the temple is neat and clean having idols of budhe neelkantha, vasuki snake ,maa parvati, lord bhairon ji and big metal idol of lord nandi ji. the situation is near banks of bagmati river and 12 sivalinga are also siutated on the banks of the river. unfortunately river is having no flow of pure water. performance of cremation alongside river is not good. it increases air pollution and against the hindu feelings.places of cremation and pashubali should be separated from temple premises. nepal being hindu rashtra must consider this fact. however there is no snatching of money by pandas and mandir is having 4 gates for darshan.while at nepal one must visit this revered place of lord siva and seek the blessings. the heritage is well recognized by unesco world heritage sites and dates back to 400 a.d. lord siva and maa parvati visited this place to bless upon the devotees according to hindu shastras.

if you come hear for an experience similar to what you get in holy cities such as varanasi then expect to be disappointed. the sadhus (or holy men) are fake and are only after money. there are kids on drugs (glue sniffing) and the river is almost dry. the guides will take you in the daytimes - but that's pointless. you must go in the evening around 7pm for the place to be interesting. and if you've not been to varanasi then you will probably get a kick out of the area.

this place is not only old, but its also very sacred. it is one of the most important heritage for the hindus. this sacred site offers a scene that is really very hard to forget. the rituals to prayers that people offer there is really something to be acknowledged. this place is huge and it is definitely a place to experience a glimpse of hindusim and its faith in the people's heart there.

well.what a life experience this was. we watched the ceremony from start to finish as the dead were carried to the holy river, feet bathed, and the custom of preparing the body for cremation.right through to the laying of the body on the wooden platform, placing the fire in the mouth and then covering the body and allowing the cremation to take place. unbelievable!

at this temple you can easily visit the burning ghats. here the hindoeist people burn their relatives. in can be very confronting, so look at yourself and expessialy your children. its an easy walk from here to the bouddanath stupa. ask your way and the people will show you.
this is the famous holiest hindu temple of nepal. thousands of travelers are visited this temple during the seasons.
pashupatinath temple has its own value for hindu religion. the idol of lord shiva is of parash rock told by our guide.

as filthy as this place appears on the outside it offers an incredible cultural experience. i visited this temple twice to do my photography. i was rewarded with amazing pics of the baba ji's to the actual cremation ceremonies that were taking place.

pashupatinath is one of the oldest hindu temples in nepal, and its burning ghats are the main cremation site for nepali hindus. being allowed to see it is a huge privilege - hindu temples are normally strict about not letting non-hindus see their rites - so if you go please do so with respect & you'll learn a great deal about funeral practices in an otherwise fairly secretive religion

the temple pashupati nath temple is the biggest temple of lord shiva. this temple is a place where a person can go and spend few time chanting prayers and the foreigners may have a look at the priests praying in each and every corner of the temple in diffferent ways.
1000 roupies, that is 10 euros and you can not get into the temple, which is reserved for hindus, the only thing interesting is the crematoria but still 1000 roupies this is a lot of money in nepal
at rs1,000 each it is getting expensive. think carefully especially if you are on a budget. there are other places that provide more and the money is being used to resatore and maintain them

brilliant! if you go to the temple early morning lets say around 6 or in the evening after 6 and afternoon at around 2 you will enjoy the calmness and can avoid the crowd also would give you a sense of spirituality and religious divine of the temple! have visited the temple 3 times in past one year every-time i visit there is new facet of hinduism i get engaged to!
really interesting. worth seeing just to see how the different cultures manage death. have opportunity to be photographed with holy men

it was beautiful .lot of spirit magical sunset views and we witnessed special monday puja for lord siva time which was an experience like no other .hundred of people chanting and singing together with live music while the sun was setting behind the beautiful roof top of the temple.amazing .must see!
culturally awakening. its worth paying someone a few ruppies to explain what is all going on. this is a 24 hour affair but i was there in the late afternoon and saw all that i needed to.

visit in the evening at about 18.30pm. each night there is a celebratory/ mystical and musical ceremony to worship lord shiva. it is a spine tingling experience, never to be forgotten. the celebration lasts for about an hour. i have always found the hindu participants so welcoming to visitors.
a sacred hindu place and very moving with the cremations happening right in front of you. i visited all the major attractions in kathmandu and found hear and boudanath stupa the two best.

excellent temple, very sacred for hindus beware the temple is right next to cremation ground. the view of cremation ground may disturb a lot of people and children so better stay away from the outer boundry of temple. other then that very nice place

i visited pashupatinath as part of a day long unesco heritage site tour. of the four major sites we visited this was my least favorite. it is a very holy place for hindus and is where all cremations in kathmandu for hindus take place. please note these cremations are visible and in the open air. bring a scarf or mask with you. you will 95% sure see at least one cremation. on the day we were there there were four occurring when we walked in. non-hindus cannot enter the temple and most end up staying on one side of the complex. i have been to a lot of holy places of all sorts of different religions before and this one didn't have the feeling like a holy place. it felt like it was a neglected place where people are easily taken advantage of. there are also "sadhus" sitting around and will let you take a picture for a price. it just overall had a negative feeling to me. i kept trying to see and feel why it was holy but i couldn't. you will also most likely get hassled from the street all the way to the temple complex by people selling things. if you go i would plan to spend less than a half hour here.

this temple is only allowed for hindus to enter, others can only view it from afar. it's right next to the funeral home of kathmandu where you may see deceased being cremated. please try not to take pictures to respect the family members of the deceased. but it's an unesco site.

the pashupatinath temple is one of the most sacred and important hindu temples in the world and dedicated to lord shiva. the temple is located on the banks of the bagmati river and you could get a good feel of hindu traditions and culture there. you can find a little pagoda town full of monkeys running around but i felt a bit uncomfortable seeing the cremation ceremony from the other side of the holy bagmati river.

we've been there in march 2013. you'll have the opportionity to see local funerals - that is - local burning of the deads. the place is interesting and gives you an insight to the local religious ceremonies. i'm not sure that it would be the best place for children.
its great place to see real, powerful face of shiva. i like sit deep inside park, on ancient stones, listening to the birds and monkeys. good for any kind of meditations if you are practicing

if you are a hindu, visit this temple but avoid doing darshan. there is no bhakti-seva among the pandits here. they are only after money and are extremely greedy. non-hindus need not worry about this as they won't let you in. my husband had to wait outside in spite of being married to a hindu.
a great temple to visit. i liked the drum beats from the attic of the entrance. the temple has no artificial constructions. its pure and magical!
the temple area is huge and is really worth visiting especially if you are a hindu. get yourself blessed.

although only hindus can enter the actual temple, there is still plenty to see on the site. the sight of burning bodies may not be for the squeamish but the rituals are fascinating. i'd recommend a guide to give you a good understanding of what's going on but, as with most things in kathmandu, negotiate a price before you start. from here you can walk to bodnath and the contrast between the two sites is worth seeing.

i always wanted to visit this temple and this time i got a chance.its a very holy place , an ancient temple of lord shiva.i went there at around 11 am.which was aarti time and i was lucky to watch all the rituals.its si spiritual and mesmerizing. can't express exactly in words.must must visit temple of kathmandu for everyone. taking snaps inside the temple is prohibited.

as well super exotic and a beautiful big hindu temple where you see them in their religious practice and also for funerals (burning the bodies like in india giving the ashes into the river) must do in kathmandu

very nice to walk on cool weather day, i was sitting down at a corner watching how the local would move around so carefreely, it is unlike where i come from, everyone seems to be either rushing home or work. beautiful culture and people.

late feb-mar 2013 pashupatinath - was a funeral and cremation place. apparently the sacred river is dirty because all things that are brought/used for the funeral ceremony must be thrown into the sacred river along with the ashes of the dead. the dead is fed with rice grain and sent off with tikka powder as final beautification, given drink from sacred river and covered in yellow cloth. packs of natural oil are placed on logs of wood, unclothed and burnt just as is, in the belief human enters the world naked and thus, will return naked. similar to what job noted in the book of job in the bible - "naked came i out of the earth, naked, shall i return." ! it is interesting to learn that the river is cleansed once a year by the head of towns, armies, police and locals. this part of temple is opened 24 hours, 365 days, very efficient indeed. a mask can be handy if you have sensitive sinus depending on the timing. if you were superstitious and not used to seeing the dead, it is better to just look from afar and leave before entering the final part of this temple. if only a way could be found to put the dead and his/er final belongings 'eco-ly' into the river.

nice place with intense feeling ,good view of shivlingums in one row ,ceremation ceremony and ghats for brahmins,vips and royal family.only thing i dont like the concept of not allowing inside non-hindu i was with the friend and he was stopped as he is foreigner so i didn't feel like to go inside too.main logic behind this is that temple is known as pashupati (god of animals) and somehow they linked animal category only to cow and stops non-hindu to be sure that anyone who eats cow meat should not enter the main premises.but to my dismay i see many nepali people eating on streets,restaurants and common places buffalo meat which is also from the same family line of cow and animal too for this also they have a logic that buffalo is a carrier of yama and a symbol of devil so eating it is considered eating the devil? crazy logic as i myself is a hindu and traveled most of spiritual places and some spiritual rituals and beliefs is not understandable by me and many others too people are simply following it because its going on from many generations and as hindu we really have to understand what is logical and which is not instead of just following it as our elders did.

i would have given this place top marks except for the heavy feeling one has upon entering it. definitely, one of the holiest hindu places in nepal. but the whole open cremation process that is open for everyone to see is hard to describe. definitely not for the faint hearted as the deceased is not fully covered.

first of all, the ticket counter made me pay even though i am indian. the tour guide hovering around us tried to stop me from going in with my ticket because when i did, everyone was really pissed that i was forced to pay and made me get my money back - which was nice. the temple itself is beautiful but the surrounding area that the tourists get to see is not really worth the entrance fee.

i visited this temple while on my recent trip to nepal. it is a must see in kathmandu. it is a huge campus and finding the main temple may require some enquiries. you will see a lot of sadhus - most of them dressed in dazzling orange dresses. don't be surprised if some of them are smoking dope - after all, they are worshipers of lord shiva. couple of things i didn't like about the temple - 1. only indians are allowed to go inside the main temple. hinduism is never restrictive. foreigners have access to all outer temples and the outer area of the main temple campus. 2. there is a cremating ground right inside the campus - that adds a bit of uneasiness.
a must see for all visitors to nepal. there are hundreds of hindu and buddhist temples in ktm but this should be in the top of the list. visited thrice in my 11 days stay. srinivasan muscat, oman
this is a fascinating cultural experience for visitors. give yourself the time to visit. if you can, read up on hinduism and hindu death rituals before you visit.
learn a bit about hinduism and nepal first, if you can. some of what the guides will tell you is hogwash, but it's always entertaining.

it is quite unlike a shiva temple. it's a shanti peeth and only hindus are allowed in the temple. the place is divine and has a good feel to it. one one side of the temple there is a cremation ground which has a bad stench to it. overall good experience and recommended. beware of the touts on the way

this is a unesco supported site and is undergoing quite a bit of redevelopment. you can also witness a live cremation! something i found upsetting in nepal in general is that you cannot enter a hindu temple unless you are a hindu.

everyone should come here to witness the public hindu cremations. as a westerner, when you get there you should pay for a guide (negotiate first - no more than a few hundred rs) to understand more of why its a public ceremony and the reasons behind the part of the ceremony. it's a sad yet fascinating place, but like i said, worth seeing once.

we discretely viewed a funeral from the far bank of the bagmati river. while the smell and smoke were unpleasant, it was something we had never experienced in our lives. the sadhus roaming the grounds, various families waiting for their loved ones to be cremated, and people with various health problems begging for alms added to the tone. as non-hindus, we were not allowed to enter the temple itself, but we did walk up to the front and look inside as far as we were able. the children using magnets to fish for coins in the river was interesting as well.

love it or hate the sight of burning bodies is something many people find quit emotional. the bagmati is not a big river at this point so you don't get the same feel as varanasi but then you are much closer so its feels more personal. make sure you walk up to the tombs on the hill as well and keep your eye out for monkeys !

pashupatinath is a hindu temple on the banks of the bagmati river in deopatan, a village 3 km northwest of kathmandu. it is dedicated to a manifestation of shiva called pashupati (lord of animals). it attracts thousands of pilgrims each year and has become well known far beyond the kathmandu valley. the temple is barred to non-hindus, but a good view of the temple can be had from the opposite bank of the river.

near this temple is a cremation site along the river. i found this very interesting, as there were several cremations going on at the time. our guide explained about the hindu customs regarding death and cremation. non hindus may not enter the temple.

we visited the pashupatinath temple on the first day of the legendary birthday of lord shiva, with at least a half a million hindus. surprisingly things were far more peaceful than expected. don´t miss it ! don´t start off before 3 pm. take the shortcut from the airport side and scramble to the temples an ghats on the eastern side of the bagmati river. here you can get close contact to pilgrims and sadhus. leave after sunset, when things get a little freaky.
you can not miss this place. you should be prepared for the visit. to see the cremations all around. you should visit both sides of the river and walk around. enjoy it

to visit this area and be viewing mourning families from across the river then observing their deceased family member being cremated in front of people taking photos was quite interesting to say the least. it made me feel both entranced and intrusive at the same time. a very worth while place to visit. an entry fee is payable and be wary of people who push themselves on you as a guide then expect payment.

this is a great place to visit and is an integral part of hindu/neplaese culture. spare at least an hour for a good look round and a walk up the hill for some great views. many have suggested asking an unofficial guide which are very inexpensive.

the temple itself is off limits to all non-hindu's and so you can only walk around the outer grounds and if you are into that sort of thing, watch the bodies being cremated beside the filthy stream. ok for an hour or so, but not worth the rp.500 entry fee in my opinion.

a visit to pashupati temple in kathmandu is an absolute must. this is a busy place with many different things to see and experience. i was amazed how on one level i felt as if i was part of the process involved with families dealing with the death of their loved ones contrasted with the cheeky monkeys who run around ready to snatch anything you might have. these two things happen side by side so you experience the emotion of life and death at the same time.it is a place you can take time to reflect on what is really important in life, and it is quite moving to see the rituals involved around death and yet this is a happy place to visit. go and visit and just see what comes up for you.

we happened to be fortunate enough to visit here the day prior to shiva's 'big celebration' day so things were really ''pumping' here and literally 'thousands were there., so there was lots to capitivate you. not so sure if it would have been so diverse on a 'quioet day' as with all 'tourist sites' around ktm you are reaching into the back pocket for entrance fees again- and not that cheap either!

to visit this place without a guide can feel a bit uncomplete. luckily there was a young student at the entrance offering his duty for.you will pay me in the end as much as you want. so i learned a lot about this amazing place. hope at least most of it was truth :)
heritage architecture. great place to have serenity. the temple is a place to take blessings. beautiful temple.

the temple is an amazing place to visit from funeral pyres and saddhus, votive shrines and monkeys.all gives an interesting insight into the culture. shame about the river and the litter. well worth a visit.

this temple is a must see place, when visiting kathmandu. you can get a complete feel of hindu culture and vedic traditions at the site; filled with incense sticks, mantras in the air and bells ring. this temple has 4 doors and the deity can be seen from all the four doors and stays always crowded. photography and wearing shoes/slippers are not allowed in the temple premises. there are a lot of stores selling rudraksha beads around the temple and do check for the authenticity before you buy them with a good bargain. you can feed pigeons or give any offerings to the temple as wished. kindly avoid the river banks at the end of the temple as that location is used for cremating the dead (as per hindu traditions).

you have everything at pashupatinath: ancient sights, current culture (cremations); monks, monkeys, deer. just an exciting and interesting place to be. my favorite sight in katmandu. we had someone follow us around as a guide; not asked for.he just followed us around, told us about the sights, and expressed disappointment about the size of our tip.
makes you reflect on life, death and your destiny. only wish is that the govt and local people will do something positive to alleviate the litters and bad pollution to the precious river.

the temple is on the banks of the bagmati river and is ahindu temple. you have to walk quite a few stairs to reach the top platform. on the banks opposite the temple is the cremation site. we actually watched the preparation for a cremation and then the full ceremony. after the cremation, the ashes are returned to the river.
i was every much eager to see this temple but sorry to say i was not happy at all after i see because this is a place where the dead bodies are burnt which make me very sad

this place is south asia's answer to federico fellini. pashupati is sacred to hindus, especially followers of shiva. to walk its extensive grounds is to stroll through some of hinduism's most sacred lore: "here's where lord shiva leapt across the river"; "here's where a part of devi's body fell." (buddhism's too: tilopa and naropa allegedly meditated there, on the banks of the bagmati.) it's been around for more than 1700 years. so visiting it is like a wal through human history. there are gangs of monkeys and rows of fake holy men, preening their moustaches and demanding huge fees for photos. the paths along the river are an open air funeral parlor, where families cremate their dead loved ones. but the forest and temple complexes up bove are a tranquil place where real holy men meditate and pilgrims wander in peace. it's a bewildering, fascinating place. worth a visit. know that it's life "unfiltered".

don't just go to see the burning ghats. these are sacred hindu shrines, and the devoted are colorful and friendly. lot of flowers and temples. take care with your camera, the monkeys can be a bit annoying.

i was astonished mainly seeing the area of the whole paspati temple. it was big. the architecture is note worthy, the place is moderately clean. i see people have lot of time for devotion in god in nepal. the dead people are burnt openly which is bad and all ashes are thrown in the rivver which flows through.

the place is really dirty with bad smell all over the place as corpse were brunt openly and also throwing the ashes in the canal making it worse. due to burning the corpse the place is really suffocating. its better not going there unless you are hindu.

me and my husband just got back from nepal.had the chance of visiting the temple.in one word its huge and of course of religious significance for hindus.i was happy to see a world heritage.but the smoke and smell was suffocating sometimes.its one of a kind experience to explore the chita burning(cremetion).not enjoyable for all but for those who care for a heritage.its free for the saarc countries only.

pre book a puja - a day earlier by going to the temple office - enjoy being close to the lord & take eternal blessings ! a must visit for anybody spiritually inclined - located in the heart of the city - next to the airport - no ques - no trouble some temple priests !
i visited the temple just before it was about to close and while standing in front of the shiva linga i witnessed a monkey who had caught hold of a mans back.
here you can look down on a hindu temple and cremation site. you will also be accosted by sadhus (wandering hindu holy men).
this site is interesting, although non-hindu's are not allowed inside the temple. this holy site is near the bagmati river where cremation ceremonies are held daily.
blessed are the ones who get opportunity to visit the temple.there is always heavy rush so plan accordingly

well worth a visit. there's lots to see, plenty of temples and shrines in the park behind the main (hindu-only) pashupatinath temple. a lot of monkeys too, which don't mess with you if you don't annoy them. the burning pyres on the ghats are also a humbling experience.

pashupatinath temple is one of the most ancient temples in the world. had the previlege to visit the temple in feb-13. you might find some rush after 9 am so try to visit earlier to avoid the rush. the temple is very big & it might take some to visit the entire temple. please note that the entry is for hindu's only(although didn't like that). outside the temple you may find many small & big stalls for "rudraksh". remember if you are in nepa, buy the big rudraksh. the big rudraksh are the one which are found in nepal. the small ones are brough from india & they might be expensive due to that. remember to bargain heavily if you are buying.

the experience was eye-opening. i went there in the evening and saw a gathering of people along the river. they were about to do a funeral with a dead body wrapped in yellow cloth lying on the stairs by the river. the atmosphere was indescribable . haunting, too real, yet beautiful. the fact that the air you breathed in smelled of burned corpses could make you feel uncomfortable but at the same time conscious about your presence and the meaning of life and death. i wanted to explore the hospice but my friend was too scared to accompany me. we left after 10 minutes. if you haven't seen anything like this, visit this place for once.
very strange place for a westener. it is possible to follow the funeral service. odd place. i would recommend going there in the evening when it is dark.

i loved this place. we were there at sunset and the sun setting behind skeletal like trees on the top of the hills created a wonderful image. seeing the cremation fires burning and people saying their final goodbyes to their loved ones was moving but also a very life affirming experience.

we spent several hours at the temple and surrounds and found the whole thing absolutely facinating. you dont need to be hindu to respect their beliefs in the river and god(s) and how they approach and deal with the topic of death and there is no better way to learn about it than to see it in action. we had our hotel drop us off and then were appoached by a guide at the gate who was very knowledgable. my only regret is not negotiating a fee in advance as i fear we probably paid a lot more than market rate but chalked that up to another lesson learnt as a traveller. it was especially facinating to see the whole cycle - the nursing home, the hospice, the crematoria, the temple, the widows area, and even the caves and chanting mourners up the river. of course the river is filthy ( they cremate up to 50 bodies a day !) it was sad to see children trawling the river for valuables but this is not unique to the temple, nor nepal. the resident religious men will hit you up for a dollar or two for photos but its a small price to pay for a very unique photo !

a very significant temple for the hindu faith and the temple itself is not open to non-hindus. never the less, the complex is worth a visit and most significantly the crematoria that is located outside the temple along the river. this is the part that is not for everyone. standing across the river from the temple you can witness the preparation of the dead and ultimately the burning of these bodies on funeral pyres.

this was my 2nd visit to pashupatinath where i introduced my sister to this unbelievable site. first of all, non-hindus are prohibited from entering their temple, but there's plenty to experience in the environs. many foreigners are a bit shocked to see the non-stop cremations burning along the river, but they do make for some interesting photos. beware of the non-stop stream of "local guides" who will nag you to death. this site does not require a long stay and we were happy to move on to boudanath and swayambhuath for a more relaxed buddhist cultural experience.

the ultimate shiva temple in the world! i have been here twice. this trip was something special as i had arranged before arriving to get a special pooja done. the experience was more than just spiritual as you are part of ancient tradition and history. the temple priests are extremely humble and helpful with all requests. i am planning another visit - hopefully for shivarathri 2013 - which i believe is the biggest shivarathri in the world. this site comes extremely highly recommended as the temple is well maintained and kept neat and tidy - you will enjoy this temple. also, read about the tradition and history first - this will enhance your experience of the temple.

we reached the pashupatinath temple during the evening aarti and witnessed the complete ritual followed in it. the people were friendly and guided us at most times. loved the sanctity of the temple campus. the water body in the lake was serene in the evening. we only wished we could spend some more time. one can buy red coral beads and 'rudraksha' here.
great place with numerous of god and goddess all around. the environment is itself devoted. must visit place for the travelers who come to nepal.

to a westerner, the complexity of this place may be totally surreal. we had an amazing guide who explained a lot for us to be able to realize what this place is and its significance to the nepalese people. another unique place to go see and experience.

pashupati temple is the most significant shiva temples in the world which is highly regarded by hindus. it situated in kathmandu, capital of nepal, besides the bank of bagmati river. in earlier days only born hindus were allowed to enter into the temple. however, now the visitors are allowed to enter the temple but no one can take pictures inside the temple premises. the big bull made up of brass guards the door of the temple in front. the temple has four doors in four direction which opens only one time in a day. on the shiva ratri and teej festivals the temple is highly crowded and government has to deploy thousands of police men in order to manage the visitors.

the entry fee of 500 r was well worth it. keep in mind that you will have someone approach you not long after entering, just having a chat to you in english about different things around. my 9 year old twins and i went twice. the first time we just wanted to be left alone and relax but the guy wouldn't leave despite us ignoring him at first. if you don't want them to keep on, you must be really, really firm. the kids were impressed with the cows roaming around. the holy men covered in ash was another hit. if you take pictures with them they don't mind but you must give them a tip. about 30 r is ok but they will look at it as though you have given them a piece of dirt. don't worry about that. there are so many amazing things to look at. the burning pyres with families sitting around. the wrapped bodies laying beside the river, waiting to be put on the fire. this a place where the guide is definately worth every penny but fix a price first. we paid 500 r for an hour or so. it's worth walking up the beautiful steps and sitting for a while, looking over the river and the people. the prices of souvenirs is inflated here. at least 50% off. just walk away and they will follow you. we took a private taxi from thamel as it was quite warm and easier with the twins. 300r each way.

you can see firsthand the pollution in the holiest river in nepal, but if you are not hindu you cannot get into the actual temple, only the courtyards.the grounds were nothing truly exceptional. it was interesting seeing the cremation ceremonies going on.there were lots of people around, seemed to be locals. monkeys are around too, so beware of your food/bags. the old "babas" that are holy men with dreadlocks and traditional clothing are most likely only there because they want you to pay to take a photo of them, or with them. don't fall for that. i enjoyed other historic sights in kathmandu compared to this.

we hired a tour guide and experienced in one day the holiest place for hindus and buddhists - good contrast from pashupatinath and the boudha stupa. i think to really appreciate this place you do need a guide or a good travel book to find out the importance of this place to the hindus in nepal. not a lot to do and if you are not a hindu you cannot enter the temple so only an hour or so needed. the saddus were funny as there were 4 of us in our group and each time we went to get our photo taken with them they whispered to us their price. started off at 300 rupees and the last person was asked for 800! didn't get their price! on a serious note this is a place of mourning and also to be respectful to the grieving families- i didn't like when people were taking pictures of the funeral pyres like it was some tourist attraction. for me it was something i had only seen on the tv so to see it first hand was interesting but also moving.

this is the place which i would never visit again . it is worth a visit once to know the importance and the heritage importance. how a place can be kept unclean , we got to visit this place. corpse being burnt at one place , dust pollution , dirty water , noisy, urchins troubles;dirty smell; beggars , so called sadhus who sends creeps up your spine . a very uneasy feeling after this visit to this pshupatinath

very holy place with lots of pilgrims from india and abroad very serene atmosphere lots of monkeys have to be careful needs more basic toilets and wash rooms drinking water facilities and police out posts some good restaurants for snacks and tea
interesting, but you have to read to make you mind before you came the first time. very special for hindu and difficult to westerns. odor, very dirty river and observers. do not recommend to visit

visiting pashupatinath is a fascinating experience. go and sit and watch the world go by as there is something happening in every direction. take your camera as well. noone seems to mind having their photograph taken.

this temple challenged me greatly. i must admit, i could not wait to get out of there once i was inside, yet when reflecting, i am glad i visited and had the chance to feel uncomfortable. being a white woman, i was a minority and stared at by everyone there. there were bodies being cremated right in front of me and being pushed into the cement creek below. i say that it challenged my identity because i went there believing that i embraced difference and culture but realised that when placed into an environment where the caste system is explicit and "in your face" that i struggled with it immensely and did not embrace any of it. that being said, it was an experience i'll never forget

if you can come in from the side, it won't be so stressful as coming in the main entrance, where you will be hassled non-stop to buy something. i went there twice, and the second time came in from a side entrance after crossing the river, and it was really nice and local. come out near the deer park zoo part. the top of the temple is very old and peacful, so it is well worth to walk up to the top of the hill and spend some time there. here you can meet some interesting hindhu yogis also.
go relax and let the peace flow within you. nice place if you are alone and want to spend some time alone.
go here to (from a distance) experience the burning ritual of the nepali people. it is also a beautiful place with the temples, trees and holy people (saddhus).

i have been there a couple of times, and i would highly suggest spending a few dollars and getting a tour guide to explain what is going on. on two occasions, we got a tour guide and were amazed by what was going on. the first time we paid a kid near the entrance to take us around and he explained who the people were and what the ceremonies that took place there were all about. the second time, we actually got to see the entire process from the time that a body is washed in the water of the river to the actual cremation. once you understand what is taking place, it becomes much more spiritual. i highly suggest going by. and even if you dont see a cremation, the sodu's are always fun to chat with

a really interesting experience but also quite bizarre. loads of tourists arriving to watch funerals. families are saying goodbye to their loved ones whilst tourists look on. nearby a woman washes her clothes in the 'river' and another near her washing her hair. some amazing temples.

this amazing hindu complex is one of the most venerated in all east. though one can not enter the temple itself, the compound is ver large and there are many other smaller temples one can visit. here people burn their dead.

very interesting temple complex. not all accessible to non hindus, but lots to see. you may see the cremations taking place, which i found interesting, but some may not. you can get pestered a bit here , and the sadhus will want money for a photo. the entrance fee is a bit steep, but well worth a visit for a couple of hours
it's one of the holiest places for hindus where they cremate the dead. not for the faint-hearted. visitors are allowed up to the bridge. a "must see"!

the place is worth being seen for its architecture, offering various constructions of hinduism style, but also for the activity going around it, it's obviously a religious place placed high in the esteem of the city people

a great spiritual place for me and fulfilled a longheld wish to visit and pray. fortunately landed on a day when it was not crowded and had the opportunity to soak in the place and atmosphere. caution when utilising the services of pandits around the temple, it is a nexus of indian pandits and out to make some good money. did not feel the recitals & chanting by them had any spiritual connections.
have one of the locals guide you through. it's worth the $10 to have a guide. really neat experience, especially if cremations occurring.

this is a very sacred, important place to the people, for a westerner it is quite disturbing in one way , fasinating in another, the holy river is here, where the dead are brought to be creamated, visually it is quite challenging

it was a very spiritual place and seeing so many devoted hindus is really amazing. the temple is beautiful, luckily as i and my family are hindus, we were able to enter and it is a truly magical temple, there is something special about it that i would definitely recommend other hindus to visit. if you cannot go inside, it is still nice to soak up the atmosphere outside and witness people going about their business.

i felt a bit uncomfortable watching the cremation ceremony from the opposite side of the river.the river is filthy and really, i couldn't wait to leave. our guide was describing what was happening during the ceremony but i felt sorry for the families as it is such a tourist attraction with everyone taking photos. there were monkeys running around which took your mind off what was happening across the river.for a few minutes. this is a place i won't forget in a hurry and a definite insight into another culture.

we had been recommended to make the pashupatinath temple our first stop and were glad we did. it gives an insight into hindu culture that it is hard to find elsewhere. it is not beautiful and it is not clean and there is poverty and there are beggars and there are funeral pyres and cremation ceremonies and there are criplings and the river is incredibly filthy. still, it is thought-provoking and different and quite unforgettable. following a tour of the grounds we walked from pashupatinath to bodnath along the route described in the lonely planet guidebook and recommend that peaceful stroll through suburbian kathmandu.
a fascinating insight and an amazing window into another culture. not to be missed when visiting ktm

this is an amazing temple, and dedicated to the godess pashupati, an ephitet of the godess shiva. it has two remarcable parts, the crematory area and the pagoda area, both separated by the main river. the crematory area is, generally, full of tourist that doesn't respect at all the pain of the families that are suffering for their deads. it's a frivolous fact to see how the tourist shoot photos and more photos without thinking in this, it has became a show. my reccomendation is to watch this ritual with respect and silence, but above all, to visit the pagoda area in which only a few tourist are interested to go. you can go just going to the right following the path of the river, climbing the long tram of stairs. you will find a little pagoda town full of monkeys. beware of them, they are wild, dont show or give them food, otherwise they may attack you, i've seen it. treat them like they are: animals. if you're lucky, you'll be able to see the monks that live there doing their tasks. this is a really pacific place where you can enjoy the peace of the swayambhunath.
i liked the place for it's sense of spirituality and our place in the world order. i would go back and visit here again when i return to kathmandu.

in our western society, we don't have many encounter with death, in pashupatinath things are different, i saw many cremation ceremony. it felt very strange to know that dead body were lying under the pile of wood and dried herb, don't worry it's all we can see. it's impossible to see the actual body. this experience has touched me deeply, i highly reccomend a visit to this site.

this is a splendid piece of architecture. very different from the temples one sees in india, or the gopurams in southern part of india. the pagoda like style is very unique for a temple. there are lot of small temples in the vicinity. the statues of the deities and the shiva lingam too is huge in size , and worth a look . temple premises tend to be crowded, and littered be ready for the same.
very thought provoking the hospice on the banks of the river then the funeral pyres. lots of sadhu. i found it a beautiful calm place

the temple is something you should see on a visit, as it is the holiest place in hinduism, but be prepared for filth. the grounds are littered with trash, burning waste, and burning bodies. you can witness cremations happening right in front of you on wooden pyres. out of respect, we took very few photos and only when we knew it was ok. the families of the deceased light the fires and in most cases their relative has died only hours before. we hired one of the numerous guides to tell us about the temple and i would actually advise it. they do know a lot and its fun to talk to them. but negotiate a price before. a reasonable price is around ten dollars us, paid at the end. if your guide goes above and beyond, feel free to give a small tip as extra. our guide was great, but if you don't like any that approach you, just waive them off until you find someone you like. there is a lot of history here and its worth a look.

pashupatinath temple is one of the most significant hindu temples of lord shiva in the world, located on the banks of the bagmati river in the eastern part of kathmandu, the capital of nepal. the temple served as the seat of the national deity, lord pashupatinath. the temple is listed in unesco world heritage sites. pashupatinath temple has a reputation of “the temple of living beings”. every day, thousands of pilgrims from all over the world come to pay homage to this temple. come here to get a deep explore of hinduism.with its astonishing architectural beauty, it is the embodiment of faith, religion, culture and tradition. pashupatinath is a holy temple of hindu lord shiva who is known as pashupati that means the master of the whole universe. the most important festival celebrated in this temple is shivaratri or the nights of lord shiva. the river bagmati that flows next to pashupatinath temple is also a holy river with many sacred properties. hindu devotee plunge into the holy bagmati river daily and recite the verse from the ancient vedas. it is believed that plunging or bathing into the holy river releases from the cycle of rebirth. thousands of pilgrims from all over the world come to pay homage to this temple, so it has a reputation of “the temple of living beings”.
hindu temple with all the trimmings. cremations, wandering sadhus, puja ceremonies, you name it. don't miss it!

shri pashupatinath temple is one of the most sacred of the hindu temples, is the lord shiva temple. it is said that this is over 2000 years old and is very unique due to its architecture and also the story of its existance. only hindu religion people are allowed to enter the temple. the priests of the temple have always been from india for generations, and it continues. monday is the sacred day of lord shiva, and therefore many devotees from nepal and abroad visit the temple. carrying mobile phones, camera and any article made from leather is strictly prohibited. there are lockers provided free of cost, to safely store your belongings. there is one main temple, surrounded by several smaller temples in the main premises. a huge 'nandi' bull is seen facing lord shiva. the main shiv lingam has four faces of lord shiva in four directions.

this place is so incredibly different from anything we've seen before pashupatinath is one of the most sacred places in the hindu religion. it is set up much like the jewish temple of old, with different courtyards for those who are hindi & those who are not. to the right of the complex is a white hospice building where the near death await their final hour so that they might have their feet placed in the holy bahgmati river while they die, so that their spirit may escape on to paradise. try to find a guide. but please try to take picture with discretion.

this temple is not to be missed for an insight into the death hindu rituals. nuff said. our guide actually brought us to the other side of the river where are can have a good view of the funeral rites. some explanations would be good to explain what the family members are actually doing. this site is a mini-version of what you can see on the banks of the ganges. everything is up close and easily viewed. a visit to kathmandu would not be complete without this
